topics.title: Random Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Random Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Random Sampling** is a statistical sampling technique in which every individual member of a population (of size $N$) has an equal, independent, and non-zero probability of being selected into the sample (of size $n$). When this condition is strictly met, the resulting subset of observations is called a **Simple Random Sample (SRS)**, and the procedure is formally termed **Simple Random Sampling (SRS)**.

The KTU 2024 Scheme defines random sampling under two cardinal operational regimes:

1. **Sampling With Replacement (SWR):** Each selected unit is returned to the population before the next draw, making the draws **statistically independent**. The population size effectively remains $N$ at every draw.
2. **Sampling Without Replacement (SWOR):** Each selected unit is permanently removed, making the draws **statistically dependent**. The population size reduces by one after each draw, i.e., $N, N-1, N-2, \ldots, N-n+1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Engineering Statistics & Quality Control, the term "Random Sampling" most commonly refers to **Simple Random Sampling Without Replacement (SRSWOR)** because it provides **smaller sampling error** for the same sample size, which is a recurring 14-mark examination theme.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lottery Ticket Method

Imagine a teacher has a class of **120 students** ($N = 120$) and needs to pick **10 students** ($n = 10$) for a quality inspection audit of a manufacturing lab.

- **With Replacement (SWR):** The teacher writes every roll number on a chit, draws one, **notes the name, and puts the chit back** into the bag. The same student *could* be picked twice. Each draw is a fresh, independent event.
- **Without Replacement (SWOR):** The teacher draws 10 chits one by one and **keeps them out** of the bag. Once a roll number is drawn, it cannot appear again. The 10 selected students are all unique.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in Engineering?** In quality control, when we randomly pick **50 components** from a production batch of **1000** to test tensile strength, we almost always test *different* components (SWOR). If we tested the *same* component repeatedly (SWR), the data would be artificially correlated, and our estimates of process variability would be **biased low**.

## 1.3 Standard Terminology in KTU Notation

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $N$ | Population size (finite/infinite) | Unitless count |
| $n$ | Sample size | Unitless count |
| $\mu$ | Population mean | Depends on variable |
| $\sigma^2$ | Population variance | Square of variable unit |
| $\bar{x}$ | Sample mean estimator | Depends on variable |
| $s^2$ | Sample variance (unbiased) | Square of variable unit |
| $n/N$ | Sampling fraction | Dimensionless ratio |
| $1 - n/N$ | Finite Population Correction (FPC) | Dimensionless ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Finite Population Correction (FPC)** factor, denoted mathematically as $\sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}$, is the single most important KTU high-yield concept that distinguishes a finite-population sample mean from an infinite-population sample mean. Always check whether the question states $N$ explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Logic of Simple Random Sampling

The procedure is governed by **four foundational principles**, which KTU examiners explicitly test in Part A (3-mark) questions:

1. **Principle of Equal Probability (Epistemic Fairness):** Every unit $i$ in the population has the same chance $\frac{1}{N}$ of being selected in any single draw.
2. **Principle of Independence (only in SWR):** The selection of one unit does not influence the probability of selecting another.
3. **Principle of Representativeness:** The sample should mirror the population's structural characteristics (mean, variance, distribution).
4. **Principle of Unbiasedness:** The expected value of every sample statistic must equal the corresponding population parameter.

## 2.2 The Sampling Distribution of the Sample Mean ($\bar{x}$)

The **sample mean** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ is a **random variable** because different random samples yield different values of $\bar{x}$. The probability distribution of $\bar{x}$ across all possible samples of size $n$ is called its **sampling distribution**.

### 2.2.1 Mean of the Sampling Distribution

By the **Central Limit Theorem** and the **law of large numbers**, the mean of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ is exactly equal to the population mean, regardless of the underlying population distribution:

$$E(\bar{x}) = \mu_{\bar{x}} = \mu$$

### 2.2.2 Variance of the Sampling Distribution (SWR — Infinite Population)

When sampling is done **with replacement** (or from an effectively infinite population), the variance of the sample mean is simply the population variance divided by the sample size:

$$\sigma^2_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

The **standard error of the mean** is:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

### 2.2.3 Variance of the Sampling Distribution (SWOR — Finite Population)

When sampling is done **without replacement** from a finite population, the variance is **reduced** by a multiplicative factor called the **Finite Population Correction (FPC)**:

$$\sigma^2_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{N-n}{N-1}$$

The **standard error** becomes:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The FPC $\sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}$ is always $\leq 1$. It tells the quality engineer that **sampling without replacement is more efficient** because it reduces estimation error. When $n \ll N$ (rule of thumb: $n/N \leq 0.05$), the FPC $\approx 1$ and can be ignored, justifying the use of the simpler infinite-population formula.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / KTU Note |
|---|---------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Sample Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ | Always defined |
| 2 | Mean of $\bar{x}$ | $E(\bar{x}) = \mu$ | Unbiased estimator |
| 3 | Variance of $\bar{x}$ (Infinite / SWR) | $\sigma^2_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$ | Use when $N$ is not given or $n/N \leq 0.05$ |
| 4 | Variance of $\bar{x}$ (Finite / SWOR) | $\sigma^2_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \dfrac{N-n}{N-1}$ | Use when both $N$ and $n$ are given explicitly |
| 5 | Finite Population Correction (FPC) | $\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}$ | Always $\leq 1$ |
| 6 | Standard Error of Mean | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \text{FPC}$ | Reported with $\pm$ in QC reports |
| 7 | Sample Variance (Unbiased) | $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ | Divides by $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) |
| 8 | Variance of Sample Sum | $\sigma^2_{\sum x_i} = n\sigma^2$ (SWR) | Used in quality cost modelling |
| 9 | Variance of Sample Proportion | $\sigma^2_{\hat{p}} = \dfrac{pq}{n}$ (Infinite) | $q = 1-p$ |
| 10 | Finite-Pop Proportion Variance | $\sigma^2_{\hat{p}} = \dfrac{pq}{n} \cdot \dfrac{N-n}{N-1}$ | Used in attribute inspection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** The divisor in sample variance is $n-1$, **not** $n$. Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ leads to a *biased* estimate of $\sigma^2$ — a 2-mark deduction that examiners consistently apply.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Application

In **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** of a ball-bearing manufacturing line, an engineer randomly samples $n = 25$ bearings from a lot of $N = 500$. Measuring their diameters gives $\bar{x} = 10.02$ mm and $s = 0.05$ mm. The engineer computes:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} \approx \frac{0.05}{\sqrt{25}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{500-25}{499}} \approx 0.01 \times 0.975 = 0.00975 \text{ mm}$$

A **95% confidence interval** for the true mean diameter is $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}} = 10.02 \pm 0.0191$ mm. If the engineering tolerance is $\pm 0.03$ mm, the process is well within control — a decision driven entirely by the **FPC-adjusted standard error** of random sampling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mean and Variance of the Sample Mean (SWR Case)

Let $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ be a simple random sample drawn **with replacement** from a population with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$. Each $x_i$ is an independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variable with $E(x_i) = \mu$ and $\text{Var}(x_i) = \sigma^2$.

### Step 1 — Express the Sample Mean as a Linear Combination

The sample mean is defined as:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \frac{1}{n} x_1 + \frac{1}{n} x_2 + \cdots + \frac{1}{n} x_n$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Expectation Operator

By the **linearity of expectation**, we can distribute $E$ across the sum:

$$E(\bar{x}) = E\left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(x_i)$$

Since $E(x_i) = \mu$ for every $i$:

$$E(\bar{x}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu = \frac{1}{n} \cdot n\mu = \mu$$

This proves the **unbiasedness** of $\bar{x}$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Variance Operator

Using the property that $\text{Var}(aX) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$ and that independent variates have **additive variances**:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \text{Var}\left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right) = \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(x_i)$$

Since $\text{Var}(x_i) = \sigma^2$ for every $i$ and the draws are independent:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

### Step 4 — Derive the Standard Error

Taking the positive square root of the variance gives the **standard error of the mean**:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{x})} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma^2}{n}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Variance of the Sample Mean (SWOR Case)

In **Simple Random Sampling Without Replacement (SRSWOR)**, the $x_i$ are **no longer independent** because removing a unit changes the population composition. The correct variance formula must account for **pairwise covariances**.

### Step 1 — Expand the Variance Using the Covariance Identity

For any random variables (dependent or independent):

$$\text{Var}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(x_i) + 2\sum_{i<j} \text{Cov}(x_i, x_j)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Population Covariance

The covariance between two distinct draws $x_i$ and $x_j$ from a finite population is given by the standard finite-population covariance formula:

$$\text{Cov}(x_i, x_j) = -\frac{\sigma^2}{N-1}$$

The **negative sign** captures the *depletion* effect: when one large value is drawn, it is removed, slightly lowering the population mean and thus the expected value of the next draw.

### Step 3 — Count the Number of Covariance Pairs

The number of ordered pairs $(i, j)$ with $i < j$ in a sample of size $n$ is:

$$\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

$$\text{Var}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right) = n\sigma^2 + 2 \cdot \frac{n(n-1)}{2} \cdot \left( -\frac{\sigma^2}{N-1} \right)$$

$$\text{Var}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right) = n\sigma^2 - \frac{n(n-1)\sigma^2}{N-1}$$

Factor out $n\sigma^2$:

$$\text{Var}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right) = n\sigma^2 \left[ 1 - \frac{n-1}{N-1} \right] = n\sigma^2 \cdot \frac{N-n}{N-1}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Variance of the Mean

Apply $\text{Var}(\bar{x}) = \dfrac{1}{n^2}\text{Var}\left(\sum x_i\right)$:

$$\sigma^2_{\bar{x}} = \frac{1}{n^2} \cdot n\sigma^2 \cdot \frac{N-n}{N-1} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{N-n}{N-1}$$

This is the **Finite Population Correction (FPC)** form, the KTU 14-mark standard.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A batch of $N = 200$ steel rods has a mean tensile strength of $\mu = 450$ MPa and variance $\sigma^2 = 100$ (MPa)$^2$. A simple random sample of $n = 25$ rods is drawn **without replacement**. Find the expected value and standard error of the sample mean.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Expected Value of $\bar{x}$:**

$$E(\bar{x}) = \mu = 450 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the FPC factor:**

$$\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{200-25}{199}} = \sqrt{\frac{175}{199}} = \sqrt{0.8794} \approx 0.9378$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Standard Error:**

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \text{FPC} = \frac{\sqrt{100}}{\sqrt{25}} \cdot 0.9378 = \frac{10}{5} \cdot 0.9378 = 2 \times 0.9378 = 1.8756 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — 95% Confidence Interval (Z = 1.96):**

$$\bar{x} \in 450 \pm 1.96 \times 1.8756 = 450 \pm 3.676 \text{ MPa}$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true mean tensile strength of the batch lies between **446.32 MPa and 453.68 MPa**. If the design specification requires $\geq 440$ MPa, the batch passes the quality check.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: SWR vs. SWOR

| Aspect | Sampling With Replacement (SWR) | Sampling Without Replacement (SWOR) |
|--------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Independence of draws | Yes | No |
| Variance of $\bar{x}$ | $\dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$ | $\dfrac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \dfrac{N-n}{N-1}$ |
| Magnitude of variance | Larger | Smaller (more efficient) |
| Practical use | Simulations, bootstrap | Standard QC inspection |
| Covariance between draws | $0$ | $-\dfrac{\sigma^2}{N-1}$ |
| Standard KTU phrase | "Infinite population" | "Finite population" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flowchart: Simple Random Sampling Without Replacement (SRSWOR)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step A0: Define Population of size N] --> A1[Step A1: Construct Sampling Frame list of all N units]
    A1 --> A2[Step A2: Generate Random Numbers using RNG or Table]
    A2 --> A3{Step A3: Unit available in frame?}
    A3 -- Yes --> A4[Step A4: Select the unit and record measurement]
    A3 -- No --> A5[Step A5: Skip the duplicate random number]
    A4 --> A6[Step A6: Remove selected unit from frame]
    A5 --> A2
    A6 --> A7{Step A7: Sample size equals n?}
    A7 -- No --> A2
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Step A8: Compute sample mean xbar and variance s2]
    A8 --> A9[Step A9: Apply FPC factor to standard error]
    A9 --> A10[Step A10: Construct Confidence Interval for population mean mu]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Random Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Input Layer: Population N, Sample Size n, Random Seed] --> B2[Randomization Engine: Pseudo-RNG / Chits]
    B2 --> B3[Selection Module: SRSWOR or SRSWR]
    B3 --> B4[Data Collection Layer: Record x1, x2, ..., xn]
    B4 --> B5[Statistical Estimator: Compute xbar, s2]
    B5 --> B6[Variance Engine: Apply FPC if finite]
    B6 --> B7[Output Layer: Standard Error, CI, Decision]
    B7 --> B8[QC Decision: Accept Lot or Reject Lot]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Apply FPC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Start: Is population size N given?] --> C2{Question 1: Is N explicitly stated?}
    C2 -- No --> C3[Treat as Infinite Population]
    C2 -- Yes --> C4{Question 2: Is sampling fraction n slash N greater than 0.05?}
    C4 -- No --> C5[FPC approximately 1: Use simple formula sigma over sqrt n]
    C4 -- Yes --> C6[Apply full FPC: Multiply by sqrt of N-n over N-1]
    C3 --> C7[End: Report Standard Error]
    C5 --> C7
    C6 --> C7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In the SRSWOR flowchart, the loop from A3 back to A2 represents the **de-duplication step** — if a random number maps to an already-selected unit, it is rejected and a new draw is made. This is the operational core of "without replacement" logic in KTU 14-mark numerical problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Architecture Insight:** The **Variance Engine** (block B6) is the only block that is conditional. Every other block is deterministic. KTU examiners test whether students can correctly identify which branch of this architecture applies for a given numerical problem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Simple Random Sampling. State the conditions under which Simple Random Sampling Without Replacement (SRSWOR) is preferred over Simple Random Sampling With Replacement (SRSWR).**

**Model Answer:**

**Simple Random Sampling (SRS)** is a sampling technique in which every possible sample of size $n$ drawn from a population of size $N$ has an **equal probability of selection**, and every individual unit has an equal and independent chance of being included in the sample.

**SRSWOR is preferred over SRSWR when:**

1. The population is **finite** and the sample size $n$ is **non-negligible** compared to $N$ (typically $n/N > 0.05$).
2. **Estimation efficiency** matters: SRSWOR provides a **smaller variance** of the sample mean by the factor $\dfrac{N-n}{N-1} < 1$.
3. **Practical inspection** of physical items is involved (e.g., destructive testing of components), where re-testing a unit is either impossible or wasteful.
4. The population units are **distinct and identifiable**, allowing a sampling frame to be constructed.

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiner's Tip:* Always mention the FPC factor explicitly. It is a 1-mark keyword.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Finite Population Correction (FPC)? When can it be ignored in practical quality control applications?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Finite Population Correction (FPC)** is the multiplicative factor $\sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}}$ applied to the standard error of the mean when sampling from a finite population without replacement. It accounts for the **reduction in sampling variability** caused by removing already-selected units from the population.

Mathematically:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}$$

**The FPC can be ignored when:**

- The sampling fraction $n/N \leq 0.05$ (i.e., sample size is less than 5% of population size), because then $\sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}} \approx 1$.
- The population is treated as **infinite** (e.g., continuous manufacturing process where $N$ is effectively unbounded).
- The sample size is small relative to the lot size in bulk material inspection.

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiner's Tip:* Stating the 5% rule explicitly is worth 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

A manufacturing company produces a batch of $N = 1000$ ball bearings. The diameter of the bearings follows a distribution with population mean $\mu = 15.00$ mm and population variance $\sigma^2 = 0.04$ mm$^2$.

**(a)** A simple random sample of $n = 50$ bearings is drawn **without replacement**. Calculate the **expected value** and **standard error** of the sample mean. (7 marks)

**(b)** If a second sample of $n = 200$ bearings is drawn **with replacement** from the same population, calculate the standard error and compare it with part (a). Comment on the engineering significance. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

### **Part (a) — SRSWOR Standard Error**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**

$$N = 1000, \quad n = 50, \quad \mu = 15.00 \text{ mm}, \quad \sigma^2 = 0.04 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Expected value of $\bar{x}$:**

$$E(\bar{x}) = \mu = 15.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the sampling fraction:**

$$\frac{n}{N} = \frac{50}{1000} = 0.05$$

Since the sampling fraction equals exactly 0.05, the FPC should be applied.

**Step 4 — Compute the FPC factor:**

$$\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{1000-50}{999}} = \sqrt{\frac{950}{999}} = \sqrt{0.9509} \approx 0.9752$$

**Step 5 — Compute the variance of $\bar{x}$:**

$$\sigma^2_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \frac{N-n}{N-1} = \frac{0.04}{50} \cdot \frac{950}{999} = 0.0008 \times 0.9509 = 0.0007607 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Compute the standard error:**

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \sqrt{0.0007607} \approx 0.02758 \text{ mm} \approx 0.0276 \text{ mm}$$

### **Part (b) — SRSWR Standard Error and Comparison**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters for SRSWR:**

$$n = 200, \quad \sigma^2 = 0.04 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (same population)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the variance of $\bar{x}$ under SRSWR:**

$$\sigma^2_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} = \frac{0.04}{200} = 0.0002 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard error:**

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \sqrt{0.0002} \approx 0.01414 \text{ mm} \approx 0.0141 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Part (a):**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{0.0141}{0.0276} \approx 0.511$$

The SRSWR sample with $n = 200$ has **less than half** the standard error of the SRSWOR sample with $n = 50$.

**Engineering Significance:**

- Although SRSWR with $n=200$ yields a smaller standard error numerically, this comparison is **not entirely fair** because the sample sizes differ.
- For a fair comparison: a SRSWOR sample of $n = 200$ would give $\sigma_{\bar{x}} \approx \dfrac{0.2}{\sqrt{200}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{800}{999}} \approx 0.0126$ mm, even smaller.
- **Conclusion:** SRSWOR is more statistically efficient per unit drawn, because it does not waste information on duplicate units.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

A quality control inspector needs to estimate the mean life of bulbs produced in a lot of $N = 500$ bulbs. The population standard deviation of bulb life is known to be $\sigma = 100$ hours. A simple random sample of $n = 25$ bulbs is drawn without replacement and tested. The sample mean is $\bar{x} = 1200$ hours.

**(a)** Compute the **standard error of the mean** and construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true mean life of the bulbs in the lot. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the company specification requires a minimum mean life of **1150 hours**, determine whether the lot **passes** the quality check. Justify your answer using the confidence interval. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

### **Part (a) — Standard Error and Confidence Interval**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$N = 500, \quad n = 25, \quad \sigma = 100 \text{ hours}, \quad \bar{x} = 1200 \text{ hours}, \quad Z_{0.025} = 1.96$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sampling fraction:**

$$\frac{n}{N} = \frac{25}{500} = 0.05$$

The FPC is applicable.

**Step 3 — Compute the FPC factor:**

$$\text{FPC} = \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{500-25}{499}} = \sqrt{\frac{475}{499}} = \sqrt{0.9519} \approx 0.9756$$

**Step 4 — Compute the standard error:**

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \text{FPC} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{25}} \cdot 0.9756 = \frac{100}{5} \cdot 0.9756 = 20 \times 0.9756 = 19.513 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the margin of error (E):**

$$E = Z_{0.025} \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}} = 1.96 \times 19.513 = 38.245 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6 — Construct the 95% Confidence Interval:**

$$\bar{x} \pm E = 1200 \pm 38.245$$

$$\text{CI}_{95\%} = (1161.755, \ 1238.245) \text{ hours}$$

### **Part (b) — Quality Decision**

**Step 1 — Compare with specification:**

The specification requires a minimum mean life of $\mu_0 = 1150$ hours.

**Step 2 — Examine the lower bound of the confidence interval:**

$$\text{Lower Limit} = 1161.755 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

Since the **entire 95% confidence interval lies above 1150 hours** (the lower bound 1161.755 > 1150), we can state with 95% confidence that the true mean bulb life exceeds the minimum specification.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

> **The lot PASSES the quality check.** At the 5% significance level, there is no statistical evidence that the mean bulb life falls below 1150 hours.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the FPC factor (–2 marks):** Many students write $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ without multiplying by $\sqrt{(N-n)/(N-1)}$. Since the sampling fraction is exactly 0.05, examiners *expect* the FPC.
> 2. **Wrong Z-value (–1 mark):** Using $Z = 2$ instead of $1.96$ for 95% confidence is a frequent mistake. Always use $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$.
> 3. **Misinterpreting the confidence interval (–2 marks):** Stating "we are 95% sure the sample mean is 1200" is incorrect. The correct interpretation is: "we are 95% confident that the **population mean** $\mu$ lies in the interval."
> 4. **Forgetting units (–1 mark):** Always write "hours" after the numerical answer. Marks are deducted for unitless final answers in engineering courses.
> 5. **Skipping Step 1 (parameter list):** Examiners reward clearly stated parameter lists. Always start with "$N = ...$, $n = ...$, $\sigma = ...$".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Random Sampling (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Random Sampling = every unit has equal probability of selection; subset is called a **Simple Random Sample (SRS)**.
- [ ] **Two regimes:** **SWR** (independent draws, infinite population) vs. **SWOR** (dependent draws, finite population).
- [ ] **Unbiasedness:** $E(\bar{x}) = \mu$ **always**, regardless of sampling method.
- [ ] **Variance (Infinite/SWR):** $\sigma^2_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$.
- [ ] **Variance (Finite/SWOR):** $\sigma^2_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n} \cdot \dfrac{N-n}{N-1}$.
- [ ] **FPC factor:** $\sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N-1}} \leq 1$, never exceeds unity.
- [ ] **5% Rule of Thumb:** If $n/N \leq 0.05$, ignore FPC and use the infinite-population formula.
- [ ] **Sample variance uses $n-1$** (Bessel's correction); using $n$ gives a biased estimate.
- [ ] **Standard error** = $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \text{FPC}$.
- [ ] **95% CI:** $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}}$ ; **99% CI:** $\bar{x} \pm 2.576 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}}$.
- [ ] **Covariance** in SWOR: $\text{Cov}(x_i, x_j) = -\dfrac{\sigma^2}{N-1}$ (negative sign is critical).
- [ ] **Engineering application:** Ball-bearing diameter, tensile strength, bulb life — all use FPC-adjusted standard error for lot acceptance.
- [ ] **SRSWOR is more efficient** than SRSWR per observation drawn.
- [ ] **Always list parameters first** in numerical answers (examiner's pet mark).
- [ ] **Always retain units** in the final answer (MPa, hours, mm).
- [ ] **Population parameter** ($\mu$, $\sigma^2$) vs. **Sample statistic** ($\bar{x}$, $s^2$) notation must never be mixed.

> **End of Module 1 — Random Sampling Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stratified Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stratified Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Stratified Random Sampling** is a probability sampling technique in which the entire heterogeneous population of size $N$ is first partitioned into $L$ mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive homogeneous sub-populations called **strata**, and then an independent simple random sample is drawn from each stratum. A separate sample of size $n_h$ is selected from the $h^{th}$ stratum of size $N_h$, such that the total sample size is $n = \sum_{h=1}^{L} n_h$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A *stratum* (plural: *strata*) is a homogeneous subgroup into which the population is divided based on some known auxiliary variable (e.g., age, region, income, machine type, batch). The defining feature of stratified sampling is that the strata are formed **before** sampling, and **every element of the population belongs to exactly one stratum**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Kerala Tea Plantation Survey 🍃**
>
> Imagine you are a quality-control engineer asked to estimate the **average yield (kg/hectare)** of tea leaves across a state that has 3 distinct geographic zones: **High Range (cool/hilly)**, **Midland (moderate)**, and **Lowland (hot/plains)**. The plants in these zones grow in **very different conditions**, so the yields are highly variable if treated as one pool.
>
> A naive **Simple Random Sample (SRS)** might accidentally over-represent one zone and under-represent another, leading to a biased or inefficient estimate. But if you **first divide the state into 3 zones (strata)** and then sample **separately from each zone** in the right proportion, you get a much more precise and representative picture. This is the essence of **stratified sampling** — *"Divide and Conquer with Precision"*.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Exam Vocabulary)

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $N$ | Total population size |
| $L$ | Total number of strata |
| $N_h$ | Size of the $h^{th}$ stratum |
| $n_h$ | Sample size drawn from the $h^{th}$ stratum |
| $W_h = N_h / N$ | **Stratum weight** (proportion of population in stratum $h$) |
| $\bar{Y}_h$ | True (population) mean of stratum $h$ |
| $\bar{y}_h$ | Sample mean of stratum $h$ |
| $S_h^2$ | Population variance of stratum $h$ |
| $C_h$ | Per-unit sampling cost in stratum $h$ |

## 1.4 When Should We Stratify? (Strategic Conditions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use stratified sampling when:
> 1. The population is **clearly heterogeneous** along a known variable.
> 2. **Reliable auxiliary information** about stratum sizes $N_h$ is available.
> 3. Estimates are required **separately for each stratum** (administrative reports).
> 4. **Administrative convenience** demands it (different sampling staff per region).
> 5. The objective is to **minimize the variance** of the estimator for a given cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Step Operational Procedure

1. **Define the target population** of size $N$.
2. **Identify the stratification variable** (a characteristic that creates homogeneous groups, e.g., factory location, machine age, soil type).
3. **Divide the population into $L$ strata** such that $\sum_{h=1}^{L} N_h = N$. Strata must be **non-overlapping and exhaustive**.
4. **Decide the allocation rule** — how many units $n_h$ to draw from each stratum. The three main choices are *Proportional*, *Optimum (Neyman)*, and *Equal* allocation.
5. **Draw a Simple Random Sample** (SRS) of size $n_h$ from each stratum independently, and compute the **stratified estimator** of the population parameter.

## 2.2 The Three Allocation Strategies (High-Yield Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need different allocation strategies?** Because the optimal number of units to draw from each stratum depends on (a) how big the stratum is, (b) how variable it is internally, and (c) how expensive it is to sample. The three classical KTU-level strategies trade off these factors.

### 2.2.1 Proportional Allocation
Each stratum contributes a sample size **proportional to its size in the population**:
$$n_h = n \cdot \frac{N_h}{N} = n \cdot W_h$$

This is the simplest, fairest, and most frequently tested strategy in KTU exams.

### 2.2.2 Optimum (Neyman) Allocation
Allocates **more samples to strata that are larger *and* more internally variable** (assuming equal costs):
$$n_h = n \cdot \frac{N_h S_h}{\sum_{j=1}^{L} N_j S_j} = n \cdot \frac{W_h S_h}{\sum_{j=1}^{L} W_j S_j}$$

When per-unit costs $C_h$ differ, the **generalized Neyman formula** is:
$$n_h = n \cdot \frac{W_h S_h / \sqrt{C_h}}{\sum_{j=1}^{L} W_j S_j / \sqrt{C_j}}$$

This **minimizes the variance** of the stratified mean for a fixed total cost, hence the name *optimum*.

### 2.2.3 Equal Allocation
$$n_h = \frac{n}{L}$$

Used when stratum variances are unknown and approximately equal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU stratified-sampling problem.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Stratum weight | $W_h = \dfrac{N_h}{N}$ | $0 < W_h < 1, \quad \sum W_h = 1$ |
| 2 | Stratified estimator of population mean | $\bar{y}_{st} = \displaystyle\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \bar{y}_h$ | **Weighted average of stratum sample means** |
| 3 | Variance of $\bar{y}_{st}$ (exact) | $V(\bar{y}_{st}) = \displaystyle\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h^2 \, \dfrac{S_h^2}{n_h} \left(1 - \dfrac{n_h}{N_h}\right)$ | Includes finite population correction (fpc) |
| 4 | Variance (ignoring fpc) | $V(\bar{y}_{st}) \approx \displaystyle\sum_{h=1}^{L} \dfrac{W_h^2 S_h^2}{n_h}$ | Used when $n_h/N_h < 0.05$ |
| 5 | Variance under **Proportional Allocation** | $V_{prop} = \dfrac{1}{n} \displaystyle\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h S_h^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{n}{N}\right)$ | Eliminates the between-stratum variance |
| 6 | Variance under **Optimum Allocation** | $V_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{n}\left(\displaystyle\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h S_h\right)^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{n}{N}\right)$ | **Lowest possible variance** for given $n$ |
| 7 | Unbiased estimator of $V(\bar{y}_{st})$ | $\widehat{V}(\bar{y}_{st}) = \displaystyle\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h^2 \, \dfrac{s_h^2}{n_h} \left(1 - \dfrac{n_h}{N_h}\right)$ | Replace $S_h^2$ by sample variance $s_h^2$ |
| 8 | Estimator of population total | $\hat{\tau}_{st} = N \cdot \bar{y}_{st} = \displaystyle\sum_{h=1}^{L} N_h \bar{y}_h$ | $V(\hat{\tau}) = N^2 V(\bar{y}_{st})$ |
| 9 | Population variance decomposition | $S^2 = S_w^2 + S_b^2$ | Within + between stratum components |
| 10 | Within-stratum variance | $S_w^2 = \displaystyle\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h S_h^2$ | Equals $n \cdot V_{prop}$ (approx.) |
| 11 | Between-stratum variance | $S_b^2 = \displaystyle\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h(\bar{Y}_h - \bar{Y})^2$ | The variance **eliminated** by stratification |
| 12 | Relative efficiency vs SRS | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{SRS}}{V_{prop}} = \dfrac{S^2}{S_w^2} = 1 + \dfrac{S_b^2}{S_w^2}$ | Always $\eta \geq 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board-Exam Trap:** In formula rows above, the symbol $W_h^2$ represents the *square of the weight* — do not confuse it with the square of the population size. Likewise, $S_h$ is the **stratum standard deviation**, not the sample standard deviation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stratified sampling is the workhorse of **industrial statistics and quality control**:

- **Manufacturing:** A factory with 5 production lines (5 strata) wants to estimate the **mean diameter of bolts** produced today. Each line has a different mean diameter, so lines are stratified, and samples are drawn from each line to detect *which line* is drifting out of specification.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Estimating average air-quality index across a city by dividing into *Industrial*, *Residential*, and *Commercial* zones.
- **Medical/Biostatistics:** Clinical trials often stratify patients by *age*, *gender*, or *disease severity* to ensure balanced sub-group estimates.
- **Economics/Management:** Household-income surveys divide cities into income brackets before sampling.

The core engineering insight: **stratification converts a heterogeneous population into homogeneous groups, dramatically reducing sampling variance and increasing statistical power for the same budget**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Variance of the Stratified Sample Mean

**Goal:** Derive the variance of the estimator $\bar{y}_{st} = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \bar{y}_h$ when an SRS is drawn from each stratum.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of the estimator.**

The stratified estimator is a linear combination of independent stratum sample means:
$$\bar{y}_{st} = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \bar{y}_h$$

**Step 2 — Use the property of variance for a sum of independent random variables.**

Because $\bar{y}_1, \bar{y}_2, \dots, \bar{y}_L$ are means of **independent** samples from non-overlapping strata, they are statistically independent. Therefore:
$$V(\bar{y}_{st}) = V\!\left(\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \bar{y}_h\right) = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h^2 \, V(\bar{y}_h)$$

*Reasoning:* The cross-product terms $\text{Cov}(\bar{y}_i, \bar{y}_j)$ vanish for $i \neq j$ because the strata are disjoint and the samples are drawn independently.

**Step 3 — Substitute the standard SRS variance within each stratum.**

For an SRS of size $n_h$ drawn without replacement from a finite population of size $N_h$ with variance $S_h^2$, the well-known SRS variance is:
$$V(\bar{y}_h) = \frac{S_h^2}{n_h}\left(1 - \frac{n_h}{N_h}\right)$$

The factor $\left(1 - \dfrac{n_h}{N_h}\right)$ is the **finite population correction (fpc)**.

**Step 4 — Combine Steps 2 and 3.**

Substituting the expression for $V(\bar{y}_h)$ from Step 3 into Step 2 gives the **exact** stratified variance:
$$V(\bar{y}_{st}) = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h^2 \cdot \frac{S_h^2}{n_h}\left(1 - \frac{n_h}{N_h}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify under the assumption $n_h / N_h \ll 1$.**

When the sampling fraction is small (typically $< 5\%$), the fpc is approximately $1$, and:
$$V(\bar{y}_{st}) \approx \sum_{h=1}^{L} \frac{W_h^2 S_h^2}{n_h}$$

This is the **most commonly used form** in KTU problems. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimum (Neyman) Allocation via Cauchy–Schwarz

**Goal:** Find $n_1, n_2, \dots, n_L$ that **minimize** $V(\bar{y}_{st})$ subject to the budget constraint $\sum n_h = n$ (ignoring fpc).

**Step 1 — Set up the constrained optimization.**

We want to minimize:
$$V(\bar{y}_{st}) = \sum_{h=1}^{L} \frac{W_h^2 S_h^2}{n_h} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{h=1}^{L} n_h = n$$

**Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L}(n_1, \dots, n_L, \lambda) = \sum_{h=1}^{L} \frac{W_h^2 S_h^2}{n_h} - \lambda\!\left(\sum_{h=1}^{L} n_h - n\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take partial derivatives and set to zero.**

Differentiating with respect to $n_h$:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial n_h} = -\frac{W_h^2 S_h^2}{n_h^2} - \lambda = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; n_h^2 = -\frac{W_h^2 S_h^2}{\lambda} \;\Longrightarrow\; n_h = \frac{W_h S_h}{\sqrt{-\lambda}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the constraint $\sum n_h = n$.**

Summing the expression for $n_h$ over all strata:
$$\sum_{h=1}^{L} n_h = \frac{1}{\sqrt{-\lambda}}\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h S_h = n$$

Therefore:
$$\sqrt{-\lambda} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h S_h$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to get the allocation rule.**

Plugging this value of $\sqrt{-\lambda}$ into the expression for $n_h$:
$$n_h = \frac{W_h S_h}{\dfrac{1}{n}\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{L} W_j S_j} = n \cdot \frac{W_h S_h}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{L} W_j S_j}$$

This is the **Neyman optimum allocation** formula. $\blacksquare$

**Step 6 — Compute the minimum variance.**

Substitute $n_h$ back into $V(\bar{y}_{st})$:
$$V_{min} = \sum_{h=1}^{L} \frac{W_h^2 S_h^2}{n \cdot \dfrac{W_h S_h}{\sum W_j S_j}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h S_h \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{L} W_j S_j = \frac{1}{n}\!\left(\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h S_h\right)^{\!2}$$

This is the **smallest achievable variance** under the cost constraint. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Why Stratification Beats SRS (Efficiency Gain)

**Step 1 — Express the population variance $S^2$ in terms of strata.**

The grand population mean is $\bar{Y} = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \bar{Y}_h$. By the **law of total variance**:
$$S^2 = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \!\left[ S_h^2 + (\bar{Y}_h - \bar{Y})^2 \right] = \underbrace{\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h S_h^2}_{S_w^2} + \underbrace{\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h(\bar{Y}_h - \bar{Y})^2}_{S_b^2}$$

**Step 2 — Write the variance of the SRS estimator of $\bar{Y}$.**

For an SRS of size $n$ (ignoring fpc):
$$V_{SRS}(\bar{y}) = \frac{S^2}{n} = \frac{S_w^2 + S_b^2}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Write the variance of the stratified estimator with proportional allocation.**

With $n_h = n W_h$ (and ignoring fpc):
$$V_{prop}(\bar{y}_{st}) = \sum_{h=1}^{L} \frac{W_h^2 S_h^2}{n W_h} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h S_h^2 = \frac{S_w^2}{n}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the gain.**

$$V_{SRS} - V_{prop} = \frac{S_w^2 + S_b^2}{n} - \frac{S_w^2}{n} = \frac{S_b^2}{n}$$

The **between-stratum variance $S_b^2$ is completely eliminated** by proportional stratified sampling.

**Step 5 — State the relative efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{V_{SRS}}{V_{prop}} = \frac{S^2}{S_w^2} = 1 + \frac{S_b^2}{S_w^2} \;\geq\; 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The more *different the stratum means* are from each other (large $S_b^2$), the **bigger the gain** from stratification. If the strata are very similar in mean ($S_b^2 \approx 0$), stratification provides little benefit and SRS is nearly as good.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Reference Solution)

**Problem.** A factory has 3 machines producing the same bolt. Population data:

| Stratum (Machine) | $N_h$ | $S_h$ | $\bar{Y}_h$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 500 | 2.0 | 10.2 |
| B | 1500 | 4.0 | 12.5 |
| C | 3000 | 3.0 | 14.0 |

Total $N = 5000$. A total sample of $n = 100$ is required. Find (a) proportional allocation, (b) optimum allocation, (c) their respective variances.

**Step (a) — Proportional allocation.** Compute $W_h = N_h / N$ and $n_h = n W_h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_A &= 500/5000 = 0.10, & n_A &= 100 \times 0.10 = 10 \\
W_B &= 1500/5000 = 0.30, & n_B &= 100 \times 0.30 = 30 \\
W_C &= 3000/5000 = 0.60, & n_C &= 100 \times 0.60 = 60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — Optimum (Neyman) allocation.** Compute $W_h S_h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_A S_A &= 0.10 \times 2.0 = 0.20 \\
W_B S_B &= 0.30 \times 4.0 = 1.20 \\
W_C S_C &= 0.60 \times 3.0 = 1.80 \\
\text{Sum} &= 3.20
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_A &= 100 \times 0.20/3.20 = 6.25 \approx 6 \\
n_B &= 100 \times 1.20/3.20 = 37.5 \approx 38 \\
n_C &= 100 \times 1.80/3.20 = 56.25 \approx 56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Variances.** First compute $\sum W_h S_h^2 = (0.10)(4) + (0.30)(16) + (0.60)(9) = 0.4 + 4.8 + 5.4 = 10.6$.

- Proportional variance: $V_{prop} = 10.6 / 100 = \mathbf{0.1060}$
- Optimum variance: $V_{opt} = (3.20)^2 / 100 = 10.24 / 100 = \mathbf{0.1024}$

**Conclusion:** Optimum allocation reduces the variance by $\dfrac{0.1060 - 0.1024}{0.1060} \times 100\% \approx 3.4\%$ over proportional allocation in this case.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stratified Sampling — Operational Block Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **complete decision flow** of a stratified-sampling survey, from population definition to variance computation. This is the *conceptual architecture* a KTU examiner expects students to be able to sketch in Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pop1["Define Target Population Size N"]
    strata1["Identify Number of Strata L and Stratum Sizes N_h"]
    pop1 --> strata1
    strata1 --> alloc1{"Choose Allocation Strategy"}
    alloc1 -->|No prior info| equal1["Equal Allocation: n_h = n divided by L"]
    alloc1 -->|Stratum sizes known| prop1["Proportional Allocation: n_h = n times N_h divided by N"]
    alloc1 -->|Variances and costs known| opt1["Optimum Neyman Allocation: n_h proportional to W_h S_h over sqrt C_h"]
    equal1 --> sample1
    prop1 --> sample1
    opt1 --> sample1
    sample1["Select Independent SRS of size n_h from each Stratum h"]
    sample1 --> mean1["Compute Stratum Sample Means y_bar_h for h = 1 to L"]
    mean1 --> est1["Form Stratified Estimator: y_bar_st = sum of W_h y_bar_h"]
    est1 --> var1["Compute Variance: V of y_bar_st = sum of W_h squared S_h squared divided by n_h"]
    var1 --> compare1["Compare with SRS Variance S squared divided by n"]
    compare1 --> report1["Report Estimate, Standard Error, and 95 percent Confidence Interval"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each rectangle represents a *process step*; the diamond `alloc1` is a *decision node* that branches the workflow into one of three allocation strategies. The arrows define the **causal flow of information** — note that the *estimated variance* feeds into the *comparison with SRS*, which is the final "value-add" of stratified sampling.

## 4.2 Variance Decomposition — Block Architecture

The following diagram illustrates how the **total population variance $S^2$** splits into a *within-stratum* part $S_w^2$ and a *between-stratum* part $S_b^2$, and which part each sampling method "sees".

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    popvar["Population Variance S squared"]
    popvar --> decomp{"Variance Decomposition"}
    decomp --> within["Within Stratum Component: S_w squared = sum of W_h times S_h squared"]
    decomp --> between["Between Stratum Component: S_b squared = sum of W_h times open Y_h minus Y close squared"]
    srsbox["SRS Estimator Variance: V_SRS = open S_w squared plus S_b squared close divided by n"]
    within --> srsbox
    between --> srsbox
    strbox["Stratified Proportional Variance: V_prop = S_w squared divided by n"]
    within --> strbox
    gain["Gain from Stratification: V_SRS minus V_prop = S_b squared divided by n"]
    srsbox --> gain
    strbox --> gain
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation block:**
> - **SRS** "sees" the *full* variance $S^2 = S_w^2 + S_b^2$.
> - **Stratified sampling** automatically "removes" the between-stratum variability $S_b^2$ from the estimator.
> - The **gain in precision** equals exactly $S_b^2 / n$, which is the variance *saved* by ensuring every stratum is properly represented.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define stratified random sampling. State any two situations where stratified sampling is preferred over simple random sampling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Definition (2 marks):* Stratified random sampling is a sampling technique in which the population is first divided into $L$ non-overlapping, homogeneous subgroups called *strata*, and then a simple random sample is drawn independently from each stratum. The samples from the strata are combined to estimate the population parameter.

*Situations (1 mark — any two):*
1. When the population is **heterogeneous** with respect to a known auxiliary variable (e.g., age, region).
2. When **separate estimates** for each sub-group (stratum) are required for administrative reporting.
3. When **administrative convenience** demands different sampling teams per region.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between *proportional allocation* and *optimum (Neyman) allocation* in stratified sampling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Proportional Allocation | Optimum (Neyman) Allocation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Formula | $n_h = n \cdot N_h / N$ | $n_h = n \cdot W_h S_h / \sum W_j S_j$ |
| Information used | Stratum sizes $N_h$ only | Stratum sizes *and* standard deviations |
| Objective | Representative proportions | Minimum variance for given $n$ |
| Variance | $V_{prop} = \frac{1}{n}\sum W_h S_h^2$ | $V_{opt} = \frac{1}{n}(\sum W_h S_h)^2$ (always $\leq V_{prop}$) |
| Use case | When $S_h$ unknown or equal | When $S_h$ known/estimated from past data |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal-Choice Standard:** KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. You must answer **either the full Question A *or* the full Question B**.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the variance of the stratified sample mean estimator $\bar{y}_{st}$ when an independent SRS is drawn from each stratum. Hence obtain the variance under proportional allocation.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Define the estimator (1 mark):**
$$\bar{y}_{st} = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \bar{y}_h, \quad W_h = \frac{N_h}{N}$$

2. **Apply the variance-sum property for independent random variables (2 marks):**
$$V(\bar{y}_{st}) = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h^2 V(\bar{y}_h)$$
(Cross-product terms vanish because strata are independent.)

3. **Substitute the standard SRS-within-stratum variance with finite population correction (2 marks):**
$$V(\bar{y}_h) = \frac{S_h^2}{n_h}\left(1 - \frac{n_h}{N_h}\right)$$
$$V(\bar{y}_{st}) = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h^2 \frac{S_h^2}{n_h}\left(1 - \frac{n_h}{N_h}\right)$$

4. **Apply proportional allocation $n_h = nW_h$ and simplify (2 marks):**
$$n_h/N_h = nW_h / (NW_h) = n/N$$
$$V_{prop}(\bar{y}_{st}) = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h^2 \frac{S_h^2}{n W_h}\left(1 - \frac{n}{N}\right) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h S_h^2 \left(1 - \frac{n}{N}\right)$$

This is the required result. $\blacksquare$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A survey of household income is to be conducted in a district divided into 3 regions with the following data. A total sample of $n = 600$ households is to be drawn. Compute the sample size from each region using **(i) Proportional allocation** and **(ii) Optimum (Neyman) allocation**. Also compute the variance of the stratified mean under each method.

| Region | $N_h$ | $S_h$ (in ₹'000) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Rural | 4000 | 5 |
| Semi-urban | 3500 | 8 |
| Urban | 2500 | 12 |

Total $N = 10{,}000$. $W_h = N_h / N$ gives $W_1 = 0.40, W_2 = 0.35, W_3 = 0.25$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Proportional Allocation (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
n_1 &= 600 \times 0.40 = 240 \\
n_2 &= 600 \times 0.35 = 210 \\
n_3 &= 600 \times 0.25 = 150 \\
\text{Check: } & 240 + 210 + 150 = 600 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Variance:
$$V_{prop} = \frac{1}{600}\sum W_h S_h^2 = \frac{1}{600}\big[(0.40)(25) + (0.35)(64) + (0.25)(144)\big]$$
$$= \frac{1}{600}\big[10.0 + 22.4 + 36.0\big] = \frac{68.4}{600} = 0.1140$$

**(ii) Optimum Allocation (4 marks):**

Compute $W_h S_h$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 S_1 &= 0.40 \times 5 = 2.00 \\
W_2 S_2 &= 0.35 \times 8 = 2.80 \\
W_3 S_3 &= 0.25 \times 12 = 3.00 \\
\text{Sum} &= 7.80
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_1 &= 600 \times 2.00/7.80 \approx 153.85 \approx 154 \\
n_2 &= 600 \times 2.80/7.80 \approx 215.38 \approx 215 \\
n_3 &= 600 \times 3.00/7.80 \approx 230.77 \approx 231 \\
\text{Check: } & 154 + 215 + 231 = 600 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Variance:
$$V_{opt} = \frac{1}{600}(7.80)^2 = \frac{60.84}{600} = 0.1014$$

**Conclusion:** Optimum allocation yields variance $0.1014$ versus proportional $0.1140$, a **reduction of ~11%** — the most variable (urban) stratum receives a larger share of the sample.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **stratification** and discuss the criteria for choosing an effective stratification variable. How does stratification increase the precision of the estimator compared to SRS?

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Stratification Concept (2 marks):** Division of a heterogeneous population of $N$ units into $L$ mutually exclusive homogeneous subgroups (strata) of sizes $N_1, N_2, \dots, N_L$ such that $\sum N_h = N$. A separate SRS is drawn from each stratum, and a weighted estimator is constructed.

2. **Criteria for Effective Stratification Variable (3 marks):**
   - **Homogeneity within strata** — units in the same stratum should be similar in the characteristic of interest (small $S_h^2$).
   - **Heterogeneity between strata** — stratum means $\bar{Y}_h$ should differ widely (large $S_b^2$).
   - **Ease of identification** — auxiliary variable must be easily observable and reliable.
   - **Administrative convenience** — sampling and reporting structure should align with strata.
   - **Cost consideration** — sampling costs should be reasonable within each stratum.

3. **Precision Increase (2 marks):** Under proportional allocation:
   $$V_{prop} = \frac{S_w^2}{n}, \quad V_{SRS} = \frac{S_w^2 + S_b^2}{n}$$
   The between-stratum variance $S_b^2$ is **completely eliminated** by stratification. Gain $= S_b^2 / n \geq 0$, so $V_{prop} \leq V_{SRS}$ **always**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a survey of 3 villages with population $N_1 = 800, N_2 = 1200, N_3 = 2000$ and known standard deviations $S_1 = 4, S_2 = 6, S_3 = 5$, determine the sample size in each village using **equal, proportional, and optimum allocations** for a total of $n = 200$ observations. Which allocation gives the minimum variance?

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Weights: $W_1 = 800/4000 = 0.20, \; W_2 = 1200/4000 = 0.30, \; W_3 = 2000/4000 = 0.50$.

**(i) Equal Allocation (1 mark):**
$$n_1 = n_2 = n_3 = 200/3 \approx 67 \text{ each}$$
(Adjusted to 67, 67, 66 to sum to 200.)

**(ii) Proportional Allocation (1 mark):**
$$n_1 = 200 \times 0.20 = 40, \quad n_2 = 200 \times 0.30 = 60, \quad n_3 = 200 \times 0.50 = 100$$

**(iii) Optimum Allocation (3 marks):**

Compute $W_h S_h$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 S_1 &= 0.20 \times 4 = 0.80 \\
W_2 S_2 &= 0.30 \times 6 = 1.80 \\
W_3 S_3 &= 0.50 \times 5 = 2.50 \\
\text{Sum} &= 5.10
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_1 &= 200 \times 0.80/5.10 \approx 31.37 \approx 31 \\
n_2 &= 200 \times 1.80/5.10 \approx 70.59 \approx 71 \\
n_3 &= 200 \times 2.50/5.10 \approx 98.04 \approx 98 \\
\text{Check: } & 31 + 71 + 98 = 200 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variance Comparison (2 marks):**

Compute $\sum W_h S_h^2 = (0.20)(16) + (0.30)(36) + (0.50)(25) = 3.2 + 10.8 + 12.5 = 26.5$

Compute $\sum W_h S_h = 0.80 + 1.80 + 2.50 = 5.10$

- $V_{prop} = 26.5/200 = 0.1325$
- $V_{opt} = (5.10)^2 / 200 = 26.01/200 = 0.1301$

**Conclusion:** **Optimum (Neyman) allocation gives the minimum variance** ($0.1301 < 0.1325$), as theoretically guaranteed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Stratified Sampling questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the estimator** $\bar{y}_{st} = \sum W_h \bar{y}_h$ with a *simple average* of stratum means. **Always** weight by $W_h = N_h / N$, not by $n_h / n$.
> 2. **Forgetting the fpc term** $\big(1 - n_h/N_h\big)$ when $n_h/N_h > 5\%$. KTU problems sometimes deliberately use small populations to test this.
> 3. **Mixing up** the formula for $V_{prop}$ and $V_{opt}$: proportional uses $\sum W_h S_h^2$, optimum uses $(\sum W_h S_h)^2$. *They are not the same expression!*
> 4. **Rounding $n_h$ values** that no longer sum to $n$. After rounding, adjust the stratum with the largest fractional part to make the total exactly $n$.
> 5. **Ignoring the between-stratum variance $S_b^2$** when discussing why stratified sampling is more efficient. Always mention the *decomposition* $S^2 = S_w^2 + S_b^2$.
> 6. **Using $n_h = n W_h$ for optimum allocation** — this is a fatal error; optimum requires $S_h$ as well.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Stratified sampling** = partition population into $L$ homogeneous strata, then sample independently from each.
- **Stratum weight:** $W_h = N_h / N$, with $\sum W_h = 1$.
- **Estimator of mean:** $\bar{y}_{st} = \sum_{h=1}^{L} W_h \bar{y}_h$ (unbiased for $\bar{Y}$).
- **Estimator of total:** $\hat{\tau}_{st} = N \bar{y}_{st} = \sum N_h \bar{y}_h$.
- **General variance:** $V(\bar{y}_{st}) = \sum W_h^2 \dfrac{S_h^2}{n_h} \big(1 - \dfrac{n_h}{N_h}\big)$.
- **Proportional allocation:** $n_h = n W_h$ → $V_{prop} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum W_h S_h^2 (1 - n/N)$.
- **Optimum (Neyman) allocation:** $n_h \propto W_h S_h$ → $V_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{n}\big(\sum W_h S_h\big)^2 (1 - n/N)$.
- **Cost-weighted optimum:** $n_h \propto W_h S_h / \sqrt{C_h}$ when per-stratum costs differ.
- **Population variance decomposition:** $S^2 = S_w^2 + S_b^2$.
- **Efficiency gain:** Stratification eliminates $S_b^2 / n$ of variance → *always* $\geq$ SRS efficiency.
- **Practical rule:** If $S_b^2$ is large (very different stratum means), stratification is highly beneficial; if $S_b^2 \approx 0$, stratification adds complexity with little gain.
- **Three allocation strategies in order of variance:** $V_{opt} \leq V_{prop} \leq V_{equal}$ (for non-equal $S_h$).
- **Always check rounding:** after computing $n_h$, ensure $\sum n_h = n$ exactly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, stratified sampling is a high-weightage Module-1 topic. Memorize the **three allocation formulas**, the **variance formula with and without fpc**, and the **variance decomposition $S^2 = S_w^2 + S_b^2$**. Most 14-mark questions test these with a numerical problem — practice at least 3 such problems before the exam.

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In Statistical Inference, **Estimation** is the formal mathematical procedure of using observed sample data to approximate the unknown numerical values of population parameters. The two unknown parameters most frequently estimated in engineering and quality control applications are the **Population Mean ($\mu$)** and the **Population Variance ($\sigma^2$)**.

A **Point Estimator** is a single numerical value computed from a random sample that serves as the "best guess" for the true population parameter. Formally, if $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ is a random sample drawn from a population with an unknown parameter $\theta$, then a point estimator of $\theta$ is a function:

$$\hat{\theta} = g(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$$

The numerical value realized from a particular observed sample is called an **Estimate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept**
> A statistic computed from a sample becomes an *estimator* (rule) before data is seen, and an *estimate* (number) after data is collected. Examiners frequently test this subtle terminological distinction for **3-mark questions**.

## 1.2 The Two Principal Branches of Estimation

| Branch | Output | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Estimation** | A single value $\hat{\theta}$ | Estimating mean and variance |
| **Interval Estimation** | A range $[L, U]$ (Confidence Interval) | Quality control limits |

The current topic exclusively focuses on **Point Estimation of $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Treasure Map" Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped somewhere in Kerala. You have no idea where you are on the map (this is the **unknown population parameter**). You are allowed to throw a ball in a random direction, walk 100 paces, and then mark the spot where you stopped (this is the **sample observation**). Doing this several times gives you a cluster of marks.

- The **center of that cluster** of marks is your estimate of where you actually are — this is the **Sample Mean ($\bar{X}$)** estimating the true **Population Mean ($\mu$)**.
- The **spread (scatter) of that cluster** tells you how uncertain your position is — this is the **Sample Variance ($S^2$)** estimating the true **Population Variance ($\sigma^2$)**.

The act of converting the cluster of marks into a single "best center" and "best scatter value" is precisely *Estimating the Mean and Variance*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance (Quality Control):** In a manufacturing plant producing pistons, we cannot measure the diameter of *every* piston. We take a sample, estimate the mean diameter $\mu$, and the variance $\sigma^2$ to monitor whether the process is stable and within specification limits.

## 1.4 Key Terminology — The Vocabulary Required for KTU Answers

- **Statistic:** A function of the sample observations that does not depend on unknown parameters. Example: $\bar{X}$, $S^2$.
- **Parameter:** A fixed but unknown numerical descriptor of the population. Example: $\mu$, $\sigma^2$.
- **Sampling Distribution:** The probability distribution of a statistic over all possible samples of size $n$.
- **Standard Error (SE):** The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of an estimator. It quantifies the *precision* of the estimate.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars: How Do We Construct Estimators?

In KTU 2024 syllabus, two principal methods are used to construct estimators of the mean and variance:

### Pillar A — Method of Moments (MOM)

Founded by **Karl Pearson**. The idea is to equate sample moments to their corresponding population moments, and solve for the parameters.

**Procedure (operational logic):**
1. Compute the first $k$ sample moments: $M_1 = \bar{X}$, $M_2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i^2$, etc.
2. Equate each sample moment to its population counterpart: $E(X)$, $E(X^2)$, etc.
3. Solve the resulting system of equations for the unknown parameters.

**For mean and variance of any distribution:**
- The first population moment is $E(X) = \mu$. Equating to the first sample moment $M_1 = \bar{X}$ gives:

$$\hat{\mu}_{MOM} = \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$$

- The second central moment gives:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2_{MOM} = M_2 - (M_1)^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i^2 - \bar{X}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2$$

### Pillar B — Method of Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

Founded by **R.A. Fisher**. The principle is to find the value of the parameter that makes the observed sample *most likely* to have occurred.

**Procedure (operational logic):**
1. Write the joint probability (likelihood function):

$$L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} f(X_i \mid \theta)$$

2. Take the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood: $\ln L(\theta)$.
3. Differentiate $\ln L$ with respect to $\theta$, set the derivative to zero, and solve.
4. Verify that the second derivative is negative to confirm a maximum.

**For a Normal Population $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$**, the MLEs are:
- $\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{X}$
- $\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2$ — **This is BIASED** (note the $n$ instead of $n-1$).

## 2.2 Desirable Properties of an Estimator (The "BIG-4")

For full marks in KTU 14-mark problems, you must discuss these four properties:

### (i) Unbiasedness
An estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is **unbiased** if its expected value equals the true parameter:

$$E(\hat{\theta}) = \theta$$

The bias is defined as: $B(\hat{\theta}) = E(\hat{\theta}) - \theta$.

- $\bar{X}$ is an **unbiased** estimator of $\mu$ because $E(\bar{X}) = \mu$.
- The MOM/MLE variance $\frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ is **BIASED**; its expectation is $\frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2$.
- The **Sample Variance** $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ is the **unique unbiased** estimator of $\sigma^2$.

### (ii) Consistency
$\hat{\theta}_n$ is consistent if it converges in probability to $\theta$ as $n \to \infty$. Both $\bar{X}$ and $S^2$ are consistent estimators.

### (iii) Efficiency
Among all unbiased estimators, the one with the **smallest variance** is the most efficient. The ratio:

$$e = \frac{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_2)}{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_1)}$$

is the *relative efficiency* of $\hat{\theta}_1$ w.r.t. $\hat{\theta}_2$. $\bar{X}$ is the most efficient estimator of $\mu$ (it attains the **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound**).

### (iv) Sufficiency
A statistic $T$ is **sufficient** for $\theta$ if it captures all information about $\theta$ contained in the sample. The factorization theorem (by Neyman-Fisher) is used to identify sufficient statistics.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is exam-critical. Memorize it for 14-mark derivations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample Mean (Point Estimator of $\mu$) | $\hat{\mu} = \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Always unbiased |
| 2 | Biased Sample Variance (MOM / MLE) | $\hat{\sigma}^2_b = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ | Biased: $E(\hat{\sigma}^2_b) = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2$ |
| 3 | Unbiased Sample Variance | $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ | Unbiased: $E(S^2) = \sigma^2$ |
| 4 | Sampling Variance of $\bar{X}$ | $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$ | Requires finite $\sigma^2$ |
| 5 | Standard Error of $\bar{X}$ | $SE(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Estimated as $\frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| 6 | Unbiased Estimator of $\sigma$ (approx) | $\hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{S^2} = S$ | $S$ is itself biased for $\sigma$ |
| 7 | MLE of $\mu$ (Normal) | $\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}$ | Achieves CRLB |
| 8 | MLE of $\sigma^2$ (Normal) | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ | Biased — multiply by $\frac{n}{n-1}$ to debias |
| 9 | Variance of $S^2$ (Normal) | $\text{Var}(S^2) = \frac{2\sigma^4}{n-1}$ | From chi-square distribution |
| 10 | Cramér-Rao Lower Bound for $\mu$ | $\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) \ge \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$ | Achieved by $\bar{X}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In process control, $S$ is plotted on a control chart to monitor *precision* of a manufacturing line. A rising $S$ indicates increasing variability, signaling the process is going out of statistical control.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Dividing by $(n-1)$ Instead of $n$

The single most-asked conceptual question on this topic is: *Why $n-1$ in the denominator of $S^2$?*

**The deep reason:** Because we first use the sample to estimate $\bar{X}$, we "consume" **one degree of freedom**. The deviations $(X_i - \bar{X})$ are not independent — they always sum to zero: $\sum(X_i - \bar{X}) = 0$. So only $n-1$ of the deviations are free to vary. To obtain an *unbiased* estimator of the population variance, we must divide by $n-1$, not $n$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: MLE of the Mean $\mu$ for a Normal Population

**Given:** $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n \overset{iid}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known.

**Step 1 — Write the joint density (likelihood function).** Because the observations are independent:

$$
L(\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(X_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Combine the product into a single exponential.**

$$
L(\mu) = \left(\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\right)^{\!n} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood.**

$$
\ln L(\mu) = -n\ln(\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $\mu$ and set to zero.** (Note: only the second term depends on $\mu$.)

$$
\frac{d}{d\mu}\ln L(\mu) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2(X_i - \mu)(-1) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the resulting sum.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i - n\mu = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\mu$.**

$$
\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify it's a maximum.** The second derivative is $-\frac{n}{\sigma^2} < 0$, confirming a maximum.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: MLE of the Variance $\sigma^2$ for a Normal Population

**Given:** $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n \overset{iid}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, with $\mu$ known (or replaced by $\bar{X}$).

**Step 1 — Write the likelihood as a function of $\sigma^2$.**

$$
L(\sigma^2) = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}\right)^{\!n} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm.**

$$
\ln L(\sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $\sigma^2$ and set to zero.**

$$
\frac{d}{d\sigma^2}\ln L(\sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically.**

$$
\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} = \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2
$$

Multiplying both sides by $(\sigma^2)^2$:

$$
n\sigma^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — When $\mu$ is unknown, replace it with $\bar{X}$.**

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Proof that $S^2$ is an Unbiased Estimator of $\sigma^2$

**This derivation is the single most important proof for KTU 14-mark questions.**

**Claim:** $E\!\left[\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2\right] = \sigma^2$

**Step 1 — Start with the algebraic identity (sum of squares decomposition).**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2 + n(\bar{X} - \mu)^2
$$

This identity is verified by adding and subtracting $\bar{X}$ inside the square and expanding.

**Step 2 — Take expectations of both sides.**

$$
E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2\right] = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2\right] + n\,E\!\left[(\bar{X} - \mu)^2\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left side.** Since $X_i$ are iid with variance $\sigma^2$:

$$
E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E\!\left[(X_i - \mu)^2\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma^2 = n\sigma^2
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the second term on the right side.** This is the variance of $\bar{X}$:

$$
n\,E\!\left[(\bar{X} - \mu)^2\right] = n\,\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = n \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{n} = \sigma^2
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back and solve for the unknown expected sum.**

$$
n\sigma^2 = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2\right] + \sigma^2
$$

$$
E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2\right] = n\sigma^2 - \sigma^2 = (n-1)\sigma^2
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by $(n-1)$ to obtain the expectation of $S^2$.**

$$
E(S^2) = E\!\left[\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2\right] = \frac{(n-1)\sigma^2}{n-1} = \sigma^2
$$

$$
\boxed{E(S^2) = \sigma^2 \quad \therefore S^2 \text{ is an unbiased estimator of } \sigma^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Bonus: The bias of the MLE variance.** Since $E(\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE}) = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2 \neq \sigma^2$, the MLE underestimates the true variance by a factor of $\frac{n-1}{n}$. Multiplying by $\frac{n}{n-1}$ gives the bias-corrected unbiased estimator $S^2$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A sample of 5 steel rods gave diameters (in mm): 10.2, 9.8, 10.1, 9.9, 10.0. Estimate the population mean and variance.

**Step 1 — Compute the sample mean.**

$$
\bar{X} = \frac{10.2 + 9.8 + 10.1 + 9.9 + 10.0}{5} = \frac{50.0}{5} = 10.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the squared deviations from the mean.**

| $X_i$ | $X_i - \bar{X}$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 10.2 | +0.2 | 0.04 |
| 9.8 | -0.2 | 0.04 |
| 10.1 | +0.1 | 0.01 |
| 9.9 | -0.1 | 0.01 |
| 10.0 | 0.0 | 0.00 |
| **Sum** | **0.0** | **0.10** |

**Step 3 — Unbiased estimator of variance.**

$$
S^2 = \frac{0.10}{5-1} = \frac{0.10}{4} = 0.025 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Standard deviation.**

$$
S = \sqrt{0.025} = 0.1581 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Standard error of the mean.**

$$
SE(\bar{X}) = \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.1581}{\sqrt{5}} = 0.0707 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Report:** The mean diameter is estimated as $\hat{\mu} = 10.0$ mm with a standard error of **0.0707 mm**. The variance is estimated as $\hat{\sigma}^2 = 0.025$ mm².

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Estimation Pipeline

The following diagram maps the *operational flow* of how a raw sample is processed into final mean and variance estimates. This is the architecture students should be able to reproduce in a 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Sample X1 X2 ... Xn] --> B[Compute Sample Mean mu hat]
    A --> C[Compute Squared Deviations]
    B --> C
    C --> D[Apply Bessel Correction n minus 1]
    D --> E[Unbiased Sample Variance S squared]
    A --> F[Build Likelihood Function L theta]
    F --> G[Take Log Likelihood ln L]
    G --> H[Differentiate and Set to Zero]
    H --> I[Verify Second Derivative Negative]
    I --> J[Final MLE Estimates mu hat and sigma squared hat]
    B --> K[Compute Standard Error]
    E --> K
    K --> L[Final Inference Report]
    J --> L
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Estimator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Have a Sample] --> Q{Is Distribution Known?}
    Q -- Yes Normal --> R[Use MLE]
    Q -- No --> S[Use Method of Moments]
    R --> T{Parameter is mu or sigma squared?}
    T -- mu --> U[mu hat equals X bar]
    T -- sigma squared --> V[sigma squared hat equals 1 by n times sum squared deviations]
    V --> W{Need Unbiased Estimator?}
    W -- Yes --> X[Multiply by n over n minus 1]
    W -- No --> Y[Keep MLE as is]
    S --> Z[Match First and Second Sample Moments]
    Z --> AA[Output: mu hat equals X bar and sigma squared hat equals 1 by n times sum]
    U --> AB[Output Report]
    X --> AB
    Y --> AB
    AA --> AB
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Bias vs Unbiasedness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Population
    A[True mu and true sigma squared] 
    end
    subgraph MLE_or_MOM
    B[Bias exists: E equals n-1 over n times sigma squared]
    end
    subgraph Unbiased_Corrected
    C[E of S squared equals sigma squared exactly]
    end
    A --> B
    A --> C
    B -- Multiply by n over n minus 1 --> C
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Properties of a Good Estimator (BIG-4)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Estimator Properties] --> P1[1 Unbiasedness: E equals theta]
    P --> P2[2 Consistency: limit as n grows equals theta]
    P --> P3[3 Efficiency: minimum variance]
    P --> P4[4 Sufficiency: captures all info]
    P1 --> Q[Apply to X bar and S squared]
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
    P4 --> Q
    Q --> R[Conclude: X bar and S squared are best estimators]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** When asked "draw the block diagram of estimation procedure," present a clear **Input → Processing → Output** architecture as above. Examiners award 2-3 marks purely for a well-labeled schematic.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define an Unbiased Estimator.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An estimator $\hat{\theta}$ of a population parameter $\theta$ is called **unbiased** if the expected value of the estimator equals the true parameter value:

$$E(\hat{\theta}) = \theta$$

The quantity $B(\hat{\theta}) = E(\hat{\theta}) - \theta$ is called the bias. If $B(\hat{\theta}) = 0$, the estimator is unbiased.

**Examples:**
- The sample mean $\bar{X}$ is an unbiased estimator of the population mean $\mu$, because $E(\bar{X}) = \mu$.
- The sample variance $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ is an unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$, because $E(S^2) = \sigma^2$.

In contrast, the MLE/Method-of-Moments variance $\frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ is biased with $E\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2\right] = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2 \neq \sigma^2$.

---

### **Question 2: State and Explain the Method of Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Method of Maximum Likelihood Estimation** is a technique for finding the most plausible value of an unknown parameter $\theta$ given observed sample data. The MLE is the value of $\theta$ that maximizes the probability (or density) of obtaining the observed sample.

**Procedure:**
1. Write the likelihood function: $L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} f(X_i \mid \theta)$.
2. Take the natural log: $\ln L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln f(X_i \mid \theta)$.
3. Differentiate w.r.t. $\theta$ and equate to zero: $\frac{d\ln L}{d\theta} = 0$.
4. Verify the second derivative is negative for a maximum.
5. The solution $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$ is the maximum likelihood estimate.

**For a Normal population**, the MLEs are $\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}$ and $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$. The MLE enjoys the desirable properties of being **consistent, asymptotically efficient, and asymptotically normal.**

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### **Question A: Estimate the Mean and Variance of a Normal Population Using MLE. Show Step-by-Step Derivations and Verify Unbiasedness.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Derive the MLE of the Mean $\mu$ (7 marks)

Given $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n \overset{iid}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known, the likelihood is:

$$
L(\mu) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(X_i-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

Taking log:

$$
\ln L(\mu) = -n\ln(\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $\mu$:

$$
\frac{d\ln L}{d\mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu) = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\sum X_i - n\mu = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i}
$$

**Verification:** $\frac{d^2\ln L}{d\mu^2} = -\frac{n}{\sigma^2} < 0$, confirming a maximum.

**Unbiasedness:** $E(\bar{X}) = E\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n}\sum E(X_i) = \frac{1}{n}(n\mu) = \mu$. Hence $\bar{X}$ is unbiased.

#### Part (b) — Derive the MLE of the Variance $\sigma^2$ and Prove $S^2$ is Unbiased (7 marks)

Treating $\mu$ as known first, the likelihood as a function of $\sigma^2$ is:

$$
L(\sigma^2) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum(X_i - \mu)^2\right)
$$

Log-likelihood:

$$
\ln L(\sigma^2) = -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum(X_i - \mu)^2
$$

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$
-\frac{n}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{2(\sigma^2)^2}\sum(X_i - \mu)^2 = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \mu)^2}
$$

When $\mu$ is unknown, replace it with $\bar{X}$, giving $\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$.

**Proof that $S^2$ is unbiased for $\sigma^2$:**

Using the identity $\sum(X_i - \mu)^2 = \sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 + n(\bar{X} - \mu)^2$ and taking expectations:

$$
n\sigma^2 = E\!\left[\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2\right] + \sigma^2
$$

$$
E\!\left[\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2\right] = (n-1)\sigma^2
$$

$$
E(S^2) = E\!\left[\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2\right] = \frac{(n-1)\sigma^2}{n-1} = \sigma^2
$$

Hence $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ is the unique unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$, while the MLE $\frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ is biased with a factor of $\frac{n-1}{n}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfall:**
> Many students write the MLE of variance with denominator $n-1$ instead of $n$ and lose 2 marks. **Remember:** the MLE is the BIASED version (denominator $n$); the *unbiased* version has denominator $n-1$. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

### **Question B: Discuss the Properties of Good Estimators (Unbiasedness, Consistency, Efficiency, Sufficiency). Apply them to the Sample Mean and Sample Variance.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Unbiasedness and Consistency (7 marks)

**1. Unbiasedness:** An estimator $\hat{\theta}_n$ of $\theta$ is unbiased if $E(\hat{\theta}_n) = \theta$ for all $n$.

- For the sample mean: $E(\bar{X}) = E\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum X_i\right) = \frac{1}{n}\sum E(X_i) = \mu$. Hence $\bar{X}$ is unbiased.
- For the sample variance: As proven in the previous question, $E(S^2) = \sigma^2$, so $S^2$ is unbiased.

**2. Consistency:** $\hat{\theta}_n$ is consistent if $\hat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ as $n \to \infty$, equivalently if $\text{Bias}(\hat{\theta}_n) \to 0$ AND $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_n) \to 0$.

- For $\bar{X}$: $\text{Bias} = 0$ for all $n$, and $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. Hence $\bar{X}$ is consistent for $\mu$.
- For $S^2$: $E(S^2) = \sigma^2$ (bias = 0), and $\text{Var}(S^2) = \frac{2\sigma^4}{n-1} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. Hence $S^2$ is consistent for $\sigma^2$.

#### Part (b) — Efficiency and Sufficiency (7 marks)

**3. Efficiency (Minimum Variance):** The **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB)** states that for any unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}$:

$$
