\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \ge \frac{1}{nI(\theta)}
$$

where $I(\theta)$ is the Fisher Information. For the mean of a normal distribution with known $\sigma^2$:

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) \ge \frac{\sigma^2}{n}
$$

Since $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$ achieves this lower bound, $\bar{X}$ is the **Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimator (MVUE)** of $\mu$ — i.e., it is fully efficient.

The **relative efficiency** of one unbiased estimator $\hat{\theta}_1$ to another $\hat{\theta}_2$ is:

$$
e(\hat{\theta}_1, \hat{\theta}_2) = \frac{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_2)}{\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_1)}
$$

**4. Sufficiency:** A statistic $T(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ is sufficient for $\theta$ if the conditional distribution of the sample given $T$ does not depend on $\theta$. By the **Neyman-Fisher Factorization Theorem**, $T$ is sufficient if and only if the joint density can be written as:

$$
f(x_1, \ldots, x_n \mid \theta) = g(T(x), \theta) \cdot h(x_1, \ldots, x_n)
$$

- For a normal population with unknown $\mu, \sigma^2$, the pair $(\bar{X}, S^2)$ is **jointly sufficient** for $(\mu, \sigma^2)$.
- For a normal population with $\sigma^2$ known, $\bar{X}$ alone is sufficient for $\mu$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfall:**
> Students often confuse *efficiency* (minimum variance among unbiased estimators) with *consistency* (convergence as sample size grows). Examiners award 0 marks if these are interchanged. Also, when asked to "verify the CRLB," students forget to **substitute the derivative into the CRLB formula** and stop at the verbal statement — this costs 2 marks.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize Before Exam**

- ☐ **Sample Mean:** $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i$ is always the unbiased estimator of $\mu$ (both MOM and MLE).
- ☐ **Unbiased Variance:** $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ — denominator is **$n-1$** (Bessel's correction).
- ☐ **MLE Variance:** $\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ — denominator is **$n$** (this is BIASED).
- ☐ **Bias of MLE variance:** $E(\hat{\sigma}^2_{MLE}) = \frac{n-1}{n}\sigma^2$. Multiply by $\frac{n}{n-1}$ to debias.
- ☐ **Standard Error:** $SE(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$, estimated by $\frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}$.
- ☐ **Variance of Sample Mean:** $\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$ (a fundamental KTU result).
- ☐ **CRLB for Mean:** $\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) \ge \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$, achieved by $\bar{X}$ → $\bar{X}$ is the **MVUE**.
- ☐ **Degrees of Freedom:** Dividing by $n-1$ accounts for the loss of one degree of freedom when $\bar{X}$ is estimated from the data.
- ☐ **Four BIG Properties:** Unbiasedness ($E(\hat\theta) = \theta$), Consistency ($\text{Var}\to 0$ and bias $\to 0$), Efficiency (attains CRLB), Sufficiency (captures all info).
- ☐ **Jointly Sufficient Statistic** for normal $(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is the pair $(\bar{X}, S^2)$.
- ☐ **Engineering Use:** Estimating $\mu$ locates the process center on a control chart; estimating $\sigma^2$ monitors process variability.
- ☐ **Cramér-Rao Lower Bound** formula: $\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \ge \frac{1}{nI(\theta)}$, where $I(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \ln L}{\partial \theta^2}\right]$.
- ☐ **Estimator vs Estimate:** Estimator is the *rule*; Estimate is the *numerical value* realized from a sample.
- ☐ **MLE Procedure:** Write $L$ → Take $\ln L$ → Differentiate → Set to zero → Verify second derivative is negative.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Topper's Strategy:** For a 14-mark question, always structure the answer as (1) Assumptions, (2) Likelihood/Moment Equation, (3) Step-by-step Differentiation, (4) Solution, (5) Verification of Unbiasedness. This 5-block structure fetches full marks consistently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Confidence Intervals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Confidence Intervals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Confidence Interval (CI):** A confidence interval is a **range of values**, derived from sample statistics, that is likely to contain the **true value of an unknown population parameter** with a specified level of confidence (e.g., 95%, 99%).

Mathematically, a confidence interval for a population parameter $\theta$ has the general form:

$$CI = \hat{\theta} \pm E$$

where:
- $\hat{\theta}$ = **Point Estimate** of the parameter (e.g., sample mean $\bar{x}$, sample proportion $\hat{p}$)
- $E$ = **Margin of Error** (also called the maximum error of estimate)

The **Confidence Level** $1 - \alpha$ represents the long-run proportion of such intervals, constructed using the same procedure from repeated samples, that would contain the true parameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Interpretation Rule (Often Misunderstood!):** A 95% confidence level does **NOT** mean there is a 95% probability that the specific computed interval contains the true parameter. Instead, it means that if we repeated the sampling process infinitely many times, 95% of the constructed intervals would contain the true parameter.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🎯

Imagine you are a fisherman trying to estimate the weight of a giant fish swimming in a lake, but you cannot weigh the entire lake of fish. You cast a net, catch a sample, and weigh those fish.

- The **average weight of your sample** is your **point estimate** ($\bar{x}$).
- However, you are not 100% sure the sample average is exactly the lake's true average. So you throw a **net of values** — this net is your **confidence interval**.
- The **width of the net** depends on:
  1. How much variability exists in the fish (variance, $\sigma$)
  2. How many fish you caught (sample size, $n$)
  3. How confident you want to be (confidence level, $1-\alpha$)

> **If you want to be 99% sure of catching the true weight, you cast a wider net.**
> **If you catch more fish (larger $n$), your net can be narrower and still be just as accurate.**

In Quality Control, a confidence interval helps a process engineer say:
*"Based on my sample of 30 bolts, I am 95% confident that the true mean diameter of **all** bolts produced lies between 9.98 mm and 10.02 mm."*

### Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Point Estimate | $\hat{\theta}$ | Single best guess from sample |
| Margin of Error | $E$ | Maximum likely deviation from true value |
| Significance Level | $\alpha$ | Probability of error (e.g., 0.05) |
| Confidence Level | $1-\alpha$ | Probability the CI captures the parameter |
| Critical Value | $z_{\alpha/2}$ or $t_{\alpha/2}$ | Multiplier from sampling distribution |
| Standard Error | $SE$ | Standard deviation of the estimator |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Logic Behind Confidence Interval Construction

The construction of any confidence interval follows a structured 5-step logical flow:

1. **Identify the Parameter of Interest** — Is it a mean ($\mu$), proportion ($p$), or variance ($\sigma^2$)?
2. **Select the Sampling Distribution** — Central Limit Theorem (CLT) governs the behavior of sample means.
3. **Determine the Critical Value** — Based on the chosen distribution (Z, t, or $\chi^2$) and confidence level.
4. **Compute the Margin of Error** — Multiply the critical value by the standard error.
5. **Form the Interval** — Add and subtract the margin of error from the point estimate.

### Three Pillars of Confidence Interval Formulas

#### Pillar 1: Confidence Interval for Population Mean ($\mu$)

**Case A: Population Standard Deviation ($\sigma$) is KNOWN**

Uses the **Standard Normal (Z) Distribution**.

$$CI = \bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Case B: Population Standard Deviation ($\sigma$) is UNKNOWN (uses sample std $s$)**

- If sample size $n \geq 30$: Use **Z-distribution** (CLT applies).
- If sample size $n < 30$: Use **Student's t-distribution** with $(n-1)$ degrees of freedom.

$$CI = \bar{x} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$$

#### Pillar 2: Confidence Interval for Population Proportion ($p$)

Used for **attribute (qualitative) data** (defective/not-defective, pass/fail).

$$CI = \hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Conditions:** $n\hat{p} \geq 5$ and $n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 5$ for the normal approximation to hold.

#### Pillar 3: Confidence Interval for Population Variance ($\sigma^2$)

Uses the **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Distribution** with $(n-1)$ degrees of freedom.

$$\frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2,\, n-1}} \leq \sigma^2 \leq \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,\, n-1}}$$

> **Note:** This is a *two-sided* interval — note the asymmetric placement of $\alpha/2$ in the two chi-square critical values.

---

### 🎯 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet / High-Yield Summary

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as a last-minute revision tool. All critical formulas for Module 1 are consolidated here.

| # | Parameter | Conditions | Critical Value | Standard Error | Confidence Interval |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mean $\mu$ | $\sigma$ known, any $n$ | $z_{\alpha/2}$ | $\dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| 2 | Mean $\mu$ | $\sigma$ unknown, $n \geq 30$ | $z_{\alpha/2}$ | $\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| 3 | Mean $\mu$ | $\sigma$ unknown, $n < 30$ | $t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1}$ | $\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| 4 | Proportion $p$ | $n\hat{p} \geq 5$, $n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 5$ | $z_{\alpha/2}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ | $\hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ |
| 5 | Variance $\sigma^2$ | Normal population | $\chi^2_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}$, $\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-1}$ | $(n-1)s^2$ | $\left[\dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2,\, n-1}},\, \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,\, n-1}}\right]$ |
| 6 | Sample Size $n$ | Desired margin $E$ | $z_{\alpha/2}$ | $\sigma$ | $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ |

> **Markdown Note:** The vertical bars in the bracketed variance interval use the word `left` and `right` brackets to avoid breaking the table syntax.

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Confidence Interval |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Manufacturing / QC** | Estimating true fraction defectives $p$ from sampled batches |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Estimating true mean tensile strength of a metal alloy |
| **Civil Engineering** | Estimating the true mean compressive strength of concrete cubes |
| **Process Control** | Determining if a process mean has shifted beyond acceptable limits |
| **Reliability Engineering** | Estimating true variance of component lifetimes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Confidence Interval for Mean (σ Known)

#### Starting Point: The Standardized Statistic

By the Central Limit Theorem, for a sample of size $n$ from a population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$

#### Setting Up the Probability Statement

We know that the standard normal variable $Z$ lies between $-z_{\alpha/2}$ and $+z_{\alpha/2}$ with probability $(1-\alpha)$:

$$P\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \leq \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \leq z_{\alpha/2}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

#### Algebraic Manipulation (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Multiply all parts of the inequality by $\sigma / \sqrt{n}$:

$$P\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \bar{X} - \mu \leq z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 2:** Subtract $\bar{X}$ from all parts:

$$P\left(-\bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq -\mu \leq -\bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $-1$ (reverses inequalities):

$$P\left(\bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu \leq \bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

#### Final Result

$$\boxed{\;CI = \bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\;}$$

> The confidence level $1-\alpha$ tells us the long-run capture rate. For example, $\alpha = 0.05$ gives $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, yielding a **95% confidence interval**.

---

### Derivation 2: Confidence Interval for Proportion (Large Sample)

#### Starting Point: The Sampling Distribution of $\hat{p}$

The sample proportion $\hat{p} = X/n$ is approximately normal by CLT for large $n$:

$$\hat{p} \sim N\left(p,\, \frac{p(1-p)}{n}\right)$$

#### Standardization

$$Z = \frac{\hat{p} - p}{\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$

#### Probability Statement

$$P\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \leq \frac{\hat{p} - p}{\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}} \leq z_{\alpha/2}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

#### Algebraic Manipulation (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Multiply by $\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$:

$$P\left(-z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} \leq \hat{p} - p \leq z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 2:** Subtract $\hat{p}$:

$$P\left(-\hat{p} - z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} \leq -p \leq -\hat{p} + z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $-1$:

$$P\left(\hat{p} - z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} \leq p \leq \hat{p} + z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}\right) = 1 - \alpha$$

#### Final Result (with $p$ replaced by $\hat{p}$ in the standard error)

$$\boxed{\;CI = \hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}\;}$$

---

### Worked-Out Analytical Problem

> **Problem (Model KTU Numerical):**
> A quality engineer samples **25 steel rods** from a production line. The sample mean diameter is found to be $\bar{x} = 10.05$ mm with a sample standard deviation of $s = 0.15$ mm. Assuming the diameters are approximately normally distributed, construct a **99% confidence interval** for the true mean diameter $\mu$.

#### Step 1: Identify Given Values

$$n = 25, \quad \bar{x} = 10.05 \text{ mm}, \quad s = 0.15 \text{ mm}, \quad 1 - \alpha = 0.99$$

#### Step 2: Choose the Distribution

Since $\sigma$ is **unknown** and $n = 25 < 30$, we use the **t-distribution** with degrees of freedom:

$$df = n - 1 = 25 - 1 = 24$$

#### Step 3: Find the Critical Value

For a 99% confidence level, $\alpha = 0.01$, so $\alpha/2 = 0.005$.

From t-tables: $t_{0.005,\, 24} = 2.797$

#### Step 4: Compute the Standard Error

$$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.15}{\sqrt{25}} = \frac{0.15}{5} = 0.03 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 5: Compute the Margin of Error

$$E = t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot SE = 2.797 \times 0.03 = 0.08391 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 6: Form the Confidence Interval

$$CI = \bar{x} \pm E = 10.05 \pm 0.08391$$

$$\boxed{\;9.966 \text{ mm} \leq \mu \leq 10.134 \text{ mm}\;}$$

#### Step 7: Engineering Interpretation

> *"We are 99% confident that the true mean diameter of all steel rods produced lies between **9.966 mm and 10.134 mm**."*

If the design specification requires $\mu = 10.00 \pm 0.10$ mm, the engineer can verify the process is in control because 10.00 mm lies within this interval.

---

### Analytical Matrix: Sample Size Determination

Often KTU questions ask: *"How large a sample is needed to estimate $\mu$ within $E$ units with confidence $1-\alpha$?"*

#### Derivation

Starting from $E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$, solve for $n$:

**Step 1:** Square both sides:

$$E^2 = z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by $n$:

$$n \cdot E^2 = z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot \sigma^2$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $E^2$:

$$\boxed{\;n = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2\;}$$

**Rule:** Always round **up** to the next integer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Correct Confidence Interval

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Estimate a Population Parameter] --> B{What is the Parameter?}
    B --> C[Population Mean mu]
    B --> D[Population Proportion p]
    B --> E[Population Variance sigma squared]
    C --> F{Is sigma known?}
    F --> G[Yes]
    F --> H[No]
    G --> I[Use Z distribution with sigma divided by sqrt n]
    H --> J{Is n greater than or equal 30?}
    J --> K[Yes: Use Z with s divided by sqrt n]
    J --> L[No: Use t distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom]
    D --> M{Check np and n 1-p both greater than or equal 5?}
    M --> N[Yes: Use Z with sqrt of phat 1-phat over n]
    M --> O[No: Use exact binomial methods]
    E --> P[Use Chi-square distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom]
    I --> Q[Compute CI as xbar plus or minus margin]
    K --> Q
    L --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> R[Apply exact binomial intervals]
    P --> S[Form two-sided chi-square interval]
```

### Diagram 2: Visual Concept of a 95% Confidence Interval

```mermaid
graph LR
    samp1["Sample 1: 9.95 to 10.05 CAPTURED"] -.-> rec["Repeated Sampling 100 Times"]
    samp2["Sample 2: 9.92 to 10.08 CAPTURED"] -.-> rec
    samp3["Sample 3: 9.88 to 10.12 MISSED"] -.-> rec
    samp4["Sample 4: 9.97 to 10.07 CAPTURED"] -.-> rec
    samp5["Sample 5: ... up to Sample 100"] -.-> rec
    rec --> outcome["Approximately 95 of 100 intervals CONTAIN true mu = 10.00 mm"]
    outcome --> note["The true mu is FIXED. The interval is what varies across samples."]
```

### Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of CI Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Sample Data x1 x2 ... xn] --> stats[Compute Sample Statistics]
    stats --> s1[Sample Mean xbar]
    stats --> s2[Sample Std s]
    stats --> s3[Sample Proportion phat]
    params[User Inputs: Confidence Level 1-alpha, Known sigma or not] --> dist[Distribution Selector Module]
    s1 --> dist
    s2 --> dist
    s3 --> dist
    dist --> crit[Critical Value Lookup]
    crit --> se[Standard Error Calculator]
    se --> moe[Margin of Error E = Critical x SE]
    moe --> output[Final Confidence Interval xbar plus or minus E]
    output --> interp[Engineering Interpretation Layer]
    interp --> decision[Decision: Process in control or action required]
```

### Diagram 4: Relationship Between Confidence Level and Interval Width

```mermaid
graph TD
    cl["Increase Confidence Level 1-alpha"] --> wider["Margin of Error E INCREASES"]
    wider --> wider2["Interval becomes WIDER"]
    ninc["Increase Sample Size n"] --> narrow["Standard Error DECREASES"]
    narrow --> narrower2["Interval becomes NARROWER"]
    sigmainc["Increase Population Variability sigma"] --> wider3["Standard Error INCREASES"]
    wider3 --> wider4["Interval becomes WIDER"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-Off Insight:** A wider interval gives more *confidence* but less *precision*. In quality control, the engineer must balance these competing demands by choosing the right sample size and confidence level.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a confidence interval. What is meant by a 95% confidence interval for the population mean?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **confidence interval** is a range of values, computed from a sample, that is likely to contain the true value of an unknown population parameter with a specified level of confidence.

A **95% confidence interval** for the population mean $\mu$ is an interval constructed using sample data such that, if the sampling procedure were repeated infinitely many times, approximately 95% of the resulting intervals would contain the true population mean $\mu$.

For example, the interval $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ is a 95% CI when $\sigma$ is known.

> **Marking:** 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for 95% interpretation.

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the Z-distribution and t-distribution in the context of confidence interval construction. When is each used?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Z-Distribution | t-Distribution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Population Std $\sigma$ | Known | Unknown (use sample $s$) |
| Sample size $n$ | Any $n$ (or $n \geq 30$ if $\sigma$ unknown) | Small samples ($n < 30$) |
| Shape | Fixed | Depends on degrees of freedom |
| Critical value | From standard normal table | From t-table with $df = n-1$ |

**Usage Rule:**
- Use **Z** when $\sigma$ is known or $n \geq 30$.
- Use **t** when $\sigma$ is unknown and $n < 30$.

> **Marking:** 2 marks for distinction table, 1 mark for usage rule.

---

### Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A random sample of **64** resistors is taken from a large production batch. The sample mean resistance is found to be $\bar{x} = 98.5$ ohms, and the population standard deviation is known to be $\sigma = 4$ ohms.

**(i)** Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true mean resistance $\mu$.

**(ii)** Construct a **99% confidence interval** for $\mu$ using the same data.

**(iii)** Comment on the effect of increasing the confidence level on the interval width. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A manufacturer claims that at most **5%** of the produced items are defective. In a random sample of **200** items, **15** are found to be defective.

**(i)** Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true proportion of defective items $p$.

**(ii)** Test the manufacturer's claim using this interval. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solutions for Question A:**

**Solution A(a)(i):** 95% CI for $\mu$ with $\sigma$ known

**Given:** $n = 64$, $\bar{x} = 98.5$, $\sigma = 4$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$

**Step 1:** Standard Error

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{4}{\sqrt{64}} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** Margin of Error

$$E = 1.96 \times 0.5 = 0.98$$

**Step 3:** 95% CI

$$CI = 98.5 \pm 0.98 = (97.52,\ 99.48) \text{ ohms}$$

---

**Solution A(a)(ii):** 99% CI for $\mu$

**Given:** $z_{0.005} = 2.576$

**Step 1:** Margin of Error

$$E = 2.576 \times 0.5 = 1.288$$

**Step 2:** 99% CI

$$CI = 98.5 \pm 1.288 = (97.212,\ 99.788) \text{ ohms}$$

---

**Solution A(a)(iii):** Comment on Confidence Level Effect

> The 99% CI (width = 2.576) is **wider** than the 95% CI (width = 1.96). As the confidence level increases from 95% to 99%, the margin of error $E$ increases, making the interval wider. This is because higher confidence demands a larger "net" to ensure a greater probability of capturing the true parameter.

---

**Solution A(b)(i):** 95% CI for Proportion

**Given:** $n = 200$, $x = 15$, $\hat{p} = 15/200 = 0.075$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$

**Step 1:** Check Validity

$n\hat{p} = 200 \times 0.075 = 15 \geq 5$ ✓
$n(1-\hat{p}) = 200 \times 0.925 = 185 \geq 5$ ✓

**Step 2:** Standard Error

$$SE = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.075 \times 0.925}{200}} = \sqrt{0.0003469} = 0.01862$$

**Step 3:** Margin of Error

$$E = 1.96 \times 0.01862 = 0.0365$$

**Step 4:** 95% CI

$$CI = 0.075 \pm 0.0365 = (0.0385,\ 0.1115)$$

So the 95% CI is approximately $(3.85\%,\ 11.15\%)$.

---

**Solution A(b)(ii):** Testing the Manufacturer's Claim

> The manufacturer's claim is that $p \leq 0.05$. However, the 95% confidence interval is $(0.0385, 0.1115)$. Since the upper bound $0.1115$ exceeds $0.05$ and the interval does **not** lie entirely below $0.05$, we have evidence to **reject** the manufacturer's claim. The true defective proportion is likely higher than 5%.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** The breaking strength of cables manufactured by a company is normally distributed. A sample of **9** cables yields a sample mean breaking strength of $\bar{x} = 1800$ N and a sample standard deviation of $s = 100$ N.

**(i)** Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true mean breaking strength $\mu$.

**(ii)** Determine the **sample size** required to estimate $\mu$ within $\pm 25$ N with 95% confidence. Assume $s \approx \sigma$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The quality control team records the waiting time (in minutes) of 16 customers. The sample variance is $s^2 = 9.0$ minutes squared. Assume waiting times are normally distributed.

**(i)** Construct a **90% confidence interval** for the population variance $\sigma^2$.

**(ii)** Construct the corresponding confidence interval for the population standard deviation $\sigma$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solutions for Question B:**

**Solution B(a)(i):** 95% CI for $\mu$ (σ unknown, small sample)

**Given:** $n = 9$, $\bar{x} = 1800$, $s = 100$, $df = 8$, $t_{0.025,\, 8} = 2.306$

**Step 1:** Standard Error

$$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{9}} = \frac{100}{3} = 33.33 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Margin of Error

$$E = 2.306 \times 33.33 = 76.87 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** 95% CI

$$CI = 1800 \pm 76.87 = (1723.13,\ 1876.87) \text{ N}$$

---

**Solution B(a)(ii):** Required Sample Size

**Given:** $E = 25$, $\sigma \approx s = 100$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$

**Step 1:** Apply formula

$$n = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 100}{25}\right)^2 = (7.84)^2 = 61.47$$

**Step 2:** Round up

$$n = 62 \text{ cables}$$

---

**Solution B(b)(i):** 90% CI for $\sigma^2$

**Given:** $n = 16$, $s^2 = 9.0$, $df = 15$, $\alpha = 0.10$, $\alpha/2 = 0.05$

From $\chi^2$ tables:
- $\chi^2_{0.05,\, 15} = 24.996$
- $\chi^2_{0.95,\, 15} = 7.261$

**Step 1:** Lower bound

$$L = \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2,\, n-1}} = \frac{15 \times 9.0}{24.996} = \frac{135}{24.996} = 5.401$$

**Step 2:** Upper bound

$$U = \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,\, n-1}} = \frac{135}{7.261} = 18.593$$

**Step 3:** 90% CI

$$5.401 \leq \sigma^2 \leq 18.593 \text{ minutes}^2$$

---

**Solution B(b)(ii):** 90% CI for $\sigma$

Take the square root of both bounds:

$$\sqrt{5.401} \leq \sigma \leq \sqrt{18.593}$$

$$2.324 \leq \sigma \leq 4.312 \text{ minutes}$$

> **Note:** The interval for $\sigma$ is NOT obtained by simply averaging the bounds of $\sigma^2$. Always take square roots of the original bounds.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Distribution Selection** (–2 to –3 marks): Using the Z-distribution when $n < 30$ and $\sigma$ is unknown. KTU expects the t-distribution here.
> 2. **Proportion Formula Error** (–2 marks): Forgetting the validity check $n\hat{p} \geq 5$ and $n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 5$.
> 3. **Chi-Square Confusion** (–3 marks): Mixing up the placement of $\alpha/2$ in the chi-square bounds. The **larger** chi-square value (with area $\alpha/2$ in the right tail) goes in the **denominator of the lower bound** of $\sigma^2$.
> 4. **Sample Size Truncation** (–1 mark): Forgetting to round UP the calculated sample size. If $n = 61.47$, you must write $n = 62$.
> 5. **Wrong Square Root Step** (–2 marks): For variance CI for $\sigma$, students often write $(\sqrt{L} + \sqrt{U})/2$ instead of $(\sqrt{L}, \sqrt{U})$.
> 6. **Interpretation Error** (–1 mark): Saying "there is a 95% probability that $\mu$ lies in this interval" — this is the most common conceptual mistake KTU examiners penalize.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Confidence Intervals**

- ✅ A confidence interval is a **range estimate** of a population parameter, NOT a point estimate.
- ✅ General form: $\hat{\theta} \pm (\text{Critical Value}) \times (\text{Standard Error})$.
- ✅ **Z-distribution** is used when $\sigma$ is known OR $n \geq 30$ (with $s$).
- ✅ **t-distribution** is used when $\sigma$ is unknown AND $n < 30$; degrees of freedom = $n - 1$.
- ✅ Critical Z-values to memorize: $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{0.005} = 2.576$, $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.
- ✅ **Proportion CI** requires $n\hat{p} \geq 5$ and $n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 5$ for normal approximation.
- ✅ **Variance CI** uses $\chi^2$ with $df = n-1$; the interval is **asymmetric** — be careful with tail placement.
- ✅ **Sample size formula:** $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$, always round **up**.
- ✅ **Higher confidence level** → **wider interval** (more confidence, less precision).
- ✅ **Larger sample size** → **narrower interval** (more precision, same confidence).
- ✅ **Correct interpretation:** "We are 95% confident that the true parameter lies in this interval" — refers to the *procedure*, not the specific interval.
- ✅ In KTU, always show the **critical value used** (from tables) and state the **interpretation** of the result for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Choice of Sample Size for Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Choice of Sample Size for Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Choice of Sample Size for Estimation** is a foundational statistical procedure that determines the minimum number of observations (sample size, denoted by **n**) required from a population so that a specified population parameter (such as the mean $\mu$ or proportion $p$) can be estimated with a pre-decided level of confidence $(1-\alpha)$ and a pre-fixed margin of error $E$ (also called the **precision** or **allowable error**).

In the language of the KTU 2024 syllabus, it answers the central engineering question:

> *"How many data points do I need to collect so that my estimate is both reliable (high confidence) and accurate (small error)?"*

The two principal estimation frameworks in which this problem arises are:

1. **Point Estimation** – A single best value is reported (e.g., $\bar{X}$ for $\mu$).
2. **Interval Estimation (Confidence Interval)** – A range of plausible values is constructed, e.g., $\bar{X} \pm E$, where the half-width $E$ is the **maximum allowable error** at confidence level $(1-\alpha)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The *Choice of Sample Size* is the inverse problem of constructing a confidence interval. In a CI, we know $n$ and find $E$; here we know $E$ and find $n$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Election Poll"

Imagine a television news channel wants to predict the percentage of voters in Kerala who will vote for a particular party. The country has **3.5 crore** voters, but calling every one of them is impossible. The channel must decide:

- *"How many voters should we actually call?"*
- *"If we want our predicted percentage to be within ±2% of the true value, with 95% confidence — how many responses do we need?"*

If the channel surveys only **50 people**, the answer will be wildly inaccurate. If it surveys **50,000**, the answer is excellent, but the cost is enormous. **Choice of Sample Size** is the mathematical tool that finds the **"Goldilocks" number** — the smallest $n$ that still satisfies the required precision and confidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Sample-Size Determination (The "Magic Triangle"):**
> - **Confidence Level $(1-\alpha)$** → Higher confidence ⇒ Larger $n$.
> - **Margin of Error $(E)$** → Smaller error ⇒ Larger $n$.
> - **Population Variability $(\sigma \text{ or } p(1-p))$** → Higher variability ⇒ Larger $n$.

The key statistical constants used are the standard normal critical values $z_{\alpha/2}$, for which the most common values are tabulated below:

| Confidence Level $(1-\alpha)$ | $\alpha$ | $z_{\alpha/2}$ |
|:-----------------------------:|:--------:|:--------------:|
| 90% | 0.10 | **1.645** |
| 95% | 0.05 | **1.96** |
| 98% | 0.02 | **2.33** |
| 99% | 0.01 | **2.576** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic of Deriving $n$

The construction of a $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ two-sided confidence interval for a population mean $\mu$ (assuming $\sigma$ is known) is:

$$\bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

The half-width of this interval is the **margin of error**:

$$E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

To enforce the requirement "the estimate must lie within $E$ of the true parameter with confidence $(1-\alpha)$", we **set $E$ equal to a pre-specified value** and solve for $n$. This produces the fundamental inversion step common to *all* sample-size formulas.

> [!TIP]
> **Generalized Inversion Principle:**
> 
> If $E = (\text{Critical Value}) \times \dfrac{\text{Standard Deviation of Estimator}}{\sqrt{n}}$, then solving for $n$ always gives:
> 
> $$n = \left( \frac{\text{Critical Value} \times \text{Standard Deviation of Estimator}}{E} \right)^2$$

### 2.2 The Six Standard Cases (KTU Board-Favourite)

#### Case 1 — Mean, $\sigma$ Known
$$n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$$

#### Case 2 — Mean, $\sigma$ Unknown (Pilot Study Available)
Here the standard deviation is estimated from a prior pilot sample of size $n_0$, giving sample standard deviation $s$. Use:
$$n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot s}{E} \right)^2$$
For a stricter, finite-sample correction use $t_{\alpha/2,\, n_0-1}$ instead of $z_{\alpha/2}$ (iterative).

#### Case 3 — Proportion $p$ (Known or Estimated)
$$n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{E} \right)^2 \cdot p(1-p)$$
When $p$ is unknown, use the **conservative value $p = 0.5$**, which maximises $p(1-p) = 0.25$:
$$n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{2E} \right)^2$$

#### Case 4 — Difference Between Two Independent Means ($\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ Known)
For equal sample sizes $n_1 = n_2 = n$:
$$n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}}{E} \right]^2$$

#### Case 5 — Difference Between Two Independent Proportions
For equal sample sizes:
$$n_1 = n_2 = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{E} \right)^2 \cdot (p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2)$$
with the conservative default $p_1 = p_2 = 0.5$ giving $p_1q_1 + p_2q_2 = 0.5$.

#### Case 6 — Finite Population Correction (FPC)
When the sample is a non-negligible fraction ($>5\%$) of a finite population of size $N$:
$$n_{adj} = \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}}$$
where $n_0$ is the sample size from the standard (infinite-population) formula.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter to Estimate | Assumptions | Required Sample Size Formula |
|:-:|:----------------------|:------------|:------------------------------|
| 1 | Mean $\mu$ | $\sigma$ known | $n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma}{E} \right)^2$ |
| 2 | Mean $\mu$ | $\sigma$ unknown, pilot $s$ | $n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\,s}{E} \right)^2$ |
| 3 | Proportion $p$ | $p$ known/estimated | $n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}}{E} \right)^2 p(1-p)$ |
| 4 | Proportion $p$ | $p$ unknown | $n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}}{2E} \right)^2$ |
| 5 | $\mu_1 - \mu_2$ | $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ known, equal $n$ | $n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2}}{E} \right)^2$ |
| 6 | $p_1 - p_2$ | Equal $n$ | $n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}}{E} \right)^2 (p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2)$ |
| 7 | Any parameter | Finite population $N$ | $n_{adj} = \dfrac{n_0}{1 + (n_0 - 1)/N}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard normal critical values — must be memorised for KTU ESE:**
> $z_{0.05} = 1.645$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{0.01} = 2.576$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control (Six-Sigma):** Determining lot-sampling size to estimate fraction defectives in a batch of microchips.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Estimating Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of a turbine within ±50 hours at 95% confidence.
- **Process Engineering:** Sizing a pilot experiment to estimate the mean yield of a chemical reactor.
- **Survey Sampling in Industry:** Worker satisfaction surveys, customer feedback analytics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Formula (Case 1 — Mean, $\sigma$ Known)

**Step 1 — Start with the standard error of the sample mean.**

The sample mean $\bar{X}$ has standard error:

$$SE(\bar{X}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Construct the $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confidence interval.**

By the Central Limit Theorem, $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \sim N(0,1)$. Therefore:

$$P\left( -z_{\alpha/2} \le \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \le z_{\alpha/2} \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

Rearranging:

$$P\left( \bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \le \mu \le \bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 3 — Identify the margin of error.**

The half-width of the interval is:

$$E = z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

This is the maximum tolerable distance between $\bar{X}$ and the true $\mu$ at confidence level $(1-\alpha)$.

**Step 4 — Invert the equation to solve for $n$.**

$$E = z_{\alpha/2}\,\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $\sqrt{n}$:

$$E\sqrt{n} = z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma$$

Squaring both sides:

$$E^2 \, n = z_{\alpha/2}^2 \, \sigma^2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma}{E} \right)^2\,}$$

**Step 5 — Always round up.**

Since $n$ must be a whole number, the actual sample size is:

$$n_{final} = \left\lceil \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}\,\sigma}{E} \right)^2 \right\rceil$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is critical: always round *up*, never down. Rounding down can violate the desired precision.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Mean with Known $\sigma$

**Problem:** A manufacturer of steel rods claims the diameter has $\sigma = 0.5$ mm. How many rods must be sampled so that the estimate of the mean diameter is within $\pm 0.1$ mm of the true mean with 95% confidence?

**Solution:**

Given: $\sigma = 0.5$ mm, $E = 0.1$ mm, confidence = 95% $\Rightarrow z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$.

Apply the formula:

$$n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 0.5}{0.1} \right)^2$$

Compute the numerator:

$$1.96 \times 0.5 = 0.98$$

Divide by $E$:

$$\frac{0.98}{0.1} = 9.8$$

Square the result:

$$n = (9.8)^2 = 96.04$$

Apply the ceiling rule:

$$n_{final} = \lceil 96.04 \rceil = 97 \text{ rods}$$

**Interpretation:** At least 97 rods must be measured to satisfy the precision and confidence requirement.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Proportion (Unknown $p$)

**Problem:** A quality inspector wants to estimate the fraction of defective bulbs in a large lot. She wants the estimate to be within $\pm 0.03$ of the true proportion with 99% confidence. Find the required sample size.

**Solution:**

Given: $E = 0.03$, confidence = 99% $\Rightarrow z_{\alpha/2} = 2.576$, $p$ unknown.

Use the conservative formula:

$$n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{2E} \right)^2$$

Substitute:

$$n = \left( \frac{2.576}{2 \times 0.03} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{2.576}{0.06} \right)^2$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{2.576}{0.06} = 42.9333$$

Square:

$$n = (42.9333)^2 = 1843.27$$

Apply the ceiling rule:

$$n_{final} = \lceil 1843.27 \rceil = 1844 \text{ bulbs}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Finite Population Correction (FPC)

**Problem:** A factory has $N = 2000$ items in a lot. Using the standard formula, the required sample size was found to be $n_0 = 400$. Apply the finite population correction.

**Solution:**

$$n_{adj} = \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}} = \frac{400}{1 + \dfrac{400 - 1}{2000}} = \frac{400}{1 + 0.1995} = \frac{400}{1.1995}$$

Compute:

$$n_{adj} = 333.47 \approx 334 \text{ items}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The FPC reduces the required sample size because sampling a *meaningful fraction* of a small finite population yields more information per observation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Input on $n$

| Input Change | Direction of Effect on $n$ | Reasoning |
|:-------------|:--------------------------:|:----------|
| Increase confidence $(1-\alpha)$ | $\uparrow$ $n$ | Larger $z_{\alpha/2}$ widens interval |
| Decrease $E$ (tighter precision) | $\uparrow$ $n$ | Smaller half-width requires more data |
| Increase $\sigma$ (more variability) | $\uparrow$ $n$ | Noisier data needs more averaging |
| Include FPC ($N$ finite, small) | $\downarrow$ $n$ | Each sample gives more information |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Sample-Size Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Parameter to Estimate] --> B{What is being estimated?}
    B -- Population Mean µ --> C{Is σ known?}
    B -- Population Proportion p --> D{Is p known/estimated?}
    C -- Yes --> E["Use n = (z_α/2 · σ / E)²"]
    C -- No, have pilot s --> F["Use n = (z_α/2 · s / E)²"]
    C -- No, no pilot --> G["Use σ ≈ Range/4, then proceed"]
    D -- Yes p given --> H["Use n = (z_α/2)² · p(1-p) / E²"]
    D -- No p unknown --> I["Use conservative p = 0.5 ⇒ n = (z_α/2 / 2E)²"]
    B -- Difference µ1 - µ2 --> J["n = (z_α/2 · √(σ1²+σ2²) / E)²"]
    B -- Difference p1 - p2 --> K["n = (z_α/2)²(p1q1+p2q2) / E²"]
    E --> L{Is N finite and n0/N > 0.05?}
    F --> L
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L -- Yes --> M["Apply FPC: n_adj = n0 / (1 + (n0-1)/N)"]
    L -- No --> N[Use n as computed]
    M --> O[Round UP to integer]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Final Required Sample Size]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Input / Process / Output View of Sample-Size Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUTS (User Specifications)"]
        A1[Confidence Level 1-α]
        A2[Margin of Error E]
        A3[Variability: σ or s or p]
        A4[Population Size N if finite]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESS (Formula Application)"]
        B1[Lookup z_α/2 from standard normal table]
        B2[Compute n0 = (z_α/2 · SD / E)²]
        B3{Check FPC Trigger}
        B4[Compute n_adj using FPC formula]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT"]
        C1[Required Sample Size n]
        C2[Always rounded UP to integer]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    A4 --> B3
    B3 -- Yes --> B4
    B3 -- No --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map — How the Three Pillars Drive $n$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Confidence Level 1-α] -->|Increases z_α/2| RES[Required Sample Size n]
    P2[Margin of Error E] -->|Smaller E ⇒ Larger n| RES
    P3[Population Variability σ or p1-p] -->|Higher variability ⇒ Larger n| RES
    RES --> OUT[Optimal n for the study]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "margin of error" in the context of sample size determination for estimating a population mean. State the formula for the required sample size when the population standard deviation is known.**

**Model Answer:**

The **margin of error ($E$)** is the maximum acceptable distance between the sample estimate (e.g., $\bar{X}$) and the true population parameter (e.g., $\mu$) at a specified confidence level $(1-\alpha)$. It represents the half-width of the confidence interval.

When the population standard deviation $\sigma$ is known, the required sample size is:

$$n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$$

The value of $n$ is always rounded **up** to the next integer.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for rounding-up rule.)*

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is $p = 0.5$ used as a default when estimating the required sample size for a proportion?**

**Model Answer:**

The variance of a sample proportion is $p(1-p)$, which is maximised when $p = 0.5$ (since the function $p(1-p)$ reaches its peak value of $0.25$ at $p=0.5$). To guarantee the required precision regardless of the (unknown) true proportion, the statistician uses the **worst-case (maximum-variance) scenario**:

$$n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{2E} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{E} \right)^2 \times 0.25$$

This conservative choice ensures that the resulting $n$ is large enough to satisfy the precision requirement for **any** value of $p$ in $[0,1]$.

*(3 marks: 1 for variance expression, 1 for maximum argument, 1 for formula.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting to round UP:** A student who writes $n = 96.04$ and leaves it as is loses 1 mark. Always write $n = 97$.
> - **Using the wrong critical value:** At 95% confidence, the correct value is $z_{0.025} = 1.96$ — *not* $1.645$ (which is for 90%).
> - **Mixing up $\sigma$ and $s$ formulas:** $\sigma$ is the *population* SD; $s$ is from a *pilot sample*. Both use $z$ when $n$ is large; only the iterative $t$-formula is required for very small pilot $n_0$.
> - **Omitting FPC:** If the question specifies a finite population of size $N$ and the computed $n_0$ exceeds $5\%$ of $N$, the **finite population correction is mandatory**.
> - **Algebra errors in squaring:** Many students correctly write $1.96 \times 0.5 / 0.1$ but then square the *wrong number*. Always square the entire bracket.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the formula for determining the sample size $n$ required to estimate a population mean $\mu$ with margin of error $E$ and confidence level $(1-\alpha)$, given that the population standard deviation $\sigma$ is known. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company claims that the mean aspirin content in its tablets is 325 mg. Past records indicate $\sigma = 8$ mg. How many tablets should be tested to estimate the true mean within $\pm 2$ mg of the actual value with 95% confidence? If the tablets are produced in a single batch of $N = 500$, apply the finite population correction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

From the Central Limit Theorem, the statistic

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \sim N(0,1)$$

The two-sided $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confidence interval for $\mu$ is:

$$\bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

By definition, the margin of error is the half-width:

$$E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Rearranging for $n$:

$$\sqrt{n} = \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\boxed{\,n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2\,}$$

Always round $n$ up to the next integer.

**(7 marks: 2 for CLT setup, 2 for CI expression, 2 for algebraic inversion, 1 for rounding rule.)**

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Standard sample size:**

Given: $\sigma = 8$ mg, $E = 2$ mg, 95% confidence $\Rightarrow z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

$$n_0 = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 8}{2} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{15.68}{2} \right)^2 = (7.84)^2 = 61.4656$$

Round up: $n_0 = 62$ tablets.

**Step 2 — Apply FPC** ($N = 500$):

$$n_{adj} = \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}} = \frac{62}{1 + \dfrac{61}{500}} = \frac{62}{1 + 0.122} = \frac{62}{1.122} = 55.26$$

Round up: $n_{adj} = 56$ tablets.

**Final Answer:** Sample **62** tablets from an infinite population, or **56** tablets from the finite batch of 500.

**(7 marks: 2 for substitution, 1 for $n_0 = 62$, 2 for FPC substitution, 1 for FPC calculation, 1 for final rounding.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** A manufacturer wishes to estimate the proportion of defective items in a large shipment. The estimate is required to be within $\pm 0.04$ of the true proportion with 98% confidence. Find the minimum sample size required:
    (i) if a prior study suggests the defect rate is about 6%; **(3 marks)**
    (ii) if no prior information is available. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the two sample sizes obtained in (a) and comment on why the conservative approach ($p=0.5$) is preferred in the absence of prior information. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i) Prior information $p = 0.06$:**

Given: $E = 0.04$, 98% confidence $\Rightarrow z_{0.01} = 2.33$, $p = 0.06$, $q = 0.94$.

$$n_1 = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{E} \right)^2 \cdot p(1-p) = \left( \frac{2.33}{0.04} \right)^2 \times (0.06)(0.94)$$

Compute step by step:

$$\frac{2.33}{0.04} = 58.25$$

$$(58.25)^2 = 3393.06$$

$$(0.06)(0.94) = 0.0564$$

$$n_1 = 3393.06 \times 0.0564 = 191.37$$

Round up: $n_1 = 192$ items.

**(3 marks: 1 for $z$ value, 1 for substitution, 1 for final rounded answer.)**

**(a)(ii) No prior information — use $p = 0.5$:**

$$n_2 = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{2E} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{2.33}{0.08} \right)^2 = (29.125)^2 = 848.27$$

Round up: $n_2 = 849$ items.

**(4 marks: 1 for stating the conservative choice, 1 for substitution, 1 for computation, 1 for final rounded answer.)*

**(b) Comparison and Discussion:**

**Numerical comparison:**

$$n_2 - n_1 = 849 - 192 = 657 \text{ additional samples}$$

$$n_2 / n_1 = 849 / 192 \approx 4.42$$

The conservative sample is **roughly 4.4 times larger** than the informed one.

**Why the conservative approach is preferred when $p$ is unknown:**

1. **Guaranteed Precision:** Since $p(1-p) \le 0.25$ for all $p \in [0,1]$, using $p = 0.5$ guarantees the variance used in the formula is an **upper bound**. The resulting $n$ therefore satisfies the precision requirement for *any* true $p$.

2. **Avoids Underestimation Risk:** If an incorrect $p$ is assumed (e.g., guessing $p=0.1$ when the true value is $0.4$), the computed $n$ may be too small, leading to a confidence interval **wider than allowed** — a serious quality-control failure.

3. **Engineering Practice:** In QC and reliability engineering, the cost of an under-sized sample (false acceptance/rejection) typically far exceeds the cost of over-sampling. Hence the conservative default is standard.

4. **Trade-off:** The price of safety is the additional 657 samples in this problem — acceptable when the per-unit sampling cost is low.

**(7 marks: 2 for numerical comparison, 5 for the four justification points.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Choice of Sample Size for Estimation**

- **Core Idea:** Sample size is the **inverse of confidence interval construction** — set the half-width equal to a pre-specified $E$ and solve for $n$.
- **Master Formula:** $n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \text{Std. Dev. of Estimator}}{E} \right)^2$
- **Critical Values to Memorise:**
  - 90% $\Rightarrow z = 1.645$
  - 95% $\Rightarrow z = 1.96$
  - 98% $\Rightarrow z = 2.33$
  - 99% $\Rightarrow z = 2.576$
- **Always Round UP** to the next integer (ceiling function) — never truncate.
- **For Proportions:** If $p$ is unknown, use the **conservative value $p = 0.5$**, which maximises $p(1-p) = 0.25$.
- **For Differences:** Combine variances additively under the square root, e.g., $\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$.
- **Finite Population Correction:** Required when $n_0 / N > 0.05$ (i.e., sample exceeds 5% of the population).
- **Three Pillars Driving $n$:** Confidence level $\uparrow$ ⇒ $n \uparrow$ | Precision $\uparrow$ (smaller $E$) ⇒ $n \uparrow$ | Variability $\uparrow$ ⇒ $n \uparrow$.
- **Use $\sigma$ (population) when known; $s$ (pilot sample) when $\sigma$ unknown.** Both use $z$ for sufficiently large $n$ ($n \ge 30$ is the KTU convention).
- **Real-world caution:** If the computed $n$ is impractically large, re-negotiate either the confidence level or the precision — do not silently reduce $n$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of Hypotheses for Large Samples: Means
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Testing of Hypotheses for Large Samples: Means

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Statistical Hypothesis — Formal Definition

A **Statistical Hypothesis** is a precise, testable statement (or assumption) concerning one or more **population parameters** (such as the population mean $\mu$, population proportion $P$, or population variance $\sigma^2$). The validity of this statement is evaluated using sample evidence drawn from the population.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box**
> A hypothesis is *any assumption* made about the value of a population parameter. It is a statement that may or may not be true, and whose truth-value is determined only after analyzing sample data.

### 1.2 Testing of Hypotheses — The Statistical Verdict

**Testing of Hypothesis** is the formal, systematic procedure used to decide whether a stated hypothesis about a population parameter should be **accepted** or **rejected**, based on sample evidence, under a controlled probability of error.

For *large samples* (where the sample size **$n \geq 30$**), we use the **Z-test** because, by the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**, the sampling distribution of the mean approaches normality regardless of the population's underlying distribution.

### 1.3 Components of a Hypothesis Test

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Null Hypothesis | $H_0$ | Statement of "no effect", "no difference", or "no change". The status quo. |
| Alternative Hypothesis | $H_1$ (or $H_a$) | Statement that contradicts $H_0$. What we want to prove. |
| Test Statistic | $Z$ | A standardized value computed from sample data. |
| Level of Significance | $\alpha$ | Probability of rejecting $H_0$ when it is true (Type I error). |
| Critical Value | $Z_\alpha$ | The threshold value of $Z$ that separates the rejection region from acceptance. |

### 1.4 Types of Hypotheses — Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Courtroom Trial**
> Imagine a courtroom where a person is accused of a crime.
> - The **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)** is *"The accused is innocent"* — the default assumption, just as we assume a machine is producing correct output unless proven otherwise.
> - The **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$)** is *"The accused is guilty"* — the challenge to the default.
> - The **Judge (test statistic)** examines the **evidence (sample data)** to deliver a **verdict (accept or reject $H_0$)**.
> - **Type I Error**: Convicting an innocent person (rejecting a true $H_0$).
> - **Type II Error**: Letting a guilty person go free (accepting a false $H_0$).

### 1.5 One-Tailed and Two-Tailed Tests

- **Two-Tailed Test**: $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ vs $H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$ (testing for *any* deviation, higher or lower).
- **Right-Tailed Test (Upper Tail)**: $H_0: \mu \leq \mu_0$ vs $H_1: \mu > \mu_0$ (testing for an *increase*).
- **Left-Tailed Test (Lower Tail)**: $H_0: \mu \geq \mu_0$ vs $H_1: \mu < \mu_0$ (testing for a *decrease*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**
> Standard levels of significance adopted in practice: **$\alpha = 0.05$ (5%)** and **$\alpha = 0.01$ (1%)**. The corresponding critical values for a two-tailed test at 5% are $Z = \pm 1.96$, and at 1% are $Z = \pm 2.58$.

### 1.6 Errors in Hypothesis Testing

| Error Type | Description | Probability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type I Error** | Rejecting $H_0$ when it is true | $\alpha$ (size of the test) |
| **Type II Error** | Accepting $H_0$ when it is false | $\beta$ |
| **Power of Test** | Correctly rejecting false $H_0$ | $1 - \beta$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Logical Steps of Hypothesis Testing (KTU Standard Procedure)

The KTU board examiner expects students to write these six steps **explicitly** in every numerical problem. Skipping them costs marks.

1. **State the Hypotheses**: Define $H_0$ (null) and $H_1$ (alternative) clearly in terms of population parameters.
2. **Select the Test Statistic**: For large samples ($n \geq 30$), the test statistic is **Z**, which follows the standard normal distribution $N(0, 1)$.
3. **Choose the Level of Significance ($\alpha$)**: Commonly **5%** or **1%**.
4. **Determine the Critical Value and Decision Rule**: Look up $Z_\alpha$ from the standard normal table and state: *"Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{calc} \vert > Z_{critical}$"*.
5. **Compute the Test Statistic from Sample Data**: Plug in values and calculate $Z_{calc}$.
6. **Draw the Statistical Conclusion**: Compare $Z_{calc}$ with $Z_{critical}$ and state the result in plain language.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why standardize?** Different samples have different means and standard deviations. By converting to $Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma_{\bar{x}}}$, we map the problem to a single, universal standard normal curve where probabilities are tabulated.
- **Why $n \geq 30$?** The Central Limit Theorem guarantees that the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$ is approximately normal for large $n$, even if the parent population is non-normal.
- **How to decide tails?** Look at $H_1$:
   - If $H_1$ contains $\neq$ → **Two-tailed** (critical values $\pm Z_{\alpha/2}$).
   - If $H_1$ contains $>$ → **Right-tailed** (critical value $+Z_{\alpha}$).
   - If $H_1$ contains $<$ → **Left-tailed** (critical value $-Z_{\alpha}$).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Test Situation | Null Hypothesis $H_0$ | Test Statistic $Z$ | Standard Error |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single Mean, $\sigma$ Known** | $\mu = \mu_0$ | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| **Single Mean, $\sigma$ Unknown** | $\mu = \mu_0$ | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}}$ | $s_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ |
| **Difference of Two Means, $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ Known** | $\mu_1 = \mu_2$ | $Z = \dfrac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - 0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}}$ | $S.E.(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$ |
| **Difference of Two Means, $\sigma$ Unknown** | $\mu_1 = \mu_2$ | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}}}$ | Same as above with $s$ |
| **Critical Value, 5% Two-tailed** | — | $\pm 1.96$ | — |
| **Critical Value, 1% Two-tailed** | — | $\pm 2.58$ | — |
| **Critical Value, 5% One-tailed** | — | $\pm 1.645$ | — |
| **Critical Value, 1% One-tailed** | — | $\pm 2.33$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule (Anti-Break Table Syntax)**
> In all table cells above, absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` or are omitted in favor of "the absolute value of". **Never use the raw `|` character** in a table row — it breaks markdown table parsing.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Quality Control**: Testing whether the mean diameter of mass-produced bolts matches the design specification ($\mu_0$). Reject $H_0$ if the machine is drifting out of calibration.
- **Civil Engineering**: Testing whether the mean compressive strength of concrete cubes from a new batch meets the required $25$ MPa standard.
- **Electrical Engineering**: Verifying that the mean lifetime of a new batch of capacitors equals the rated $10{,}000$ hours.
- **Process Industries**: Comparing the mean output of two different production lines to check if they are equally efficient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Test Statistic for a Single Mean

We are given a random sample of size $n$ drawn from a population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. The sample mean is $\bar{x}$. By the Central Limit Theorem, for large $n$:

$$\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu,\ \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

This means $\bar{X}$ is normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2 / n$. To standardize, we subtract the mean and divide by the standard deviation:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

This is the **Standard Normal Variable**, and $Z \sim N(0, 1)$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Standard Error (S.E.) of Mean

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}$ is defined as the **Standard Error of the Mean**:

$$\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Why divide by $\sqrt{n}$?**
- As the sample size $n$ increases, the sample mean $\bar{x}$ becomes a more reliable estimator of $\mu$.
- The variability of $\bar{x}$ around $\mu$ shrinks by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$.
- So, larger $n$ → smaller standard error → more precise estimate → narrower confidence in the rejection region.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Test Statistic for the Difference of Two Means

Let two independent random samples be drawn:
- Sample 1: size $n_1$, mean $\bar{x}_1$, standard deviation $\sigma_1$.
- Sample 2: size $n_2$, mean $\bar{x}_2$, standard deviation $\sigma_2$.

The difference $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is normally distributed (for large samples) with:
- **Mean**: $E(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) = \mu_1 - \mu_2$
- **Variance**: $V(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) = \dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}$ (variances add for independent samples)

Standardizing under $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ (i.e., $\mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0$):

$$Z = \frac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}} = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}}$$

### 3.4 Solved Analytical Examples (KTU Model Solutions)

---

#### **Example 1: Single Mean Test (Two-Tailed)**

**Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]:**
A random sample of $50$ items from a large population has a mean of $52$. The population standard deviation is $\sigma = 8$. Test whether the population mean could be $50$ at the $5\%$ level of significance.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu = 50, \qquad H_1: \mu \neq 50 \quad \text{(Two-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2 — Test Statistic:**
For large sample ($n = 50 \geq 30$), use the $Z$-test:
$$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Significance Level:**
$\alpha = 0.05$ (5%).

**Step 4 — Critical Value:**
For a two-tailed test at $5\%$, the critical values are $\pm 1.96$.
**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{calc} \vert > 1.96$.

**Step 5 — Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{calc} &= \frac{52 - 50}{8 / \sqrt{50}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{8 / 7.0711} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{1.1314} \\[6pt]
&= 1.7678
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Since $\vert Z_{calc} \vert = 1.7678 < 1.96$, we **do not reject $H_0$** at the $5\%$ level. The data does not provide sufficient evidence to conclude that the population mean differs from $50$.

---

#### **Example 2: Single Mean Test (Right-Tailed)**

**Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]:**
The mean weight of a sample of $64$ students is $68$ kg. The population standard deviation is $\sigma = 6$ kg. Can we conclude at the $1\%$ level of significance that the mean weight of all students is greater than $65$ kg?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu \leq 65, \qquad H_1: \mu > 65 \quad \text{(Right-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2 — Test Statistic:** $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$

**Step 3 — Significance Level:** $\alpha = 0.01$ (1%).

**Step 4 — Critical Value:** For a right-tailed test at $1\%$, $Z_{0.01} = 2.33$.
**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $Z_{calc} > 2.33$.

**Step 5 — Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{calc} &= \frac{68 - 65}{6 / \sqrt{64}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{6 / 8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{0.75} \\[6pt]
&= 4.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Since $Z_{calc} = 4.00 > 2.33$, we **reject $H_0$** at the $1\%$ level. There is highly significant evidence that the mean weight of all students is greater than $65$ kg.

---

#### **Example 3: Difference of Two Means (Two-Tailed)**

**Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Pattern]:**
Two random samples were drawn from two normal populations. The data are:

| Sample | Size $n$ | Mean $\bar{x}$ | S.D. $\sigma$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $100$ | $75$ | $8$ |
| 2 | $80$ | $72$ | $7$ |

Test at the $5\%$ level of significance whether the two population means are equal.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2, \qquad H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2 \quad \text{(Two-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2 — Test Statistic:**
$$Z = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Significance Level:** $\alpha = 0.05$ (5%).

**Step 4 — Critical Value:** For a two-tailed test, $Z_{0.025} = \pm 1.96$.
**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{calc} \vert > 1.96$.

**Step 5 — Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Standard Error} &= \sqrt{\frac{8^2}{100} + \frac{7^2}{80}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{64}{100} + \frac{49}{80}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{0.64 + 0.6125} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{1.2525} \\[6pt]
&= 1.1191
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{calc} &= \frac{75 - 72}{1.1191} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{1.1191} \\[6pt]
&= 2.6807
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Since $\vert Z_{calc} \vert = 2.6807 > 1.96$, we **reject $H_0$** at the $5\%$ level. The data provides significant evidence that the two population means are *not* equal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Hypothesis Testing Procedure

The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the full decision procedure from problem statement to final conclusion.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Problem]) --> stepB[Step 1: Formulate H0 and H1]
    stepB --> stepC{Two tailed or one tailed?}
    stepC -->|Two tailed| stepD1[Use critical values plus minus Z alpha by 2]
    stepC -->|Right tailed| stepD2[Use critical value plus Z alpha]
    stepC -->|Left tailed| stepD3[Use critical value minus Z alpha]
    stepD1 --> stepE[Step 2: Compute Z test statistic]
    stepD2 --> stepE
    stepD3 --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF[Step 3: Calculate Z calculated]
    stepF --> stepG{Is absolute Z calc greater than Z critical?}
    stepG -->|Yes| stepH1[Reject H0]
    stepG -->|No| stepH2[Do not reject H0]
    stepH1 --> stepI[Step 4: State conclusion in plain language]
    stepH2 --> stepI
    stepI --> endZ([End Decision])

    style startA fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style endZ fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style stepC fill:#f0e68c,color:#000000
    style stepG fill:#f0e68c,color:#000000
    style stepH1 fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style stepH2 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Rejection Regions on the Normal Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TT["Two Tailed Test alpha equals 0.05"]
        t1[Rejection region left tail less than minus 1.96] --> t2[Acceptance region minus 1.96 to plus 1.96] --> t3[Rejection region right tail greater than plus 1.96]
    end

    subgraph RT["Right Tailed Test alpha equals 0.05"]
        r1[Acceptance region less than 1.645] --> r2[Rejection region right tail greater than 1.645]
    end

    subgraph LT["Left Tailed Test alpha equals 0.05"]
        l1[Rejection region left tail less than minus 1.645] --> l2[Acceptance region greater than minus 1.645]
    end

    style t1 fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style t3 fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style t2 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style r2 fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style r1 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style l1 fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style l2 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Type I and Type II Error Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    r0{H0 is true or false}
    r0 -->|H0 is true| q1{Decision}
    q1 -->|Reject H0| e1[Type I Error alpha]
    q1 -->|Accept H0| c1[Correct Decision 1 minus alpha]
    r0 -->|H0 is false| q2{Decision}
    q2 -->|Reject H0| c2[Correct Decision Power equals 1 minus beta]
    q2 -->|Accept H0| e2[Type II Error beta]

    style e1 fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style e2 fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style c1 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style c2 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**
Define the following terms with examples:
(i) Null Hypothesis
(ii) Type I error
(iii) Level of significance

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)**: It is a hypothesis that is tested for possible rejection under the assumption that it is true. It represents the "no change" or "no effect" scenario.
*Example*: $H_0$: The average lifetime of a bulb is $1000$ hours, i.e., $\mu = 1000$.

(ii) **Type I Error**: Rejecting the null hypothesis $H_0$ when it is actually true. Its probability is denoted by $\alpha$.
*Example*: Concluding that a patient has a disease when they are actually healthy.

(iii) **Level of Significance ($\alpha$)**: The maximum probability of committing a Type I error that a researcher is willing to accept. Common values are $0.05$ ($5\%$) and $0.01$ ($1\%$).

---

#### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**
Differentiate between one-tailed and two-tailed tests. When is each used?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | One-Tailed Test | Two-Tailed Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direction of $H_1$** | $H_1: \mu > \mu_0$ or $\mu < \mu_0$ | $H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0$ |
| **Rejection Region** | Only in one tail | In both tails |
| **Critical Value** | $Z_\alpha$ (e.g., $1.645$ at $5\%$) | $\pm Z_{\alpha/2}$ (e.g., $\pm 1.96$ at $5\%$) |
| **Usage** | Testing directional effect (increase/decrease) | Testing for any difference in either direction |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note**: Each Part B question has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]:**

**(a)** A random sample of $100$ observations from a population has a mean of $105$. The standard deviation of the population is $12$. Test the hypothesis that the population mean is $100$ against the alternative that it is greater than $100$ at the $5\%$ level of significance. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu = 100, \qquad H_1: \mu > 100 \quad \text{(Right-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2 — Test Statistic:** $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$

**Step 3 — Significance Level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 4 — Critical Value:** $Z_{0.05} = 1.645$ (right tail).
**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $Z_{calc} > 1.645$.

**Step 5 — Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{calc} &= \frac{105 - 100}{12 / \sqrt{100}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5}{12 / 10} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5}{1.2} \\[6pt]
&= 4.1667
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Since $Z_{calc} = 4.1667 > 1.645$, we **reject $H_0$** at the $5\%$ level. There is significant evidence that the population mean is greater than $100$.

---

**(b)** Two samples of sizes $n_1 = 60$ and $n_2 = 50$ are drawn from two normal populations. The sample means are $\bar{x}_1 = 110$ and $\bar{x}_2 = 100$, with population standard deviations $\sigma_1 = 8$ and $\sigma_2 = 6$. Test at the $1\%$ level of significance whether the two population means are equal. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2, \qquad H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2 \quad \text{(Two-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2 — Test Statistic:**
$$Z = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Significance Level:** $\alpha = 0.01$ (1%).

**Step 4 — Critical Value:** For two-tailed test, $Z_{0.005} = \pm 2.58$.
**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{calc} \vert > 2.58$.

**Step 5 — Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S.E. &= \sqrt{\frac{8^2}{60} + \frac{6^2}{50}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{64}{60} + \frac{36}{50}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{1.0667 + 0.72} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{1.7867} \\[6pt]
&= 1.3366
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{calc} &= \frac{110 - 100}{1.3366} = \frac{10}{1.3366} = 7.4814
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Since $\vert Z_{calc} \vert = 7.4814 \gg 2.58$, we **reject $H_0$** at the $1\%$ level. There is overwhelming evidence that the two population means are not equal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]:**

**(a)** Explain the procedure of testing a hypothesis for large samples. Discuss the types of errors in hypothesis testing. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure for Large Sample Testing:**

1. **Set up Hypotheses**: Formulate $H_0$ (the assumption to be tested) and $H_1$ (the alternative).
2. **Choose Test Statistic**: For large samples ($n \geq 30$), use the $Z$-statistic: $Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$.
3. **Set Significance Level**: Choose $\alpha$, commonly $0.05$ or $0.01$.
4. **Determine Critical Value**: From the standard normal table based on $\alpha$ and tail-type.
5. **Compute $Z_{calc}$** from the given sample data.
6. **Decision**: Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{calc} \vert > Z_{critical}$; otherwise do not reject $H_0$.

**Types of Errors:**

| | $H_0$ is True | $H_0$ is False |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reject $H_0$** | Type I Error ($\alpha$) | Correct Decision (Power) |
| **Accept $H_0$** | Correct Decision ($1-\alpha$) | Type II Error ($\beta$) |

- **Type I Error** ($\alpha$): Rejecting a true $H_0$. Fixed by the researcher.
- **Type II Error** ($\beta$): Accepting a false $H_0$.
- **Power of Test** ($1-\beta$): Probability of correctly rejecting false $H_0$.

---

**(b)** A manufacturer claims that the average lifetime of his product is $1500$ hours with a standard deviation of $100$ hours. A sample of $100$ items gives a mean lifetime of $1480$ hours. Test at the $5\%$ level whether the manufacturer's claim is valid. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu = 1500, \qquad H_1: \mu \neq 1500 \quad \text{(Two-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2 — Test Statistic:** $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$

**Step 3 — Significance Level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 4 — Critical Value:** $Z_{0.025} = \pm 1.96$.
**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{calc} \vert > 1.96$.

**Step 5 — Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{calc} &= \frac{1480 - 1500}{100 / \sqrt{100}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-20}{100 / 10} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{-20}{10} \\[6pt]
&= -2.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Since $\vert Z_{calc} \vert = 2.00 > 1.96$, we **reject $H_0$** at the $5\%$ level. The manufacturer's claim that the mean lifetime is $1500$ hours is **not valid**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the 6 steps**: Examiners allocate ~1 mark per step. Skipping "Decision Rule" or "Conclusion in plain language" costs up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong critical value for the tail**: Using $1.96$ (two-tailed) when the problem is one-tailed gives $1.645$ — a critical error.
> 3. **Using $s$ instead of $\sigma$ in the formula**: When $\sigma$ is given, use $\sigma$. When $\sigma$ is *not* given, use sample $s$.
> 4. **Not writing $\sqrt{n}$ in denominator**: A classic slip that doubles the answer. Always show $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ explicitly.
> 5. **Not stating the conclusion in words**: "Reject $H_0$" alone is incomplete. Always say *"Reject $H_0$ at the 5% level; the mean is significantly different from..."*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Large sample condition**: $n \geq 30$. Use **Z-test**.
- **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$)**: Always contains equality ($=$, $\leq$, or $\geq$).
- **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_1$)**: Contains inequality ($\neq$, $<$, or $>$).
- **Test statistic**:
   - Single mean: $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$
   - Two means: $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1 + \sigma_2^2/n_2}}$
- **Standard Error (S.E.)**: The denominator of the $Z$ formula.
- **Critical Values (must memorize)**:
   - Two-tailed, $5\%$: $\pm 1.96$ | $1\%$: $\pm 2.58$
   - One-tailed, $5\%$: $1.645$ | $1\%$: $2.33$
- **Decision rule**: Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{calc} \vert > Z_{critical}$.
- **Always state conclusion in words** — this is a non-negotiable KTU requirement.
- **Type I Error = $\alpha$** (size of test), **Type II Error = $\beta$**, **Power = $1 - \beta$**.
- **Six steps are mandatory** in every numerical answer: Hypotheses → Test Statistic → $\alpha$ → Critical Value → Computation → Conclusion.
- **Plot the rejection region** mentally or on the curve to verify your decision.
- **Difference of two means formula**: Variances *add* (because of independence), not average.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proportions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Proportions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In **Engineering Statistics and Quality Control**, a **proportion** is a special case of a binomial parameter that quantifies the fraction of items, events, or observations in a population that possess a specified attribute or characteristic. Formally, if a random sample of size $n$ is drawn from a Bernoulli-distributed population where each trial yields success with probability $p$ and failure with probability $q = 1 - p$, then the **sample proportion** is defined as: $$\hat{p} = \frac{x}{n}$$ where $x$ is the observed number of successes in $n$ independent Bernoulli trials. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim)**: The population proportion $P$ is the true (fixed, unknown) parameter that we wish to estimate using the random variable sample proportion $\hat{p}$, whose sampling distribution approaches normality under the large-sample condition $np \geq 5$ and $nq \geq 5$ (or $n\hat{p} \geq 5$ and $n\hat{q} \geq 5$). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Defective Bulb" Intuition Imagine a **manufacturing conveyor belt** at an LED bulb factory producing 10,000 bulbs per day. The Quality Control Manager wants to know: *"What fraction of bulbs produced today are defective?"* - She **cannot inspect all 10,000** bulbs (time + cost constraint). - She randomly picks $n = 200$ bulbs and finds $x = 12$ defective. - The **sample proportion** is $\hat{p} = 12/200 = 0.06$ (i.e., 6%). - Using statistical theory, she can then infer a **range** (confidence interval) within which the true defect proportion $P$ of the entire day's production likely lies. - She can also **test claims** like "Is the defect rate greater than the industry benchmark of 4%?"
