## 2.1 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Conceptual Flow

The F-distribution is **not derived from scratch** but constructed from two chi-square distributions. Here is the logical chain:

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Foundation Chain**
> 1. **Step 1:** Start with $U \sim \chi^2_{\nu_1}$ and $V \sim \chi^2_{\nu_2}$, independent.
> 2. **Step 2:** Standardize each by dividing by its own degrees of freedom: $U/\nu_1$ and $V/\nu_2$. These become **mean chi-square variates** with expectation equal to 1.
> 3. **Step 3:** Take the ratio: $F = \dfrac{U/\nu_1}{V/\nu_2}$.
> 4. **Step 4:** The probability density of this ratio is derived using the **Jacobian transformation technique** for ratios of random variables.
> 5. **Step 5:** The resulting PDF is the **F-distribution PDF** with parameters $(\nu_1, \nu_2)$.

## 2.2 Probability Density Function (PDF)

The **probability density function** of the F-distribution with $\nu_1$ numerator and $\nu_2$ denominator degrees of freedom is given by:

$$f(x; \nu_1, \nu_2) = \frac{1}{B(\nu_1/2, \nu_2/2)} \cdot \left(\frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\right)^{\nu_1/2} \cdot \frac{x^{(\nu_1/2) - 1}}{\left[1 + \left(\frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\right)x\right]^{(\nu_1 + \nu_2)/2}}$$

for $x > 0$, where $B(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the **Beta function** defined as:

$$B(a, b) = \frac{\Gamma(a)\Gamma(b)}{\Gamma(a+b)} = \int_0^1 t^{a-1}(1-t)^{b-1} \, dt$$

and $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the **Gamma function**.

### Simplified Form (using Gamma function explicitly):

$$f(x; \nu_1, \nu_2) = \frac{\Gamma\left(\frac{\nu_1+\nu_2}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\left(\frac{\nu_1}{2}\right)\Gamma\left(\frac{\nu_2}{2}\right)} \cdot \left(\frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\right)^{\nu_1/2} \cdot \frac{x^{\nu_1/2 - 1}}{\left(1 + \frac{\nu_1 x}{\nu_2}\right)^{(\nu_1+\nu_2)/2}}$$

## 2.3 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)

The CDF has **no closed-form expression** in terms of elementary functions. It is computed via the relationship with the **regularized incomplete beta function** $I_x(a, b)$:

$$F(x; \nu_1, \nu_2) = I_{\frac{\nu_1 x}{\nu_1 x + \nu_2}}\!\left(\frac{\nu_1}{2}, \frac{\nu_2}{2}\right)$$

where:

$$I_z(a, b) = \frac{B(z;\, a, b)}{B(a, b)} = \frac{\displaystyle\int_0^z t^{a-1}(1-t)^{b-1}\,dt}{\displaystyle\int_0^1 t^{a-1}(1-t)^{b-1}\,dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why no closed-form CDF?**
> Unlike the normal or chi-square distributions, the F-distribution CDF involves the **incomplete beta function**, which must be evaluated numerically. In KTU examinations, students are expected to use **F-distribution tables** rather than integrating the PDF directly.

## 2.4 Properties of the F-Distribution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Properties (Memorize These for 14-Mark Questions)**

1. **Support:** $F \in (0, \infty)$ — strictly positive.
2. **Asymmetry:** The F-distribution is **positively skewed** (right-skewed), unlike the normal distribution.
3. **Skewness decreases** as both $\nu_1$ and $\nu_2$ increase.
4. **As $\nu_2 \to \infty$** with fixed $\nu_1$, the F-distribution approaches the **chi-square distribution divided by $\nu_1$**.
5. **As both $\nu_1, \nu_2 \to \infty$**, the F-distribution approaches the **normal distribution** (after standardization).
6. **Mean** exists only if $\nu_2 > 2$:

$$E[F] = \frac{\nu_2}{\nu_2 - 2}, \quad \nu_2 > 2$$

7. **Variance** exists only if $\nu_2 > 4$:

$$\text{Var}(F) = \frac{2 \nu_2^2 (\nu_1 + \nu_2 - 2)}{\nu_1 (\nu_2 - 2)^2 (\nu_2 - 4)}, \quad \nu_2 > 4$$

8. **Mode** (most likely value):

$$\text{Mode} = \frac{\nu_2(\nu_1 - 2)}{\nu_1(\nu_2 + 2)}, \quad \nu_1 > 2$$

9. **Reciprocal Property (CRITICAL):**

$$\frac{1}{F_{\nu_1, \nu_2}} = F_{\nu_2, \nu_1}$$

This means:

$$F_{1-\alpha}(\nu_1, \nu_2) = \frac{1}{F_{\alpha}(\nu_2, \nu_1)}$$

10. **Relationship with t-distribution:**

$$t_{\nu}^2 = F_{1, \nu}$$

That is, the square of a t-distributed random variable with $\nu$ degrees of freedom is F-distributed with $(1, \nu)$ degrees of freedom.

11. **Relationship with Chi-square:**

$$\chi^2_{\nu} = \nu \cdot F_{\nu, \infty}$$

12. **Special Case:** $F_{1, 1}$ has a **Cauchy distribution** (no defined mean or variance).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table. It is your lifeline for the KTU ESE.**

| Property | Formula | Condition |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| **Definition** | $F = \dfrac{U/\nu_1}{V/\nu_2}$, $U \sim \chi^2_{\nu_1}$, $V \sim \chi^2_{\nu_2}$ | Independent |
| **PDF** | $f(x) = \dfrac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu_1+\nu_2}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu_1}{2}\right)\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu_2}{2}\right)} \left(\dfrac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\right)^{\nu_1/2} \dfrac{x^{\nu_1/2-1}}{\left(1+\frac{\nu_1 x}{\nu_2}\right)^{(\nu_1+\nu_2)/2}}$ | $x > 0$ |
| **Mean** | $E[F] = \dfrac{\nu_2}{\nu_2-2}$ | $\nu_2 > 2$ |
| **Variance** | $\dfrac{2\nu_2^2(\nu_1+\nu_2-2)}{\nu_1(\nu_2-2)^2(\nu_2-4)}$ | $\nu_2 > 4$ |
| **Mode** | $\dfrac{\nu_2(\nu_1-2)}{\nu_1(\nu_2+2)}$ | $\nu_1 > 2$ |
| **Reciprocal** | $F_{1-\alpha}(\nu_1,\nu_2) = \dfrac{1}{F_{\alpha}(\nu_2,\nu_1)}$ | Always |
| **t-F link** | $t_{\nu}^2 = F_{1,\nu}$ | Always |
| **$\chi^2$-F link** | $\chi^2_{\nu} = \nu \cdot F_{\nu,\infty}$ | Limit |
| **CDF** | $I_{\nu_1 x/(\nu_1 x + \nu_2)}\!\left(\tfrac{\nu_1}{2},\tfrac{\nu_2}{2}\right)$ | Numerical |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Absolute Value Notation:** Notice that in the table, we have used `\vert`-style notation or the regular fraction format. In markdown tables, **never** use the pipe character `|` for absolute values, as it will break the table parser. Use `|x|` carefully outside tables, or rewrite as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The F-distribution is the **statistical backbone** of:

- **ANOVA (Analysis of Variance):** Testing whether 3 or more group means are equal by comparing between-group variance to within-group variance.
- **F-test for variance equality:** Two-sample variance comparison.
- **Overall F-test in regression:** Testing if a regression model has any explanatory power.
- **Nested model comparison:** Testing if additional predictors significantly improve a model.
- **Quality engineering:** Verifying that the variance of a new process matches the variance of an established one.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the F-Distribution PDF

### 3.1.1 Setup and Starting Point

Let $U \sim \chi^2_{\nu_1}$ and $V \sim \chi^2_{\nu_2}$ be independent.

The PDF of a chi-square random variable $U$ with $\nu_1$ degrees of freedom is:

$$f_U(u) = \frac{1}{2^{\nu_1/2}\,\Gamma(\nu_1/2)}\, u^{\nu_1/2 - 1}\, e^{-u/2}, \quad u > 0$$

Similarly for $V$:

$$f_V(v) = \frac{1}{2^{\nu_2/2}\,\Gamma(\nu_2/2)}\, v^{\nu_2/2 - 1}\, e^{-v/2}, \quad v > 0$$

Since $U$ and $V$ are independent, the joint PDF is:

$$f_{U,V}(u, v) = f_U(u) \cdot f_V(v)$$

### 3.1.2 Define New Variables

We define the F-statistic and an auxiliary variable:

$$F = \frac{U/\nu_1}{V/\nu_2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad U = \frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2} \cdot F \cdot V$$

Let us define a second variable $W = V$ (the auxiliary variable) to enable a 2D transformation.

So we have the transformation:
- $u = \dfrac{\nu_1}{\nu_2} w \cdot f$
- $v = w$

where $f$ and $w$ are the new variables (corresponding to $F$ and $W$).

### 3.1.3 Compute the Jacobian

The Jacobian matrix $J$ of the transformation $(f, w) \mapsto (u, v)$ is:

$$J = \begin{vmatrix} \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial f} & \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial w} \\[8pt] \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial f} & \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial w} \end{vmatrix} = \begin{vmatrix} \dfrac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\,w & \dfrac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\,f \\[6pt] 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix}$$

Computing the determinant:

$$|J| = \frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\,w \cdot 1 - 0 = \frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\,w$$

### 3.1.4 Substitute into Joint PDF

Substituting $u = \frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2} w f$ and $v = w$ into $f_{U,V}(u,v)$ and multiplying by $|J|$:

$$f_{F,W}(f, w) = f_U\!\left(\frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}wf\right) \cdot f_V(w) \cdot \frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\,w$$

Substituting the chi-square PDFs:

$$f_{F,W}(f, w) = \frac{1}{2^{\nu_1/2}\,\Gamma(\nu_1/2)} \left(\frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}wf\right)^{\nu_1/2 - 1} e^{-\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}wf}$$

$$\times \frac{1}{2^{\nu_2/2}\,\Gamma(\nu_2/2)}\, w^{\nu_2/2 - 1} e^{-w/2} \cdot \frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\,w$$

### 3.1.5 Marginalize out W

To get the marginal PDF of $F$, integrate out $w$:

$$f_F(f) = \int_0^\infty f_{F,W}(f, w)\, dw$$

Combining the constants:

$$f_F(f) = \frac{\left(\frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\right)^{\nu_1/2 - 1} \cdot \frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}}{2^{(\nu_1+\nu_2)/2}\,\Gamma(\nu_1/2)\,\Gamma(\nu_2/2)} \cdot f^{\nu_1/2 - 1} \cdot \int_0^\infty w^{\nu_1/2 + \nu_2/2 - 1} e^{-\frac{w}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\nu_1 f}{\nu_2}\right)} dw$$

The integral has the form $\int_0^\infty w^{a-1} e^{-bw} dw = \Gamma(a)/b^a$. With $a = \frac{\nu_1+\nu_2}{2}$ and $b = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\nu_1 f}{\nu_2}\right)$:

$$\int_0^\infty w^{(\nu_1+\nu_2)/2 - 1} e^{-\frac{w}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\nu_1 f}{\nu_2}\right)} dw = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu_1+\nu_2}{2}\right)}{\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\nu_1 f}{\nu_2}\right)\right]^{(\nu_1+\nu_2)/2}}$$

### 3.1.6 Final Assembled PDF

Substituting back and simplifying:

$$f_F(f) = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu_1+\nu_2}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu_1}{2}\right)\,\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu_2}{2}\right)} \cdot \left(\frac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\right)^{\nu_1/2} \cdot \frac{f^{\nu_1/2 - 1}}{\left(1 + \frac{\nu_1 f}{\nu_2}\right)^{(\nu_1+\nu_2)/2}}$$

for $f > 0$. This is the **F-distribution PDF** with $(\nu_1, \nu_2)$ degrees of freedom. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Mean

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Derivation**

Starting from:

$$F = \frac{U/\nu_1}{V/\nu_2}, \quad U \sim \chi^2_{\nu_1}, \quad V \sim \chi^2_{\nu_2}$$

Compute $E[F]$:

$$E[F] = E\!\left[\frac{U/\nu_1}{V/\nu_2}\right] = \frac{\nu_2}{\nu_1} \cdot E\!\left[\frac{U}{V}\right]$$

Since $U$ and $V$ are independent:

$$E\!\left[\frac{U}{V}\right] = E[U] \cdot E\!\left[\frac{1}{V}\right]$$

We know $E[U] = \nu_1$. For $E[1/V]$ where $V \sim \chi^2_{\nu_2}$:

$$E\!\left[\frac{1}{V}\right] = \int_0^\infty \frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{1}{2^{\nu_2/2}\,\Gamma(\nu_2/2)}\, v^{\nu_2/2 - 1}\, e^{-v/2}\, dv$$

$$= \frac{1}{2^{\nu_2/2}\,\Gamma(\nu_2/2)} \int_0^\infty v^{\nu_2/2 - 2}\, e^{-v/2}\, dv$$

The integral is $\Gamma(\nu_2/2 - 1) / 2^{\nu_2/2 - 1}$ (using the standard gamma integral), valid only when $\nu_2/2 - 1 > 0$, i.e., $\nu_2 > 2$.

$$E\!\left[\frac{1}{V}\right] = \frac{\Gamma(\nu_2/2 - 1)}{2^{\nu_2/2 - 1} \cdot 2^{\nu_2/2}\,\Gamma(\nu_2/2)} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot (\nu_2/2 - 1)} = \frac{1}{\nu_2 - 2}$$

Therefore:

$$E[F] = \frac{\nu_2}{\nu_1} \cdot \nu_1 \cdot \frac{1}{\nu_2 - 2} = \frac{\nu_2}{\nu_2 - 2}$$

**Condition:** $\nu_2 > 2$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Proof of the Reciprocal Property

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favourite**

**Statement:** If $F \sim F(\nu_1, \nu_2)$, then $\dfrac{1}{F} \sim F(\nu_2, \nu_1)$.

**Proof:**

By definition, if $U \sim \chi^2_{\nu_1}$ and $V \sim \chi^2_{\nu_2}$ are independent, then:

$$F = \frac{U/\nu_1}{V/\nu_2} \sim F(\nu_1, \nu_2)$$

Now consider the reciprocal:

$$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{V/\nu_2}{U/\nu_1}$$

This is itself a ratio of two independent scaled chi-square variates, but with $V$ in the numerator and $U$ in the denominator:

$$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{V/\nu_2}{U/\nu_1} \sim F(\nu_2, \nu_1)$$

by direct application of the definition. $\blacksquare$

**Corollary (used for table lookups):**

$$P(F_{\nu_1, \nu_2} > F_\alpha(\nu_1, \nu_2)) = \alpha$$

By the reciprocal property:

$$P\!\left(\frac{1}{F_{\nu_1, \nu_2}} < \frac{1}{F_\alpha(\nu_1, \nu_2)}\right) = \alpha$$

But $\dfrac{1}{F_{\nu_1, \nu_2}} \sim F_{\nu_2, \nu_1}$, so:

$$P\!\left(F_{\nu_2, \nu_1} < \frac{1}{F_\alpha(\nu_1, \nu_2)}\right) = \alpha$$

This means:

$$F_{1-\alpha}(\nu_2, \nu_1) = \frac{1}{F_\alpha(\nu_1, \nu_2)}$$

Rearranging:

$$F_{1-\alpha}(\nu_1, \nu_2) = \frac{1}{F_\alpha(\nu_2, \nu_1)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: F-Test for Equality of Two Variances

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Problem Format (10–14 Marks)**

**Problem:** A Quality Control Engineer measures the diameter (in mm) of bearings from two machines. The samples yield:

- Machine A: $n_1 = 11$, $s_1^2 = 0.025$ mm²
- Machine B: $n_2 = 16$, $s_2^2 = 0.012$ mm²

Test at the **5% level of significance** whether the variances of the two machines are equal. Assume normality.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: State the hypotheses.**

$$H_0: \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 \quad \text{(variances are equal)}$$

$$H_1: \sigma_1^2 \neq \sigma_2^2 \quad \text{(variances differ, two-tailed)}$$

**Step 2: Compute the test statistic.**

The F-statistic for testing equality of variances is:

$$F = \frac{s_1^2}{s_2^2} = \frac{0.025}{0.012} = 2.0833$$

Degrees of freedom: $\nu_1 = n_1 - 1 = 10$, $\nu_2 = n_2 - 1 = 15$.

**Step 3: Determine the critical value.**

For a **two-tailed test** at $\alpha = 0.05$, we use $\alpha/2 = 0.025$ in each tail.

The upper critical value:

$$F_{0.025}(10, 15) = 3.06 \quad \text{(from F-tables)}$$

The lower critical value, using the reciprocal property:

$$F_{0.975}(10, 15) = \frac{1}{F_{0.025}(15, 10)} = \frac{1}{3.52} = 0.2841$$

**Step 4: Decision rule.**

Reject $H_0$ if $F < 0.2841$ or $F > 3.06$.

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

Computed $F = 2.0833$. Since $0.2841 < 2.0833 < 3.06$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

> **Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, there is **insufficient evidence** to conclude that the variances of the two machines are different. The machines are statistically consistent in their variability. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Test

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Matrix for F, t, and Chi-Square Tests**

| Scenario | Test | Statistic | Distribution |
|----------|------|-----------|--------------|
| Compare 2 means, known $\sigma$ | Z-test | $\dfrac{\bar{x}-\mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ | Normal |
| Compare 2 means, unknown $\sigma$ | t-test | $\dfrac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p\sqrt{1/n_1+1/n_2}}$ | t |
| Compare 2 variances | F-test | $\dfrac{s_1^2}{s_2^2}$ | F |
| Compare 3+ means | ANOVA | $\dfrac{MS_{\text{between}}}{MS_{\text{within}}}$ | F |
| Test 1 variance | Chi-square | $\dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2}$ | $\chi^2$ |
| Goodness of fit | Chi-square | $\sum \dfrac{(O-E)^2}{E}$ | $\chi^2$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: How F-Distribution is Constructed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Independent Standard Normal Samples] --> B[Square Each Observation]
    B --> C[Sum of Squares: U = Sum of n1 squared values]
    B --> D[Sum of Squares: V = Sum of n2 squared values]
    C --> E[U follows Chi-square with nu1 df]
    D --> F[V follows Chi-square with nu2 df]
    E --> G[Scale: Divide U by nu1]
    F --> H[Scale: Divide V by nu2]
    G --> I[Form Ratio: F = U/nu1 divided by V/nu2]
    H --> I
    I --> J[F follows F-distribution with nu1, nu2]
    J --> K{Application}
    K --> L[F-test: Compare 2 Variances]
    K --> M[ANOVA: Compare 3+ Means]
    K --> N[Regression: Overall Model F-test]
    K --> O[DOE: Factor Significance Tests]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD
    style J fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57C00
    style K fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style L fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style M fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style N fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style O fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Anatomy of the F-Distribution PDF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: F value] --> B[Numerator: f to power nu1/2 minus 1]
    A --> C[Denominator Bracket: 1 plus nu1 f by nu2]
    C --> D[Power: nu1 plus nu2 divided by 2]
    B --> E[Combine with Constant]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    F[Gamma Function Ratio] --> E
    G[Scale Factor: nu1 by nu2 raised to nu1/2] --> E
    E --> H[Output: Probability Density f of F]

```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: F-Test Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define H0 and H1] --> B[Step 2: Choose Significance Level alpha]
    B --> C[Step 3: Compute Sample Variances s1 squared and s2 squared]
    C --> D[Step 4: Form F Statistic: F equals s1 squared over s2 squared]
    D --> E[Step 5: Determine Degrees of Freedom nu1 equals n1 minus 1 and nu2 equals n2 minus 1]
    E --> F{Step 6: Two Tailed or One Tailed?}
    F -->|Two Tailed| G[Use alpha/2 in Each Tail]
    F -->|One Tailed| H[Use alpha in Single Tail]
    G --> I[Look Up Critical Values in F Table]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Step 7: Compare Computed F with Critical Region]
    J -->|F in Rejection Region| K[Reject H0: Variances Differ]
    J -->|F not in Rejection Region| L[Fail to Reject H0: Variances Equal]
    K --> M[Engineering Conclusion]
    L --> M
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define the F-distribution. State any four of its important properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The F-distribution is a continuous probability distribution defined as the ratio of two independent chi-square variates, each divided by its respective degrees of freedom. If $U \sim \chi^2_{\nu_1}$ and $V \sim \chi^2_{\nu_2}$ are independent, then:

$$F = \frac{U/\nu_1}{V/\nu_2} \sim F(\nu_1, \nu_2)$$

**Four important properties:**

1. **Range:** $F \in (0, \infty)$; the F-distribution is defined only for positive values.
2. **Asymmetry:** It is positively (right) skewed. The skewness decreases as degrees of freedom increase.
3. **Reciprocal Property:** $\dfrac{1}{F_{\nu_1, \nu_2}} \sim F_{\nu_2, \nu_1}$, i.e., $F_{1-\alpha}(\nu_1, \nu_2) = \dfrac{1}{F_{\alpha}(\nu_2, \nu_1)}$.
4. **Relationship with t-distribution:** $t_{\nu}^2 \sim F_{1, \nu}$, meaning the square of a t-statistic with $\nu$ df follows an F-distribution with 1 and $\nu$ df.
5. **Mean:** $E[F] = \dfrac{\nu_2}{\nu_2 - 2}$ provided $\nu_2 > 2$.

*(Any four well-explained properties fetch full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Show that $t^2_{\nu}$ follows an F-distribution with $(1, \nu)$ degrees of freedom.**

**Model Answer:**

Let $T \sim t_{\nu}$ (Student's t-distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom). The PDF of t-distribution is:

$$f_T(t) = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu+1}{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\nu\pi}\,\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right)} \left(1 + \frac{t^2}{\nu}\right)^{-(\nu+1)/2}, \quad t \in (-\infty, \infty)$$

The square of a standard normal $Z$ is $\chi^2_1$, i.e., $Z^2 \sim \chi^2_1$. Also, the t-distribution can be expressed as:

$$T = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{V/\nu}}$$

where $Z \sim N(0,1)$, $V \sim \chi^2_{\nu}$, independent.

Therefore:

$$T^2 = \frac{Z^2/1}{V/\nu} = \frac{\chi^2_1/1}{\chi^2_{\nu}/\nu}$$

By the definition of F-distribution, this is exactly $F \sim F(1, \nu)$. Hence $t^2_{\nu} = F_{1, \nu}$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Order of df:** Students frequently write $F(\nu_1, \nu_2)$ in the wrong order. Always numerator first, denominator second.
> - **Two-tailed F-test:** Forgetting to use $\alpha/2$ in the table for two-tailed tests is a common 2-mark deduction.
> - **Reciprocal Property:** When the table doesn't have a value, you MUST use the reciprocal property and explicitly state the transformation.
> - **Hypothesis Statement:** Writing $H_0: \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$ as "$H_0:$ variances are zero" or similar nonsense loses 1–2 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) Derive the mean and variance of the F-distribution with $(\nu_1, \nu_2)$ degrees of freedom.** (7 marks)

**(b) Two types of electric bulbs from two manufacturers are tested for length of life. The following data are obtained:**

| Manufacturer | Sample Size $n$ | Sample Variance $s^2$ (hours²) |
|--------------|-----------------|--------------------------------|
| Brand A | 13 | 480 |
| Brand B | 9 | 720 |

**Test at the 5% level of significance whether the variances of the lifetimes of bulbs from the two brands are equal. Use the F-test.** (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a): Derivation of Mean and Variance

**Step 1: Definition.**

Let $F = \dfrac{U/\nu_1}{V/\nu_2}$ where $U \sim \chi^2_{\nu_1}$ and $V \sim \chi^2_{\nu_2}$ are independent.

**Step 2: Mean derivation.**

$$E[F] = E\!\left[\frac{U/\nu_1}{V/\nu_2}\right] = \frac{\nu_2}{\nu_1} \cdot E[U] \cdot E\!\left[\frac{1}{V}\right]$$

Using $E[U] = \nu_1$ and $E[1/V] = \dfrac{1}{\nu_2 - 2}$ (from the moment of inverse chi-square, valid for $\nu_2 > 2$):

$$E[F] = \frac{\nu_2}{\nu_1} \cdot \nu_1 \cdot \frac{1}{\nu_2 - 2} = \frac{\nu_2}{\nu_2 - 2}$$

**Step 3: Variance derivation.**

We need $E[F^2]$ first:

$$E[F^2] = E\!\left[\frac{U^2/\nu_1^2}{V^2/\nu_2^2}\right] = \frac{\nu_2^2}{\nu_1^2} \cdot E[U^2] \cdot E\!\left[\frac{1}{V^2}\right]$$

For $U \sim \chi^2_{\nu_1}$:
$$E[U^2] = \text{Var}(U) + (E[U])^2 = 2\nu_1 + \nu_1^2 = \nu_1(\nu_1 + 2)$$

For $V \sim \chi^2_{\nu_2}$, we need $E[1/V^2]$. The second moment of the inverse chi-square is:

$$E\!\left[\frac{1}{V^2}\right] = \frac{1}{(\nu_2 - 2)(\nu_2 - 4)}, \quad \nu_2 > 4$$

Therefore:

$$E[F^2] = \frac{\nu_2^2}{\nu_1^2} \cdot \nu_1(\nu_1 + 2) \cdot \frac{1}{(\nu_2-2)(\nu_2-4)} = \frac{\nu_2^2(\nu_1+2)}{\nu_1(\nu_2-2)(\nu_2-4)}$$

**Step 4: Compute variance.**

$$\text{Var}(F) = E[F^2] - (E[F])^2 = \frac{\nu_2^2(\nu_1+2)}{\nu_1(\nu_2-2)(\nu_2-4)} - \frac{\nu_2^2}{(\nu_2-2)^2}$$

Factor out $\dfrac{\nu_2^2}{(\nu_2-2)^2}$:

$$\text{Var}(F) = \frac{\nu_2^2}{(\nu_2-2)^2}\left[\frac{(\nu_1+2)(\nu_2-2)}{\nu_1(\nu_2-4)} - 1\right]$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$\frac{(\nu_1+2)(\nu_2-2) - \nu_1(\nu_2-4)}{\nu_1(\nu_2-4)} = \frac{\nu_1\nu_2 - 2\nu_1 + 2\nu_2 - 4 - \nu_1\nu_2 + 4\nu_1}{\nu_1(\nu_2-4)} = \frac{2\nu_1 + 2\nu_2 - 4}{\nu_1(\nu_2-4)} = \frac{2(\nu_1+\nu_2-2)}{\nu_1(\nu_2-4)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(F) = \frac{2\nu_2^2(\nu_1+\nu_2-2)}{\nu_1(\nu_2-2)^2(\nu_2-4)}}, \quad \nu_2 > 4 \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Part (b): F-Test for Two Variances

**Step 1: Hypotheses.**

$$H_0: \sigma_A^2 = \sigma_B^2 \quad \text{(variances are equal)}$$

$$H_1: \sigma_A^2 \neq \sigma_B^2 \quad \text{(two-tailed test)}$$

**Step 2: Significance level.**

$\alpha = 0.05$; for two-tailed test, use $\alpha/2 = 0.025$.

**Step 3: Compute the F-statistic.**

Always place the **larger sample variance in the numerator**:

$$F = \frac{s_B^2}{s_A^2} = \frac{720}{480} = 1.5$$

Degrees of freedom: $\nu_1 = n_B - 1 = 8$, $\nu_2 = n_A - 1 = 12$.

**Step 4: Critical value from F-tables.**

$$F_{0.025}(8, 12) = 3.51$$

**Step 5: Decision rule.**

Reject $H_0$ if $F > F_{0.025}(8, 12) = 3.51$.

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

Computed $F = 1.5 < 3.51$, so we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

> **Conclusion:** At the 5% level of significance, there is no significant evidence that the variances of the lifetimes of bulbs from the two brands differ. The brands are statistically comparable in variability. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) State and prove the reciprocal property of the F-distribution. Hence, given that $F_{0.05}(6, 8) = 3.58$, find $F_{0.95}(6, 8)$.** (7 marks)

**(b) A machine produces metal sheets of thickness 5 mm. Six sheets are produced by Operator X (variance $s_X^2 = 0.012$) and eight sheets by Operator Y (variance $s_Y^2 = 0.038$). Test at 1% level of significance whether Operator Y's machine has greater variance than Operator X's. Assume normality.** (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a): Reciprocal Property

**Statement:** If $F \sim F(\nu_1, \nu_2)$, then $\dfrac{1}{F} \sim F(\nu_2, \nu_1)$.

**Proof:** Let $U \sim \chi^2_{\nu_1}$ and $V \sim \chi^2_{\nu_2}$ be independent. By definition:

$$F = \frac{U/\nu_1}{V/\nu_2} \sim F(\nu_1, \nu_2)$$

Taking the reciprocal:

$$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{V/\nu_2}{U/\nu_1}$$

This is a ratio of two independent scaled chi-squares with $V$ (having $\nu_2$ df) in the numerator and $U$ (having $\nu_1$ df) in the denominator. By definition:

$$\frac{1}{F} \sim F(\nu_2, \nu_1) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Corollary (Table Lookup):**

$$F_{1-\alpha}(\nu_1, \nu_2) = \frac{1}{F_{\alpha}(\nu_2, \nu_1)}$$

**Application:**

Given $F_{0.05}(6, 8) = 3.58$, we need $F_{0.95}(6, 8)$.

Using the corollary with $\alpha = 0.05$, $\nu_1 = 6$, $\nu_2 = 8$:

$$F_{0.95}(6, 8) = \frac{1}{F_{0.05}(8, 6)}$$

The problem gives us $F_{0.05}(6, 8) = 3.58$, but we need $F_{0.05}(8, 6)$, which is a different value not provided. So we use the corollary in a different form:

$$F_{0.95}(6, 8) = \frac{1}{F_{0.05}(8, 6)}$$

Without $F_{0.05}(8, 6)$ directly, we use the alternative form:

$$F_{1-\alpha}(\nu_1, \nu_2) = \frac{1}{F_{\alpha}(\nu_2, \nu_1)} \Rightarrow F_{0.95}(6, 8) = \frac{1}{F_{0.05}(8, 6)}$$

(If the problem intends direct use of given value, the standard textbook result is often quoted as: $F_{0.95}(\nu_1, \nu_2) = 1/F_{0.05}(\nu_2, \nu_1)$. In many KTU tables, $F_{0.05}(8, 6) \approx 4.15$, so $F_{0.95}(6, 8) \approx 0.241$.)

**Note for students:** If the problem provides $F_{0.05}(6, 8) = 3.58$ and asks for $F_{0.95}(6, 8)$ using a symmetric table, the expected answer relies on the corollary:

$$F_{0.95}(6, 8) = \frac{1}{F_{0.05}(8, 6)} \quad \text{(value from table)}$$

#### Part (b): One-Tailed F-Test

**Step 1: Hypotheses.**

$$H_0: \sigma_Y^2 \leq \sigma_X^2 \quad \text{(Y's variance is not greater)}$$

$$H_1: \sigma_Y^2 > \sigma_X^2 \quad \text{(Y's variance is greater, one-tailed upper)}$$

**Step 2: Significance level.** $\alpha = 0.01$, one-tailed upper test.

**Step 3: Test statistic.**

$$F = \frac{s_Y^2}{s_X^2} = \frac{0.038}{0.012} = 3.1667$$

Degrees of freedom: $\nu_1 = n_Y - 1 = 7$, $\nu_2 = n_X - 1 = 5$.

**Step 4: Critical value.**

For a one-tailed upper test at $\alpha = 0.01$:

$$F_{0.01}(7, 5) \approx 10.46 \quad \text{(from F-tables)}$$

**Step 5: Decision.**

Since computed $F = 3.17 < 10.46$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

> **Conclusion:** At the 1% level of significance, there is insufficient evidence to conclude that Operator Y's machine has significantly greater variance than Operator X's. The two operators are statistically equivalent in production variability. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — F-Distribution in 60 Seconds**

- **Definition:** $F = (U/\nu_1) / (V/\nu_2)$ where $U, V$ are independent chi-squares.
- **Notation:** $F \sim F(\nu_1, \nu_2)$ — numerator df first, denominator df second.
- **Shape:** Positively skewed, defined for $F > 0$, becomes symmetric as df grow.
- **Mean:** $E[F] = \nu_2 / (\nu_2 - 2)$, valid for $\nu_2 > 2$.
- **Variance:** $\dfrac{2\nu_2^2(\nu_1+\nu_2-2)}{\nu_1(\nu_2-2)^2(\nu_2-4)}$, valid for $\nu_2 > 4$.
- **Mode:** $\dfrac{\nu_2(\nu_1-2)}{\nu_1(\nu_2+2)}$, valid for $\nu_1 > 2$.
- **Reciprocal Property:** $1/F_{\nu_1,\nu_2} \sim F_{\nu_2,\nu_1}$. Use $F_{1-\alpha}(\nu_1, \nu_2) = 1/F_{\alpha}(\nu_2, \nu_1)$ for table lookups.
- **t-F Bridge:** $t^2_{\nu} = F_{1, \nu}$.
- **$\chi^2$-F Bridge:** $\chi^2_{\nu} = \nu \cdot F_{\nu, \infty}$.
- **PDF:** $f(x) = \dfrac{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu_1+\nu_2}{2}\right)}{\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu_1}{2}\right)\Gamma\!\left(\frac{\nu_2}{2}\right)} \left(\dfrac{\nu_1}{\nu_2}\right)^{\nu_1/2} \dfrac{x^{\nu_1/2 - 1}}{\left(1 + \frac{\nu_1 x}{\nu_2}\right)^{(\nu_1+\nu_2)/2}}$.
- **Applications:** F-test for variance equality, ANOVA, regression model F-test, DOE.
- **F-test Rule:** Place larger sample variance in the numerator; for two-tailed tests, use $\alpha/2$.
- **CDF:** No closed form; use regularized incomplete beta function or F-tables.
- **Limit Behavior:** As $\nu_1, \nu_2 \to \infty$, F $\to$ 1 (concentrates at 1 in probability).
- **Special Case:** $F_{1,1}$ is the Cauchy distribution — no defined mean or variance!

> [!CAUTION]
> **Mnemonic for F-test Setup:** **"Bigger-on-Top"** — always place the larger sample variance in the numerator of the F-statistic. This avoids confusion with which tail of the F-distribution to use. Combined with the reciprocal property, you can always work with upper-tail tables.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: F-Tests and Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** F-Tests and Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of F-Test

The **F-test** is a classical statistical hypothesis testing procedure developed by **Sir Ronald Aylmer Fisher** (1920s) that uses the ratio of two sample variances to test the equality of variances of two normal populations, evaluate the overall significance of a regression model, or perform one-way and two-way **ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim)**: The F-test is any statistical test in which the test statistic has an F-distribution under the null hypothesis. It is most often used when comparing statistical models that have been fitted to a data set, with the purpose of identifying the model that best fits the population from which the data were sampled.

The **F-statistic** is defined as the ratio of two independent chi-square variates, each divided by its corresponding degrees of freedom:

$$F = \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2} = \frac{\text{Larger Sample Variance}}{\text{Smaller Sample Variance}}$$

where $S_1^2$ and $S_2^2$ are independent estimators of the population variances, derived from two independent random samples of sizes $n_1$ and $n_2$ drawn from normally distributed populations.

## 1.2 The F-Distribution

The **F-distribution** is a continuous probability distribution that arises frequently as the null distribution of a test statistic, most notably in the F-test for equality of variances and in ANOVA. If $U \sim \chi^2_m$ and $V \sim \chi^2_n$ are two independent chi-square random variables with $m$ and $n$ degrees of freedom respectively, then the ratio

$$F = \frac{U/m}{V/n}$$

follows an F-distribution with $(m, n)$ degrees of freedom, denoted $F \sim F(m, n)$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Quality Inspector's Ruler"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy**: Imagine two factory machines producing ball bearings. A quality inspector wants to know if **Machine A** is as *consistent* as **Machine B**. The F-test works like a "consistency ratio" ruler:
> 
> - The inspector measures the **spread (variance)** of ball sizes from each machine over 30 samples.
> - The F-test computes the **ratio of these two spreads**.
> - If the ratio is close to **1**, the machines are equally consistent (variance ratio ≈ 1).
> - If the ratio is **far from 1** (either too small or too large), the machines differ in their consistency.
> 
> In essence, the F-test is a **"litmus paper"** that tells you whether two variances (spreads) are statistically the same or significantly different.

## 1.4 Why F-Test Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Quality Control**, the F-test is foundational for:
> - Comparing the precision of two manufacturing processes.
> - Verifying **homogeneity of variance** (a critical assumption before applying the t-test for two independent samples).
> - **ANOVA**: Determining whether 3 or more production lines have the same mean output.
> - **Regression Analysis**: Testing the overall significance of a regression model (whether predictors are useful).

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Parameters

- **Degrees of Freedom (Numerator)**: $\nu_1 = n_1 - 1$ (associated with the larger variance in numerator)
- **Degrees of Freedom (Denominator)**: $\nu_2 = n_2 - 1$
- **Significance Level**: Typically $\alpha = 0.05$ (5%) in KTU problems
- **Test Statistic Range**: $F \in [0, \infty)$
- **Critical Values**: Tabulated in F-distribution tables for given $(\nu_1, \nu_2, \alpha)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic of the F-Statistic

The F-statistic emerges naturally from the **independence of sample variances** under the assumption of normality. The logical derivation chain is:

1. **Step 1**: Draw two independent random samples of sizes $n_1$ and $n_2$ from two normal populations $N(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ and $N(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$.
2. **Step 2**: Compute the **sample variances** $S_1^2$ and $S_2^2$ using the unbiased estimators:

$$S_1^2 = \frac{1}{n_1 - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} (X_i - \bar{X})^2, \quad S_2^2 = \frac{1}{n_2 - 1} \sum_{j=1}^{n_2} (Y_j - \bar{Y})^2$$

3. **Step 3**: Recognize that $\frac{(n_1-1)S_1^2}{\sigma_1^2} \sim \chi^2_{n_1-1}$ and $\frac{(n_2-1)S_2^2}{\sigma_2^2} \sim \chi^2_{n_2-1}$ (a property of normal distributions).
4. **Step 4**: Form the ratio. If $H_0: \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$ is true, then:

$$F = \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2} = \frac{\sigma_2^2 \cdot \chi^2_{n_1-1}/(n_1-1)}{\sigma_1^2 \cdot \chi^2_{n_2-1}/(n_2-1)} = \frac{\chi^2_{n_1-1}/(n_1-1)}{\chi^2_{n_2-1}/(n_2-1)}$$

5. **Step 5**: This ratio follows an **F-distribution** with $(n_1-1, n_2-1)$ degrees of freedom.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why divide by $(n-1)$?** To obtain an **unbiased estimator** of population variance: $E[S^2] = \sigma^2$.
- **Why form a ratio?** The F-distribution is the natural distribution of *variance ratios* under normality.
- **Why two parameters $(m, n)$?** Each chi-square has its own degrees of freedom, giving the F-distribution **two independent shape parameters**.

## 2.3 Detailed Properties of the F-Distribution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Properties (Examiners' Favourites):**

### Property 1: Shape and Skewness
The F-distribution is **positively skewed (right-skewed)** and is **asymptotic** to the horizontal axis. It is bounded on the left by **zero** and extends to **infinity** on the right.

### Property 2: Two Parameters
The F-distribution depends on two parameters — the **numerator degrees of freedom** $(m)$ and the **denominator degrees of freedom** $(n)$. Each positive integer pair $(m, n)$ defines a unique distribution.

### Property 3: Range
$$F \in (0, \infty)$$

### Property 4: Reciprocity Property (Most Important!)
$$F_{m,n} = \frac{1}{F_{n,m}}$$

This property is the cornerstone of two-tailed F-tests. For a two-tailed test at significance level $\alpha$:

$$F_{m,n;\,\alpha/2} = \frac{1}{F_{n,m;\,1-\alpha/2}}$$

### Property 5: Moments
- **Mean** exists only if $n > 2$: $E[F] = \frac{n}{n-2}$
- **Variance** exists only if $n > 4$: $\text{Var}(F) = \frac{2n^2(m+n-2)}{m(n-2)^2(n-4)}$

### Property 6: Relationship with Chi-Square
If $U \sim \chi^2_m$, then $\frac{U}{m} \sim F(m, \infty)$ in the limiting sense.

### Property 7: Relationship with t-Distribution
If $T \sim t_n$, then $T^2 \sim F(1, n)$. This is the foundation of the equivalence between t-test and one-way ANOVA with two groups.

### Property 8: Mode
The **mode** of the F-distribution (most probable value) is:

$$\text{Mode} = \frac{n(m-2)}{m(n+2)} \quad \text{for } m > 2$$

### Property 9: Limiting Behavior
As $m \to \infty$ and $n \to \infty$, $F$ converges to **1** (in probability), consistent with the law of large numbers.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Condition / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| F-statistic | $F = \dfrac{S_1^2}{S_2^2}$ | Numerator always larger variance |
| F from chi-squares | $F = \dfrac{\chi^2_m / m}{\chi^2_n / n}$ | Independent chi-squares |
| Degrees of freedom | $\nu_1 = n_1 - 1,\ \nu_2 = n_2 - 1$ | From sample sizes |
| Mean of F | $E[F] = \dfrac{n}{n-2}$ | Only if $n > 2$ |
| Variance of F | $\text{Var}(F) = \dfrac{2n^2(m+n-2)}{m(n-2)^2(n-4)}$ | Only if $n > 4$ |
| Reciprocity | $F_{m,n;\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{F_{n,m;1-\alpha}}$ | Critical value relation |
| F from t | $F = T^2$ | When $\nu_1 = 1$ |
| PDF of F | $f(F) = \dfrac{\Gamma\left(\frac{m+n}{2}\right)}{\Gamma(m/2)\Gamma(n/2)} \cdot \dfrac{(m/n)^{m/2} F^{m/2-1}}{[1+(m/n)F]^{(m+n)/2}}$ | For $F > 0$ |
| One-tailed rejection | Reject $H_0$ if $F > F_{\alpha;\,m,n}$ | Right-tailed test |
| Two-tailed rejection | Reject $H_0$ if $F > F_{\alpha/2;\,m,n}$ | $S_1^2 > S_2^2$ convention |

## 2.5 The F-Test Procedure (Step-by-Step for KTU Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Examination Workflow:**

1. **State the Hypotheses**:
   - $H_0: \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$ (variances are equal)
   - $H_1: \sigma_1^2 \neq \sigma_2^2$ (two-tailed) or $\sigma_1^2 > \sigma_2^2$ (one-tailed)

2. **Compute the Test Statistic**:
   $$F = \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2} \quad \text{(put larger variance in numerator)}$$

3. **Determine Critical Value**: From F-table at $(\nu_1, \nu_2, \alpha)$.

4. **Decision Rule**:
   - If $F_{\text{calculated}} > F_{\text{critical}}$ → **Reject $H_0$**
   - Otherwise → **Do not reject $H_0$**

5. **Conclusion**: State in the context of the problem.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application of F-Test** |
|---|---|
| Manufacturing | Comparing precision of two CNC machines |
| Pharmaceuticals | Comparing variability of two drug formulations |
| Civil Engineering | Comparing strength variability of two concrete batches |
| Electronics | Comparing noise variance of two communication channels |
| Process Control | ANOVA for comparing 3+ production lines |
| Regression | Test of overall model significance |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PDF of the F-Distribution

The F-distribution's probability density function is derived by transforming two independent chi-square variates. Here is the exhaustive derivation:

### Step 1: Start with Two Independent Chi-Square Variables

Let $U \sim \chi^2_m$ and $V \sim \chi^2_n$ be independent. The joint density is the product of their individual densities:

$$f_{U,V}(u,v) = \frac{1}{2^{m/2}\Gamma(m/2)} u^{m/2-1} e^{-u/2} \cdot \frac{1}{2^{n/2}\Gamma(n/2)} v^{n/2-1} e^{-v/2}$$

### Step 2: Form the F-Statistic

Define $F = \dfrac{U/m}{V/n} = \dfrac{nU}{mV}$ and a second auxiliary variable $W = V$. The inverse transformation is:

$$U = \frac{m}{n} F W, \quad V = W$$

### Step 3: Compute the Jacobian

The Jacobian of the transformation $(F, W) \to (U, V)$ is:

$$J = \begin{vmatrix} \frac{\partial U}{\partial F} & \frac{\partial U}{\partial W} \\ \frac{\partial V}{\partial F} & \frac{\partial V}{\partial W} \end{vmatrix} = \begin{vmatrix} \frac{m}{n}W & \frac{m}{n}F \\ 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = \frac{m}{n} W$$

### Step 4: Substitute into the Joint Density

$$f_{F,W}(f,w) = f_{U,V}\left(\frac{m}{n}fw, w\right) \cdot \left|\frac{m}{n}w\right|$$

Expanding:

$$f_{F,W}(f,w) = \frac{1}{2^{m/2}\Gamma(m/2)}\left(\frac{m}{n}fw\right)^{m/2-1} e^{-(m/2n)fw} \cdot \frac{1}{2^{n/2}\Gamma(n/2)} w^{n/2-1} e^{-w/2} \cdot \frac{m}{n}w$$

### Step 5: Marginalize Over $W$ (Integrate Out)

To obtain the marginal density of $F$, integrate over $w \in (0, \infty)$:

$$f_F(f) = \int_0^\infty f_{F,W}(f,w)\, dw$$

Collecting terms involving $w$:

$$w^{(m/2-1)+(n/2-1)+1} = w^{(m+n)/2-1}$$

The exponential term gives $e^{-w[(mf)/(2n) + 1/2]} = e^{-w(mf+n)/(2n)}$.

After performing the gamma integral $\int_0^\infty w^{(m+n)/2-1} e^{-w(mf+n)/(2n)} dw = \frac{\Gamma((m+n)/2)}{[(mf+n)/(2n)]^{(m+n)/2}}$, we obtain the PDF of F.

### Step 6: Final Result

$$\boxed{f_F(f) = \frac{\Gamma\left(\frac{m+n}{2}\right)}{\Gamma(m/2)\Gamma(n/2)} \cdot \left(\frac{m}{n}\right)^{m/2} \cdot \frac{f^{m/2-1}}{\left[1 + \frac{m}{n}f\right]^{(m+n)/2}}, \quad f > 0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification**: Setting $m = 1$ recovers the squared t-distribution, and setting $n \to \infty$ recovers the chi-square distribution scaled by $m$ — both classical limit theorems in statistics.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean of the F-Distribution

### Step 1: Use the F-Statistic Representation

Let $U \sim \chi^2_m$ and $V \sim \chi^2_n$ be independent, so $F = \dfrac{U/m}{V/n}$.

### Step 2: Condition on $V$

$$E[F] = E_V \left[ E\left[\frac{U/m}{V/n} \Big| V \right] \right] = E_V \left[ \frac{n}{V} \cdot \frac{E[U]}{m} \right] = E_V \left[ \frac{n}{V} \cdot \frac{m}{m} \right] = n \cdot E\left[\frac{1}{V}\right]$$

since $E[U] = m$ for chi-square.

### Step 3: Use the Mean of $1/V$ where $V \sim \chi^2_n$

For $V \sim \chi^2_n$, the moment $E[V^{-1}]$ exists if $n > 2$, and:

$$E\left[\frac{1}{V}\right] = \frac{1}{n-2}$$

### Step 4: Combine

$$E[F] = n \cdot \frac{1}{n-2} = \frac{n}{n-2}$$

This is valid only when $n > 2$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reciprocity Property $F_{m,n} = 1/F_{n,m}$

### Step 1: Definition

If $F \sim F(m, n)$, then $F = \dfrac{U/m}{V/n}$ where $U \sim \chi^2_m$ and $V \sim \chi^2_n$.

### Step 2: Form the Reciprocal

$$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{V/n}{U/m}$$

### Step 3: Recognize the Structure

The expression $\dfrac{V/n}{U/m}$ is exactly the ratio of two independent chi-square variates ($V$ with $n$ d.f. in numerator, $U$ with $m$ d.f. in denominator), which by definition follows $F(n, m)$.

$$\frac{1}{F} \sim F(n, m) \implies F_{m,n} = \frac{1}{F_{n,m}}$$

### Step 4: Critical Value Consequence

For a two-tailed test, the lower critical value at $\alpha/2$ for $F(m,n)$ equals the reciprocal of the upper critical value at $1-\alpha/2$ for $F(n,m)$:

$$F_{m,n;\,\alpha/2} = \frac{1}{F_{n,m;\,1-\alpha/2}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Two-Sample F-Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)**

**Problem**: Two machines produce steel rods. A sample of 11 rods from Machine A has variance $25 \text{ mm}^2$, and a sample of 16 rods from Machine B has variance $16 \text{ mm}^2$. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the variances differ significantly.

### Solution:

**Step 1: State Hypotheses**
$$H_0: \sigma_A^2 = \sigma_B^2, \quad H_1: \sigma_A^2 \neq \sigma_B^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the F-Statistic**

Larger variance in numerator: $S_A^2 = 25$, $S_B^2 = 16$.

$$F = \frac{S_A^2}{S_B^2} = \frac{25}{16} = 1.5625$$

**Step 3: Determine Degrees of Freedom**

$$\nu_1 = n_A - 1 = 11 - 1 = 10, \quad \nu_2 = n_B - 1 = 16 - 1 = 15$$

**Step 4: Find Critical Value**

From F-table at $\alpha = 0.05$, two-tailed, $F_{0.025;\,10,15} = 3.06$ (approximately).

**Step 5: Decision**

$$F_{\text{calculated}} = 1.5625 < F_{\text{critical}} = 3.06$$

**Conclusion**: **Do not reject $H_0$**. There is no significant difference between the variances of the two machines at the 5% level.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix

| **Test Type** | **$H_0$** | **$H_1$** | **Rejection Region** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two-tailed | $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$ | $\sigma_1^2 \neq \sigma_2^2$ | $F > F_{\alpha/2;\,\nu_1,\nu_2}$ |
| One-tailed (right) | $\sigma_1^2 \le \sigma_2^2$ | $\sigma_1^2 > \sigma_2^2$ | $F > F_{\alpha;\,\nu_1,\nu_2}$ |
| One-tailed (left) | $\sigma_1^2 \ge \sigma_2^2$ | $\sigma_1^2 < \sigma_2^2$ | $F < F_{1-\alpha;\,\nu_1,\nu_2}$ |

## 3.6 F-Distribution Connection Triangle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Relationship Triangle for KTU Board Exams:**

The three fundamental distributions are interconnected:
- $\chi^2 \to F$: $\dfrac{\chi^2_m / m}{\chi^2_n / n} \sim F(m, n)$
- $t \to F$: $T^2 \sim F(1, n)$
- $F \to \chi^2$: When $n \to \infty$, $F(m, n) \to \chi^2_m / m$

This triangle is essential for solving **composite problems** where you must convert between distributions.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: F-Test Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start F-Test Problem] --> B[Identify Two Samples]
    B --> C[Compute Sample Variances S1² and S2²]
    C --> D{S1² > S2²?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Set S1² as Numerator]
    D -- No --> F[Set S2² as Numerator]
    E --> G[Calculate F = Larger / Smaller]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Degrees of Freedom]
    H --> I[Determine nu1 = n1 - 1]
    I --> J[Determine nu2 = n2 - 1]
    J --> K[State Hypotheses H0 and H1]
    K --> L{Test Type?}
    L -- Two-tailed --> M[Find F alpha/2 from table]
    L -- One-tailed --> N[Find F alpha from table]
    M --> O{F_calc > F_critical?}
    N --> O
    O -- Yes --> P[Reject H0: Variances Differ]
    O -- No --> Q[Do Not Reject H0: Variances Equal]
    P --> R[Write Conclusion in Context]
    Q --> R
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define the F-distribution. State any four of its properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The **F-distribution** with parameters $m$ and $n$ (numerator and denominator degrees of freedom respectively) is the distribution of the ratio:

$$F = \frac{\chi^2_m / m}{\chi^2_n / n}$$

where $\chi^2_m$ and $\chi^2_n$ are two independent chi-square random variables.

**Four Properties:**
1. The F-distribution is **continuous** and takes values in $(0, \infty)$.
2. It is **positively skewed** (right-skewed) with a single mode.
3. It depends on **two parameters** $m$ and $n$, called numerator and denominator degrees of freedom.
4. It satisfies the **reciprocity property**: $F_{m,n} = 1/F_{n,m}$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for properties)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: When is the F-test used? Write the formula for the F-statistic.**

**Model Answer:**

The **F-test** is used in the following situations:
1. To test the **equality of variances** of two normal populations.
2. To test the **significance of a regression model** (overall F-test).
3. In **ANOVA** to compare means of three or more groups.

The **F-statistic** is computed as the ratio of two sample variances:

$$F = \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2}$$

where $S_1^2$ and $S_2^2$ are sample variances from two independent samples of sizes $n_1$ and $n_2$, with the larger variance placed in the numerator. Under $H_0: \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$, this statistic follows an F-distribution with $(n_1-1, n_2-1)$ degrees of freedom.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for uses, 1 mark for formula)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** State and prove the **reciprocity property** of the F-distribution. If $F \sim F(8, 12)$, find the value $c$ such that $P(F > c) = 0.05$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **mean and variance** of the F-distribution $F(m, n)$. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Statement**: If $F \sim F(m, n)$, then $\dfrac{1}{F} \sim F(n, m)$.

**Proof**:

Let $U \sim \chi^2_m$ and $V \sim \chi^2_n$ be two independent chi-square random variables. By definition:

$$F = \frac{U/m}{V/n} = \frac{nU}{mV}$$

Forming the reciprocal:

$$\frac{1}{F} = \frac{V/n}{U/m} = \frac{mV}{nU}$$

This is the ratio of a chi-square variate $V$ with $n$ degrees of freedom (divided by $n$) to a chi-square variate $U$ with $m$ degrees of freedom (divided by $m$). By the definition of the F-distribution:

$$\frac{1}{F} \sim F(n, m) \implies F_{m,n} = \frac{1}{F_{n,m}} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Finding $c$**: Given $F \sim F(8, 12)$ and $P(F > c) = 0.05$, we need $c = F_{0.05;\,8,12}$.

From the F-table: $F_{0.05;\,8,12} = 2.85$ (approximately).

Therefore, $c = 2.85$.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for statement, 3 marks for proof, 2 marks for table lookup)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Mean Derivation**:

$$E[F] = E\left[\frac{U/m}{V/n}\right] = \frac{n}{m} E[U] \cdot E\left[\frac{1}{V}\right]$$

(since $U$ and $1/V$ are independent).

For $U \sim \chi^2_m$: $E[U] = m$.

For $V \sim \chi^2_n$: $E[1/V] = \dfrac{1}{n-2}$ (valid for $n > 2$).

Substituting:

$$E[F] = \frac{n}{m} \cdot m \cdot \frac{1}{n-2} = \frac{n}{n-2}$$

**Variance Derivation**:

$$\text{Var}(F) = E[F^2] - (E[F])^2$$

We need $E[F^2] = E\left[\dfrac{U^2/m^2}{V^2/n^2}\right] = \dfrac{n^2}{m^2} E[U^2] E[1/V^2]$.

For $U \sim \chi^2_m$: $E[U^2] = \text{Var}(U) + (E[U])^2 = 2m + m^2 = m(m+2)$.

For $V \sim \chi^2_n$: $E[1/V^2] = \dfrac{1}{(n-2)(n-4)}$ (valid for $n > 4$).

Therefore:

$$E[F^2] = \frac{n^2}{m^2} \cdot m(m+2) \cdot \frac{1}{(n-2)(n-4)} = \frac{n^2(m+2)}{m(n-2)(n-4)}$$

And:

$$\text{Var}(F) = \frac{n^2(m+2)}{m(n-2)(n-4)} - \frac{n^2}{(n-2)^2}$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(F) = \frac{2n^2(m+n-2)}{m(n-2)^2(n-4)}}$$

*(7 marks: 3 marks for mean, 4 marks for variance)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Two random samples are drawn from two normal populations:

| Sample | Size | Variance |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 13 | 160 |
| 2 | 9 | 95 |

Test at the **5% significance level** whether the two population variances are equal. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the relationship between the **F-distribution, t-distribution, and chi-square distribution**. State the conditions under which each transforms into the other. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: State the Hypotheses**

$$H_0: \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2, \quad H_1: \sigma_1^2 \neq \sigma_2^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the F-Statistic**

Since $S_1^2 = 160 > S_2^2 = 95$, place $S_1^2$ in the numerator:

$$F = \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2} = \frac{160}{95} = 1.684$$

**Step 3: Determine Degrees of Freedom**

$$\nu_1 = n_1 - 1 = 13 - 1 = 12, \quad \nu_2 = n_2 - 1 = 9 - 1 = 8$$

**Step 4: Find Critical Value (Two-Tailed Test)**

For a two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$, we need $F_{0.025;\,12,8}$:

From the F-table: $F_{0.025;\,12,8} \approx 4.20$.

**Step 5: Decision**

$$F_{\text{calculated}} = 1.684 < F_{\text{critical}} = 4.20$$

**Conclusion**: Since the calculated F is less than the critical value, we **do not reject $H_0$**. There is no significant evidence at the 5% level to conclude that the population variances differ.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for hypotheses, 2 marks for F-statistic, 2 marks for critical value, 2 marks for decision and conclusion)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**The Interconnection Triangle:**

The three distributions are connected through the following fundamental relationships:

**1. Chi-Square to F-Distribution:**

If $U \sim \chi^2_m$ and $V \sim \chi^2_n$ are independent, then:

$$F = \frac{U/m}{V/n} \sim F(m, n)$$

This shows that the F-distribution is fundamentally a **ratio of scaled chi-square variates**.

**2. t-Distribution to F-Distribution:**

If $T \sim t_n$, then $T^2 \sim F(1, n)$.

**Proof sketch**: If $Z \sim N(0,1)$ and $V \sim \chi^2_n$ are independent, then $T = Z/\sqrt{V/n}$. Squaring:

$$T^2 = \frac{Z^2}{V/n} = \frac{\chi^2_1 / 1}{\chi^2_n / n} \sim F(1, n)$$

This is the foundation of the equivalence between the **two-sample t-test** and **one-way ANOVA with two groups**.

**3. F to Chi-Square (Limiting Case):**

As $n \to \infty$, $F(m, n) \to \chi^2_m / m$.

**Implication**: The chi-square test for a single variance is a **limiting case** of the F-test.

**Summary Table:**

| Transformation | Formula | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| $\chi^2 \to F$ | $F = (\chi^2_m/m)/(\chi^2_n/n)$ | Two independent chi-squares |
| $t \to F$ | $F = T^2$ | $T \sim t_n$ |
| $F \to \chi^2$ | $\chi^2_m = m \cdot F(m, \infty)$ | Limit as $n \to \infty$ |

*(7 marks: 2 marks for each relationship, 1 mark for summary table)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to put the larger variance in the numerator**: This invalidates the entire test. Always verify $S_1^2 > S_2^2$ before computing $F$.
> 2. **Swapping degrees of freedom**: $\nu_1$ corresponds to the *numerator* sample, not the first sample mentioned in the problem.
> 3. **Confusing one-tailed and two-tailed critical values**: For two-tailed tests, use $F_{\alpha/2}$, not $F_{\alpha}$.
> 4. **Not stating the conclusion in context**: Marks are deducted for generic conclusions. Always tie back to the engineering problem.
> 5. **Omitting the verification of normality**: The F-test *requires* both populations to be normal. State this assumption explicitly.
> 6. **Incorrectly using t-table instead of F-table**: The t-test compares means; the F-test compares variances.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Definition**: F-distribution is the ratio of two independent scaled chi-square variates: $F = (\chi^2_m/m)/(\chi^2_n/n)$.
- [ ] **F-statistic**: $F = S_1^2/S_2^2$ (larger variance always in numerator for a two-tailed test).
- [ ] **Degrees of Freedom**: $\nu_1 = n_1 - 1$ (numerator), $\nu_2 = n_2 - 1$ (denominator).
- [ ] **Range**: $F \in (0, \infty)$ — never negative.
- [ ] **Shape**: Right-skewed, with mode = $n(m-2)/[m(n+2)]$ for $m > 2$.
- [ ] **Mean**: $E[F] = n/(n-2)$, requires $n > 2$.
- [ ] **Variance**: $\text{Var}(F) = 2n^2(m+n-2)/[m(n-2)^2(n-4)]$, requires $n > 4$.
- [ ] **Reciprocity**: $F_{m,n} = 1/F_{n,m}$ — most important property for KTU.
- [ ] **t-F connection**: $T^2 \sim F(1, n)$ — bridges t-test and ANOVA.
- [ ] **chi-F connection**: $F(m, n) \to \chi^2_m/m$ as $n \to \infty$.
- [ ] **Decision rule**: Reject $H_0$ if $F_{\text{calc}} > F_{\text{critical}}$.
- [ ] **Assumption**: Both populations must be **normal**.
- [ ] **Two-tailed test**: Use $F_{\alpha/2;\nu_1,\nu_2}$ and place larger variance in numerator.
- [ ] **Applications**: Variance comparison, ANOVA, regression significance testing.
- [ ] **KTU Tip**: Always quote the F-table value with correct $(\nu_1, \nu_2)$ ordering — examiners are strict about this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Contingency Table** (also called a *Cross-Classification Table* or *Two-Way Frequency Table*) is a matrix-like display used in statistics that classifies a sample of observations according to two or more categorical variables. The fundamental purpose of a contingency table is to investigate whether the two classification criteria (often called **attributes**) are **independent** of each other, using the **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test of Independence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> A contingency table is an $r \times c$ frequency table (where $r$ = number of rows = categories of Attribute A, and $c$ = number of columns = categories of Attribute B) that summarizes the observed joint frequencies $O_{ij}$ of $N$ items classified simultaneously under two qualitative attributes. The associated $\chi^2$ test determines whether the row and column attributes are statistically associated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a quality-control manager in a biscuit factory. You want to know: **"Does the *shift* in which a biscuit is produced (Morning, Evening, Night) affect the *quality outcome* (Defective vs. Non-Defective)?"**

- You count biscuits from each shift and tally how many were defective.
- You place these counts in a grid: rows = shifts, columns = quality outcomes.
- The grid is your **contingency table**.
- If the defective rate is roughly the same in every shift, the two attributes (shift and quality) are likely **independent**. If some shifts produce far more defects, they are **dependent** or **associated**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** A contingency table is essentially a "head-count" grid that lets us see if two characteristics tend to "travel together." The $\chi^2$ test quantifies how far our actual counts deviate from the counts we would expect if the two characteristics were *completely unrelated*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Favourites)

- **Observed Frequency ($O_{ij}$):** The actual count of items falling in cell $(i, j)$.
- **Expected Frequency ($E_{ij}$):** The count we *would* expect if the two attributes were independent.
- **Marginal Totals ($R_i, C_j$):** Row sums and column sums.
- **Grand Total ($N$):** The total number of observations.
- **Degrees of Freedom (df):** $(r-1)(c-1)$ for an $r \times c$ contingency table.
- **Attributes:** The two categorical variables (e.g., "Smoking Habit" and "Health Status").

> [!TIP]
> **Why is it called "Contingency"?** The word *contingent* means "depending on." The cell frequencies are *contingent upon* (i.e., depend on) which row and column they fall into. This is a fancy, exam-worthy term to drop in your 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logic of the $\chi^2$ Test of Independence

The test operates on the following five-step logical framework:

1. **State the Hypotheses:**
   - Null Hypothesis $H_0$: The two attributes $A$ and $B$ are **independent** (no association).
   - Alternative Hypothesis $H_1$: The two attributes $A$ and $B$ are **dependent** (there is an association).

2. **Compute Expected Frequencies under $H_0$:**
   If $A$ and $B$ are independent, the probability of a cell event is the product of the marginal probabilities. This leads to the expected count formula.

3. **Measure the Discrepancy:**
   For each cell, compare the *observed* count $O_{ij}$ to the *expected* count $E_{ij}$ using the squared standardized deviation.

4. **Aggregate over all cells:**
   Sum the discrepancies to obtain a single test statistic $\chi^2$.

5. **Decision Rule:**
   Compare the calculated $\chi^2$ with the *critical* (table) value $\chi^2_{\alpha, (r-1)(c-1)}$ at a chosen level of significance (typically $\alpha = 0.05$).
   - If $\chi^2_{calc} > \chi^2_{table}$ → **Reject $H_0$** (attributes are associated).
   - If $\chi^2_{calc} \le \chi^2_{table}$ → **Accept $H_0$** (attributes are independent).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **only reference** you need for solving any KTU 14-mark problem on this topic.

| Quantity | Formula | Description / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| Expected Frequency (cell $ij$) | $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \times C_j}{N}$ | $R_i$ = row $i$ total, $C_j$ = column $j$ total, $N$ = grand total |
| $\chi^2$ Test Statistic | $\chi^2 = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c}\frac{(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ | Sum is over all $r \times c$ cells; $E_{ij} \ge 5$ recommended |
| Degrees of Freedom | $\text{df} = (r-1)(c-1)$ | For an $r \times c$ table; for $2 \times 2$ table, df $= 1$ |
| Yate's Correction (for $2 \times 2$) | $\chi^2 = \displaystyle\sum \frac{(\vert O_{ij}-E_{ij}\vert - 0.5)^2}{E_{ij}}$ | Applied only when df $= 1$ to discretize the continuous $\chi^2$ |
| Coefficient of Contingency | $C = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{\chi^2 + N}}$ | $0 \le C < 1$; measures strength of association |
| Maximum value of $C$ | $C_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m-1}{m}}$, where $m = \min(r,c)$ | Used to compute the corrected coefficient |
| Tcherprow's Coefficient | $T = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{N\sqrt{(r-1)(c-1)}}}$ | $-1 \le T \le 1$ (analogous to correlation) |

### 2.3 Conditions / Assumptions for Validity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the $\chi^2$ approximation to be **statistically valid** in a contingency table:
> 1. **Sample size** should be reasonably large (commonly $N \ge 50$).
> 2. **All expected frequencies** $E_{ij}$ should be $\ge 5$. If any $E_{ij} < 5$, either combine rows/columns or use Fisher's exact test.
> 3. The sample must be **random** and each observation classified into **exactly one cell**.
> 4. Categories must be **mutually exclusive** and **exhaustive**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Quality Control | Testing if defect type is independent of machine / shift / operator. |
| Medical Research | Testing if a disease is associated with an exposure factor. |
| Marketing | Testing if consumer preference is independent of demographic group. |
| Reliability Engineering | Testing if failure mode is independent of operating conditions. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Expected Frequency Formula

**Starting premise:** Under $H_0$, the row attribute $A$ and column attribute $B$ are **statistically independent**. Two events $A_i$ (item is in row $i$) and $B_j$ (item is in column $j$) are independent if and only if:

$$P(A_i \cap B_j) = P(A_i) \cdot P(B_j)$$

We estimate the probabilities from the sample **marginal proportions**:

$$\hat{P}(A_i) = \frac{R_i}{N}, \qquad \hat{P}(B_j) = \frac{C_j}{N}$$

By the independence assumption, the expected proportion in cell $(i,j)$ is:

$$\hat{P}(A_i \cap B_j) = \frac{R_i}{N} \cdot \frac{C_j}{N}$$

Multiplying by the grand total $N$ to obtain the expected **count**:

$$E_{ij} = N \cdot \hat{P}(A_i \cap B_j) = N \cdot \frac{R_i}{N} \cdot \frac{C_j}{N}$$

Therefore, after simplification:

$$\boxed{E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the $\chi^2$ Statistic

For a single cell, the **Pearson residual** is:

$$z_{ij} = \frac{O_{ij} - E_{ij}}{\sqrt{E_{ij}}}$$

Squaring it removes the sign and emphasises large deviations:

$$z_{ij}^2 = \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

Summing over all $r \times c$ cells aggregates the total standardised deviation across the entire table:

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c}\frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

Under $H_0$ and the large-sample assumption, this statistic follows a **Chi-Square distribution** with $(r-1)(c-1)$ degrees of freedom.

### 3.3 Why $(r-1)(c-1)$ Degrees of Freedom?

Once we fix the **marginal totals** of the table, the contents of the cells are not all free to vary:
- For $r$ rows, only $(r-1)$ row entries are free (the $r^{th}$ is determined by the row total).
- Similarly, $(c-1)$ column entries are free.
- Furthermore, the grand total $N$ is fixed.

Hence the number of "free" cells is $(r-1)(c-1)$.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — A $3 \times 2$ Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A study was conducted on **300 employees** to investigate whether *smoking habit* is associated with the *occurrence of lung disease*. The data obtained is as follows:

| Smoking Status \ Health | Lung Disease ($B_1$) | No Lung Disease ($B_2$) | Row Total ($R_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heavy Smoker ($A_1$) | 40 | 60 | 100 |
| Moderate Smoker ($A_2$) | 30 | 70 | 100 |
| Non-Smoker ($A_3$) | 20 | 80 | 100 |
| **Column Total ($C_j$)** | **90** | **210** | **$N = 300$** |

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: Smoking habit and lung disease are independent.
- $H_1$: Smoking habit and lung disease are associated.
- Level of significance: $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 2 — Compute Expected Frequencies** using $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{11} &= \frac{100 \times 90}{300} = 30 \\
E_{12} &= \frac{100 \times 210}{300} = 70 \\
E_{21} &= \frac{100 \times 90}{300} = 30 \\
E_{22} &= \frac{100 \times 210}{300} = 70 \\
E_{31} &= \frac{100 \times 90}{300} = 30 \\
E_{32} &= \frac{100 \times 210}{300} = 70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the $\chi^2$ Statistic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \sum \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(40-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(60-70)^2}{70} + \frac{(30-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(70-70)^2}{70} + \frac{(20-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(80-70)^2}{70} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{100}{30} + \frac{100}{70} + 0 + 0 + \frac{100}{30} + \frac{100}{70} \\[4pt]
&= 3.3333 + 1.4286 + 0 + 0 + 3.3333 + 1.4286 \\[4pt]
&= 9.5238
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine Degrees of Freedom:**

$$\text{df} = (r-1)(c-1) = (3-1)(2-1) = 2 \times 1 = 2$$

**Step 5 — Critical Value:**

$$\chi^2_{0.05, \, 2} = 5.991 \quad \text{(from $\chi^2$ table)}$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

$$\chi^2_{calc} = 9.5238 > \chi^2_{table} = 5.991$$

Therefore, **reject $H_0$** at the 5% level of significance.

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**
There is sufficient statistical evidence to conclude that smoking habit and lung disease are **significantly associated** (i.e., not independent).

---

### 3.5 Bonus Example — $2 \times 2$ Table **With Yate's Correction**

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A factory has two machines $M_1$ and $M_2$. In a sample of 200 items, $M_1$ produced 20 defectives and $M_2$ produced 35 defectives. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the machines are equally efficient (i.e., defect rate is independent of machine).

**Contingency Table:**

| Machine \ Quality | Defective ($B_1$) | Non-Defective ($B_2$) | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M_1$ ($A_1$) | 20 | 80 | 100 |
| $M_2$ ($A_2$) | 35 | 65 | 100 |
| **Total** | **55** | **145** | **200** |

**Expected Frequencies:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{11} &= \frac{100 \times 55}{200} = 27.5 \\
E_{12} &= \frac{100 \times 145}{200} = 72.5 \\
E_{21} &= \frac{100 \times 55}{200} = 27.5 \\
E_{22} &= \frac{100 \times 145}{200} = 72.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Without Yate's Correction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \frac{(20-27.5)^2}{27.5} + \frac{(80-72.5)^2}{72.5} + \frac{(35-27.5)^2}{27.5} + \frac{(65-72.5)^2}{72.5} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{56.25}{27.5} + \frac{56.25}{72.5} + \frac{56.25}{27.5} + \frac{56.25}{72.5} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0455 + 0.7759 + 2.0455 + 0.7759 \\[4pt]
&= 5.6428
\end{aligned}
$$

**With Yate's Correction** (df = 1, mandatory for $2 \times 2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2_{Y} &= \sum \frac{(\vert O_{ij} - E_{ij}\vert - 0.5)^2}{E_{ij}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(\vert 20-27.5\vert - 0.5)^2}{27.5} + \frac{(\vert 80-72.5\vert - 0.5)^2}{72.5} + \frac{(\vert 35-27.5\vert - 0.5)^2}{27.5} + \frac{(\vert 65-72.5\vert - 0.5)^2}{72.5} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(7-0.5)^2}{27.5} + \frac{(7-0.5)^2}{72.5} + \frac{(7-0.5)^2}{27.5} + \frac{(7-0.5)^2}{72.5} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{42.25}{27.5} + \frac{42.25}{72.5} + \frac{42.25}{27.5} + \frac{42.25}{72.5} \\[4pt]
&= 1.5364 + 0.5828 + 1.5364 + 0.5828 \\[4pt]
&= 4.2384
\end{aligned}
$$

**Degrees of Freedom:** $\text{df} = (2-1)(2-1) = 1$.

**Critical Value:** $\chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$.

**Decision:** $\chi^2_Y = 4.2384 > 3.841$ → **Reject $H_0$**. The two machines are significantly different in defect rates.

> [!TIP]
> **Board-exam observation:** Always apply Yate's correction when df = 1. The corrected $\chi^2$ is always **smaller** than the uncorrected value, making it more conservative (harder to reject $H_0$). This avoids over-claiming significance from a small $2 \times 2$ table.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — $\chi^2$ Test of Independence

The following flowchart captures the **algorithmic decision pipeline** a KTU examiner expects a student to draw in their answer sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Observed Frequencies Oij in r x c Table] --> B[Formulate Hypotheses H0 independence vs H1 association]
    B --> C[Compute Row Totals Ri and Column Totals Cj]
    C --> D[Compute Grand Total N]
    D --> E[Calculate Expected Frequencies Eij = Ri x Cj divided by N]
    E --> F{All Eij at least 5?}
    F -- No --> G[Pool small categories or use Fisher exact test]
    F -- Yes --> H[Compute chi-square sum over all cells of Oij minus Eij squared divided by Eij]
    H --> I{Table is 2 by 2 with df equals 1?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Apply Yate correction: subtract 0.5 from absolute Oij minus Eij]
    I -- No --> K[Use standard uncorrected formula]
    J --> L[Determine df equals r minus 1 times c minus 1]
    K --> L
    G --> L
    L --> M[Look up critical chi-square value at alpha equals 0.05 from table]
    M --> N{chi-square calc greater than chi-square table?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Reject H0 conclude attributes are associated]
    N -- No --> P[Accept H0 conclude attributes are independent]
    O --> Q[Optional: compute coefficient of contingency C for strength]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[END write conclusion in plain English]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#c00000,stroke:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#f9a825,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000000
    style I fill:#f9a825,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000000
    style N fill:#f9a825,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Layout of a Generic $r \times c$ Contingency Table

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CON_TABLE[r x c Contingency Table]
        H[Attributes] --- V1[Column Attribute B1] & V2[Column Attribute B2] & V3[Column Attribute Bj] & V4[Column Attribute Bc]
        V1 --- C11[Cell O11] & C21[Cell O21] & Ci1[Cell Oi1] & Cr1[Cell Or1]
        V2 --- C12[Cell O12] & C22[Cell O22] & Ci2[Cell Oi2] & Cr2[Cell Or2]
        V3 --- C1j[Cell O1j] & C2j[Cell O2j] & Cij[Cell Oij] & Crj[Cell Orj]
        V4 --- C1c[Cell O1c] & C2c[Cell O2c] & Cic[Cell Oic] & Crc[Cell Orc]
    end
```

### 4.3 Decision-Tree Diagram — When to Apply Yate's Correction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compute chi-square statistic] --> B{Table Dimension}
    B -- 2x2 table --> C[Apply Yate correction]
    B -- Greater than 2x2 --> D[Use standard formula]
    C --> E[Compare with chi-square table at df equals 1]
    D --> F[Compare with chi-square table at df equals r minus 1 times c minus 1]
    E --> G[Decision]
    F --> G
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Define a contingency table. What is its purpose in the context of the $\chi^2$ test?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A contingency table is a two-way frequency table that classifies $N$ observations simultaneously under two categorical attributes, organized as $r$ rows and $c$ columns. Its purpose is to display the **observed joint frequencies** $O_{ij}$ in a structured grid so that the **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) test of independence** can be applied to determine whether the two attributes are statistically associated. The cell entry $O_{ij}$ represents the count of items that belong to row category $i$ and column category $j$. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **State the formula for the expected frequency in any cell of a contingency table and briefly explain its derivation under the assumption of independence.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The expected frequency in cell $(i, j)$ is given by:
$$E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}$$
where $R_i$ is the $i^{th}$ row total, $C_j$ is the $j^{th}$ column total, and $N$ is the grand total. Under the null hypothesis of independence, the probability of an item falling in cell $(i, j)$ equals the product of the marginal probabilities, i.e., $P(A_i \cap B_j) = P(A_i) \cdot P(B_j) = \dfrac{R_i}{N} \cdot \dfrac{C_j}{N}$. Multiplying by $N$ gives the expected count. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** The following data is collected from a survey of **200 workers** to study the relationship between **years of experience** and **job satisfaction**. Test at the 5% level of significance whether these two attributes are independent using the $\chi^2$ test.

| Experience \ Satisfaction | Satisfied | Neutral | Dissatisfied | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Less than 5 years | 30 | 20 | 10 | 60 |
| 5 to 10 years | 40 | 25 | 15 | 80 |
| More than 10 years | 20 | 25 | 15 | 60 |
| **Total** | **90** | **70** | **40** | **200** |

> **(b) [7 Marks]** If the calculated $\chi^2$ value in part (a) is significant, compute the **coefficient of contingency** and comment on the strength of association.

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — $\chi^2$ Test of Independence [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: Years of experience and job satisfaction are independent.
- $H_1$: They are associated.
- $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 2 — Expected Frequencies** using $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{11} &= \frac{60 \times 90}{200} = 27 \\
E_{12} &= \frac{60 \times 70}{200} = 21 \\
E_{13} &= \frac{60 \times 40}{200} = 12 \\
E_{21} &= \frac{80 \times 90}{200} = 36 \\
E_{22} &= \frac{80 \times 70}{200} = 28 \\
E_{23} &= \frac{80 \times 40}{200} = 16 \\
E_{31} &= \frac{60 \times 90}{200} = 27 \\
E_{32} &= \frac{60 \times 70}{200} = 21 \\
E_{33} &= \frac{60 \times 40}{200} = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — $\chi^2$ Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \sum \frac{(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(30-27)^2}{27} + \frac{(20-21)^2}{21} + \frac{(10-12)^2}{12} + \frac{(40-36)^2}{36} + \frac{(25-28)^2}{28} + \frac{(15-16)^2}{16} + \frac{(20-27)^2}{27} + \frac{(25-21)^2}{21} + \frac{(15-12)^2}{12} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{9}{27} + \frac{1}{21} + \frac{4}{12} + \frac{16}{36} + \frac{9}{28} + \frac{1}{16} + \frac{49}{27} + \frac{16}{21} + \frac{9}{12} \\[4pt]
&= 0.3333 + 0.0476 + 0.3333 + 0.4444 + 0.3214 + 0.0625 + 1.8148 + 0.7619 + 0.7500 \\[4pt]
&= 4.8692
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Degrees of Freedom:** $\text{df} = (3-1)(3-1) = 4$.

**Step 5 — Critical Value:** $\chi^2_{0.05, 4} = 9.488$.

**Step 6 — Decision:** $4.8692 < 9.488$ → **Accept $H_0$**.

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** At the 5% level of significance, the data does **not** provide sufficient evidence to conclude that years of experience and job satisfaction are associated. They are independent. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Coefficient of Contingency [7 Marks]**

Since $\chi^2_{calc} = 4.8692$ and $N = 200$:

$$C = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{\chi^2 + N}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.8692}{4.8692 + 200}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.8692}{204.8692}} = \sqrt{0.02377} = 0.1542$$

**Maximum value of $C$** (corrected, since $m = \min(3, 3) = 3$):

$$C_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{m-1}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = 0.8165$$

**Corrected coefficient:**

$$C_{corr} = \frac{C}{C_{max}} = \frac{0.1542}{0.8165} = 0.1889$$

**Comment:** The corrected coefficient of contingency is only about **0.189**, which indicates a **very weak association** between years of experience and job satisfaction. This is consistent with our failure to reject $H_0$ in part (a). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** A study of **150 patients** examined the relationship between **blood group** and the **occurrence of a particular disease**. The observed data is given below. Test at the 5% level whether blood group and disease occurrence are independent.

| Blood Group \ Disease | Diseased | Not Diseased | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 25 | 35 | 60 |
| B | 20 | 40 | 60 |
| O | 15 | 15 | 30 |
| **Total** | **60** | **90** | **150** |

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute and interpret the **Tcherprow's coefficient of mean square contingency** for the data in part (a).

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — $\chi^2$ Test of Independence [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: Blood group and disease are independent.
- $H_1$: They are associated.
- $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 2 — Expected Frequencies:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{11} &= \frac{60 \times 60}{150} = 24 \\
E_{12} &= \frac{60 \times 90}{150} = 36 \\
E_{21} &= \frac{60 \times 60}{150} = 24 \\
E_{22} &= \frac{60 \times 90}{150} = 36 \\
E_{31} &= \frac{30 \times 60}{150} = 12 \\
E_{32} &= \frac{30 \times 90}{150} = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — $\chi^2$ Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \frac{(25-24)^2}{24} + \frac{(35-36)^2}{36} + \frac{(20-24)^2}{24} + \frac{(40-36)^2}{36} + \frac{(15-12)^2}{12} + \frac{(15-18)^2}{18} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{24} + \frac{1}{36} + \frac{16}{24} + \frac{16}{36} + \frac{9}{12} + \frac{9}{18} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0417 + 0.0278 + 0.6667 + 0.4444 + 0.7500 + 0.5000 \\[4pt]
&= 2.4306
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Degrees of Freedom:** $\text{df} = (3-1)(2-1) = 2$.

**Step 5 — Critical Value:** $\chi^2_{0.05, 2} = 5.991$.

**Step 6 — Decision:** $2.4306 < 5.991$ → **Accept $H_0$**.

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** At the 5% level, blood group and disease occurrence are **independent** (no significant association). **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Tcherprow's Coefficient [7 Marks]**

Tcherprow's coefficient is defined as:

$$T = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{N \sqrt{(r-1)(c-1)}}}$$

Substituting $\chi^2 = 2.4306$, $N = 150$, $r = 3$, $c = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \sqrt{\frac{2.4306}{150 \cdot \sqrt{(3-1)(2-1)}}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{2.4306}{150 \cdot \sqrt{2}}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{2.4306}{150 \cdot 1.4142}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{2.4306}{212.132}} \\[4pt]
&= \sqrt{0.01146} \\[4pt]
&= 0.1070
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $T \approx 0.107$ is **very close to 0**, confirming that the association between blood group and disease is **negligibly weak** — they are essentially independent. Tcherprow's $T$ lies in $[-1, 1]$, with $0$ indicating no association and $\pm 1$ indicating perfect association. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state hypotheses explicitly** — A 14-mark answer without $H_0$ and $H_1$ loses 1 mark outright.
> 2. **Using $|x|$ symbol inside a markdown table** — Always use the absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in your handwritten answer to keep tables clean.
> 3. **Forgetting Yate's correction in $2 \times 2$ tables** — If the examiner sets a $2 \times 2$ problem, omitting Yate's correction will cost you 1–2 marks. **Always apply it when df = 1.**
> 4. **Mis-stating degrees of freedom** — Common error: writing $r + c - 1$ instead of $(r-1)(c-1)$. The former is the formula for goodness-of-fit, not independence.
> 5. **Wrong table-value lookup** — At $\alpha = 0.05$ with df $= 2$, the critical value is **5.991**, not 5.991 and not 7.815 (which is at 0.025). Be careful.
> 6. **Forgetting to comment in plain English** — A numerical conclusion alone is incomplete. Always add: *"Hence, at the 5% level of significance, the two attributes are independent / associated."*
> 7. **Expected frequency $E_{ij} < 5$** — Always verify this assumption. If violated, pool categories or mention Fisher's exact test.
> 8. **Squaring $(O - E)$ incorrectly** — Many students write $(O^2 - E^2)$ instead of $(O - E)^2$. This is a fatal arithmetic error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam!**

- [x] **Definition:** A contingency table is an $r \times c$ matrix of joint observed frequencies $O_{ij}$ classifying $N$ items by two categorical attributes.
- [x] **Hypotheses:** $H_0$: attributes independent $\leftrightarrow$ $H_1$: attributes associated.
- [x] **Expected Frequency Formula:** $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}$ — this is the **single most important formula** on the topic.
- [x] **$\chi^2$ Statistic:** $\chi^2 = \sum \sum \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$.
- [x] **Degrees of Freedom:** $\text{df} = (r-1)(c-1)$. For $2 \times 2$ tables, $\text{df} = 1$.
- [x] **Yate's Correction:** Mandatory for $2 \times 2$ tables; uses $(\vert O - E \vert - 0.5)^2$ instead of $(O - E)^2$.
- [x] **Decision Rule:** If $\chi^2_{calc} > \chi^2_{table}$ at chosen $\alpha$, **reject $H_0$**.
- [x] **Coefficient of Contingency $C$:** Measures strength of association; $0 \le C < C_{max}$. Always use $C_{max}$-corrected version for comparison.
- [x] **Tcherprow's Coefficient $T$:** Normalised form; $0 \le \vert T \vert \le 1$.
- [x] **Validity Conditions:** $N \ge 50$, all $E_{ij} \ge 5$, random sample, mutually exclusive categories.
- [x] **Key Insight:** If attributes are perfectly independent, all expected frequencies will make the $\chi^2$ value small. Large $\chi^2$ = strong evidence against independence.
- [x] **Common $\chi^2$ Table Values (at $\alpha = 0.05$):**
    - df = 1 → 3.841
    - df = 2 → 5.991
    - df = 3 → 7.815
    - df = 4 → 9.488
    - df = 5 → 11.070
- [x] **Engineering Insight:** In Six-Sigma and quality audits, contingency tables are routinely used to test if **defect type** is independent of **shift**, **operator**, or **machine** — a fundamental diagnostic in root-cause analysis.

> [!TIP]
