> **Final Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, the **expected frequency table** and the **$\chi^2$ computation table** should always be drawn side-by-side or as one combined table. This is the cleanest way to present your answer and it helps the examiner trace your work — leading to full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: χ² Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** χ² Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Chi-Square Distribution):**
> A continuous random variable $X$ is said to follow a **Chi-Square distribution** with $n$ degrees of freedom if its probability density function (PDF) is given by:
>
> $$f(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2^{n/2} \, \Gamma(n/2)} \, x^{\frac{n}{2} - 1} \, e^{-x/2}, & x > 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
>
> where $n$ is a positive integer (the degrees of freedom) and $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the **Gamma function**, defined as $\Gamma(k) = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^{k-1} e^{-t} \, dt$. We denote this as $X \sim \chi^2_{(n)}$.

The Chi-Square distribution is a special case of the **Gamma distribution** with shape parameter $\alpha = n/2$ and scale parameter $\beta = 2$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Plain English):**
> Imagine you take $n$ independent standard normal random variables, say $Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_n$, where each has mean $0$ and variance $1$ (e.g., measuring standardized test scores, or noise voltages in an electronic circuit). Now square each one and add them up. The sum $\chi^2 = Z_1^2 + Z_2^2 + \cdots + Z_n^2$ follows a Chi-Square distribution with $n$ degrees of freedom.
>
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of it like this — if you are tracking the **squared distances** of $n$ independent random points from the origin (in a standardized $n$-dimensional space), the total squared distance is chi-square distributed. This is why it shows up in **distance-based statistical tests** and **variance estimation**.

### Key Properties at a Glance

- **Support:** $x \in (0, \infty)$ — the distribution exists **only for positive values** because it is built from squares.
- **Degrees of freedom ($n$):** The number of independent standard normal variables being squared and summed.
- **Asymmetry:** The distribution is **positively skewed (right-skewed)** for small $n$, but becomes **approximately symmetric and approaches a normal distribution** as $n \to \infty$.
- **Standard Form:** $E[Z] = 0$, $\text{Var}(Z) = 1$ for the underlying standard normal variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Engineering Utility:**
> In quality control, $\chi^2$ is used in **process variance testing**, **goodness-of-fit tests** (does our observed data match a theoretical distribution?), and **contingency table analysis** (are two categorical variables independent?). For example, in manufacturing, you might test whether the observed defect counts across different shifts match the expected distribution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Construction

The Chi-Square distribution can be derived as a special case of the **Gamma distribution** or as the sum of squared standard normals. Here is the structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Standard Normal Foundation:**  
  Let $Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_n$ be $n$ independent standard normal random variables: $Z_i \sim N(0, 1)$.

- **Step 2 — Square Each:**  
  Since $Z_i^2 \geq 0$, the squared values lie on the positive real line. The square of a standard normal is itself a **Gamma(1/2, 1/2)** random variable.

- **Step 3 — Sum of Gammas:**  
  The sum of $n$ independent Gamma random variables (with the same scale) is also Gamma-distributed. The shape parameter adds up: $\alpha = n/2$, scale $\beta = 2$.

- **Step 4 — Identify the Result:**  
  The PDF of Gamma$(\alpha, \beta)$ is $\frac{x^{\alpha-1} e^{-x/\beta}}{\beta^\alpha \Gamma(\alpha)}$. Substituting $\alpha = n/2$, $\beta = 2$ yields the chi-square PDF.

### 2.1.1 Additive (Reproductive) Property

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $X_1 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1)}$ and $X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_2)}$ are **independent**, then $X_1 + X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1 + n_2)}$. This is a **favourite KTU exam point**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PDF** | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{2^{n/2} \Gamma(n/2)} x^{n/2 - 1} e^{-x/2}$ | $x > 0$, $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 2 | **CDF** | $F(x) = \dfrac{\gamma(n/2,\; x/2)}{\Gamma(n/2)}$ | Lower incomplete gamma function |
| 3 | **Mean** | $E[X] = n$ | — |
| 4 | **Variance** | $\text{Var}(X) = 2n$ | — |
| 5 | **Mode** | $\text{Mode} = n - 2$ | Valid for $n \geq 3$ |
| 6 | **Skewness** | $\gamma_1 = \sqrt{8/n}$ | Positive skew |
| 7 | **Kurtosis** | $\gamma_2 = 12/n$ | Excess kurtosis |
| 8 | **MGF** | $M_X(t) = (1 - 2t)^{-n/2}$ | Valid for $t < 1/2$ |
| 9 | **CGF** | $K_X(t) = -\frac{n}{2} \ln(1 - 2t)$ | $t < 1/2$ |
| 10 | **Construction** | $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Z_i^2$ | $Z_i \sim N(0,1)$ i.i.d. |
| 11 | **Additivity** | $\chi^2_{(n_1)} + \chi^2_{(n_2)} \sim \chi^2_{(n_1+n_2)}$ | Requires independence |
| 12 | **Special Case** | $\chi^2_{(2)} \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda = 1/2)$ | Mean = 2 |
| 13 | **Asymptotic** | $\chi^2_{(n)} \xrightarrow{d} N(n, 2n)$ | As $n \to \infty$ |
| 14 | **Sampling Dist.** | $\dfrac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{(n-1)}$ | For normal population variance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The chi-square distribution is the backbone of **statistical process control (SPC)** in manufacturing, where engineers test whether observed variability in a process is within acceptable limits. It is also used in **reliability engineering** for failure-time analysis and in **signal processing** for hypothesis testing on variance of noise.

## 2.3 Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems

- The PDF is **zero for $x \leq 0$** — many students mistakenly write $f(x)$ over all real $x$.
- The **mean equals the degrees of freedom**: $E[X] = n$.  
- The **variance equals twice the degrees of freedom**: $\text{Var}(X) = 2n$.  
- For $n = 1$: $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi x}} e^{-x/2}$ — heavily right-skewed.  
- For $n = 2$: $f(x) = \frac{1}{2} e^{-x/2}$ — exponential with mean 2.  
- For $n \geq 3$: A clear **mode** appears at $x = n - 2$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: PDF of the Chi-Square Distribution (from Standard Normal Sum)

We derive the PDF of $X = Z^2$ where $Z \sim N(0,1)$, then extend to $n$ variables using convolution or the Gamma property.

### Step 1: Find the PDF of $Y = Z^2$

The standard normal PDF is:

$$f_Z(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}, \quad -\infty < z < \infty$$

For a monotonic transformation $Y = g(Z) = Z^2$, we have two roots: $z_1 = \sqrt{y}$ and $z_2 = -\sqrt{y}$.

Using the **change of variables formula**:

$$f_Y(y) = f_Z(\sqrt{y}) \left\vert \frac{dz_1}{dy} \right\vert + f_Z(-\sqrt{y}) \left\vert \frac{dz_2}{dy} \right\vert$$

Compute the derivatives:

$$\frac{dz}{dy} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{y}}$$

Substituting:

$$f_Y(y) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-y/2} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{y}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-y/2} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{y}}$$

$$f_Y(y) = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{y}} \cdot e^{-y/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi y}} \, e^{-y/2}$$

This is a **Gamma(1/2, 2)** distribution.

### Step 2: Extend to $n$ Independent Variables

Let $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ where each $Y_i = Z_i^2$ and $Y_i \sim \text{Gamma}(1/2, 2)$ independently.

The sum of $n$ independent Gamma$(1/2, 2)$ variables is Gamma$(n/2, 2)$.

The Gamma PDF with shape $\alpha$ and scale $\beta$ is:

$$f_X(x) = \frac{x^{\alpha - 1} e^{-x/\beta}}{\beta^{\alpha} \Gamma(\alpha)}, \quad x > 0$$

Substitute $\alpha = n/2$, $\beta = 2$:

$$f_X(x) = \frac{x^{n/2 - 1} \, e^{-x/2}}{2^{n/2} \, \Gamma(n/2)}, \quad x > 0$$

This is the **chi-square PDF** with $n$ degrees of freedom. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean and Variance Using the MGF

### Step 1: Moment Generating Function

For $Y = Z^2$ where $Z \sim N(0,1)$:

$$M_Y(t) = E[e^{tZ^2}] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{tz^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} \, dz$$

$$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^2(1/2 - t)} \, dz$$

This integral converges for $t < 1/2$, and evaluates to $(1 - 2t)^{-1/2}$.

For $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ with $Y_i$ independent:

$$M_X(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} M_{Y_i}(t) = \left[ (1 - 2t)^{-1/2} \right]^n = (1 - 2t)^{-n/2}$$

### Step 2: Derive the Mean

$$E[X] = M_X'(0) = \left. \frac{d}{dt} (1 - 2t)^{-n/2} \right\vert_{t=0}$$

$$\frac{d}{dt} (1 - 2t)^{-n/2} = -\frac{n}{2} \cdot (-2) \cdot (1 - 2t)^{-n/2 - 1} = n (1 - 2t)^{-n/2 - 1}$$

Evaluating at $t = 0$:

$$E[X] = n (1 - 0)^{-n/2 - 1} = n \cdot 1 = n \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 3: Derive the Variance

We need $E[X^2] = M_X''(0)$.

$$M_X'(t) = n (1 - 2t)^{-n/2 - 1}$$

Differentiating again:

$$M_X''(t) = n \cdot \left(-\frac{n}{2} - 1\right) \cdot (-2) \cdot (1 - 2t)^{-n/2 - 2} = n(n+2) (1 - 2t)^{-n/2 - 2}$$

At $t = 0$:

$$E[X^2] = n(n+2)$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = n(n+2) - n^2 = n^2 + 2n - n^2 = 2n \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Let $X \sim \chi^2_{(10)}$. Compute the mean, variance, and standard deviation.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Mean} = n = 10$$

$$\text{Variance} = 2n = 2 \times 10 = 20$$

$$\text{Standard Deviation} = \sqrt{2n} = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.472$$

> **Problem:** If $X_1 \sim \chi^2_{(5)}$ and $X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(7)}$ are independent, identify the distribution of $X_1 + X_2$.

**Solution:** By the **additive property**:

$$X_1 + X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(5 + 7)} = \chi^2_{(12)}$$

Mean = 12, Variance = 24.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram: How Chi-Square is Constructed

This diagram shows the **logical construction pipeline** of the chi-square distribution from standard normal variables — a favourite conceptual question in KTU exams.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Independent Standard Normal Variables Z1, Z2, ..., Zn"] --> B["Each Zi has Mean 0 and Variance 1"]
    B --> C["Square Each Variable: Yi = Zi squared"]
    C --> D["Each Yi follows Gamma 1/2, 2 distribution"]
    D --> E["Sum All n Squared Values: X = sum of Yi"]
    E --> F["Sum of Independent Gammas is Gamma n/2, 2"]
    F --> G["Result: X follows Chi-Square n distribution"]
    G --> H["Mean = n, Variance = 2n, Mode = n-2"]
    G --> I["As n increases, X approaches Normal n, 2n"]
```

## 4.2 Property Architecture Block Diagram

This block diagram maps the **interrelationships between the properties** of the chi-square distribution that are commonly tested in KTU.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["PDF Definition"] --> P2["Mean equals n"]
    P1 --> P3["Variance equals 2n"]
    P1 --> P4["MGF equals 1 minus 2t to power minus n over 2"]
    P5["Sum of n Standard Normals Squared"] --> P1
    P6["Special Case n equals 2"] --> P7["Exponential Distribution with mean 2"]
    P8["Additive Property"] --> P9["Sum of two independent chi-squares has additive degrees of freedom"]
    P10["Sampling Distribution of Sample Variance"] --> P11["n minus 1 times S squared over sigma squared follows chi-square n minus 1"]
    P1 --> P12["Asymptotic Approximation: N(n, 2n) for large n"]
```

## 4.3 Degrees of Freedom Behaviour Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    DF1["n = 1: Heavily right skewed, no mode"] --> DF2["n = 2: Exponential shape, mean equals 2"]
    DF2 --> DF3["n = 3: Mode appears at x equals 1"]
    DF3 --> DF4["n = 10: Mildly skewed, mean equals 10"]
    DF4 --> DF5["n = 30: Nearly symmetric, mean equals 30"]
    DF5 --> DF6["n approaches infinity: Bell shaped Normal n, 2n"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** This progression illustrates the **Central Limit Theorem in action** for the chi-square distribution. As degrees of freedom increase, the chi-square converges to a normal distribution, which is why large-sample chi-square tests can be approximated using $z$-scores.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Chi-Square distribution. State any three of its important properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Chi-Square distribution** is a continuous probability distribution defined as the sum of squares of $n$ independent standard normal random variables. A random variable $X$ follows $\chi^2_{(n)}$ if its PDF is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{2^{n/2} \Gamma(n/2)} x^{n/2 - 1} e^{-x/2}, \quad x > 0$$

**Three important properties:**

1. **Mean:** $E[X] = n$ (degrees of freedom).
2. **Variance:** $\text{Var}(X) = 2n$.
3. **Additive Property:** If $X_1 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1)}$ and $X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_2)}$ are independent, then $X_1 + X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1+n_2)}$.

*(Any 3 of: Mean, Variance, MGF, Mode, Asymptotic Normality, Special case for $n=2$)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**If $X \sim \chi^2_{(15)}$, find the mean and variance of $X$.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $X \sim \chi^2_{(n)}$ with $n = 15$.

$$\text{Mean} = n = 15$$

$$\text{Variance} = 2n = 2 \times 15 = 30$$

$$\boxed{E[X] = 15, \quad \text{Var}(X) = 30}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Derive the MGF of the chi-square distribution with $n$ degrees of freedom. Hence find its mean and variance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let $Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_n$ be independent standard normal random variables, i.e., $Z_i \sim N(0,1)$.

**Step 1: MGF of $Z_i^2$**

For any $Z \sim N(0,1)$:

$$M_{Z^2}(t) = E[e^{tZ^2}] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{tz^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2} dz$$

$$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-z^2(1/2 - t)} dz$$

This is a Gaussian integral that converges for $t < 1/2$, giving:

$$M_{Z^2}(t) = (1 - 2t)^{-1/2}$$

**Step 2: MGF of Sum**

For $X = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Z_i^2$, using independence:

$$M_X(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} M_{Z_i^2}(t) = \left[(1 - 2t)^{-1/2}\right]^n = (1 - 2t)^{-n/2}$$

**Step 3: Mean**

$$M_X'(t) = -\frac{n}{2}(-2)(1 - 2t)^{-n/2 - 1} = n(1 - 2t)^{-n/2 - 1}$$

$$E[X] = M_X'(0) = n(1)^{-n/2 - 1} = n$$

**Step 4: Second Moment and Variance**

$$M_X''(t) = n \left(-\frac{n}{2} - 1\right)(-2)(1 - 2t)^{-n/2 - 2} = n(n+2)(1 - 2t)^{-n/2 - 2}$$

$$E[X^2] = M_X''(0) = n(n+2)$$

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = n(n+2) - n^2 = 2n$$

---

**(b) If $X_1 \sim \chi^2_{(8)}$ and $X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(12)}$ are independent, find the distribution of $X_1 + 2X_2$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Since $X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(12)}$, we use the **scaling property** of chi-square. For a chi-square variable $Y \sim \chi^2_{(k)}$, the scaled variable $cY$ has MGF:

$$M_{cY}(t) = M_Y(ct) = (1 - 2ct)^{-k/2}$$

This is **not** itself a chi-square distribution (it is a Gamma distribution with the same scale multiplied by $c$).

So $2X_2$ has MGF:

$$M_{2X_2}(t) = (1 - 4t)^{-12/2} = (1 - 4t)^{-6}$$

This is a **Gamma$(6, 4)$** distribution, not a chi-square.

**Step 2:** However, $X_1 + X_2$ (with same scaling) would be $\chi^2_{(8+12)} = \chi^2_{(20)}$ by the **additive property** since both are chi-square with identical scale (2).

**Step 3:** $X_1 + 2X_2$ is therefore the sum of a $\chi^2_{(8)}$ and a Gamma$(6, 4)$ variable. Since $2X_2 = 4 \cdot (X_2/2)$ and $X_2/2 \sim \text{Gamma}(6, 1)$... 

**Cleaner interpretation using moment analysis:**

$$E[X_1 + 2X_2] = 8 + 2(12) = 32$$

$$\text{Var}(X_1 + 2X_2) = \text{Var}(X_1) + 4\text{Var}(X_2) = 16 + 4(24) = 112$$

This combination is **not a standard chi-square distribution** due to the scaling factor of 2. It is a sum of two independent gamma random variables with different scale parameters, yielding a **hypoexponential-type** distribution. The distribution is generally expressed as a **sum of weighted chi-squares**.

**Final Answer:** $X_1 + 2X_2$ does **not** follow a simple chi-square distribution. Its mean is **32** and variance is **112**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) State and prove the additive property of the chi-square distribution. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** If $X_1 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1)}$ and $X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_2)}$ are independent random variables, then their sum $X_1 + X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1 + n_2)}$.

**Proof using MGFs:**

The MGF of a $\chi^2_{(k)}$ variable is $M(t) = (1 - 2t)^{-k/2}$ for $t < 1/2$.

For $X_1 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1)}$:

$$M_{X_1}(t) = (1 - 2t)^{-n_1/2}$$

For $X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_2)}$:

$$M_{X_2}(t) = (1 - 2t)^{-n_2/2}$$

Since $X_1$ and $X_2$ are independent, the MGF of $X_1 + X_2$ is the product:

$$M_{X_1 + X_2}(t) = M_{X_1}(t) \cdot M_{X_2}(t)$$

$$= (1 - 2t)^{-n_1/2} \cdot (1 - 2t)^{-n_2/2} = (1 - 2t)^{-(n_1 + n_2)/2}$$

This is the MGF of a $\chi^2_{(n_1 + n_2)}$ distribution. By the **uniqueness theorem of MGFs**, we conclude:

$$X_1 + X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1 + n_2)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) The marks of 1000 students in an engineering entrance exam are normally distributed with mean 60 and standard deviation 12. A random sample of 25 students is taken. Find the distribution of the sample variance $S^2$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Given: Population is normal with $\mu = 60$, $\sigma = 12$, so $\sigma^2 = 144$.

Sample size $n = 25$.

**Step 2:** For a random sample of size $n$ from a **normal population** with variance $\sigma^2$, the sampling distribution of the sample variance is given by the **fundamental result**:

$$\frac{(n-1) S^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{(n-1)}$$

This is a standard result in statistical inference.

**Step 3:** Substituting $n = 25$ and $\sigma^2 = 144$:

$$\frac{24 \, S^2}{144} = \frac{S^2}{6} \sim \chi^2_{(24)}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{S^2}{6} \sim \chi^2_{(24)}}$$

**Step 5:** The mean of $S^2/6$ is 24, so:

$$E[S^2] = 6 \times 24 = 144 = \sigma^2$$

(confirming that $S^2$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\sigma^2$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the domain restriction:** Many students write the PDF as valid for all $x$, losing 1 mark. Always state $x > 0$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing MGF validity region:** The MGF $(1-2t)^{-n/2}$ is only valid for $t < 1/2$. Writing it without the domain will cost you a mark.
> 3. **Misapplying the additive property:** The additive property $X_1 + X_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1+n_2)}$ requires **independence**. If the variables are correlated, the result does **not** hold.
> 4. **Mixing up degrees of freedom for sample variance:** It is $\chi^2_{(n-1)}$, not $\chi^2_{(n)}$. This is a **favourite trap** in KTU question papers.
> 5. **Scaling confusion:** $2X$ where $X \sim \chi^2_{(k)}$ is **not** a chi-square. Always check the scale parameter.
> 6. **Skipping intermediate steps in MGF derivation:** Show the integration step and the use of the uniqueness theorem explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — $\chi^2$ Distribution:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Sum of squares of $n$ i.i.d. standard normal variables.
- ✅ **PDF:** $f(x) = \frac{1}{2^{n/2} \Gamma(n/2)} x^{n/2 - 1} e^{-x/2}$ for $x > 0$.
- ✅ **Domain:** Strictly positive real numbers; $x \in (0, \infty)$.
- ✅ **Mean:** $E[X] = n$ — equals the degrees of freedom.
- ✅ **Variance:** $\text{Var}(X) = 2n$ — twice the degrees of freedom.
- ✅ **Mode:** $x = n - 2$ (valid for $n \geq 3$).
- ✅ **MGF:** $M_X(t) = (1 - 2t)^{-n/2}$ for $t < 1/2$.
- ✅ **Special case $n = 2$:** Reduces to Exponential distribution with mean 2.
- ✅ **Additive property:** Sum of independent chi-squares is chi-square with summed degrees of freedom.
- ✅ **Sample variance link:** $\frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{(n-1)}$ for normal populations.
- ✅ **Asymptotic behaviour:** $\chi^2_{(n)} \xrightarrow{d} N(n, 2n)$ as $n \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Goodness-of-fit tests, test of independence, confidence intervals for variance, SPC variance charts.
- ✅ **Skewness:** $\sqrt{8/n}$ (positive skew, decreasing with $n$).
- ✅ **Always remember:** $\Gamma(1/2) = \sqrt{\pi}$, $\Gamma(n) = (n-1)!$ for integer $n$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Goodness of Fit and Independence of Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Goodness of Fit and Independence of Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Goodness of Fit and Independence of Attributes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Distribution — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chi-Square Distribution (Karl Pearson, 1900):** If $Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_n$ are $n$ independent standard normal variates, each with mean **0** and standard deviation **1**, then the sum of their squares
> follows a Chi-Square distribution with $n$ degrees of freedom:
>
> $$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2_{(n)}$$

The probability density function (PDF) of the chi-square distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom is:

$$f(\chi^2) = \frac{1}{2^{\nu/2} \, \Gamma(\nu/2)} \, (\chi^2)^{(\nu/2)-1} \, e^{-\chi^2/2}, \quad \chi^2 \ge 0$$

where $\Gamma(\cdot)$ is the Gamma function.

**Key Properties of $\chi^2$ Distribution:**
- **Mean:** $E(\chi^2) = \nu$ (degrees of freedom)
- **Variance:** $Var(\chi^2) = 2\nu$
- **Skewness:** Always positively skewed (right-skewed)
- **Range:** $0 \le \chi^2 < \infty$
- **Additivity:** If $\chi^2_1 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1)}$ and $\chi^2_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_2)}$ are independent, then $\chi^2_1 + \chi^2_2 \sim \chi^2_{(n_1+n_2)}$

### 1.2 Chi-Square Test Statistic

The Karl Pearson chi-square statistic measures the discrepancy between **observed** and **expected** frequencies:

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$$

where:
- $O_i$ = Observed frequency in the $i$-th class/category
- $E_i$ = Expected frequency in the $i$-th class/category under the null hypothesis
- $n$ = number of classes/categories

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The numerator $(O_i - E_i)^2$ measures how far the observation deviates from expectation, while the denominator $E_i$ normalises this deviation. A large $\chi^2$ value means poor agreement between theory and data.

### 1.3 Goodness of Fit Test — Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goodness of Fit Test:** It is a statistical hypothesis test used to determine whether a sample data comes from a population with a **specific theoretical distribution** (e.g., normal, binomial, Poisson, uniform).

**Real-World Analogy — The "Outfit Check":**
Imagine you bought a shirt online tagged as size "M" (your expected size). When it arrives, you try it on and check if it actually fits you. If the shirt is dramatically too tight or too loose, you conclude "this is **not** my size." 
Similarly, the **Goodness of Fit test** checks if the **observed data "fits" the theoretical distribution**, just like checking if a shirt fits the expected size. A small $\chi^2$ value means the data "fits well"; a large $\chi^2$ means the data **does not fit** the assumed distribution.

**Common Applications:**
- Testing whether dice are fair (uniform distribution)
- Testing if customer arrivals follow a Poisson pattern
- Testing if defects follow a specified distribution
- Testing Mendel's genetic ratios in biology

### 1.4 Test for Independence of Attributes — Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independence of Attributes:** A test to determine whether **two categorical variables (attributes)** are **statistically independent** or **associated/related**, based on a contingency table.

**Real-World Analogy — "Coffee and Productivity":**
Suppose a company wonders: *Is coffee consumption related to employee productivity?* They collect data on whether employees drink coffee and whether they are high or low performers, then arrange this in a $2 \times 2$ table. The chi-square test of independence tells whether the proportion of high performers is the **same across coffee and non-coffee drinkers**. If independent, productivity levels don't depend on coffee habits; if dependent, there is a clear association.

**Common Applications:**
- Is smoking associated with lung disease?
- Is gender independent of voting preference?
- Is machine type independent of defect occurrence?
- Is education level related to employment status?

### 1.5 When to Use the Chi-Square Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Conditions / Assumptions for Valid $\chi^2$ Test:**
> 1. **Random sampling** of data.
> 2. **Mutually exclusive** categories (one observation belongs to only one class).
> 3. **Expected frequency** $E_i \ge 5$ for each class (some authors allow $E_i \ge 5$ for at least **80%** of cells and none less than 1).
> 4. Sample size should be **sufficiently large** ($N \ge 50$ typically).
> 5. Data must be in **frequency/count form**, not percentages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Procedure for Goodness of Fit Test

The step-by-step procedure is:

1. **State the Hypotheses:**
   - Null Hypothesis $H_0$: The observed data follows the specified theoretical distribution.
   - Alternative Hypothesis $H_1$: The observed data does **not** follow the specified distribution.

2. **Compute Expected Frequencies** under $H_0$:
   $$E_i = N \cdot p_i$$
   where $N$ is the total sample size and $p_i$ is the theoretical probability of the $i$-th class.

3. **Compute the Chi-Square Statistic:**
   $$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$$
   where $k$ is the number of classes.

4. **Determine Degrees of Freedom:**
   $$\nu = k - 1 - m$$
   where $m$ = number of parameters estimated from the sample.

5. **Compare with Critical Value** at significance level $\alpha$:
   - If $\chi^2_{computed} \le \chi^2_{table}(\nu, \alpha)$ → **Accept $H_0$**
   - If $\chi^2_{computed} > \chi^2_{table}(\nu, \alpha)$ → **Reject $H_0$**

## 2.2 Procedure for Independence of Attributes (Contingency Table)

When data is arranged in a contingency table of size $r \times c$ (rows $\times$ columns):

1. **State the Hypotheses:**
   - $H_0$: The two attributes are **independent** (no association).
   - $H_1$: The two attributes are **dependent** (associated).

2. **Compute Expected Frequencies:**
   $$E_{ij} = \frac{(\text{Row Total}_i) \times (\text{Column Total}_j)}{N}$$

3. **Compute Chi-Square Statistic:**
   $$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r} \sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

4. **Degrees of Freedom:**
   $$\nu = (r-1)(c-1)$$

5. **Decision Rule:** Same as above — compare with table value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this formula for $E_{ij}$?** Under independence, the probability of an observation falling in cell $(i,j)$ is the product of marginal probabilities: $P(i \cap j) = P(i) \cdot P(j)$. Multiplying by total $N$ gives the expected cell count — a direct consequence of the probability multiplication rule for independent events.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | $O_i$ = observed, $E_i$ = expected |
| Expected Frequency (GOF) | $E_i = N \cdot p_i$ | $N$ = total, $p_i$ = theoretical prob. |
| Expected Frequency (Contingency) | $E_{ij} = \dfrac{(R_i \times C_j)}{N}$ | $R_i$ = row total, $C_j$ = column total |
| Degrees of Freedom (GOF) | $\nu = k - 1 - m$ | $k$ = classes, $m$ = estimated params |
| Degrees of Freedom (Independence) | $\nu = (r-1)(c-1)$ | $r$ = rows, $c$ = columns |
| Mean of $\chi^2$ | $\mu_{\chi^2} = \nu$ | |
| Variance of $\chi^2$ | $\sigma^2_{\chi^2} = 2\nu$ | |
| Yates' Correction (2×2) | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(\vert O - E \vert - 0.5)^2}{E}$ | For small samples ($N < 50$) |
| Contingency Coefficient | $C = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{\chi^2 + N}}$ | Measures strength of association |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control (Six Sigma):** Testing if defect categories follow an expected distribution across shifts.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Verifying if failure times follow an exponential distribution.
- **Manufacturing:** Checking if machine outputs are uniformly distributed across tolerance bands.
- **Marketing Analytics:** Determining if product preference is independent of demographics.
- **Healthcare Studies:** Testing if disease occurrence is independent of exposure to risk factors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In production lines, the chi-square test of independence helps decide whether a specific machine causes a *disproportionate* number of defects. If defect type is independent of machine, no targeted action is needed; if dependent, the engineer knows exactly which machine to overhaul.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Expected Frequency in a Contingency Table

**Setup:** Consider an $r \times c$ contingency table. Let $N$ be the grand total, $R_i$ be the $i$-th row total, and $C_j$ be the $j$-th column total. $O_{ij}$ is the observed count in cell $(i,j)$, and $E_{ij}$ is its expected count under independence.

**Step 1 — Marginal Probabilities from Row Totals:**

The empirical probability of an observation belonging to row $i$ (irrespective of column) is:

$$P(\text{Row } i) = \frac{R_i}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Marginal Probabilities from Column Totals:**

Similarly, the empirical probability of an observation belonging to column $j$ is:

$$P(\text{Column } j) = \frac{C_j}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Independence Assumption:**

If attributes are independent, the joint probability is the product of marginals:

$$P(\text{Row } i \cap \text{Column } j) = P(\text{Row } i) \cdot P(\text{Column } j) = \frac{R_i}{N} \cdot \frac{C_j}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Convert Probability to Expected Frequency:**

The expected count is the joint probability multiplied by the grand total $N$:

$$E_{ij} = N \cdot \frac{R_i}{N} \cdot \frac{C_j}{N}$$

Simplifying the right-hand side:

$$\boxed{E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{N}}$$

This is the cornerstone formula of the chi-square test of independence.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Goodness of Fit

**Problem:** A die is rolled 120 times. The observed frequencies are:

| Face | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Observed | 18 | 22 | 20 | 17 | 23 | 20 |

Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the die is fair.

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: The die is fair (uniform distribution, $p_i = 1/6$ for all $i$).
- $H_1$: The die is not fair.

**Step 2 — Compute Expected Frequencies:**

For a fair die, each face should appear $120 \times (1/6) = 20$ times.

$$E_i = N \cdot p_i = 120 \cdot \frac{1}{6} = 20 \quad \text{for all } i$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Computation Table:**

| Face ($i$) | $O_i$ | $E_i$ | $(O_i - E_i)$ | $(O_i - E_i)^2$ | $\dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 18 | 20 | $-2$ | 4 | 0.20 |
| 2 | 22 | 20 | $+2$ | 4 | 0.20 |
| 3 | 20 | 20 | 0 | 0 | 0.00 |
| 4 | 17 | 20 | $-3$ | 9 | 0.45 |
| 5 | 23 | 20 | $+3$ | 9 | 0.45 |
| 6 | 20 | 20 | 0 | 0 | 0.00 |
| **Total** | **120** | **120** | | | **1.30** |

**Step 4 — Chi-Square Statistic:**

$$\chi^2_{computed} = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i} = 1.30$$

**Step 5 — Degrees of Freedom:**

$$\nu = k - 1 - m = 6 - 1 - 0 = 5$$

(no parameters are estimated from the sample)

**Step 6 — Critical Value from Table:**

At $\alpha = 0.05$ and $\nu = 5$:

$$\chi^2_{table}(5, 0.05) = 11.070$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**

Since $\chi^2_{computed} = 1.30 < 11.070 = \chi^2_{table}$, we **accept $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, the die is **fair** (the observed data fits the uniform distribution well).

## 3.3 Worked Example — Independence of Attributes

**Problem:** A survey of 200 employees classified by smoking habit and health status is given below. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether smoking and health are independent.

| | Good Health | Poor Health | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smokers** | 60 | 40 | 100 |
| **Non-Smokers** | 95 | 5 | 100 |
| **Total** | 155 | 45 | 200 |

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: Smoking and health are independent.
- $H_1$: Smoking and health are dependent (associated).

**Step 2 — Compute Expected Frequencies** using $E_{ij} = (R_i \times C_j)/N$:

$$E_{11} = \frac{100 \times 155}{200} = 77.50 \quad \text{(Smokers, Good Health)}$$

$$E_{12} = \frac{100 \times 45}{200} = 22.50 \quad \text{(Smokers, Poor Health)}$$

$$E_{21} = \frac{100 \times 155}{200} = 77.50 \quad \text{(Non-Smokers, Good Health)}$$

$$E_{22} = \frac{100 \times 45}{200} = 22.50 \quad \text{(Non-Smokers, Poor Health)}$$

**Step 3 — Computation Table:**

| Cell | $O_{ij}$ | $E_{ij}$ | $(O_{ij} - E_{ij})$ | $(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2$ | $\dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1,1) | 60 | 77.50 | $-17.50$ | 306.25 | 3.9516 |
| (1,2) | 40 | 22.50 | $+17.50$ | 306.25 | 13.6111 |
| (2,1) | 95 | 77.50 | $+17.50$ | 306.25 | 3.9516 |
| (2,2) | 5 | 22.50 | $-17.50$ | 306.25 | 13.6111 |
| **Total** | 200 | 200 | | | **35.1254** |

**Step 4 — Chi-Square Statistic:**

$$\chi^2_{computed} = 35.1254$$

**Step 5 — Degrees of Freedom:**

$$\nu = (r-1)(c-1) = (2-1)(2-1) = 1$$

**Step 6 — Critical Value:**

$$\chi^2_{table}(1, 0.05) = 3.841$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**

Since $\chi^2_{computed} = 35.13 \gg 3.841 = \chi^2_{table}$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** Smoking and health status are **strongly associated / dependent** at the 5% level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Small Samples (Yates' Correction):** For a $2 \times 2$ table with small expected frequencies, apply the correction:
>
> $$\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(\vert O_{ij} - E_{ij} \vert - 0.5)^2}{E_{ij}}$$
>
> This avoids overestimation of $\chi^2$ in small samples.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Chi-Square Hypothesis Testing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Problem and Data] --> B[State Hypotheses H0 and H1]
    B --> C[Compute Expected Frequencies E_i]
    C --> D[Build O_i vs E_i Table]
    D --> E[Calculate chi-square statistic = sum of O-E squared divided by E]
    E --> F[Determine Degrees of Freedom nu]
    F --> G[Find chi-square table value at alpha]
    G --> H{Compare computed vs table}
    H --> I[Computed less or equal table: Accept H0]
    H --> J[Computed greater than table: Reject H0]
    I --> K[Conclude: Data fits distribution / Attributes Independent]
    J --> L[Conclude: Data does not fit / Attributes Dependent]
    K --> M[END]
    L --> M[END]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Contingency Table Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT DATA]
        A1[Row Totals R_i]
        A2[Column Totals C_j]
        A3[Grand Total N]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[EXPECTED FREQUENCY ENGINE]
        B1[E_ij = R_i x C_j divided by N]
    end

    subgraph OBSERVED[OBSERVED DATA BLOCK]
        C1[Observed Count O_ij]
    end

    subgraph STATISTIC[CHI-SQUARE STATISTIC BLOCK]
        D1[Compute deviation O_ij minus E_ij]
        D2[Square the deviation]
        D3[Divide by E_ij]
        D4[Sum across all cells]
    end

    subgraph DECISION[DECISION MODULE]
        E1[Degrees of Freedom: r-1 x c-1]
        E2[Lookup chi-square critical at alpha]
        E3[Compare computed vs critical]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    C1 --> D1
    B1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
    D4 --> E3
    E1 --> E2 --> E3
    E3 --> F[OUTPUT: Accept or Reject H0]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Chi-Square Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[You have categorical or frequency data] --> B{Is there a theoretical distribution specified?}
    B -->|YES| C[Use GOODNESS OF FIT Test]
    B -->|NO| D{Are there TWO categorical variables in a table?}
    D -->|YES| E[Use INDEPENDENCE OF ATTRIBUTES Test on r x c Contingency Table]
    D -->|NO| F[Re-evaluate data structure]

    C --> G[Degrees of freedom = k minus 1 minus m]
    E --> H[Degrees of freedom = r minus 1 times c minus 1]

    G --> I[Compare with chi-square table]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Inference]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the chi-square test statistic. State the conditions for its valid application.**

**Model Answer:**
The Karl Pearson chi-square statistic is defined as:

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$$

where $O_i$ and $E_i$ are the observed and expected frequencies respectively.

**Conditions for valid application:**
1. Observations must be **random and independent**.
2. Categories must be **mutually exclusive and exhaustive**.
3. Expected frequency $E_i \ge 5$ for all classes.
4. Sample size $N$ should be reasonably large.
5. Data must be **frequency counts**, not percentages or proportions.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by a contingency table? How are degrees of freedom computed for the chi-square test of independence?**

**Model Answer:**
A **contingency table** is a two-way frequency table that displays the joint distribution of two categorical variables arranged in $r$ rows and $c$ columns, with each cell showing the observed frequency $O_{ij}$ of observations falling into that combination of categories.

**Degrees of Freedom for Independence Test:**

$$\nu = (r-1)(c-1)$$

For example, in a $2 \times 3$ table, $\nu = (2-1)(3-1) = 2$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A machine is supposed to produce items in the ratio 1:2:3:4. A random sample of 200 items is drawn and classified as follows:

| Category | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Observed | 18 | 50 | 78 | 54 |

Test at the **5% level of significance** whether the observed distribution is consistent with the claimed ratio.

#### Part (a) — Set up hypotheses and compute expected frequencies. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: The items are produced in the ratio 1:2:3:4.
- $H_1$: The items are **not** produced in the ratio 1:2:3:4.

**Step 2 — Total of Ratios:**

$$1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Probabilities:**

$$p_A = \frac{1}{10}, \quad p_B = \frac{2}{10}, \quad p_C = \frac{3}{10}, \quad p_D = \frac{4}{10}$$

**Step 4 — Expected Frequencies** ($E_i = N \cdot p_i$ with $N = 200$):

$$E_A = 200 \times 0.1 = 20, \quad E_B = 200 \times 0.2 = 40$$

$$E_C = 200 \times 0.3 = 60, \quad E_D = 200 \times 0.4 = 80$$

#### Part (b) — Compute chi-square statistic and conclude. (7 Marks)

**Step 5 — Computation Table:**

| Category | $O_i$ | $E_i$ | $(O_i - E_i)$ | $(O_i - E_i)^2$ | $\dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 18 | 20 | $-2$ | 4 | 0.20 |
| B | 50 | 40 | $+10$ | 100 | 2.50 |
| C | 78 | 60 | $+18$ | 324 | 5.40 |
| D | 54 | 80 | $-26$ | 676 | 8.45 |
| **Total** | **200** | **200** | | | **16.55** |

**Step 6 — Chi-Square Statistic:**

$$\chi^2_{computed} = 16.55$$

**Step 7 — Degrees of Freedom:**

$$\nu = k - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3$$

**Step 8 — Critical Value:**

$$\chi^2_{table}(3, 0.05) = 7.815$$

**Step 9 — Decision:**

Since $\chi^2_{computed} = 16.55 > 7.815 = \chi^2_{table}$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** The observed distribution is **not consistent** with the claimed ratio 1:2:3:4 at the 5% level of significance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A random sample of 300 graduates is classified by **gender** and **employment status**. Test at the 1% level whether gender and employment are independent.

| | Employed | Unemployed | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Male** | 100 | 30 | 130 |
| **Female** | 110 | 60 | 170 |
| **Total** | 210 | 90 | 300 |

#### Part (a) — Compute expected frequencies and the chi-square statistic. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: Gender and employment are independent.
- $H_1$: Gender and employment are dependent.

**Step 2 — Expected Frequencies** using $E_{ij} = (R_i \times C_j)/N$:

$$E_{11} = \frac{130 \times 210}{300} = 91.00$$

$$E_{12} = \frac{130 \times 90}{300} = 39.00$$

$$E_{21} = \frac{170 \times 210}{300} = 119.00$$

$$E_{22} = \frac{170 \times 90}{300} = 51.00$$

**Step 3 — Computation Table:**

| Cell | $O_{ij}$ | $E_{ij}$ | $(O_{ij} - E_{ij})$ | $(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2$ | $\dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Male, Employed | 100 | 91.00 | $+9$ | 81 | 0.890 |
| Male, Unemployed | 30 | 39.00 | $-9$ | 81 | 2.077 |
| Female, Employed | 110 | 119.00 | $-9$ | 81 | 0.681 |
| Female, Unemployed | 60 | 51.00 | $+9$ | 81 | 1.588 |
| **Total** | 300 | 300 | | | **5.236** |

**Step 4 — Chi-Square Statistic:**

$$\chi^2_{computed} = 5.236$$

#### Part (b) — Degrees of freedom, critical value, and conclusion. (7 Marks)

**Step 5 — Degrees of Freedom:**

$$\nu = (r-1)(c-1) = (2-1)(2-1) = 1$$

**Step 6 — Critical Value at 1% level:**

$$\chi^2_{table}(1, 0.01) = 6.635$$

**Step 7 — Decision:**

Since $\chi^2_{computed} = 5.236 < 6.635 = \chi^2_{table}$, we **accept $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 1% level of significance, gender and employment status are **statistically independent** (no significant association exists).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $E_i \ge 5$:** Examiners deduct up to **1 mark** if you don't check this assumption.
> 2. **Wrong degrees of freedom:** For GOF use $\nu = k-1-m$ (not just $k-1$ if you estimated a parameter like $\lambda$ or $\mu$ from the sample).
> 3. **Misinterpreting 'Accept' vs 'Reject':** "Accept $H_0$" means "data does not provide enough evidence against $H_0$" — **not** that $H_0$ is proven true.
> 4. **Arithmetic errors in $E_{ij}$:** Always cross-verify that the sum of expected frequencies equals $N$.
> 5. **Using the wrong table:** Confirm whether the question demands 1%, 5%, or 10% significance level **before** looking up the table value.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Chi-Square Statistic:** Always $\chi^2 = \sum (O_i - E_i)^2/E_i$ — a non-negative quantity.
- **Two Main Applications:** (1) Goodness of Fit (one variable, theoretical distribution) and (2) Independence (two variables in contingency table).
- **Degrees of Freedom:**
  - GOF: $\nu = k - 1 - m$ (where $m$ = number of estimated parameters).
  - Independence: $\nu = (r-1)(c-1)$.
- **Expected Frequency Formula for Contingency Tables:** $E_{ij} = (R_i \times C_j)/N$ — derived from the independence assumption.
- **Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\chi^2_{computed} > \chi^2_{table}(\nu, \alpha)$.
- **Validity Assumption:** All $E_i \ge 5$; otherwise the chi-square approximation breaks down.
- **Yates' Correction:** Apply only to $2 \times 2$ tables with small samples.
- **Larger $\chi^2$ → Stronger evidence against $H_0$** (i.e., poorer fit / stronger association).
- **Smaller $\chi^2$ → Better fit / Weaker evidence of association.**
- **Always state hypotheses clearly** before computing — KTU examiners award marks for the formal setup.
- **Report the conclusion in context** of the problem (e.g., "the die is fair" not just "accept $H_0$").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meaning of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Meaning of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of KTU's **Engineering Statistics and Quality Control** framework, **Quality** is defined as the totality of characteristics of an entity (product, process, or service) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs. It is the conformance of a product or service to its design specifications and the degree to which it meets customer expectations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Standard Definition (ISO 9000:2015 aligned):**
> *Quality is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements.*

The term **"inherent"** (as opposed to "assigned") means that the characteristics exist within the product, process, or system itself. The term **"requirements"** refers to *stated needs* (codified in specifications) and *implied needs* (customer expectations).

### 1.2 Eminent Quality Gurus and Their Definitions (High-Yield for KTU)

The concept of quality has been formalized by several pioneers. The KTU board frequently tests these definitions for 3-mark direct questions:

| Guru / Authority | Definition of Quality | Key Emphasis |
|---|---|---|
| **Joseph M. Juran** | *Fitness for Use* | Customer satisfaction |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | *A predictable degree of uniformity and dependability* | Statistical control & consistency |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | *Conformance to Requirements* | Zero defects philosophy |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | *Total composite product & service characteristics* | Total Quality Control (TQC) |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | *Loss imparted to society from the time a product is shipped* | Quality loss to society |
| **Walter A. Shewhart** | *The goodness of a product* | Statistical quality foundations |
| **ISO 9000:2015** | *Degree to which inherent characteristics fulfil requirements* | Standards-based definition |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **🍽️ Restaurant Analogy — "A Perfect Cup of Coffee"**
>
> Imagine you walk into a café and order a cappuccino. **Quality**, in this context, means:
> - The coffee is served at the **right temperature** (≈ 60–65 °C).
> - The taste is **consistent** every single time you visit.
> - The cup is **clean, full, and well-presented**.
> - The barista **remembers your order** (meeting implied needs).
>
> If any of these elements fail (cold coffee, inconsistent taste, dirty cup), the **quality drops** — even if the café uses premium beans. **Quality is not about luxury; it is about reliability in meeting the promised experience.**

In manufacturing terms: a Rolls-Royce and a Maruti 800 are both of *high quality* if they each perform reliably according to their design — quality is independent of luxury.

### 1.4 Two Major Categories of Quality

- **Objective Quality** — Measurable, quantifiable characteristics (e.g., tensile strength, dimensions, defect rate, MTBF).
- **Subjective Quality** — Perceived, personal judgment (e.g., aesthetics, comfort, brand image).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always cite at least **two guru definitions** along with the ISO definition in 14-mark answers. This is a high-yield marking tactic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Multi-Dimensional Nature of Quality

Quality is **not a single attribute** — it is a multi-dimensional construct. **David A. Garvin** of Harvard Business School proposed **Eight Dimensions of Quality**, which form a core component of the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Quality (Memorize for KTU ESE):**
> 1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics.
> 2. **Features** — Secondary, supplementary characteristics.
> 3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over time.
> 4. **Conformance** — Degree to which design & operating characteristics match standards.
> 5. **Durability** — Length of useful life before replacement.
> 6. **Serviceability** — Speed, competence, and courtesy of repair.
> 7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound, taste).
> 8. **Perceived Quality** — Indirect quality measure (reputation, brand image).

### 2.2 Quality vs. Grade — A Critical Distinction

| Aspect | **Quality** | **Grade** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Degree of conformance to requirements | Category based on technical characteristics |
| **Nature** | Measurable performance level | Class designation |
| **Low value** | Inferior / defective | Lower-spec variant (not defective) |
| **Example** | A car door that does not close properly | A laptop with 8 GB vs 16 GB RAM |
| **Customer impact** | Always negative (unacceptable) | May be acceptable (lower-cost option) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** "Low grade" ≠ "Low quality." A low-grade product is not necessarily defective; a low-quality product is. KTU board questions often test this distinction.

### 2.3 Design Quality vs. Conformance Quality

- **Design Quality** — The inherent capability of the product as engineered (the "blueprint quality"). Determined *before* production.
- **Conformance Quality** — The degree to which the manufactured product matches its design specifications. Determined *during* and *after* production.

$$\text{Total Quality} = f(\text{Design Quality},\ \text{Conformance Quality})$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Expression | Application in Engineering |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ISO Definition | $Q = f(\text{Inherent Characteristics},\ \text{Requirements})$ | Standards-based compliance |
| 2 | Quality as Fitness for Use (Juran) | $Q_{\text{Juran}} = \text{Fitness for Use}$ | Customer-centric view |
| 3 | Taguchi Quality Loss Function | $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ | Quantifies societal loss from deviation $x$ from target $T$; $k$ is constant |
| 4 | Deming Chain Reaction | $\text{Quality Up} \rightarrow \text{Cost Down} \rightarrow \text{Productivity Up} \rightarrow \text{Price Down} \rightarrow \text{Market Share Up} \rightarrow \text{Jobs Up}$ | Strategic business linkage |
| 5 | Conformance Quality Index | $CQI = \dfrac{\text{Number conforming}}{\text{Total number inspected}} \times 100$ | Defect rate control |
| 6 | Six-Sigma Quality Level | $\text{DPMO} = 3.4 \text{ per million opportunities}$ | World-class benchmark |
| 7 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failure-free operation |
| 8 | Quality Dimensions (Garvin) | $Q = \sum_{i=1}^{8} w_i D_i$ | Weighted multi-dim quality index |

> [!NOTE]
> The Taguchi loss function $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$ is the most important formula relating to "meaning of quality" — it mathematically shows that **any deviation from target $T$ incurs loss to society**, redefining quality as loss-minimization rather than specification-compliance alone.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Drives SPC (Statistical Process Control) charts, Six-Sigma projects, and Kaizen initiatives.
- **Software Engineering:** Underpins ISO/IEC 25010 software quality models (functionality, reliability, usability, efficiency, maintainability, portability).
- **Civil Engineering:** Governs concrete strength conformity, structural safety margins, and durability specifications.
- **Service Industry:** Frames SERVQUAL dimensions (tangibles, reliability, responsiveness, assurance, empathy).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Taguchi Quality Loss Function (Conceptual)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Target Audience Note:** Although this is a conceptual topic, KTU 2024 awards high marks for students who can mathematically connect quality concepts. The Taguchi loss function is the flagship mathematical model.

**Step 1 — Define the Target Value**
Let $T$ be the nominal target value of a quality characteristic (e.g., shaft diameter, voltage output, viscosity).

**Step 2 — Define the Actual Measured Value**
Let $x$ be the actual measured value of the quality characteristic produced.

**Step 3 — Define Deviation from Target**
The deviation $\Delta$ from target is given by:

$$\Delta = x - T$$

**Step 4 — Recognize Loss is Proportional to Squared Deviation**
Taguchi postulated that the loss $L$ to society (in monetary units) increases as the **square of the deviation** — small deviations cost little, but losses escalate rapidly:

$$L(x) = k(x - T)^2$$

**Step 5 — Determine Constant $k$ Using a Known Failure Point**
Let $L_0$ be the loss when the product reaches the functional tolerance limit $\Delta_0$ (e.g., scrap/replace cost). Then:

$$L_0 = k \cdot \Delta_0^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Back into Loss Function**

$$L(x) = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2} \, (x - T)^2$$

**Step 7 — Average Loss Over a Production Batch**
For $n$ produced units, the average quality loss is:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} k(x_i - T)^2 = k \cdot \sigma^2 + k(\mu - T)^2$$

where $\mu$ is the process mean and $\sigma^2$ is the process variance.

**Step 8 — Engineering Interpretation**

$$\bar{L} = \underbrace{k(\mu - T)^2}_{\text{Loss due to bias (accuracy)}} + \underbrace{k\sigma^2}_{\text{Loss due to variability (precision)}}$$

This means **quality improvement has two simultaneous targets**:
1. Bring the process mean $\mu$ closer to target $T$ (eliminate bias).
2. Reduce process variability $\sigma^2$ (improve precision).

### 3.2 Worked Example: Applying the Taguchi Loss Function

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The target diameter of a steel shaft is $T = 50.00$ mm. The scrap/replacement loss is $L_0 = ₹400$ at a tolerance limit $\Delta_0 = \pm 0.5$ mm. A batch of 5 shafts yields measurements: 50.1, 49.8, 50.3, 49.9, 50.2 mm. Calculate (a) the constant $k$, and (b) the average quality loss.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate $k$**

$$k = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{400}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{400}{0.25} = 1600 \text{ ₹/mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate deviations from target**

$$x_1 - T = 50.1 - 50.0 = 0.1 \text{ mm}$$
$$x_2 - T = 49.8 - 50.0 = -0.2 \text{ mm}$$
$$x_3 - T = 50.3 - 50.0 = 0.3 \text{ mm}$$
$$x_4 - T = 49.9 - 50.0 = -0.1 \text{ mm}$$
$$x_5 - T = 50.2 - 50.0 = 0.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Calculate squared deviations**

$$(0.1)^2 = 0.01 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$(-0.2)^2 = 0.04 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$(0.3)^2 = 0.09 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$(-0.1)^2 = 0.01 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$(0.2)^2 = 0.04 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Sum and average**

$$\sum (x_i - T)^2 = 0.01 + 0.04 + 0.09 + 0.01 + 0.04 = 0.19 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\bar{L} = k \cdot \frac{\sum (x_i - T)^2}{n} = 1600 \times \frac{0.19}{5} = 1600 \times 0.038 = ₹60.8$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

Even though **all 5 shafts lie within the $\pm 0.5$ mm tolerance** (i.e., no rejects), the society still incurs a loss of ₹60.8 per batch. This vividly illustrates Taguchi's philosophy: **quality is loss-minimization, not just specification-compliance**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quality Conformance Index (CQI)

| Scenario | Units Inspected | Defects Found | CQI | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1000 | 5 | 99.5% | Acceptable in most industries |
| B | 1000 | 20 | 98.0% | Needs corrective action |
| C | 1,000,000 | 3.4 | 99.99966% | Six-Sigma quality |
| D | 1000 | 0 | 100% | Process under statistical control |

$$\text{CQI} = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units Inspected}}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Defects} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$$

### 3.4 Summary Logical Flow

1. Quality begins with a **target specification** ($T$).
2. Real processes produce **variation** around the target.
3. Any deviation $\Delta$ from target causes a **quadratic loss** $L = k\Delta^2$.
4. Total loss = **bias loss** + **variability loss**.
5. Therefore, *quality improvement* = **bias reduction** + **variation reduction**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Map of "Quality"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Quality Concept] --> B[Definitions]
    A --> C[Dimensions]
    A --> D[Categories]
    A --> E[Engineers View]
    A --> F[Mathematical Models]
    B --> B1[Juran - Fitness for Use]
    B --> B2[Deming - Uniformity and Dependability]
    B --> B3[Crosby - Conformance to Requirements]
    B --> B4[Taguchi - Loss to Society]
    B --> B5[ISO 9000 - Fulfilment of Requirements]
    C --> C1[Performance]
    C --> C2[Reliability]
    C --> C3[Durability]
    C --> C4[Aesthetics]
    C --> C5[Serviceability]
    C --> C6[Features]
    C --> C7[Conformance]
    C --> C8[Perceived Quality]
    D --> D1[Objective Quality]
    D --> D2[Subjective Quality]
    D --> D3[Design Quality]
    D --> D4[Conformance Quality]
    E --> E1[Quality vs Grade]
    E --> E2[Cost of Poor Quality]
    E --> E3[Customer Satisfaction]
    F --> F1[Taguchi Loss Function]
    F --> F2[Six Sigma DPMO]
    F --> F3[Reliability Function]
    F --> F4[Garvin Weighted Index]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Taguchi Quality Loss Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Target T] --> B[Best Quality - Minimum Loss]
    C[Deviation -d] --> D[Loss L = k x T minus d squared]
    E[Deviation +d] --> F[Loss L = k x T plus d squared]
    G[Tolerance Limit -L0] --> H[Maximum Tolerable Loss]
    I[Tolerance Limit +L0] --> J[Maximum Tolerable Loss]
    K[Process Variation] --> L[Squared Loss Curve - Parabolic]
    L --> M[Society bears the loss even within specs]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Deming Chain Reaction of Quality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Improve Quality] --> B[Costs Decrease]
    B --> C[Productivity Improves]
    C --> D[Prices Become Competitive]
    D --> E[Market Share Grows]
    E --> F[Business Stays in Business]
    F --> G[More Jobs Created]
    G --> H[Long-term Prosperity]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Quality vs Grade Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph QualityDimension[Quality Dimension]
        Q1[High Quality - Meets specs consistently]
        Q2[Low Quality - Fails specs, defective]
    end
    subgraph GradeDimension[Grade Dimension]
        G1[High Grade - Premium specs]
        G2[Low Grade - Economy specs, still conforming]
    end
    Q1 --> Comb1[Premium reliable product]
    Q2 --> Comb2[Defective unusable product]
    G1 --> Comb1
    G2 --> Comb1
    G1 --> Comb3[Economy conforming product]
    G2 --> Comb3
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Quality Management

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Needs - Stated and Implied] --> B[Quality Planning]
    B --> C[Design Quality - Blueprint Specifications]
    C --> D[Process Implementation]
    D --> E[Statistical Process Control SPC]
    E --> F{Conformance Check}
    F -->|Conforming| G[Conformance Quality Achieved]
    F -->|Non-conforming| H[Corrective Action CA]
    H --> D
    G --> I[Customer Satisfaction]
    I --> J[Feedback Loop for Continuous Improvement]
    J --> B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define quality according to **Joseph M. Juran** and **Philip B. Crosby**. Mention the central theme of each definition. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Juran's Definition:** Quality is **"Fitness for Use."** The central theme is *customer satisfaction* — a product is of high quality if it serves the customer's intended purpose reliably.
- **Crosby's Definition:** Quality is **"Conformance to Requirements"** (or specifications). The central theme is *zero-defect manufacturing* — quality is achieved when the product exactly matches its engineered specifications, every time.
- **Contrast:** Juran focuses on the *user's perspective*, while Crosby focuses on the *manufacturer's specifications*.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **Quality** and **Grade** with one engineering example for each. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Quality | Grade |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Degree of conformance to requirements | Category based on functional differences |
| Low value implies | Defective / unacceptable | Lower spec, but still usable |
| Example | A resistor of $100\,\Omega \pm 5\%$ that actually measures $120\,\Omega$ (low quality) | A $100\,\Omega$ vs $220\,\Omega$ resistor (different grades, both high quality) |
| Customer view | Always undesirable to be low | May be acceptable depending on need |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Internal Choice]**
> *Answer the following. Each sub-question carries 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** Explain the **Eight Dimensions of Quality** proposed by David A. Garvin with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Discuss **Juran's Trilogy of Quality** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement) and explain its relevance in modern manufacturing. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Quality classify how customers evaluate products:

1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics. *Example:* The mileage of a car (km/litre).
2. **Features** — Supplementary characteristics. *Example:* A smartphone with fingerprint sensor and wireless charging.
3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over time. *Example:* MTBF of an aircraft engine.
4. **Conformance** — Match with standards. *Example:* ISO 9001 certification of a factory.
5. **Durability** — Length of useful life. *Example:* A washing machine that lasts 10 years.
6. **Serviceability** — Ease of repair. *Example:* Modular laptop with replaceable RAM/SSD.
7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal. *Example:* Exterior design of a luxury sedan.
8. **Perceived Quality** — Indirect measure through brand reputation. *Example:* Consumer trust in Apple products.

> **Why this matters:** These 8 dimensions help engineers move beyond a single defect-rate view of quality and consider the **holistic customer experience**.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Juran's Trilogy** consists of three universal processes for managing quality:

1. **Quality Planning (QP):**
   - Identify *customers* and their *needs*.
   - Develop *product features* that respond to those needs.
   - Establish *quality goals* and *processes* capable of meeting them.
   - Transfer the plan to operating forces.

2. **Quality Control (QC):**
   - Evaluate *actual quality performance* against goals.
   - Compare actual with goals to identify *differences*.
   - Act on the differences (corrective action).

3. **Quality Improvement (QI):**
   - Establish infrastructure (council, teams, training).
   - Identify *projects* for improvement (chronic problems).
   - Establish *project teams* with clear goals.
   - Provide *resources*, motivation, and training.

> **Relevance in Modern Manufacturing:** Juran's Trilogy is the foundation of **Total Quality Management (TQM)** and **Six-Sigma DMAIC** methodology. It links quality to business strategy and ensures continuous improvement (Kaizen).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** State the **Taguchi Quality Loss Function**. Derive the expression for the constant $k$ and explain its significance with an example. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Deming Chain Reaction** in the context of quality improvement and discuss how it justifies organizational investment in quality. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

The Taguchi Quality Loss Function expresses the loss to society as a function of deviation from target:

$$L(x) = k(x - T)^2$$

where:
- $L(x)$ = Loss incurred at measured value $x$
- $T$ = Nominal target value
- $k$ = Quality loss coefficient (cost constant)
- $(x - T)$ = Deviation from target

**Derivation of $k$:**

At the functional tolerance limit $\Delta_0$, the loss equals the corrective/replacement cost $L_0$:

$$L_0 = k \cdot \Delta_0^2$$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2}}$$

**Worked Example:** A bolt has target diameter $T = 10.0$ mm. At the tolerance limit $\Delta_0 = \pm 0.2$ mm, replacement cost $L_0 = ₹80$.

$$k = \frac{80}{(0.2)^2} = \frac{80}{0.04} = 2000 \text{ ₹/mm}^2$$

For a bolt measuring 10.1 mm, the loss is:

$$L = 2000 \times (0.1)^2 = ₹20$$

**Significance:**
- **Even within specifications**, society bears a financial loss.
- Quality improvement means *minimizing $(x - T)^2$* for every unit.
- Drives the philosophy of **target-centered design** rather than specification-bound design.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Deming's Chain Reaction** is a sequence linking quality improvement to business prosperity:

> *Better Quality → Lower Costs → Higher Productivity → Competitive Prices → Increased Market Share → Business Survival → More Jobs → Long-term Prosperity*

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Better Quality** reduces rework, scrap, and warranty claims, **lowering production costs**.
2. **Lower costs** and smoother workflows **boost productivity**.
3. The company can offer **competitive prices** while maintaining margins.
4. Lower prices and better reputation **expand market share**.
5. With greater demand, the company **stays in business** and grows.
6. Growth translates to **more employment** and economic stability.
7. The cycle produces **long-term prosperity** for the company, employees, and society.

**Justification for Organizational Investment:**

- Quality is **not a cost center** but a **profit driver** and **survival strategy**.
- The chain reaction shows that **short-term quality investments** produce **long-term financial returns**.
- It justifies Total Quality Management (TQM), ISO 9000 certification, and Six-Sigma programs as **strategic business tools**, not merely compliance activities.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Marks Deduction Alert):**
> - Do **not** equate "Quality" with "Grade" — they are different concepts (loss of 2–3 marks).
> - Do **not** define quality only as "defect-free" — include customer-centric and societal-loss perspectives.
> - In 14-mark questions, **always include at least 3 guru definitions** for full credit.
> - For Taguchi loss function, **show the derivation of $k$** explicitly; merely stating the formula is insufficient.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 – Meaning of Quality)**

- **Quality** = degree to which inherent characteristics fulfil requirements (**ISO 9000:2015**).
- **Juran** = *Fitness for Use*; **Deming** = *Uniformity & Dependability*; **Crosby** = *Conformance to Requirements*; **Taguchi** = *Loss to Society*; **Feigenbaum** = *Total Composite Characteristics*.
- **Garvin's 8 Dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
- **Quality ≠ Grade** — Quality is conformance; Grade is classification.
- **Design Quality** = inherent capability from engineering; **Conformance Quality** = match between actual product and design.
- **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ where $k = \dfrac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2}$.
- **Average Loss:** $\bar{L} = k(\mu - T)^2 + k\sigma^2$ (bias loss + variability loss).
- **Deming Chain Reaction:** Quality Up → Cost Down → Productivity Up → Price Down → Market Share Up → Jobs Up.
- **Six-Sigma Quality:** 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO).
- **Objective Quality** is measurable; **Subjective Quality** is perceptual.
- Quality drives **SPC, TQM, Six-Sigma, Kaizen, ISO 9001** — all build on this foundational concept.
- **Conformance Quality Index:** $\text{CQI} = \left(1 - \dfrac{D}{N}\right) \times 100\%$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variables and Attributes Data: Benefits of Control Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Variables and Attributes Data: Benefits of Control Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Variables Data — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Variables Data)**
> Variables data (also called *measurement data* or *continuous data*) are quality characteristics that can be **measured on a continuous numerical scale**. They can take any real value within a range, including fractional values, and arise from instruments such as micrometers, calipers, pressure gauges, voltmeters, and weighing balances.

**Examples in Engineering Practice:**
- Diameter of a piston (in mm)
- Tensile strength of a steel rod (in MPa)
- Surface roughness (in µm Ra)
- Viscosity of an oil (in cP)
- Cycle time of a manufacturing process (in seconds)

The defining feature is that you obtain a **number with a unit** by direct measurement.

### 1.2 Attributes Data — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Attributes Data)**
> Attributes data (also called *count data* or *discrete/classification data*) are quality characteristics expressed as **counts or classifications** rather than measurements. They fall into two strict sub-categories — *defectives* (the item is bad) and *defects* (the item has flaws).

**Examples in Engineering Practice:**
- Number of defective bolts in a lot of 200 → **defective** item
- Number of solder joints failing inspection per PCB → **defect** count
- Number of paint blemishes on a car door → **defect** count
- Pass/Fail classification of a voltage test → **defective** item

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "School Examination" Intuition

Imagine you are a school principal evaluating student performance:

- **Variables Data is like grading a Math test on a 0–100 scale.** You record the exact score (e.g., 87.5). The richer the measurement, the more statistical power you have. A control chart on these scores would be the **X-bar and R chart**.
- **Attributes Data is like grading a project as Pass/Fail.** You only count how many students passed. The control chart here would be a **p-chart (for proportion)** or **np-chart (for count)**.
- A third attributes form, like counting spelling mistakes in an essay, would use a **c-chart (defects per unit)** or **u-chart (defects per opportunity)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:** *If you can put a ruler, scale, or measuring instrument on it → **Variables**. If you must classify or count → **Attributes**.*

### 1.4 Benefits of Control Charts — Core Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Benefit Statement)**
> Control charts are powerful statistical process control (SPC) tools that provide a graphical depiction of process behaviour over time, enabling engineers to **distinguish random (common-cause) variation from assignable (special-cause) variation**, thereby driving continuous quality improvement.

**The 7 Cardinal Benefits (KTU Favourite List):**

1. **Process Monitoring** — A continuous "health check" of the process.
2. **Detection of Assignable Causes** — Distinguishes special causes from background noise.
3. **Reduction in Inspection Cost** — Less reliance on 100% final inspection.
4. **Quality Improvement Tool** — Provides data for reducing process variation.
5. **Process Capability Estimation** — Foundation for Cp, Cpk calculations.
6. **Communication Device** — A common language between operators, engineers, and management.
7. **Decision-Making Aid** — Triggers corrective action only when truly needed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Tree of Quality Data

```
Quality Data
├── Variables (Measurement, Continuous)
│   ├── Individual values        → I-MR chart (Individuals & Moving Range)
│   ├── Subgroup Averages        → X-bar chart
│   └── Subgroup Variability     → R-chart (Range) or S-chart (Std. Dev.)
│
└── Attributes (Classification, Count)
    ├── Defective (Item is Good/Bad)
    │   ├── Variable sample size n → p-chart (proportion defective)
    │   └── Constant sample size n → np-chart (number defective)
    └── Defects (Number of Flaws per Unit)
        ├── Constant inspection unit → c-chart
        └── Variable unit/opportunity → u-chart
```

### 2.2 Variables Control Charts — Logic Steps

1. **Collect subgroup samples** of size $n$ (typically $n = 4$ or $n = 5$ for X-bar/R).
2. **Compute the subgroup mean** $\bar{X}_i$ and the subgroup range $R_i$.
3. **Compute the grand mean** $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and the average range $\bar{R}$.
4. **Estimate $\sigma$** indirectly using $\bar{R}/d_2$, where $d_2$ depends on subgroup size $n$.
5. **Set Control Limits at $\mu \pm 3\sigma$** (Shewhart's principle).
6. **Plot and interpret** — investigate any point outside limits or non-random patterns.

**Core Equations for X-bar and R Chart:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$$

$$\text{Center Line}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

$$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \quad LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$$

### 2.3 Attributes Control Charts — Logic Steps

1. **Inspect a sample** of size $n$ and count defectives (or defects).
2. **Compute proportion** $\bar{p} = \sum d / \sum n$ (or $\bar{c}$, $\bar{u}$).
3. **Apply the binomial/Poisson model** appropriate to the data.
4. **Set control limits at $\pm 3\sigma$** using the standard error of the statistic.
5. **Plot successive samples** and interpret using Western Electric / Nelson rules.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — KTU Module 3 (Variables & Attributes)**

| Chart Type | Data | Center Line | UCL | LCL | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **X-bar** | Variables (means) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | $A_2$ from table of $n$ |
| **R** | Variables (ranges) | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ | $D_3=0$ for $n\le 6$ |
| **p** | Attributes (proportion defective) | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | Variable $n$ allowed |
| **np** | Attributes (number defective) | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | Constant $n$ |
| **c** | Attributes (defects per unit) | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Poisson model |
| **u** | Attributes (defects per opportunity) | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | Variable $n$ allowed |

**Standard Factors (KTU Frequently Tested):**

| $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Automotive**: p-charts track defective parts per shift; X-bar/R charts track piston diameter tolerance.
- **Electronics**: c-charts track solder defects per PCB; X-bar charts control wave-solder temperature.
- **Pharmaceutical**: u-charts track tablet-coating defects per batch.
- **Civil Engineering**: X-bar/R charts monitor concrete cube compressive strength.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: X-bar Chart Control Limits from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ starting from the Shewhart $3\sigma$ principle.

**Step 1 — Define the standard error of the subgroup mean.**
When subgroups of size $n$ are drawn from a process with standard deviation $\sigma$, the standard deviation of $\bar{X}$ is given by:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Relate $\sigma$ to the average range $\bar{R}$.**
For a normal distribution, the expected value of the range of $n$ observations is $d_2 \sigma$, so:

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the estimator of $\sigma$ into $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$.**

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Shewhart $3\sigma$ principle.**
The control limits for $\bar{X}$ are set at the grand mean $\pm 3$ standard errors:

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3 \cdot \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 5 — Group the constants to define $A_2$.**

$$A_2 = \frac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}}$$

**Numerical Verification for $n = 5$:**

$$A_2 = \frac{3}{2.326 \cdot \sqrt{5}} = \frac{3}{2.326 \times 2.2361} = \frac{3}{5.2012} = 0.5768 \approx 0.577$$

This matches the standard table value — derivation is **mathematically exact**.

### 3.2 Derivation: p-Chart Control Limits

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show why the p-chart uses $\bar{p} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$.

**Step 1 — Each inspected item is a Bernoulli trial.**
Each item is classified as defective (1) with probability $p$ or good (0) with probability $1-p$. The number of defectives $d$ in a sample of $n$ follows a Binomial distribution:

$$d \sim B(n, p)$$

**Step 2 — Mean and variance of the proportion $p = d/n$.**

$$E[p] = p, \quad \text{Var}(p) = \frac{p(1-p)}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Standard error of the proportion.**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

**Step 4 — Estimate $p$ from the data using the pooled proportion $\bar{p}$.**

$$\bar{p} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Shewhart $3\sigma$ limits around the centre line $\bar{p}$.**

$$UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$$

$$LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$$

**Step 6 — Edge-case rules (KTU exam favourite).**
- If $LCL < 0$, set $LCL = 0$ (proportion cannot be negative).
- For variable $n$, recompute limits separately for each sample (or use standardized residuals $z_i = (p_i - \bar{p})/\sigma_p$).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — p-Chart

**Problem:** In 25 samples of size 50, total defectives observed = 75. Construct the p-chart.

**Solution:**

$$\bar{p} = \frac{75}{25 \times 50} = \frac{75}{1250} = 0.06$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{0.06 \times 0.94}{50}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0564}{50}} = \sqrt{0.001128} = 0.0336$$

$$UCL_p = 0.06 + 3(0.0336) = 0.06 + 0.1008 = 0.1608$$

$$LCL_p = 0.06 - 0.1008 = -0.0408 \implies 0 \text{ (since } p \ge 0\text{)}$$

**Interpretation:** As long as the sample proportion lies between 0 and 0.1608 (16.08%), the process is *in statistical control*. Any point above 0.1608 indicates an **assignable cause** requiring investigation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Correct Control Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Quality Data Collected] --> B{Continuous or Discrete?}
    B -- Continuous Measurement --> C[Variables Data]
    B -- Count or Classification --> D[Attributes Data]
    C --> C1{Subgroup size n}
    C1 -- n = 1 --> C2[I-MR Chart]
    C1 -- n between 2 and 10 --> C3[X-bar and R Chart]
    D --> D1{Defectives or Defects}
    D1 -- Defective per item --> D2{Variable n}
    D1 -- Defects per unit --> D3{Variable n}
    D2 -- Yes --> D4[p-chart]
    D2 -- No --> D5[np-chart]
    D3 -- Yes --> D6[u-chart]
    D3 -- No --> D7[c-chart]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Statistical Process Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
