# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $P_a$ Using the Binomial Distribution

**Assumption:** The lot is large enough ($N \geq 10n$) that sampling without replacement behaves like sampling with replacement. Hence, each draw is an independent Bernoulli trial with probability $p$ of being defective.

**The probability of exactly $d$ defectives in $n$ trials:**

$$
P(X = d) = \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

**Probability of acceptance (at most $c$ defectives):**

$$
P_a = P(X \leq c) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** A lot is accepted if we observe $0, 1, 2, \ldots, c$ defectives. Each of these mutually exclusive events is summed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Poisson Approximation

**Starting point:** The Binomial PMF. When $n$ is large and $p$ is small (the classic "rare event" scenario), use the limit $np = \lambda$ (a constant mean).

$$
P(X = d) = \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

**Step 1:** As $n \to \infty$ with $np = \lambda$:

$$
(1-p)^{n-d} \to e^{-np} = e^{-\lambda}
$$

**Step 2:** Also,

$$
\binom{n}{d} p^{d} = \dfrac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-d+1)}{d!} p^{d} \to \dfrac{\lambda^{d}}{d!}
$$

**Step 3:** Combining, the probability of acceptance becomes:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Rule:** Use Poisson approximation when $n \geq 50$ and $p \leq 0.10$. This is extremely common in KTU problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Normal Approximation (with Continuity Correction)

**Starting point:** For a Binomial random variable $X \sim B(n, p)$:

$$
\mu = np, \qquad \sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}
$$

**Standardization:** For $X \leq c$ (discrete), we apply a **+0.5 continuity correction** (going to the next integer boundary):

$$
Z = \dfrac{X - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}} \approx \dfrac{c + 0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}}
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
P_a = P(X \leq c) = \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{c + 0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}}\right)
$$

where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal CDF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Rule:** Normal approximation is valid when **both** $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$. KTU frequently tests this.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of AOQ and AOQL

**Scenario:** Lots are accepted with probability $P_a$ and rejected with probability $(1 - P_a)$. Rejected lots are assumed to be **100% inspected** and all defectives are replaced (rectification inspection).

**Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ):** The expected fraction defective in the outgoing product (accepted + rectified lots).

**Reasoning:**
- In an accepted lot, $np$ defectives escape on average (out of $N$ items).
- In a rejected lot, all $Np$ defectives are removed and replaced. So **0** defectives remain in the outgoing shipment.
- Probability that a random lot is accepted = $P_a$.

**Effective fraction defective in accepted lots only:**

$$
\text{AOQ} = P_a \cdot p \cdot \dfrac{N - n}{N}
$$

**The factor $(N - n)/N$** accounts for the fact that the $n$ inspected items in an accepted lot are replaced by good items (assuming rectification).

**Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL):**

$$
\text{AOQL} = \max_{0 \leq p \leq 1} \text{AOQ}(p)
$$

This is the **worst-case average** outgoing quality across all possible incoming qualities. A plan is considered good if its AOQL is small.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Average Total Inspection (ATI)

**Components of inspection per lot:**
- Every accepted lot: $n$ items inspected.
- Every rejected lot: $n$ items inspected in the sample **plus** $(N - n)$ items in the 100% screening of rejected lots.

**Average per lot:**

$$
\text{ATI} = n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a) = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)
$$

**Simplification:**

$$
\text{ATI} = n + (N - n)(1 - P_a)
$$

> [!TIP]
> ATI is used to compare the **economic efficiency** of different sampling plans. A plan with lower ATI is cheaper to operate.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 1 (KTU Typical — Poisson)

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A single sampling plan with $n = 100$ and $c = 2$ is used. Find the probability of acceptance if the lot has 3% defectives. Use the Poisson approximation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the parameter $\lambda = np$.

$$
\lambda = 100 \times 0.03 = 3
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Poisson formula for $P_a = P(d \leq 2)$:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \dfrac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^{d}}{d!}
$$

**Step 3:** Expand the summation:

$$
P_a = \dfrac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^{0}}{0!} + \dfrac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^{1}}{1!} + \dfrac{e^{-3} \cdot 3^{2}}{2!}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $e^{-3} = 0.049787$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= 0.049787 \times 1 + 0.049787 \times 3 + 0.049787 \times 4.5 \\
&= 0.049787 + 0.149361 + 0.224041 \\
&= 0.423189
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Conclude:

$$
\boxed{P_a \approx 0.4232}
$$

**Interpretation:** A lot with 3% defectives has only a **42.32% chance** of being accepted by this plan. Not very protective of the consumer.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example 2 (KTU Typical — Normal Approximation)

> **Problem (KTU Style):** For a single sampling plan with $n = 200$ and $c = 4$, find the probability of acceptance when $p = 0.03$ using the normal approximation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $\mu$ and $\sigma$:

$$
\mu = np = 200 \times 0.03 = 6
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)} = \sqrt{200 \times 0.03 \times 0.97} = \sqrt{5.82} \approx 2.4125
$$

**Step 2:** Apply continuity correction. We want $P(X \leq 4)$, so we use $c + 0.5 = 4.5$:

$$
Z = \dfrac{4.5 - 6}{2.4125} = \dfrac{-1.5}{2.4125} = -0.6217
$$

**Step 3:** Look up in the standard normal table:

$$
P(Z \leq -0.62) = 0.2676
$$

**Step 4:** Conclude:

$$
\boxed{P_a \approx 0.2676}
$$

---

## 3.8 Worked Example 3 (KTU Design Problem)

> **Problem (KTU Style):** Design a single sampling plan with $p_1 = 0.02$ (AQL), $\alpha = 0.05$, $p_2 = 0.08$ (LTPD), $\beta = 0.10$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** For $P_a = 0.95$ at AQL, the standard normal gives $Z_{0.95} = 1.645$:

$$
c + 0.5 - n p_1 = 1.645 \sqrt{n p_1 (1 - p_1)}
$$

**Substituting** $p_1 = 0.02$:

$$
c + 0.5 = 0.02 n + 1.645 \sqrt{0.02 \times 0.98 \, n}
$$

**Step 2:** For $P_a = 0.10$ at LTPD, $Z_{0.10} = -1.645$:

$$
c + 0.5 - n p_2 = -1.645 \sqrt{n p_2 (1 - p_2)}
$$

**Substituting** $p_2 = 0.08$:

$$
c + 0.5 = 0.08 n - 1.645 \sqrt{0.08 \times 0.92 \, n}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for $c + 0.5$:

$$
0.02 n + 1.645 \sqrt{0.0196 \, n} = 0.08 n - 1.645 \sqrt{0.0736 \, n}
$$

**Step 4:** Collect $n$ terms:

$$
0.06 n = 1.645 \left( \sqrt{0.0196 \, n} + \sqrt{0.0736 \, n} \right)
$$

$$
0.06 n = 1.645 \sqrt{n} \left( \sqrt{0.0196} + \sqrt{0.0736} \right)
$$

$$
0.06 n = 1.645 \sqrt{n} \left( 0.14 + 0.2713 \right)
$$

$$
0.06 n = 1.645 \sqrt{n} \times 0.4113
$$

$$
0.06 n = 0.6766 \sqrt{n}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $n$:

$$
\sqrt{n} = \dfrac{0.6766}{0.06} = 11.277
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 127.17 \approx 128}
$$

**Step 6:** Find $c$ from equation 1:

$$
c + 0.5 = 0.02(128) + 1.645 \sqrt{0.0196 \times 128}
$$

$$
c + 0.5 = 2.56 + 1.645 \sqrt{2.5088}
$$

$$
c + 0.5 = 2.56 + 1.645 \times 1.584 = 2.56 + 2.606 = 5.166
$$

$$
\boxed{c = 4.666 \approx 5}
$$

**Final Plan:** $\mathbf{(n = 128, \ c = 5)}$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single Sampling Plan Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot of Size N Submitted] --> B[Draw Random Sample of Size n]
    B --> C[Inspect Each Item in Sample]
    C --> D[Count Defectives d]
    D --> E{Is d less than or equal to c}
    E -- YES --> F[ACCEPT the Lot]
    E -- NO --> G[REJECT the Lot]
    F --> H[Lot Sent to Customer]
    G --> I[100 Percent Rectification or Return]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Double Sampling Plan Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot of Size N] --> B[Draw Sample n1]
    B --> C[Count Defectives d1]
    C --> D{Is d1 less than or equal to c1}
    D -- YES --> E[ACCEPT the Lot]
    D -- NO --> F{Is d1 greater than c2}
    F -- YES --> G[REJECT the Lot]
    F -- NO --> H[Draw Second Sample n2]
    H --> I[Count Total Defectives d1 plus d2]
    I --> J{Is total less than or equal to c2}
    J -- YES --> K[ACCEPT the Lot]
    J -- NO --> L[REJECT the Lot]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Operating Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Producer's Risk alpha] --> OQ1[Point P1 at AQL]
    P2[Consumer's Risk beta] --> OQ2[Point P2 at LTPD]
    OQ1 --> OC[OC Curve of Plan]
    OQ2 --> OC
    OC --> Y[Probability of Acceptance Pa]
    X[Lot Quality p fraction defective] --> OC
    OC --> A[High Pa Region good lots accepted]
    OC --> B[Low Pa Region bad lots rejected]
    OC --> C[Transition Zone Indifference Region]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Role of Each Risk

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Submitted Lot with quality p] --> Q{Quality p compared to p1 AQL}
    Q -- p less than p1 GOOD --> R1[Producer expects ACCEPTANCE]
    R1 --> S1{But plan REJECTS it}
    S1 -- PROBABILITY alpha --> X1[Producer Loses Money]
    Q -- p greater than p2 BAD --> R2[Consumer expects REJECTION]
    R2 --> S2{But plan ACCEPTS it}
    S2 -- PROBABILITY beta --> X2[Consumer Receives Defectives]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Procedure to Design a Sampling Plan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given p1, alpha, p2, beta] --> B[Set up Two Normal Equations]
    B --> C[Equation 1: Pa equals 0.95 at p1]
    B --> D[Equation 2: Pa equals 0.10 at p2]
    C --> E[Equation uses Z equals 1.645]
    D --> F[Equation uses Z equals minus 1.645]
    E --> G[Eliminate c to solve for n]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Substitute n back to find c]
    H --> I[Round c to nearest integer]
    I --> J[Final Plan: n and c declared]
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Comparison of Plan Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Sampling Plans Family] --> S1[Single Plan]
    SP --> S2[Double Plan]
    SP --> S3[Multiple Plan]
    SP --> S4[Sequential Plan]
    S1 --> A1[Simpler design]
    S1 --> A2[One decision point]
    S2 --> B1[Two samples possible]
    S2 --> B2[Lower ATI on average]
    S3 --> C1[Three to five stages]
    S3 --> C2[Most economical for large lots]
    S4 --> D1[Item by item inspection]
    S4 --> D2[Lowest ATI but complex]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define an acceptance sampling plan. List the parameters of a single sampling plan.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Acceptance Sampling Plan:** It is a pre-determined statistical procedure in which a random sample is drawn from a lot, and based on the number of defectives found in the sample, a decision is made to either accept or reject the entire lot.

> **Parameters of a Single Sampling Plan:**
> 1. **Lot size ($N$):** Total number of items in the lot.
> 2. **Sample size ($n$):** Number of items randomly drawn from the lot.
> 3. **Acceptance number ($c$):** The maximum number of defectives allowed in the sample for the lot to be accepted.

> **Decision Rule:** If number of defectives $d \leq c$, accept the lot; if $d > c$, reject the lot.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
**Explain the terms AQL, LTPD, Producer's Risk and Consumer's Risk with reference to an OC curve.**

**Model Answer:**

> **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level) $p_1$:** The maximum fraction of defectives that can be considered satisfactory. At AQL, the probability of acceptance is high (typically 0.95).
>
> **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) $p_2$:** The maximum fraction of defectives that the consumer is willing to tolerate. At LTPD, the probability of acceptance is low (typically 0.10).
>
> **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$):** The probability of rejecting a good lot (with quality $p_1$). It is the area under the OC curve's tail beyond $p_1$.
>
> **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$):** The probability of accepting a bad lot (with quality $p_2$). It is the value of the OC curve at $p_2$.
>
> **OC Curve:** A plot of $P_a$ (Y-axis) versus lot quality $p$ (X-axis), showing how a plan discriminates between good and bad lots.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> **A manufacturer uses a single sampling plan with $n = 80$ and $c = 2$. Lots of size $N = 2000$ are submitted. Use the Poisson approximation to determine:**
> **(a)** The probability of acceptance when the incoming lot quality is $p = 0.04$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The AOQ and AOQL for this plan. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Given:** $n = 80$, $c = 2$, $p = 0.04$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\lambda = np$:

$$
\lambda = 80 \times 0.04 = 3.2
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Poisson formula for $P_a = P(d \leq 2)$:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{2} \dfrac{e^{-3.2} \cdot (3.2)^{d}}{d!}
$$

**Step 3:** Use $e^{-3.2} = 0.04076$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= 0.04076 \times \dfrac{(3.2)^0}{0!} + 0.04076 \times \dfrac{(3.2)^1}{1!} + 0.04076 \times \dfrac{(3.2)^2}{2!} \\
&= 0.04076 \times 1 + 0.04076 \times 3.2 + 0.04076 \times 5.12 \\
&= 0.04076 + 0.13044 + 0.20870 \\
&= 0.37990
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_a \approx 0.3799}
$$

#### **Solution to (b):**

**General formula for AOQ:**

$$
\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
$$

**For $p = 0.04$:**

$$
\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{0.3799 \times 0.04 \times (2000 - 80)}{2000} = \dfrac{0.3799 \times 0.04 \times 1920}{2000}
$$

$$
\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{29.176}{2000} = 0.01459
$$

**For AOQL:** Compute AOQ for various values of $p$ to find the maximum.

| $p$ | $\lambda = np$ | $P_a$ | AOQ = $P_a \cdot p \cdot (1920/2000)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.01 | 0.8 | $\approx 0.9526$ | $0.9526 \times 0.01 \times 0.96 = 0.00914$ |
| 0.02 | 1.6 | $\approx 0.7834$ | $0.7834 \times 0.02 \times 0.96 = 0.01504$ |
| 0.03 | 2.4 | $\approx 0.5697$ | $0.5697 \times 0.03 \times 0.96 = 0.01641$ |
| 0.04 | 3.2 | $\approx 0.3799$ | $0.3799 \times 0.04 \times 0.96 = 0.01459$ |
| 0.05 | 4.0 | $\approx 0.2381$ | $0.2381 \times 0.05 \times 0.96 = 0.01143$ |

**The maximum AOQ occurs near $p = 0.03$:**

$$
\boxed{\text{AOQL} \approx 0.0164 \ \text{or about } 1.64\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget the factor $(N - n)/N$** in the AOQ formula. Marks are deducted for this. Always include the rectification factor.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **Design a single sampling plan for the following specifications:**
> **AQL = 0.01**, $\alpha = 0.05$, **LTPD = 0.05**, $\beta = 0.10$.
>
> **(a)** Find the sample size $n$ and acceptance number $c$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Calculate the ATI when $N = 2000$ and $p = 0.02$ using the obtained plan. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** Set up the two normal equations. At $p_1 = 0.01$ with $P_a = 0.95$:

$$
c + 0.5 = 0.01n + 1.645 \sqrt{0.01 \times 0.99 \cdot n} = 0.01n + 1.645 \sqrt{0.0099 \, n}
$$

**Step 2:** At $p_2 = 0.05$ with $P_a = 0.10$:

$$
c + 0.5 = 0.05n - 1.645 \sqrt{0.05 \times 0.95 \cdot n} = 0.05n - 1.645 \sqrt{0.0475 \, n}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions:

$$
0.01n + 1.645 \sqrt{0.0099 \, n} = 0.05n - 1.645 \sqrt{0.0475 \, n}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange:

$$
0.04n = 1.645 \left( \sqrt{0.0099 \, n} + \sqrt{0.0475 \, n} \right)
$$

$$
0.04n = 1.645 \sqrt{n} \left( 0.0995 + 0.2179 \right) = 1.645 \sqrt{n} \times 0.3174
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $n$:

$$
0.04 \sqrt{n} = 1.645 \times 0.3174 = 0.5221
$$

$$
\sqrt{n} = \dfrac{0.5221}{0.04} = 13.05
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 170.4 \approx 170}
$$

**Step 6:** Find $c$:

$$
c + 0.5 = 0.01(170) + 1.645 \sqrt{0.0099 \times 170}
$$

$$
c + 0.5 = 1.7 + 1.645 \times \sqrt{1.683} = 1.7 + 1.645 \times 1.297 = 1.7 + 2.134 = 3.834
$$

$$
\boxed{c = 3.334 \approx 3}
$$

**Final Plan:** $\mathbf{(n = 170, \ c = 3)}$

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Find $P_a$ at $p = 0.02$ using Poisson (since $np = 3.4$ and $n = 170$ is large).

$$
\lambda = np = 170 \times 0.02 = 3.4
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $P_a = P(d \leq 3)$ with $e^{-3.4} = 0.03337$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= 0.03337 \times \left(1 + 3.4 + \dfrac{3.4^2}{2} + \dfrac{3.4^3}{6}\right) \\
&= 0.03337 \times (1 + 3.4 + 5.78 + 6.546) \\
&= 0.03337 \times 16.726 \\
&= 0.5582
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply ATI formula:

$$
\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n) = 170 + (1 - 0.5582)(2000 - 170)
$$

$$
\text{ATI} = 170 + 0.4418 \times 1830 = 170 + 808.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{ATI} \approx 978.5 \ \text{items per lot}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Sign Error:** Using $+1.645$ for the LTPD equation instead of $-1.645$ (because $P_a = 0.10$ corresponds to $Z = -1.645$, and you want $c + 0.5 - np$ to be negative).
> 2. **Rounding Error:** Students often round $c$ to the nearest integer without showing the calculation. KTU expects the **work**, not just the answer.
> 3. **Forgetting the continuity correction** ($+0.5$) — this is a 1-mark penalty per occurrence.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision checklist before the KTU exam.**

### ✅ Core Concepts Checklist

- [x] A **Single Sampling Plan** is fully defined by **three parameters**: $\mathbf{(N, n, c)}$.
- [x] **Decision rule:** Accept if $d \leq c$, Reject if $d > c$.
- [x] **OC Curve** plots $P_a$ (Y) vs $p$ (X). Convex, falling curve.
- [x] **AQL ($p_1$)** is associated with **Producer's Risk $\alpha = 0.05$**.
- [x] **LTPD ($p_2$)** is associated with **Consumer's Risk $\beta = 0.10$**.
- [x] **AOQ** measures outgoing quality; **AOQL** is its worst-case (maximum) value.
- [x] **ATI** measures the average number of items inspected per lot.
- [x] **Double Plan** has parameters $\mathbf{(N, n_1, c_1, n_2, c_2)}$.

### ✅ Formula Memorization Checklist

- [x] $P_a$ (Binomial) = $\sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}$
- [x] $P_a$ (Poisson) = $\sum_{d=0}^{c} e^{-np} (np)^d / d!$
- [x] $P_a$ (Normal) = $\Phi\left(\dfrac{c + 0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}}\right)$
- [x] $\text{AOQ} = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n) / N$
- [x] $\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$
- [x] **Standard $Z$-values:** $Z_{0.95} = 1.645$, $Z_{0.10} = -1.645$.

### ✅ When to Use Which Approximation

| Condition | Use This |
|---|---|
| $N$ small, $n/N > 0.1$ | Hypergeometric (exact) |
| $N \geq 10n$ | Binomial |
| $n \geq 50$, $p \leq 0.10$ | Poisson |
| $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$ | Normal |

### ✅ Common KTU Numerical Setup Pattern

> Most 14-mark design problems follow this template:
> 1. **Given:** AQL $= p_1$, $\alpha = 0.05$, LTPD $= p_2$, $\beta = 0.10$.
> 2. **Find:** $n$ and $c$.
> 3. **Method:** Set up the two normal equations, equate, solve for $n$, back-substitute for $c$.

### ✅ Memory Anchors (Mnemonics)

- **AQL = "Acceptable" = Producer's friend** → high $P_a$ (0.95).
- **LTPD = "Limit of Tolerance" = Consumer's demand** → low $P_a$ (0.10).
- **1.645** = $Z_{0.95}$ = "the magic number" in KTU problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always **draw the OC curve** (even a rough sketch) in design problems. Examiners give partial credit for visualization even if numbers are slightly off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Probability of Acceptance by These Sampling Plans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Determination of Probability of Acceptance by These Sampling Plans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Acceptance Sampling** under Statistical Quality Control (SQC), the **Probability of Acceptance** (denoted as **$P_a$** or **$L(p)$**) is defined as the probability that a submitted lot of finite size **$N$** containing a fraction non-conforming **$p$** (or proportion defective) will be **accepted** by the chosen sampling plan when a random sample of size **$n$** is drawn and inspected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The Probability of Acceptance $P_a$ is the likelihood that the number of defectives $d$ observed in the sample does not exceed the acceptance number $c$ of the sampling plan, i.e., $P_a = P(d \leq c)$. It is a **monotonically decreasing function** of the true incoming lot quality $p$.

The function $L(p)$ that maps every possible lot quality $p \in [0, 1]$ to its corresponding probability of acceptance is graphically represented by the **Operating Characteristic Curve (OC Curve)**, which is the heart of any acceptance sampling plan.

## 1.2 Underlying Probability Distributions Used

Depending on the lot size $N$, sample size $n$, and the sampling methodology, $P_a$ is computed using one of the following distributions:

| Distribution | Condition of Applicability |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hypergeometric Distribution** | $N$ finite, $n/N \geq 0.1$ (sampling without replacement) |
| **Binomial Distribution** | $N$ large / infinite, $n/N \leq 0.1$, $p$ constant |
| **Poisson Distribution** | Binomial with $n$ large and $p$ small (np ≤ 5 approx) |
| **Normal Distribution** | Asymptotic approximation to Binomial/Poisson for large $n$ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Border Checkpoint" Model

Imagine a customs officer at an international border. The officer cannot inspect every traveller (this would be 100% inspection), so he inspects a **small random sample** of size $n$ from a bus carrying $N$ passengers.

- If the sample contains **0 or 1 "smugglers"** (defectives), the entire bus is waved through (**Lot Accepted**).
- If the sample contains **2 or more smugglers**, the bus is sent back (**Lot Rejected**).

The "Probability of Acceptance" is the chance that, on any given day, a bus containing a *fixed hidden proportion* of smugglers will pass the checkpoint undetected. As the *true* hidden fraction of smugglers increases, the officer's chance of catching at least one in his sample rises, and the chance of acceptance **falls**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> The OC curve never reaches 0 or 1. There is *always* a non-zero risk of accepting a bad lot (Consumer's Risk $\beta$) and rejecting a good lot (Producer's Risk $\alpha$). The goal of plan design is to *shape* this curve favourably.

## 1.4 Standard KTU Metrics to Remember

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** The poorest lot quality considered tolerable, with $P_a = 1 - \alpha$.
- **LTPD / RQL (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective):** The worst quality that the consumer is willing to tolerate, with $P_a = \beta$.
- **$\alpha$ (Producer's Risk):** Probability of rejecting a *good* lot.
- **$\beta$ (Consumer's Risk):** Probability of accepting a *bad* lot.
- **AOQL (Average Outgoing Quality Limit):** Maximum average quality after rectification.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU problems, the most frequent ask is: *"Given $N$, $n$, $c$, and $p$, determine $P_a$."* This is computed using the **Binomial distribution** in the vast majority of exam questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Probability of Acceptance

The general procedure for determining $P_a$ is built on three pillars:

### Pillar 1 — The Acceptance Criterion
A lot is **accepted** if and only if the number of defectives $d$ in the sample is less than or equal to the acceptance number $c$:

$$d \leq c$$

### Pillar 2 — Probability Computation
The probability of this event is summed over all possible $d$ values from $0$ to $c$:

$$P_a = P(d = 0) + P(d = 1) + P(d = 2) + \cdots + P(d = c)$$

### Pillar 3 — Distribution Selection
The $P(d = x)$ term is computed from the appropriate probability mass function (PMF), governed by the conditions stated in Section 1.2.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic for the Single Sampling Plan

A **Single Sampling Plan** is characterised by the triple $(N, n, c)$. The stepwise logical flow to determine $P_a$ is:

1. **Identify** the lot size $N$, sample size $n$, acceptance number $c$, and incoming quality $p$.
2. **Classify** the situation: Is $N$ large? If $N \geq 10n$, treat the lot as infinite (Binomial). Otherwise, use Hypergeometric.
3. **Compute** the mean number of defectives in the sample: $\mu = np$.
4. **Evaluate** the cumulative probability $P(d \leq c)$ using the chosen distribution.
5. **Interpret** the result: a high $P_a$ (close to 1) means the plan accepts most lots at that quality level; a low $P_a$ (close to 0) means the plan is rejecting almost everything.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table compiles **every formula** you will need for determining $P_a$ in KTU 2024 examinations. Each row is exam-ready.

| # | Scenario | Formula for $P_a$ | Conditions / Boundary |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Binomial — Exact** | $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{n-x}$ | $N$ large or infinite; $p$ constant; $n$ finite |
| 2 | **Hypergeometric — Exact** | $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{D}{x}\binom{N-D}{n-x}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | $N$ finite; $n/N \geq 0.1$; $D = Np$ |
| 3 | **Poisson Approximation** | $P_a \approx \displaystyle\sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^{x}}{x!}$ | $n \geq 20$, $p \leq 0.05$, $np \leq 5$ |
| 4 | **Normal Approx. to Binomial** | $P_a \approx \Phi\!\left(\frac{c + 0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{-0.5 - np}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}}\right)$ | $np \geq 5$ and $n(1-p) \geq 5$ |
| 5 | **Double Sampling Plan** | $P_a = P_{a1} + P_{r1} \cdot P_{a2}$ | Where $P_{a1}$ = accept on 1st sample, $P_{r1}$ = go to 2nd sample, $P_{a2}$ = accept on 2nd sample |
| 6 | **Producer's Risk** | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ | $p_1$ = AQL |
| 7 | **Consumer's Risk** | $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ | $p_2$ = LTPD |
| 8 | **Average Sample Number (ASN)** | For Double Plan: $ASN = n_1 + n_2(1 - P_{a1} - P_{r1})$ | Used for comparison of plans |
| 9 | **AOQL Formula** | $AOQL = \displaystyle\frac{p(P_a)(N-n)}{N}$ | Rectified inspection assumed |
| 10 | **LTPD Definition** | $P_a \vert_{p = LTPD} = \beta$ | Typically $\beta = 0.10$ |
| 11 | **AQL Definition** | $P_a \vert_{p = AQL} = 1 - \alpha$ | Typically $\alpha = 0.05$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem states "use Poisson approximation," explicitly verify $np \leq 5$ in your solution. Examiners deduct marks if you blindly apply a formula without justifying the assumption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Probability of Acceptance framework is the **backbone of modern industrial quality assurance**:

- **Automotive Sector:** Tier-1 suppliers use MIL-STD-105E / ISO 2859-1 plans; $P_a$ is tabulated for every $(n, c, p)$ triple.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Every batch of tablets undergoes attribute sampling where $P_a$ determines batch release.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** Incoming component inspection (resistors, ICs) uses AQL-based plans where $P_a$ for $p = AQL \approx 0.95$.
- **Defence Procurement:** Government contracts use LTPD-based plans where $P_a$ for $p = LTPD \approx 0.10$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why engineers care:** Computing $P_a$ before production allows procurement teams to negotiate sampling plans with suppliers and pre-emptively bound the risk of accepting substandard materials.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — $P_a$ Under Binomial Distribution

**Starting Premise:** A lot contains a large (effectively infinite) number of items, each independently having probability $p$ of being defective. A random sample of size $n$ is drawn. The number of defectives $X$ in the sample follows:

$$X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$$

**Step 1:** Write the Binomial PMF:

$$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{n-x}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Step 2:** State the acceptance event. The lot is accepted if $X \leq c$:

$$E_{\text{accept}} = \{X = 0\} \cup \{X = 1\} \cup \cdots \cup \{X = c\}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Addition Rule for mutually exclusive events:

$$P_a = P(X \leq c) = P(X=0) + P(X=1) + \cdots + P(X=c)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the PMF and sum:

$$P_a = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{n-x}$$

This is the **closed-form expression** for $P_a$ in a single sampling plan under Binomial assumptions. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — $P_a$ Under Poisson Approximation

**Starting Premise:** When $n$ is large and $p$ is small such that $np = \lambda$ is moderate, the Binomial distribution converges to Poisson($\lambda$).

**Step 1:** State Poisson PMF with mean $\lambda = np$:

$$P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{x}}{x!}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

**Step 2:** Acceptance criterion remains $X \leq c$:

$$P_a = P(X \leq c) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np} (np)^{x}}{x!}$$

**Step 3:** Practical evaluation: Use the **incomplete gamma function** relationship:

$$P_a = \frac{\Gamma(c+1, np)}{c!} = 1 - \frac{\gamma(c+1, np)}{c!}$$

where $\gamma$ is the lower incomplete gamma function, and tabulated in standard Poisson cumulative tables as $F(c; \lambda)$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — $P_a$ for a Double Sampling Plan

A **double sampling plan** is defined as $(N, n_1, n_2, c_1, c_2, r_1)$ where $c_1 < c_2 < r_1$.

**Step 1:** **First Sample of size $n_1$:**
- If $d_1 \leq c_1$: **Accept** the lot on first sample. Probability = $P_{a1}$.
- If $d_1 \geq r_1$: **Reject** the lot. Probability = $P_{r1}$.
- If $c_1 < d_1 < r_1$: Take a second sample of size $n_2$. Probability = $P_{c1}$.

**Step 2:** **Second Sample of size $n_2$ (given first was inconclusive):**
- Combine defectives: $d = d_1 + d_2$.
- If $d \leq c_2$: **Accept**. Probability = $P_{a2}$.
- If $d > c_2$: **Reject**. Probability = $P_{r2}$.

**Step 3:** **Total Probability of Acceptance** uses the law of total probability:

$$P_a = P_{a1} + P_{c1} \cdot P_{a2}$$

Expanding symbolically:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= P(X_1 \leq c_1) + P(c_1 < X_1 < r_1) \cdot P(X_1 + X_2 \leq c_2 \mid c_1 < X_1 < r_1) \\
&= \sum_{x_1=0}^{c_1} \binom{n_1}{x_1} p^{x_1}(1-p)^{n_1-x_1} \\
&\quad + \sum_{x_1=c_1+1}^{r_1-1} \binom{n_1}{x_1} p^{x_1}(1-p)^{n_1-x_1} \cdot \sum_{x_2=0}^{c_2-x_1} \binom{n_2}{x_2} p^{x_2}(1-p)^{n_2-x_2}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **standard form** tested in KTU Module 4 problems. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Single Sampling Plan

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A manufacturing lot has a known fraction defective $p = 0.05$. A single sampling plan with $n = 50$ and $c = 3$ is applied. Find $P_a$ using the Binomial distribution.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters. $n = 50$, $p = 0.05$, $c = 3$, $q = 1 - p = 0.95$.

**Step 2:** Compute the mean and variance of the Binomial:
- $\mu = np = 50 \times 0.05 = 2.5$
- $\sigma^2 = npq = 50 \times 0.05 \times 0.95 = 2.375$
- $\sigma = \sqrt{2.375} \approx 1.5411$

**Step 3:** Compute $P(X = 0)$:

$$P(X=0) = \binom{50}{0}(0.05)^0 (0.95)^{50} = (0.95)^{50}$$

Evaluating:
$$\ln(0.95^{50}) = 50 \ln(0.95) = 50 \times (-0.05129) = -2.5645$$
$$P(X=0) = e^{-2.5645} \approx 0.0769$$

**Step 4:** Compute $P(X = 1)$:

$$P(X=1) = \binom{50}{1}(0.05)^1 (0.95)^{49} = 50 \times 0.05 \times (0.95)^{49}$$

We have $(0.95)^{49} = (0.95)^{50} / 0.95 = 0.0769 / 0.95 = 0.0810$.
$$P(X=1) = 2.5 \times 0.0810 \approx 0.2025$$

**Step 5:** Compute $P(X = 2)$:

$$P(X=2) = \binom{50}{2}(0.05)^2 (0.95)^{48} = \frac{50 \times 49}{2} \times 0.0025 \times (0.95)^{48}$$

$$\binom{50}{2} = 1225$$
$$(0.95)^{48} = (0.95)^{50} / (0.95)^2 = 0.0769 / 0.9025 \approx 0.0853$$
$$P(X=2) = 1225 \times 0.0025 \times 0.0853 \approx 0.2611$$

**Step 6:** Compute $P(X = 3)$:

$$P(X=3) = \binom{50}{3}(0.05)^3 (0.95)^{47}$$

$$\binom{50}{3} = \frac{50 \times 49 \times 48}{6} = 19600$$
$$(0.05)^3 = 0.000125$$
$$(0.95)^{47} = (0.95)^{50} / (0.95)^3 = 0.0769 / 0.857375 \approx 0.0898$$
$$P(X=3) = 19600 \times 0.000125 \times 0.0898 \approx 0.2199$$

**Step 7:** Sum to obtain $P_a$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= P(X=0) + P(X=1) + P(X=2) + P(X=3) \\
&= 0.0769 + 0.2025 + 0.2611 + 0.2199 \\
&= 0.7604
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $P_a \approx 0.7604$ or **76.04%**.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification with Poisson:** Here $np = 2.5 \leq 5$, so Poisson is valid. Using $P(X \leq 3 \mid \lambda = 2.5)$ from standard tables gives $P_a \approx 0.7576$, which is very close to the Binomial result (small difference due to continuity). This validates our calculation.

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example — Double Sampling Plan

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A double sampling plan has $n_1 = 30$, $c_1 = 1$, $r_1 = 4$, $n_2 = 40$, $c_2 = 3$. If the lot quality is $p = 0.03$, find $P_a$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** First sample probabilities with $n_1 = 30$, $p = 0.03$, $q = 0.97$, $np = 0.9$:

$$P(X_1 = 0) = (0.97)^{30} \approx 0.4010$$
$$P(X_1 = 1) = 30 \times 0.03 \times (0.97)^{29} \approx 0.3720$$
$$P(X_1 = 2) = \binom{30}{2}(0.03)^2(0.97)^{28} \approx 0.1685$$
$$P(X_1 = 3) = \binom{30}{3}(0.03)^3(0.97)^{27} \approx 0.0498$$

**Step 2:** Compute $P_{a1}$ (accept on first sample, $X_1 \leq 1$):

$$P_{a1} = P(X_1=0) + P(X_1=1) = 0.4010 + 0.3720 = 0.7730$$

**Step 3:** Compute $P_{r1}$ (reject on first sample, $X_1 \geq 4$). For brevity:
$$P_{r1} = 1 - P(X_1 \leq 3) = 1 - (0.4010 + 0.3720 + 0.1685 + 0.0498) = 1 - 0.9913 = 0.0087$$

**Step 4:** Compute $P_{c1}$ (take second sample, $X_1 \in \{2, 3\}$):

$$P_{c1} = P(X_1=2) + P(X_1=3) = 0.1685 + 0.0498 = 0.2183$$

**Step 5:** Second sample probabilities with $n_2 = 40$, $p = 0.03$, $q = 0.97$, $np = 1.2$:

$$P(X_2 = 0) = (0.97)^{40} \approx 0.2957$$
$$P(X_2 = 1) = 40 \times 0.03 \times (0.97)^{39} \approx 0.3658$$
$$P(X_2 = 2) = \binom{40}{2}(0.03)^2(0.97)^{38} \approx 0.2207$$
$$P(X_2 = 3) = \binom{40}{3}(0.03)^3(0.97)^{37} \approx 0.0864$$

**Step 6:** Conditional acceptance probabilities given $X_1 = x_1$:

For $X_1 = 2$ (need $X_2 \leq 1$ to have $X_1 + X_2 \leq 3$):
$$P(X_2 \leq 1) = 0.2957 + 0.3658 = 0.6615$$

For $X_1 = 3$ (need $X_2 = 0$ to have $X_1 + X_2 \leq 3$):
$$P(X_2 = 0) = 0.2957$$

**Step 7:** Total $P_{a2}$ (acceptance on second sample, weighted):

$$P_{a2} = P(X_1=2) \cdot P(X_2 \leq 1) + P(X_1=3) \cdot P(X_2 = 0)$$

$$P_{a2} = (0.1685 \times 0.6615) + (0.0498 \times 0.2957)$$

$$P_{a2} = 0.1115 + 0.0147 = 0.1262$$

**Step 8:** Total Probability of Acceptance:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_a &= P_{a1} + P_{c1} \cdot P_{a2} \\
&= 0.7730 + (0.2183 \times 0.1262) \\
&= 0.7730 + 0.0276 \\
&= 0.8006
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $P_a \approx 0.8006$ or **80.06%**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use What

| Plan Type | Use When | $P_a$ Complexity | KTU Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Single Sampling | Cost of inspection high; simple decision needed | Low — single sum | **High (Most frequent)** |
| Double Sampling | Inspectors can re-sample; need ASN optimisation | Medium — nested sum | **Medium** |
| Multiple Sampling | Very expensive items; ASN must be minimised | High — multi-stage | **Low** |
| Sequential Sampling | Continuous stream; item-by-item decision | Very High — Wald's APN | **Low (Conceptual only)** |
| Rectifying Inspection | Defectives can be replaced 100% after rejection | Add AOQL step | **Medium** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** Single and Double Sampling Plans carry the maximum weightage. The KTU question paper is highly likely to contain a 7-mark sub-question requiring explicit $P_a$ computation using Binomial or Poisson.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Determination of $P_a$

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision and computation pipeline** for determining the Probability of Acceptance in any sampling plan.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Lot of N items with fraction defective p] --> B[Choose Sampling Plan Type]
    B --> B1[Single Plan: n, c]
    B --> B2[Double Plan: n1, n2, c1, c2, r1]
    B --> B3[Multiple or Sequential Plan]

    B1 --> C{Is N large or small}
    C --> C1[N >= 10n: Use Binomial]
    C --> C2[N < 10n: Use Hypergeometric]

    C1 --> D[Compute mean = np]
    C2 --> E[Compute D = Np]

    D --> F[Apply PMF of Binomial]
    E --> G[Apply PMF of Hypergeometric]

    F --> H[Sum P X from 0 to c]
    G --> H

    B2 --> I[Compute Pa1: accept on first sample]
    I --> J[Compute Pr1: reject on first sample]
    J --> K[Compute Pc1: inconclusive, take second sample]
    K --> L[Compute Pa2: accept on second sample]
    L --> M[Total Pa = Pa1 + Pc1 times Pa2]

    B3 --> N[Sequential / Multiple Plan Logic]
    N --> O[Use Wald's APN or recursive formulas]

    H --> P[Final Pa Value]
    M --> P
    O --> P

    P --> Q[Plot or tabulate OC Curve Lp vs p]
    Q --> R[Identify Producer Risk alpha and Consumer Risk beta]
    R --> S[Plan Validated or Re Design]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve Concept

The OC curve visually summarises $P_a$ for every possible value of $p$. Below is a block-level representation of the regions on a typical OC curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OC_CURVE
    P0[Pa at p = 0: Equals 1.0 Ideal Accept]
    P1[Pa at p = AQL: Equals 1 minus alpha Approx 0.95]
    P2[Pa at p = p0.50: Indifference Quality 0.50]
    P3[Pa at p = LTPD: Equals beta Approx 0.10]
    P4[Pa at p = 1: Equals 0.0 Ideal Reject]
    end

    P0 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4

    note1[Slope and shape of OC curve depends on n and c. Larger n with fixed c makes curve steeper. Increasing c shifts curve right and up.]
    P2 -.- note1
```

## 4.3 State Machine: Lot Disposition During Sampling

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LotReceived
    LotReceived --> FirstSample: Draw n items
    FirstSample --> Accept: d is less than or equal to c
    FirstSample --> Reject: d is greater than c
    FirstSample --> Pending: c1 less than d less than r1

    Pending --> SecondSample: Draw n2 more items
    SecondSample --> Accept: d1 + d2 less than or equal to c2
    SecondSample --> Reject: d1 + d2 greater than c2

    Accept --> [*]
    Reject --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: From Sampling Plan Parameters to Risk Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Inputs: N, n, c, p] --> Dist[Select Distribution]
    Dist --> ComputePa[Compute Pa for given p]
    ComputePa --> VaryP[Vary p from 0 to 1]
    VaryP --> OCGen[Generate OC Curve]
    OCGen --> ReadOff[Read off Pa at AQL and LTPD]
    ReadOff --> Risk1[Producer Risk alpha equals 1 minus Pa at AQL]
    ReadOff --> Risk2[Consumer Risk beta equals Pa at LTPD]
    Risk1 --> Output[Output: alpha, beta, Pa table]
    Risk2 --> Output
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each block above corresponds to a computation step you must perform on paper during the KTU exam. Memorise the order: (1) choose distribution, (2) compute $P_a$, (3) vary $p$, (4) read off risks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
Define the term **Probability of Acceptance** ($P_a$) in the context of acceptance sampling. State the formula for $P_a$ under the **Binomial distribution** for a single sampling plan with parameters $n$ and $c$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Probability of Acceptance $P_a$ is the probability that a sampling plan will accept a lot when the true fraction defective in the lot is $p$. It is the cumulative probability of obtaining at most $c$ defectives in a sample of size $n$.

$$P_a = P(X \leq c) = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{n-x}$$

This function $P_a = L(p)$ is monotonically decreasing in $p$ and forms the Operating Characteristic curve. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Under what conditions is the **Poisson distribution** used as an approximation to the Binomial distribution for computing $P_a$? Write the corresponding expression.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Poisson distribution is used as an approximation when:
1. The sample size $n$ is large ($n \geq 20$),
2. The fraction defective $p$ is small ($p \leq 0.05$), and
3. The product $np \leq 5$.

The corresponding $P_a$ expression is:

$$P_a = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^{x}}{x!}$$

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often forget to **sum from $x=0$ to $c$** and instead only compute $P(X=c)$. This loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. Do **not** mix Hypergeometric and Binomial formulas in the same problem. Verify the $N$ vs $n$ condition first.
> 3. Always **state the assumption** (Binomial / Poisson / Normal) before writing the formula — examiners award 1 mark specifically for this.
> 4. In double sampling plans, the most common error is forgetting the weighting factor $P_{c1}$ when computing the final $P_a$. Use the formula $P_a = P_{a1} + P_{c1} \cdot P_{a2}$ correctly.
> 5. Do not approximate intermediate values — keep at least **4 decimal places** until the final summation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

A single sampling plan is used for lot inspection with the following parameters: lot size $N = 2000$, sample size $n = 80$, and acceptance number $c = 2$. The lot is known to have a fraction defective of $p = 0.04$.

**(a)** Determine the Probability of Acceptance $P_a$ using the **Binomial distribution**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Verify your answer using the **Poisson approximation** and compute the **Producer's Risk** $\alpha$ if the AQL is fixed at $p_1 = 0.02$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Binomial Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Check applicability of Binomial.**
$N = 2000$, $n = 80$. Since $N/n = 2000/80 = 25 \geq 10$, the lot is effectively infinite for sampling purposes. **Binomial is valid.** (1 mark)

**Step 2: Identify parameters.**
$n = 80$, $p = 0.04$, $q = 0.96$, $c = 2$.
$np = 80 \times 0.04 = 3.2$
$npq = 3.2 \times 0.96 = 3.072$
$\sigma = \sqrt{3.072} \approx 1.7527$ (1 mark)

**Step 3: Compute $P(X=0)$:**
$$P(X=0) = (0.96)^{80}$$
$$\ln(0.96^{80}) = 80 \times \ln(0.96) = 80 \times (-0.04082) = -3.2656$$
$$P(X=0) = e^{-3.2656} \approx 0.0382$$ (2 marks)

**Step 4: Compute $P(X=1)$:**
$$P(X=1) = 80 \times 0.04 \times (0.96)^{79} = 3.2 \times 0.0398 \approx 0.1273$$ (1 mark)

**Step 5: Compute $P(X=2)$:**
$$P(X=2) = \binom{80}{2} \times (0.04)^2 \times (0.96)^{78} = 3160 \times 0.0016 \times 0.0415$$
$$\approx 0.2098$$ (1 mark)

**Step 6: Sum for $P_a$:**
$$P_a = 0.0382 + 0.1273 + 0.2098 = 0.3753$$
(1 mark for summation)

**Final Answer (Part a):** $P_a \approx 0.3753$ or **37.53%**.

---

**Part (b) — Poisson Verification and Producer's Risk (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Verify Poisson conditions.**
$np = 80 \times 0.04 = 3.2 \leq 5$. **Poisson approximation is valid.** (1 mark)

**Step 2: Compute $P_a$ using Poisson with $\lambda = 3.2$:**
$$P(X=0) = e^{-3.2} = 0.0408$$
$$P(X=1) = 3.2 \times e^{-3.2} = 3.2 \times 0.0408 = 0.1307$$
$$P(X=2) = \frac{(3.2)^2}{2} e^{-3.2} = 5.12 \times 0.0408 = 0.2090$$
$$P_a^{\text{Poisson}} = 0.0408 + 0.1307 + 0.2090 = 0.3805$$ (3 marks)

**Step 3: Compare with Binomial.**
$P_a^{\text{Binomial}} = 0.3753$ vs $P_a^{\text{Poisson}} = 0.3805$. Difference is only $0.0052$ (about 1.4%), confirming the Poisson is a good approximation. (1 mark)

**Step 4: Compute $P_a$ at AQL = 0.02 using Poisson.**
For AQL: $p_1 = 0.02$, $\lambda_1 = np = 80 \times 0.02 = 1.6$.
$$P(X=0) = e^{-1.6} = 0.2019$$
$$P(X=1) = 1.6 \times 0.2019 = 0.3230$$
$$P(X=2) = \frac{1.6^2}{2} \times 0.2019 = 1.28 \times 0.2019 = 0.2584$$
$$P_a(p_1) = 0.2019 + 0.3230 + 0.2584 = 0.7833$$ (1 mark)

**Step 5: Compute Producer's Risk $\alpha$:**
$$\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1) = 1 - 0.7833 = 0.2167$$ (1 mark)

**Final Answer (Part b):** $P_a^{\text{Poisson}} \approx 0.3805$, $\alpha \approx 0.2167$ or **21.67%**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

A double sampling plan is defined as follows: $n_1 = 50$, $c_1 = 2$, $r_1 = 5$, $n_2 = 50$, $c_2 = 4$. The lot fraction defective is $p = 0.04$.

**(a)** Compute the **Probability of Acceptance** $P_a$ for this double sampling plan. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the same lot quality is checked with a single sampling plan $(n = 80, c = 3)$, find the corresponding $P_a$ and comment on which plan is **better for the consumer**. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Double Sampling Plan (7 marks):**

**Step 1: First sample probabilities, $n_1 = 50$, $p = 0.04$, $q = 0.96$:**
- $P(X_1=0) = (0.96)^{50} \approx 0.1299$
- $P(X_1=1) = 50 \times 0.04 \times (0.96)^{49} \approx 0.2706$
- $P(X_1=2) = \binom{50}{2}(0.04)^2(0.96)^{48} \approx 0.2762$
- $P(X_1=3) = \binom{50}{3}(0.04)^3(0.96)^{47} \approx 0.1841$
- $P(X_1=4) = \binom{50}{4}(0.04)^4(0.96)^{46} \approx 0.0902$

**Step 2: Acceptance on first sample ($X_1 \leq 2$):**
$$P_{a1} = 0.1299 + 0.2706 + 0.2762 = 0.6767$$ (1 mark)

**Step 3: Rejection on first sample ($X_1 \geq 5$).**
$P_{r1} = 1 - (P_{a1} + P(X_1=3) + P(X_1=4)) = 1 - (0.6767 + 0.1841 + 0.0902) = 1 - 0.9510 = 0.0490$ (1 mark)

**Step 4: Inconclusive, take second sample ($X_1 \in \{3, 4\}$):**
$$P_{c1} = 0.1841 + 0.0902 = 0.2743$$ (1 mark)

**Step 5: Second sample probabilities, $n_2 = 50$, $p = 0.04$:**
Same as first sample. So $P(X_2=0) \approx 0.1299$, $P(X_2=1) \approx 0.2706$, $P(X_2=2) \approx 0.2762$, etc. (1 mark)

**Step 6: Acceptance on second sample, conditional probabilities:**

- If $X_1 = 3$, need $X_2 \leq 1$ (so total $\leq 4$):
  $P(X_2 \leq 1) = 0.1299 + 0.2706 = 0.4005$

- If $X_1 = 4$, need $X_2 = 0$ (so total $\leq 4$):
  $P(X_2 = 0) = 0.1299$

**Step 7: Total $P_{a2}$:**
$$P_{a2} = P(X_1=3) \cdot 0.4005 + P(X_1=4) \cdot 0.1299$$
$$P_{a2} = (0.1841 \times 0.4005) + (0.0902 \times 0.1299)$$
$$P_{a2} = 0.0737 + 0.0117 = 0.0854$$ (1 mark)

**Step 8: Total $P_a$ for the double plan:**
$$P_a = P_{a1} + P_{c1} \cdot P_{a2} = 0.6767 + (0.2743 \times 0.0854) = 0.6767 + 0.0234 = 0.7001$$ (1 mark)

**Final Answer (Part a):** $P_a \approx 0.7001$ or **70.01%** for the double sampling plan.

---

**Part (b) — Comparison with Single Plan (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Single plan parameters:** $n = 80$, $c = 3$, $p = 0.04$, $q = 0.96$, $np = 3.2$.

**Step 2: Binomial probabilities:**
- $P(X=0) = (0.96)^{80} \approx 0.0382$
- $P(X=1) = 80 \times 0.04 \times (0.96)^{79} \approx 0.1273$
- $P(X=2) = \binom{80}{2}(0.04)^2(0.96)^{78} \approx 0.2098$
- $P(X=3) = \binom{80}{3}(0.04)^3(0.96)^{77} \approx 0.2238$ (3 marks)

**Step 3: Sum:**
$$P_a^{\text{single}} = 0.0382 + 0.1273 + 0.2098 + 0.2238 = 0.5991$$ (1 mark)

**Step 4: Comparison.**
- Double plan: $P_a = 0.7001$
- Single plan: $P_a = 0.5991$

**Step 5: Consumer's perspective (lower $P_a$ is better for consumer at high $p$):**
At $p = 0.04$, the single plan rejects more lots ($P_a = 0.5991$ < $0.7001$). Hence, the **single sampling plan is better for the consumer** because it has a lower probability of accepting the same moderately bad lot. The double plan is "kinder" to the producer (higher acceptance) but exposes the consumer to higher risk. (3 marks)

**Final Answer (Part b):** $P_a^{\text{single}} \approx 0.5991$. The single plan is preferable for the consumer.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **$P_a$ Definition:** $P_a = P(X \leq c)$ — probability sample defectives are at most the acceptance number.
- ✅ **Binomial $P_a$:** $\sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^{x} (1-p)^{n-x}$ — use when $N \geq 10n$.
- ✅ **Poisson $P_a$:** $\sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^{x}}{x!}$ — use when $n \geq 20$, $p \leq 0.05$, $np \leq 5$.
- ✅ **Normal Approximation $P_a$:** Use the standardised $Z$-score with **continuity correction** of $\pm 0.5$.
- ✅ **Hypergeometric $P_a$:** $\sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{D}{x}\binom{N-D}{n-x}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ — use when $N < 10n$.
- ✅ **Double Plan $P_a$:** $P_a = P_{a1} + P_{c1} \cdot P_{a2}$ — never forget $P_{c1}$ as the multiplier.
- ✅ **Producer's Risk:** $\alpha = 1 - P_a(\text{AQL})$ — usually targeted at **0.05** (5%).
- ✅ **Consumer's Risk:** $\beta = P_a(\text{LTPD})$ — usually targeted at **0.10** (10%).
- ✅ **OC Curve Property:** $P_a$ is a *decreasing* function of $p$; it passes through $(0, 1)$ and $(1, 0)$ asymptotically.
- ✅ **AOQL** = $p \cdot P_a \cdot (N-n)/N$ — relevant only for **rectifying** inspection plans.
- ✅ **Always state the assumption** (Binomial / Poisson / Normal) before plugging in numbers — earns 1 easy mark.
- ✅ **Always sum from $x=0$ to $x=c$** — partial sums are a common deduction point.
- ✅ **Units check:** $n$ and $N$ are dimensionless counts; $p$ is dimensionless fraction; $P_a$ is dimensionless probability in $[0,1]$.
- ✅ **Plan Comparison:** For the *same* $p$, lower $P_a$ = better for consumer; higher $P_a$ = better for producer.
- ✅ **Sample Size Effect:** Increasing $n$ (with $c$ fixed) makes the OC curve **steeper**, reducing both $\alpha$ and $\beta$.
- ✅ **Acceptance Number Effect:** Increasing $c$ (with $n$ fixed) shifts the OC curve **right and up**, making acceptance more lenient.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling Risks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sampling Risks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Sampling Risks

In acceptance sampling for **Quality Control**, every lot inspection decision carries an inherent probability of error. These errors are termed **Sampling Risks**. The two canonical sampling risks defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling Risk (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The probability of making an incorrect decision about a lot based on a sample drawn from it. In acceptance sampling by attributes, two mutually exclusive incorrect decisions exist, leading to two distinct risks borne by two different parties of the supply chain.

### 1.1.1 Producer's Risk (α — Type I Error)

**Producer's Risk (α)** is the probability that a **good lot** (one whose true fraction defective $p$ is at or below the **Acceptable Quality Level**, AQL) is **rejected** by the sampling plan.

$$\alpha = P(\text{Reject the lot} \mid p = p_1)$$
where $p_1$ is the AQL (or Producer's Quality Level, PQL).

In other words:
$$P_a(p_1) = 1 - \alpha$$

This is the risk the **manufacturer/producer** bears because a perfectly producible lot is unnecessarily sent back, causing rework, delays, and lost goodwill.

### 1.1.2 Consumer's Risk (β — Type II Error)

**Consumer's Risk (β)** is the probability that a **bad lot** (one whose true fraction defective $p$ is at or above the **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective**, LTPD) is **accepted** by the sampling plan.

$$\beta = P(\text{Accept the lot} \mid p = p_2)$$
where $p_2$ is the LTPD (or Consumer's Quality Level, CQL).

Equivalently:
$$P_a(p_2) = \beta$$

This is the risk the **buyer/consumer** bears because defective items reach the customer, leading to field failures, warranty costs, and brand damage.

### 1.1.3 Connection to Statistical Hypothesis Testing

| Statistical Term | Quality Control Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Null Hypothesis $H_0$ | Lot is of acceptable quality ($p \le p_1$) |
| Alternate Hypothesis $H_1$ | Lot is of unacceptable quality ($p \ge p_2$) |
| Type I Error (α) | **Producer's Risk** — reject a good lot |
| Type II Error (β) | **Consumer's Risk** — accept a bad lot |
| Power of test $(1-\beta)$ | Probability of correctly rejecting bad lot |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Irreducible Trade-off:** In any fixed sample size plan $(n, c)$, decreasing $\alpha$ automatically increases $\beta$, and vice-versa. Only by increasing $n$ (or relaxing $c$) can both risks be simultaneously reduced — but at the cost of higher inspection effort.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Classroom Test"

Imagine a **professor** setting a 10-question viva to judge whether a student truly knows the subject (an "acceptable" student) or has merely memorized the night before (an "unacceptable" student).

- **AQL student** ($p_1$): A genuine scholar. The professor wants to **pass** her, but with only 10 sampled questions, the *very unlucky* case of her stumbling on a poorly-graded answer could cause the professor to **fail** her. That unfortunate rejection is the **Producer's Risk (α)** — punishing the good.
- **LTPD student** ($p_2$): A complete fraud. He answers only 30% of the questions correctly. Yet, by sheer luck, his random guesswork on a few questions could let him **pass** the viva. That undeserved acceptance is the **Consumer's Risk (β)** — letting the bad in.

The professor's 10-question viva is the **sample**; the entire semester's performance is the **lot**. The viva is imperfect precisely *because it is a sample* — and that imperfection is the **sampling risk**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** In manufacturing, $p_1$ and $p_2$ are **never zero**. Even the best production line yields some scrap, and even the worst lot occasionally passes a sample. Sampling risks mathematically formalize the unavoidable uncertainty of *inference from sample to lot*.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Sampling Risk Analysis

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** The maximum fraction defective $p_1$ that is considered tolerable as a process average.
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective):** The fraction defective $p_2$ that the consumer is willing to tolerate only with a small probability $\beta$.
- **PQL (Producer's Quality Level):** Synonymous with AQL in many texts.
- **CQL (Consumer's Quality Level):** Synonymous with LTPD in many texts.
- **OC Curve (Operating Characteristic Curve):** A plot of $P_a$ (probability of acceptance) versus the true lot fraction defective $p$.
- **AOQ (Average Outgoing Quality):** Expected fraction defective in lots after rectifying inspection.
- **AOQL (Average Outgoing Quality Limit):** Maximum value of AOQ over all possible values of incoming $p$.
- **ATI (Average Total Inspection):** Average number of items inspected per lot under a given sampling plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specifics:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the graphical interpretation of the OC curve and the relationship $\alpha + \beta \neq 1$. The two risks are defined at *different* values of $p$, and thus their sum is meaningless.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — Foundation of Sampling Risks

The OC curve is the master graphical tool for understanding sampling risks. For an attribute single sampling plan defined by:
- $n$ = sample size
- $c$ = acceptance number (maximum defectives allowed in sample for lot acceptance)

the probability of acceptance, assuming the lot is large and sampling is with replacement (or lot is very large), is given by the **cumulative binomial probability**:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

When $n$ is large and $p$ is small, the **Poisson approximation** is used:

$$P_a(p) \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** For $np \le 5$ (rare event regime), the Poisson approximation is the standard. For $np > 5$, the binomial must be used or the **normal approximation** with continuity correction:
> $$Z = \frac{d - np + 0.5}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}}$$

### 2.1.1 Reading Sampling Risks off the OC Curve

| Region of OC Curve | Decision Outcome | Risk Involved |
|---|---|---|
| At $p = p_1$ (AQL) | $P_a = 1 - \alpha$ | Producer's Risk $\alpha$ |
| At $p = p_2$ (LTPD) | $P_a = \beta$ | Consumer's Risk $\beta$ |
| At $p = 0$ | $P_a = 1$ | Perfect lots always accepted |
| At $p = 1$ | $P_a = 0$ | Worst lots always rejected |
| Curve between $p_1$ and $p_2$ | Transition region | Indifference zone |

> [!NOTE]
> **Indifference Zone:** The range $p_1 < p < p_2$ where the OC curve falls steeply. A good sampling plan should have a steep, near-vertical OC curve in this region — meaning small changes in $p$ lead to large changes in $P_a$, thereby *minimizing* sampling risks.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Domain / Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Probability of acceptance (binomial) | $P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Exact; valid for any $n, p$ |
| 2 | Probability of acceptance (Poisson) | $P_a(p) \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | $n \ge 50$, $p \le 0.10$, $np \le 5$ |
| 3 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ | Evaluated at $p = p_1$ = AQL |
| 4 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ | Evaluated at $p = p_2$ = LTPD |
| 5 | Average Outgoing Quality | $AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}$ | Rectifying inspection, finite $N$ |
| 6 | Average Outgoing Quality (large lot) | $AOQ \approx P_a \cdot p$ | When $N \to \infty$ |
| 7 | Average Outgoing Quality Limit | $AOQL = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} [P_a \cdot p]$ | Maximum of AOQ curve |
| 8 | ATI (single sampling) | $ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$ | Rectifying inspection |
| 9 | ATI (double sampling, approx.) | $ATI \approx n_1 + n_2 P_1 + (1 - P_a)(N - n_1 - n_2)$ | Rectifying inspection |
| 10 | Required sample size (LTPD) | $n = \dfrac{\chi^2_{1-\alpha,\ 2c+2}}{2 p_2}$ | Chi-square approximation for $c \ge 1$ |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values and conditional operators in the table above are denoted by parentheses or written as conditional clauses — never as pipe symbols — to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.3 Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) — Detailed Theory

In **rectifying inspection**, all rejected lots are subjected to 100% screening, and defectives are replaced with good items. After this screening, the *expected* fraction defective leaving the inspection station is the **AOQ**.

### 2.3.1 Derivation Intuition

For a lot of size $N$ with $Np$ defectives:

- **If lot is accepted** (probability $P_a$): The lot goes through as-is. Average defectives leaving = $Np$.
- **If lot is rejected** (probability $1 - P_a$): The lot is fully screened; defectives are replaced. Average defectives leaving = $0$.

The expected number of defectives leaving per lot:
$$E[\text{defectives out}] = P_a \cdot Np + (1 - P_a) \cdot 0 = P_a \cdot Np$$

However, the sample itself is also inspected; the $n$ sampled items are replaced (assuming replacement) or are not (depending on plan). Assuming **replacement** during rectifying inspection:

$$AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot Np \cdot \frac{N-n}{N}}{N} = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}$$

This is the formula listed as **#5** in the cheat sheet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The AOQ Curve is bell-shaped:** At $p = 0$ (perfect lots), $P_a = 1$ and $AOQ = 0$. At $p = 1$ (all-defective lots), $P_a = 0$ and $AOQ = 0$. Somewhere in between, $AOQ$ reaches a maximum — the **AOQL**. This AOQL is the *worst-case* average quality the consumer will see, and is a critical design parameter.

---

## 2.4 Average Total Inspection (ATI) — Detailed Theory

ATI represents the **average number of items inspected per lot** under the given plan (including 100% inspection for rejected lots). It measures inspection workload and cost.

### 2.4.1 Single Sampling Plan

- If lot is accepted (prob. $P_a$): Inspect $n$ items only.
- If lot is rejected (prob. $1 - P_a$): Inspect all $N$ items.

$$ATI = P_a \cdot n + (1 - P_a) \cdot N = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$$

### 2.4.2 Double Sampling Plan (Approximation)

For a double sampling plan with first sample $n_1$, second sample $n_2$, acceptance numbers $c_1, c_2$, and probability of going to second sample $P_1$:

$$ATI = n_1 + n_2 P_1 + (1 - P_a)(N - n_1 - n_2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** ATI is the *cost function* of a sampling plan. Procurement managers use ATI curves to choose plans minimizing total inspection labor for a desired protection level. Double sampling plans typically have lower ATI than single sampling plans at intermediate $p$ values.

---

## 2.5 Why Sampling Risks Matter in Real Engineering

| Domain | Application of Sampling Risks |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (ISO/TS 16949)** | AQL-based inspection of components; LTPD sets warranty exposure limits |
| **Aerospace (AS9100)** | Near-zero LTPD with $\beta \le 0.10$ and very low AQL for critical parts |
| **Electronics Manufacturing** | MIL-STD-105E / ANSI Z1.4 plans for PCB assembly inspection |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Tight OC curves required by FDA; $\alpha$ and $\beta$ must be documented |
| **Civil Construction** | Concrete cube sampling; risk of accepting sub-spec concrete |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Economic Logic:** Producer's risk represents **opportunity cost** to the manufacturer (wasted good lots). Consumer's risk represents **liability cost** to the buyer (warranty, recall, reputation). The sampling plan is essentially a *contract* balancing these costs — captured mathematically by the OC curve.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the OC Curve Formula (Binomial)

We model the number of defectives $D$ in a random sample of $n$ items drawn from a large lot with true fraction defective $p$ as a **binomial random variable**:

$$D \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$$

The probability mass function is:
$$P(D = d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}, \quad d = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

The lot is **accepted** if and only if the number of defectives in the sample $D$ does not exceed the acceptance number $c$:

$$\text{Accept} \iff D \le c$$

Therefore, the probability of acceptance is the cumulative distribution function of $D$ evaluated at $c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(p) &= P(D \le c) \\
&= \sum_{d=0}^{c} P(D = d) \\
&= \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**
- **Line 1:** $P_a$ is defined as the probability that the lot is accepted.
- **Line 2:** Acceptance requires $D \le c$, so we sum the PMF over $d = 0, 1, \ldots, c$.
- **Line 3:** Substituting the binomial PMF gives the closed-form OC curve equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Poisson Approximation

When $n$ is large and $p$ is small such that $n p = \lambda$ is moderate, the binomial converges to a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda = np$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty,\ p \to 0,\ np = \lambda} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d} &= \frac{\lambda^d e^{-\lambda}}{d!}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(p) &\approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of conversion logic:**
- We use $\lambda = np$ (the mean number of defectives expected in the sample).
- The Poisson PMF $\frac{\lambda^d e^{-\lambda}}{d!}$ is substituted for the binomial PMF.
- The cumulative sum from $d = 0$ to $d = c$ gives the approximate probability of acceptance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Check:** The Poisson approximation is accurate to within 1% when $p \le 0.10$ and $np \le 5$. KTU problems frequently exploit this regime to allow use of Poisson tables.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

Consider a rectifying inspection plan on lots of size $N$ with incoming fraction defective $p$. There are two mutually exclusive outcomes for each lot:

**Case 1: Lot is Accepted** (occurs with probability $P_a$)
- The lot bypasses 100% screening.
- All $Np$ defectives in the lot are shipped to the customer.
- The fraction defective in accepted lots is $p$.
- The contribution to average outgoing quality is $P_a \cdot p$.

**Case 2: Lot is Rejected** (occurs with probability $1 - P_a$)
- The lot undergoes 100% inspection.
- All defectives are replaced with good ones.
- The fraction defective in rejected lots after rectification is $0$.
- The contribution to average outgoing quality is $(1 - P_a) \cdot 0 = 0$.

The unconditional expected fraction defective in shipped lots:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOQ(p) &= P_a \cdot p + (1 - P_a) \cdot 0 \\
&= P_a \cdot p
\end{aligned}
$$

However, this formula assumes **all $N$ items** go out. In rectifying inspection where the sample of $n$ items is also replaced (screened or assumed good), the actual outgoing quality is the average fraction defective among the $N - n$ items that are *not* in the original sample, plus the $n$ items in the sample (which were inspected and found to have at most $c$ defectives — but those defectives are typically replaced if discovered).

The most general formula, accounting for the sample being inspected and rectifying the rest:

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOQ(p) &= \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- The factor $(N - n)/N$ represents the proportion of the lot that was *not* in the original sample.
- $P_a \cdot p$ is the expected fraction defective among items going out (assuming acceptance).
- Multiplying these gives the expected fraction defective in the entire outgoing stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For large $N$** ($N \gg n$), the correction factor $(N - n)/N \to 1$, simplifying to $AOQ \approx P_a \cdot p$. This is the form most commonly used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Average Total Inspection (ATI)

For a single sampling plan $(n, c)$ with rectifying inspection on lots of size $N$:

**Case 1: Lot is Accepted** (probability $P_a$)
- Only the $n$ sample items are inspected.
- Inspection count = $n$.

**Case 2: Lot is Rejected** (probability $1 - P_a$)
- Sample is inspected ($n$ items) and then 100% screening is performed on the remainder.
- Total inspection count = $N$.

The expected number of inspections per lot:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ATI(p) &= P_a \cdot n + (1 - P_a) \cdot N \\
&= P_a \cdot n + N - P_a \cdot N \\
&= n + N - P_a \cdot N - n + P_a \cdot n \\
&= n + (N - n)(1 - P_a)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:**
- Factor out $n$: $P_a \cdot n + (1 - P_a) N = P_a \cdot n + N - P_a \cdot N = n + (N - n) - P_a(N - n) = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$.
- The final form separates the always-performed sample inspection ($n$) from the conditional full inspection ($(1 - P_a)(N - n)$).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Full Risk Computation (Binomial)

**Problem:** A single sampling plan uses $n = 50$, $c = 2$. Compute the producer's risk at $p_1 = 0.02$ and the consumer's risk at $p_2 = 0.10$.

**Step 1: Compute $P_a(p_1)$ at $p_1 = 0.02$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(0.02) &= \sum_{d=0}^{2} \binom{50}{d} (0.02)^d (0.98)^{50-d} \\
&= \binom{50}{0}(0.98)^{50} + \binom{50}{1}(0.02)(0.98)^{49} + \binom{50}{2}(0.02)^2(0.98)^{48} \\
&= (1)(0.3642) + (50)(0.02)(0.3716) + (1225)(0.0004)(0.3792) \\
&= 0.3642 + 0.3716 + 0.1858 \\
&= 0.9216
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Producer's Risk**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= 1 - P_a(p_1) = 1 - 0.9216 = 0.0784
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $P_a(p_2)$ at $p_2 = 0.10$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(0.10) &= \sum_{d=0}^{2} \binom{50}{d} (0.10)^d (0.90)^{50-d} \\
&= (0.90)^{50} + 50(0.10)(0.90)^{49} + 1225(0.01)(0.90)^{48} \\
&= 0.00515 + 0.0286 + 0.0779 \\
&= 0.1117
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Consumer's Risk**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= P_a(p_2) = 0.1117
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The producer has a **7.84%** chance of rejecting a lot that is genuinely at the AQL. The consumer has an **11.17%** chance of accepting a lot that is at the LTPD. To reduce both risks, $n$ would need to be increased or $c$ adjusted downward.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — AOQ and AOQL Computation

**Problem:** A plan has $n = 30$, $c = 1$, $N = 1000$. Calculate $AOQ$ at $p = 0.05$ and find $AOQL$ approximately.

**Step 1: Compute $P_a(0.05)$ using Poisson approximation with $\lambda = np = 30 \times 0.05 = 1.5$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(0.05) &\approx e^{-1.5} \left[ \frac{(1.5)^0}{0!} + \frac{(1.5)^1}{1!} \right] \\
&= 0.2231 \times [1 + 1.5] \\
&= 0.2231 \times 2.5 \\
&= 0.5578
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute AOQ at $p = 0.05$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOQ(0.05) &= \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} \\
&= \frac{0.5578 \times 0.05 \times 970}{1000} \\
&= \frac{27.05}{1000} \\
&= 0.02705
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Tabulate AOQ over a range of $p$ to find AOQL**

| $p$ | $np = \lambda$ | $P_a$ (Poisson, $c=1$) | $AOQ = P_a \cdot p$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.00 | 0.00 | 1.000 | 0.000 |
| 0.02 | 0.60 | 0.878 | 0.0176 |
| 0.04 | 1.20 | 0.662 | 0.0265 |
| 0.05 | 1.50 | 0.558 | 0.0279 |
| 0.06 | 1.80 | 0.463 | 0.0278 |
| 0.08 | 2.40 | 0.308 | 0.0246 |
| 0.10 | 3.00 | 0.199 | 0.0199 |
| 0.15 | 4.50 | 0.061 | 0.0092 |
| 0.20 | 6.00 | 0.018 | 0.0036 |

The maximum occurs near $p = 0.05$, giving **$AOQL \approx 0.0279$**, or about **2.79%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economic Interpretation:** Even if incoming lots have 20% defectives, the *average outgoing quality* is only 0.36% because most such lots are rejected and fully screened. The AOQL is a guarantee to the consumer: "no matter how bad the incoming stream, the average outgoing quality will never exceed 2.79%."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow in Acceptance Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot of N items arrives at inspection station] --> B[Draw random sample of n items]
    B --> C{Count defectives D in sample}
    C --> D{D ≤ c ?}
    D -->|YES| E[LOT ACCEPTED]
    D -->|NO| F[LOT REJECTED]
    E --> G[Ship lot to customer]
    F --> H[100% Rectifying Inspection]
    H --> I[Replace defectives with good items]
    I --> G
    G --> J{Outcome Classification}
    J -->|Good lot accepted| K[Correct Decision]
    J -->|Bad lot rejected| K
    J -->|Good lot rejected| L[PRODUCER RISK alpha]
    J -->|Bad lot accepted| M[CONSUMER RISK beta]
    L --> N[Loss to manufacturer]
    M --> O[Loss to customer]
```

## 4.2 Sampling Risks and OC Curve Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Incoming lot fraction defective p] --> B[OC Curve Evaluation]
    B --> C{Position on p-axis}
    C -->|p at AQL p1| D[Pa equals 1 minus alpha]
    C -->|p at LTPD p2| E[Pa equals beta]
    C -->|p equals 0| F[Pa equals 1]
    C -->|p equals 1| G[Pa equals 0]
    D --> H[Producer's Risk alpha]
    E --> I[Consumer's Risk beta]
    F --> J[No Risk: Perfect Lot]
    G --> K[No Risk: All-Defective Lot]
    H --> L[Manufacturer Loss: rework, delay]
    I --> M[Customer Loss: warranty, recall]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of OC Curve Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sampling Plan Parameters: n and c] --> B[Probability Model Selection]
    B --> C{Poisson Regime?}
    C -->|YES np le 5| D[Use Poisson Tables]
    C -->|NO| E[Use Binomial Tables]
    D --> F[Compute Pa at each p value]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Plot Pa versus p]
    G --> H[OC Curve Generated]
    H --> I[Mark p1 on x-axis]
    H --> J[Mark p2 on x-axis]
    I --> K[Read off alpha equals 1 minus Pa at p1]
    J --> L[Read off beta equals Pa at p2]
    K --> M[Sampling Risk Profile Complete]
    L --> M
```

## 4.4 OC Curve Schematic — Visualizing the Two Risks

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph AX["X-AXIS: Lot fraction defective p"]
        A0[p equals 0] --> A1[p1 equals AQL] --> A2[p2 equals LTPD] --> A3[p equals 1]
    end
    subgraph BX["Y-AXIS: Probability of Acceptance Pa"]
        B0[Pa equals 1] --> B1[Pa equals 1 minus alpha] --> B2[Pa equals beta] --> B3[Pa equals 0]
    end
    A0 -.-> B0
    A1 -.-> B1
    A2 -.-> B2
    A3 -.-> B3
    B1 --> R1[Region 1: Producer's Risk alpha]
    B2 --> R2[Region 2: Consumer's Risk beta]
    R1 --> X[Indifference Zone: steep transition between p1 and p2]
    R2 --> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The OC curve starts at the top-left corner $(p=0, P_a=1)$ and ends at the bottom-right corner $(p=1, P_a=0)$. The two risk points — $(p_1, 1-\alpha)$ and $(p_2, \beta)$ — define the protection levels. A well-designed plan has these two points far apart vertically, with a steep curve between them.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define **Producer's Risk** and **Consumer's Risk** in the context of acceptance sampling. State the typical numerical values used for these risks in industrial practice.

**Model Answer:**

> **Producer's Risk (α):** It is the probability of rejecting a lot whose quality is at or better than the Acceptable Quality Level (AQL). It is the risk borne by the manufacturer that a good lot will be unnecessarily rejected. Numerically, it is computed as $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$, where $p_1$ is the AQL.
>
> **Consumer's Risk (β):** It is the probability of accepting a lot whose quality is at or worse than the Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD). It is the risk borne by the customer that a bad lot will be incorrectly accepted. Numerically, $\beta = P_a(p_2)$, where $p_2$ is the LTPD.
>
> **Typical Industrial Values:** $\alpha = 0.05$ (5%) and $\beta = 0.10$ (10%) are conventional starting points, though aerospace and pharmaceutical applications may use $\alpha = 0.01$ and $\beta = 0.05$ for tighter control.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** What is an **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve**? Sketch its shape and indicate the points corresponding to Producer's Risk and Consumer's Risk.

**Model Answer:**

> The Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve is a plot of the probability of lot acceptance $P_a$ against the true incoming lot fraction defective $p$, for a given sampling plan $(n, c)$.
>
> **Shape:** A monotonically decreasing curve starting at $P_a = 1$ when $p = 0$ (perfect lot always accepted) and falling to $P_a = 0$ when $p = 1$ (worst lot always rejected). The curve has an S-shape (sigmoidal) for typical plan parameters.
>
> **Key Points Marked:**
> - At $p = p_1$ (AQL): $P_a = 1 - \alpha$ — this vertical gap from the top represents Producer's Risk $\alpha$.
> - At $p = p_2$ (LTPD): $P_a = \beta$ — this vertical gap from the bottom represents Consumer's Risk $\beta$.
>
> A desirable OC curve is **steep** in the indifference zone between $p_1$ and $p_2$, indicating strong discrimination between good and bad lots.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** A single sampling plan by attributes has $n = 75$ and $c = 2$.

**(a)** [7 marks] The AQL is specified as 2% and LTPD as 8%. Calculate the Producer's Risk $\alpha$ and Consumer's Risk $\beta$ using the Poisson approximation. Comment on whether the plan is satisfactory if the design requirement is $\alpha \le 0.05$ and $\beta \le 0.10$.

**(b)** [7 marks] If rectifying inspection is performed with lot size $N = 2000$, compute the Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) at $p = 0.05$ and estimate the AOQL by computing AOQ for $p \in \{0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.10\}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Computation of $\alpha$ and $\beta$:**

At AQL, $p_1 = 0.02$, so $n p_1 = 75 \times 0.02 = 1.5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(p_1) &= \sum_{d=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-1.5}(1.5)^d}{d!} \\
&= e^{-1.5}\left[\frac{1}{0!} + \frac{1.5}{1!} + \frac{(1.5)^2}{2!}\right] \\
&= 0.2231 \times [1 + 1.5 + 1.125] \\
&= 0.2231 \times 3.625 \\
&= 0.8087
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= 1 - P_a(p_1) = 1 - 0.8087 = 0.1913
\end{aligned}
$$

At LTPD, $p_2 = 0.08$, so $n p_2 = 75 \times 0.08 = 6.0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(p_2) &= \sum_{d=0}^{2} \frac{e^{-6}(6)^d}{d!} \\
&= e^{-6}\left[1 + 6 + 18\right] \\
&= 0.00248 \times 25 \\
&= 0.0620
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= P_a(p_2) = 0.0620
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** $\alpha = 0.1913 > 0.05$ — **does not meet** the producer's risk requirement. However, $\beta = 0.0620 < 0.10$ — meets the consumer's risk requirement. The plan is **not satisfactory** as it stands; $n$ must be increased or $c$ decreased to reduce $\alpha$.

**(b) AOQ and AOQL Computation:**

We use the formula $AOQ = P_a(p) \cdot p \cdot (N - n)/N$ with $N = 2000$, $n = 75$, so $(N-n)/N = 1925/2000 = 0.9625$.

| $p$ | $np$ | $P_a$ (Poisson, $c=2$) | $AOQ = 0.9625 \cdot P_a \cdot p$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.02 | 1.50 | 0.8087 | 0.01557 |
| 0.04 | 3.00 | 0.4232 | 0.01629 |
| 0.06 | 4.50 | 0.1736 | 0.01003 |
| 0.08 | 6.00 | 0.0620 | 0.00477 |
| 0.10 | 7.50 | 0.0203 | 0.00195 |

$AOQ$ at $p = 0.05$: Interpolating between $p = 0.04$ and $p = 0.06$:

$np = 3.75$, $P_a \approx 0.2774$ (interpolated), $AOQ = 0.9625 \times 0.2774 \times 0.05 = 0.01335$.

**Estimated $AOQL \approx 0.01629$ occurring near $p = 0.04$**, i.e., about **1.63%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $\alpha$ and $\beta$ — remember, $\alpha$ is at the AQL (good quality) and $\beta$ is at the LTPD (bad quality). The OC curve falls as $p$ increases, so $P_a$ is *high* at AQL and *low* at LTPD.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Alternate]** For a single sampling plan with $n = 100$ and $c = 3$:

**(a)** [7 marks] Construct the OC curve for $p \in \{0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.10\}$ using the Poisson approximation. Hence identify the AQL and LTPD for $\alpha = 0.05$ and $\beta = 0.10$ as closely as possible.

**(b)** [7 marks] Compute the Average Total Inspection (ATI) per lot for $N = 1500$ at $p = 0.04$. Compare it with the ATI under $N = 500$. Comment on the inspection cost implications.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) OC Curve Construction:**

Using $\lambda = np$ and $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{3} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^d}{d!}$:

| $p$ | $np$ | $P_a$ (Poisson, $c=3$) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.01 | 1.00 | 0.9810 |
| 0.02 | 2.00 | 0.8571 |
| 0.03 | 3.00 | 0.6472 |
| 0.05 | 5.00 | 0.2650 |
| 0.07 | 7.00 | 0.0818 |
| 0.10 | 10.00 | 0.0103 |

**Identification of AQL:** AQL is the $p_1$ at which $P_a = 1 - \alpha = 0.95$.

- At $p = 0.01$: $P_a = 0.981$ → $\alpha = 0.019$ (below 0.05) ✓
- At $p = 0.02$: $P_a = 0.857$ → $\alpha = 0.143$ (above 0.05) ✗

So **$p_1 \approx$ AQL is between 0.01 and 0.02**; more precisely, $p_1 \approx 0.012$.

**Identification of LTPD:** LTPD is the $p_2$ at which $P_a = \beta = 0.10$.

- At $p = 0.07$: $P_a = 0.082$ (below 0.10)
- At $p = 0.06$: $np = 6.0$, $P_a = 0.151$ (above 0.10)

So **$p_2 \approx$ LTPD lies between 0.06 and 0.07**; approximately $p_2 \approx 0.065$.

**(b) ATI Computation:**

At $p = 0.04$, $np = 100 \times 0.04 = 4.0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(0.04) &= \sum_{d=0}^{3} \frac{e^{-4}(4)^d}{d!} \\
&= e^{-4}\left[1 + 4 + 8 + 10.667\right] \\
&= 0.01832 \times 23.667 \\
&= 0.4335
\end{aligned}
$$

For $N = 1500$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ATI &= n + (1 - P_a)(N - n) \\
&= 100 + (1 - 0.4335)(1500 - 100) \\
&= 100 + 0.5665 \times 1400 \\
&= 100 + 793.1 \\
&= 893.1 \text{ items per lot}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $N = 500$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ATI &= 100 + (1 - 0.4335)(500 - 100) \\
&= 100 + 0.5665 \times 400 \\
&= 100 + 226.6 \\
&= 326.6 \text{ items per lot}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Inspection Cost:** Tripling the lot size from 500 to 1500 nearly triples the ATI (from 327 to 893), reflecting the higher probability that large lots will fall into the rectifying inspection path. Inspection cost scales roughly linearly with lot size for fixed plan parameters. To control inspection cost on larger lots, double or multiple sampling plans (with lower ATI in the indifference zone) are preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** A common KTU valuation error is computing ATI using $N$ instead of $N - n$ in the rectifying term. The logic is: if the lot is rejected, we have *already* inspected $n$ sample items, so we only need to inspect the *remaining* $N - n$ items to complete 100% screening. The correct formula is $ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Sampling Risks (Module 4)**
