- **Two Risks, Two Parties:** Producer's Risk $\alpha$ (Type I) hurts the *manufacturer*; Consumer's Risk $\beta$ (Type II) hurts the *buyer*. They are evaluated at *different* values of $p$ — $\alpha$ at AQL, $\beta$ at LTPD.

- **The Core Trade-off Equation:**
  - $P_a(p_1) = 1 - \alpha$ (acceptance at good quality)
  - $P_a(p_2) = \beta$ (acceptance at bad quality)

- **OC Curve Geometry:** Starts at $(0, 1)$ and ends at $(1, 0)$. Steeper curves = better discrimination. The "indifference zone" between $p_1$ and $p_2$ is where the curve transitions.

- **Probability Model Selection:**
  - **Binomial** for exact computation: $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$
  - **Poisson** when $n \ge 50$, $p \le 0.10$, $np \le 5$: $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$
  - **Normal** for $np(1-p) > 5$ with continuity correction.

- **AOQ Formulas (Rectifying Inspection):**
  - $AOQ = P_a \cdot p$ for large lots
  - $AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ for finite lots
  - **AOQL** = maximum of AOQ curve — a guarantee to the consumer

- **ATI Formulas:**
  - **Single sampling:** $ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$
  - **Double sampling:** $ATI = n_1 + n_2 P_1 + (1 - P_a)(N - n_1 - n_2)$
  - ATI is the **cost function** of the sampling plan.

- **Sample Size Determination (LTPD method):**
  $$n = \frac{\chi^2_{1-\alpha,\ 2c+2}}{2 p_2}$$
  for $c \ge 1$ (chi-square approximation with $2c + 2$ degrees of freedom).

- **Rectifying vs. Non-Rectifying:** Rectifying inspection (replace defectives in rejected lots) is the standard assumption for AOQ/ATI calculations. Without it, no items are removed and $AOQ = P_a \cdot p$ is meaningless.

- **Common KTU Numerical Tasks:**
  1. Compute $\alpha$ and $\beta$ given $n, c, p_1, p_2$.
  2. Determine $n$ given $c, p_1, p_2, \alpha, \beta$.
  3. Construct OC curve (table of $P_a$ vs. $p$).
  4. Compute AOQ and find AOQL.
  5. Compute ATI and compare plans.

- **Units & Conventions:**
  - $p$ is dimensionless (fraction, sometimes expressed as %).
  - AQL and LTPD are typically given in percent (e.g., AQL = 1%, LTPD = 5%).
  - $P_a$ is dimensionless (probability, range 0 to 1).

- **Examiner's Pet Traps:**
  - Forgetting to subtract from 1 for $\alpha$: use $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$, not $\alpha = P_a(p_1)$.
  - Using $N$ instead of $N - n$ in ATI/AOQ formulas.
  - Computing $\beta$ at AQL and $\alpha$ at LTPD — always swap the risk to the right quality level.
  - Confusing AOQ (function of $p$) with AOQL (single number — the maximum of AOQ).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Sampling for Stipulated Producer's Risk and Consumer's Risk; Concepts of AQL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Design of Sampling for Stipulated Producer's Risk and Consumer's Risk; Concepts of AQL ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Acceptance Sampling Inspection, when a **sampling plan** is designed, two mutually opposing parties are involved — the **Producer (Manufacturer)** and the **Consumer (Buyer)**. Each party wishes to be protected against an unfavourable decision on lots whose quality differs from the desired standard. This protection is quantified by two probability values, called **risks**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Producer's Risk (α):** The probability of **rejecting** a lot whose quality is at (or better than) the **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)**. Mathematically, α = 1 − Pₐ(p₁), where p₁ = AQL. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Consumer's Risk (β):** The probability of **accepting** a lot whose quality is at (or worse than) the **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD)**, also called the **Rejectable Quality Level (RQL)** or **Limiting Quality (LQ)**. Mathematically, β = Pₐ(p₂), where p₂ = LTPD. > [!NOTE] > **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL):** The maximum fraction (or percentage) of defective items in a lot that can be considered *tolerable* as a **process average**. It is the quality level for which the producer is willing to accept most of the submitted lots. The tool used to visualize the behaviour of a sampling plan over a range of incoming quality is the **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** — a plot of **Pₐ (Probability of Acceptance)** versus the **incoming fraction defective p**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **hospital diagnostic test** for a disease: - The **Producer's Risk α** is like a "false alarm" — the test wrongly rejects a *healthy* patient (analogous to rejecting a *good* lot at AQL). - The **Consumer's Risk β** is like a "missed detection" — the test wrongly accepts a *diseased* patient (analogous to accepting a *bad* lot at LTPD). Just as a doctor wants both a low false-alarm rate **and** a low missed-detection rate, an engineer designing a sampling plan wants both α and β to be small (typically **α = 0.05** and **β = 0.10**). The two specific points (p₁ = AQL, Pₐ = 1 − α) and (p₂ = LTPD, Pₐ = β) act as **anchor points** that fix the shape and position of the OC curve — and hence uniquely determine the sample size **n** and acceptance number **c**. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** "AQL protects the producer (α is *above* AQL on the rejection side). LTPD protects the consumer (β is the tail-acceptance at LTPD)." ### 1.3 Standard Risk Levels Used in KTU / Industry | Symbol | Standard Value | Interpretation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **α (Producer's Risk)** | **0.05** | 5% chance of rejecting a good lot | | **β (Consumer's Risk)** | **0.10** | 10% chance of accepting a bad lot | | AQL (p₁) | 0.1% to 10% | Process average quality | | LTPD (p₂) | 1% to 10% | Worst tolerable incoming quality | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The OC Curve — Foundation of Risk Analysis For a **single sampling plan** with sample size **n** and acceptance number **c**, the probability of acceptance, when the true fraction defective is **p**, follows the **Binomial distribution** (for large lots): $$P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}$$ For large **n** and small **p**, the **Poisson approximation** with parameter **m = np** is used: $$P_a(p) \approx \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^{i}}{i!}$$ The OC curve is a **monotonically decreasing** function of p: Pₐ(0) = 1 (perfect lot always accepted) and Pₐ(1) = 0 (completely defective lot always rejected). ### 2.2 Design Philosophy — Stipulating α and β The **design problem** is: *Given* p₁ (AQL), α, p₂ (LTPD), and β, *find* the pair (n, c) such that: $$P_a(p_1) = 1 - \alpha \quad \text{and} \quad P_a(p_2) = \beta$$ This means the OC curve must pass through **two prescribed points**: (p₁, 1 − α) and (p₂, β). The design therefore has **two equations in two unknowns** (n, c). ### 2.3 Normal Approximation to Binomial Because exact binomial summation is tedious, KTU-style design uses the **normal approximation**. For large n: $$P_a(p) \approx \Phi\!\left( \frac{np - c}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}} \right)$$ Let **q = 1 − p**. Setting the two anchor conditions: $$\frac{n p_1 - c}{\sqrt{n p_1 q_1}} = z_{1-\alpha} = z_{\alpha} \quad (\text{upper } \alpha \text{ point})$$ $$\frac{n p_2 - c}{\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}} = -z_{\beta}$$ where z_{1−α} is the standard normal value at cumulative probability (1−α), and −z_β is the standard normal value at cumulative probability β. ### 2.4 Solving the Two-Equation System Subtracting the two normal equations: $$n(p_1 - p_2) = z_{\alpha}\sqrt{n p_1 q_1} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}$$ Squaring and rearranging yields a **quadratic in √n**: $$(p_1 - p_2)^2 \cdot n = z_{\alpha}^2 \, p_1 q_1 + z_{\beta}^2 \, p_2 q_2 + 2 z_{\alpha} z_{\beta} \sqrt{p_1 q_1 p_2 q_2} \cdot \sqrt{n}$$ Let $A = (p_1 - p_2)^2$, $B = 2 z_{\alpha} z_{\beta} \sqrt{p_1 q_1 p_2 q_2}$, and $C = -(z_{\alpha}^2 p_1 q_1 + z_{\beta}^2 p_2 q_2)$. Then: $$A n - B \sqrt{n} - C' = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{n} = \frac{B + \sqrt{B^2 + 4AC}}{2A}$$ The acceptance number c is then obtained from: $$c = n p_2 + z_{\beta}\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}$$ Since **c must be an integer**, the value is **rounded down** to the nearest integer (to maintain consumer protection). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below collects every formula needed for KTU board-level problems on this topic. | # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Binomial Pₐ | $P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i}(1-p)^{n-i}$ | Exact, p in [0,1] | | 2 | Poisson Pₐ | $P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^{i}}{i!}$ | Use when n ≥ 50, p ≤ 0.1 | | 3 | Normal approx. Pₐ | $P_a(p) \approx \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{np - c}{\sqrt{np(1-p)}}\right)$ | φ is standard normal | | 4 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ | p₁ = AQL | | 5 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ | p₂ = LTPD | | 6 | Sample size (n) | $\sqrt{n} = \dfrac{z_{\alpha}\sqrt{p_1 q_1} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_2 q_2}}{p_1 - p_2}$ | **Direct formula (preferred in KTU)** | | 7 | Acceptance number (c) | $c = n p_2 + z_{\beta}\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}$ | Round down to integer | | 8 | Discriminating Ratio | $DR = p_2 / p_1 = LTPD / AQL$ | Steepness of OC curve | | 9 | Quality protection index | $Q = \dfrac{p_2 - p_1}{\sqrt{p_1 q_1 + p_2 q_2}}$ | Used in some KTU derivations | | 10 | AQL definition | Max % defective considered acceptable | Convention: p₁ (decimal) | > [!IMPORTANT] > In the **Direct formula (row 6)**, z_{α} and z_{β} are *upper* tail standard normal values: for α = 0.05, z_{0.05} = 1.645; for β = 0.10, z_{0.10} = 1.28. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive industry**: Sampling bolts, fasteners, and electronic components to AQL = 0.1% with α = 0.05. - **Pharmaceutical QC**: Sterility testing where consumer's risk must be very small (β = 0.01). - **Aerospace parts**: AS9100-compliant sampling plans for high-cost turbine blades. - **Food packaging**: AQL-based inspection of sealed cans for leak defects. - **Textile mills**: LTPD-based rejection of fabric rolls containing weaving defects. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Full Derivation — Sample Size Formula (Direct Method) **Starting Point:** The two anchor conditions using the normal approximation: $$\frac{n p_1 - c}{\sqrt{n p_1 q_1}} = z_{\alpha} \quad \quad (1)$$ $$\frac{n p_2 - c}{\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}} = -z_{\beta} \quad \quad (2)$$ **Step 1: Express c from equation (1):** $$c = n p_1 - z_{\alpha}\sqrt{n p_1 q_1}$$ **Step 2: Equate with c from equation (2):** $$n p_1 - z_{\alpha}\sqrt{n p_1 q_1} = n p_2 + z_{\beta}\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}$$ **Step 3: Rearrange by grouping n-terms on the left and z-terms on the right:** $$n(p_1 - p_2) = z_{\alpha}\sqrt{n p_1 q_1} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}$$ **Step 4: Divide both sides by (p₁ − p₂) to isolate √n:** $$n = \frac{z_{\alpha}\sqrt{n p_1 q_1} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}}{p_1 - p_2}$$ **Step 5: Factor √n from the right side (since √(npq) = √n·√(pq)):** $$n = \sqrt{n} \cdot \frac{z_{\alpha}\sqrt{p_1 q_1} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_2 q_2}}{p_1 - p_2}$$ **Step 6: Divide by √n on both sides to obtain the direct formula:** $$\sqrt{n} = \frac{z_{\alpha}\sqrt{p_1 q_1} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_2 q_2}}{p_1 - p_2}$$ **Step 7: Square to get n:** $$n = \left( \frac{z_{\alpha}\sqrt{p_1 q_1} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_2 q_2}}{p_1 - p_2} \right)^{2}$$ **Step 8: Substitute c from equation (2) into the n-equation to confirm consistency:** $$c = n p_2 - (-z_{\beta})\sqrt{n p_2 q_2} = n p_2 + z_{\beta}\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}$$ This is the **acceptance number**. Since c must be an integer, **round down** to be conservative (preserves the consumer's risk bound). --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** Design a single sampling plan with AQL (p₁) = 0.01, α = 0.05, LTPD (p₂) = 0.05, β = 0.10. **Step 1: Identify standard normal values** For α = 0.05 → z_{0.05} = 1.645 (upper 5% point) For β = 0.10 → z_{0.10} = 1.28 **Step 2: Compute q₁ and q₂** $$q_1 = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$ $$q_2 = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$$ **Step 3: Compute p₁q₁ and p₂q₂** $$p_1 q_1 = 0.01 \times 0.99 = 0.0099$$ $$p_2 q_2 = 0.05 \times 0.95 = 0.0475$$ **Step 4: Compute square roots** $$\sqrt{p_1 q_1} = \sqrt{0.0099} = 0.09950$$ $$\sqrt{p_2 q_2} = \sqrt{0.0475} = 0.21794$$ **Step 5: Apply the direct sample size formula** $$\sqrt{n} = \frac{(1.645)(0.09950) + (1.28)(0.21794)}{0.01 - 0.05}$$ **Step 6: Numerator evaluation** $$1.645 \times 0.09950 = 0.16368$$ $$1.28 \times 0.21794 = 0.27896$$ $$\text{Sum} = 0.16368 + 0.27896 = 0.44264$$ **Step 7: Denominator evaluation** $$0.01 - 0.05 = -0.04$$ **Step 8: Compute √n** $$\sqrt{n} = \frac{0.44264}{-0.04} = -11.066$$ **Step 9: Compute n** $$n = (-11.066)^{2} = 122.46$$ **Step 10: Round up to integer (always round up to maintain both risks)** $$n = 123$$ **Step 11: Compute c** $$c = n p_2 + z_{\beta}\sqrt{n p_2 q_2} = 123 \times 0.05 + 1.28 \times \sqrt{123 \times 0.0475}$$ $$c = 6.15 + 1.28 \times \sqrt{5.8425}$$ $$c = 6.15 + 1.28 \times 2.4172$$ $$c = 6.15 + 3.094$$ $$c = 9.244$$ **Step 12: Round down to integer** $$c = 9$$ **Verification of the plan (n = 123, c = 9):** Using the Poisson approximation with λ = np: At p₁ = 0.01: λ₁ = 1.23 → Pₐ(1.23) ≈ from Poisson tables ≈ 0.9512 ≈ 1 − α ✓ At p₂ = 0.05: λ₂ = 6.15 → Pₐ(6.15) ≈ from Poisson tables ≈ 0.1012 ≈ β ✓ The designed plan is **(n = 123, c = 9)**. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Scenarios | AQL (p₁) | LTPD (p₂) | α | β | Designed n | Designed c | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 0.01 | 0.05 | 0.05 | 0.10 | 123 | 9 | Tight tolerance items | | 0.025 | 0.06 | 0.05 | 0.10 | 152 | 7 | Standard inspection | | 0.04 | 0.08 | 0.05 | 0.10 | 246 | 15 | Lot-by-lot quality control | | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.10 | 245 | 19 | Loose inspection (clothing) | | 0.001 | 0.01 | 0.05 | 0.10 | 175 | 1 | Safety-critical (aerospace) | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 OC Curve — Anchor Points at AQL and LTPD The following Mermaid graph illustrates the relationship between the OC curve, the two risks α and β, and the corresponding AQL/LTPD anchor points. The curve is rendered as a coordinate schematic with labelled intercepts. ```mermaid graph LR A["Pa Axis
Probability of Acceptance
(0 to 1)"] --> B["OC Curve
Monotonically
Decreasing"] C["AQL Point
p1 = 0.01
Pa = 1 - alpha = 0.95"] --> B D["LTPD Point
p2 = 0.05
Pa = beta = 0.10"] --> B B --> E["Reject Region
(Pa less than 0.5)"] B --> F["Accept Region
(Pa greater than 0.5)"] G["Producer Risk alpha
Area Above AQL
where Pa less than 1 - alpha"] --> C H["Consumer Risk beta
Area Below LTPD
where Pa greater than beta"] --> D I["Indifference Quality p0
Pa = 0.50
p0 is midpoint"] --> B ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The OC curve drops from Pₐ = 1 (left, perfect lot) toward Pₐ = 0 (right, fully defective lot). The two anchor points (AQL on the left at high Pₐ, LTPD on the right at low Pₐ) "pin" the curve, and the steepness between them is controlled by the sample size n. ### 4.2 Design Procedure — Block-Level Flow ```mermaid graph TD stepA["Input Parameters
AQL p1, alpha
LTPD p2, beta"] --> stepB["Lookup Standard Normal
z_alpha, z_beta"] stepB --> stepC["Compute q1 = 1 - p1
Compute q2 = 1 - p2"] stepC --> stepD["Compute Numerator
z_alpha sqrt p1 q1 + z_beta sqrt p2 q2"] stepD --> stepE["Compute Denominator
p1 - p2"] stepE --> stepF["Compute sqrt n
Numerator / Denominator"] stepF --> stepG["Square to get n
Round up to integer"] stepG --> stepH["Compute c
n p2 + z_beta sqrt n p2 q2"] stepH --> stepI["Round Down c
to nearest integer"] stepI --> stepJ["Verify Plan
Check Pa at AQL and LTPD"] stepJ --> stepK{"Both Risks
Satisfied?"} stepK -->|"Yes"| stepL["Final Plan n, c
Documented"] stepK -->|"No"| stepM["Adjust alpha or beta
Recompute"] stepM --> stepB ``` ### 4.3 Risk Geometry — Visual Summary ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Producer["PRODUCER SIDE"] P1["Quality at AQL p1
Good Lot"] --> P2["Acceptance Probability
1 minus alpha = 0.95"] P2 --> P3["Decision: ACCEPT
Producer Happy"] P1 --> P4["Risk alpha = 0.05
Lot REJECTED wrongly"] end subgraph Consumer["CONSUMER SIDE"] C1["Quality at LTPD p2
Bad Lot"] --> C2["Acceptance Probability
beta = 0.10"] C2 --> C3["Decision: ACCEPT
Consumer Unhappy"] C1 --> C4["Risk beta already absorbed
Lot REJECTED rightly"] end Producer --- Consumer ``` > [!TIP] > The two **subgraphs** show how the same plan protects two different parties through different *probabilities* at *different* quality points. A single OC curve simultaneously serves both. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define Producer's Risk and Consumer's Risk in acceptance sampling. Why is it impossible to make both zero simultaneously? **Model Answer:** **Producer's Risk (α):** The probability that a sampling plan will reject a lot whose true quality is at the AQL. It represents the producer's exposure to the wrongful rejection of acceptable lots. Numerically, $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ where p₁ = AQL. **Consumer's Risk (β):** The probability that a sampling plan will accept a lot whose true quality is at the LTPD. It represents the consumer's exposure to the wrongful acceptance of bad lots. Numerically, $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ where p₂ = LTPD. **Why both cannot be zero:** The two risks cannot simultaneously be zero because the OC curve is a smooth, monotonically decreasing function of p. Forcing Pₐ(p₁) = 1 and Pₐ(p₂) = 0 would require an infinitely steep curve, which is achievable only in the **impossible limit n → ∞, c/n → p₁** (a 100% inspection, not a sampling plan). For any finite n, there is always some non-zero probability of misclassification at any cut-off point. Hence, industry accepts small but non-zero risks, conventionally α = 0.05 and β = 0.10. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Explain the concept of AQL. Why is AQL specified as a process average rather than a lot-by-lot criterion? **Model Answer:** **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL):** The maximum percentage of defective items that, for the purpose of acceptance sampling, can be considered *satisfactory* as a process average. It is the quality level at which the producer is willing to risk few rejections (typically 5%). **Why process average, not per-lot:** AQL is specified as a process average because: 1. **Statistical reasoning:** No single lot will exactly match its true fraction defective. Some variation is natural. Specifying AQL as a process average acknowledges that some lots will be slightly above and some slightly below p₁, and the plan is designed to *accept the average run* of such lots. 2. **Practical feasibility:** If AQL were a strict per-lot criterion, every minor fluctuation in production would trigger rejection, leading to excessive disruption and cost. 3. **Economic balance:** The process average reflects long-run manufacturing capability — the level at which the producer can sustain the process economically. 4. **OC curve meaning:** Pₐ(p₁) = 1 − α is a long-run probability of acceptance; it represents the fraction of *many* such lots that would be accepted, not a guarantee for a single lot. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the formula for sample size *n* of a single sampling plan for stipulated producer's risk α and consumer's risk β, given AQL = p₁ and LTPD = p₂. Use the normal approximation to the binomial distribution. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A company wants to design a single sampling plan such that lots with 1% defectives are accepted 95% of the time (α = 0.05) and lots with 5% defectives are accepted at most 10% of the time (β = 0.10). Determine the values of n and c. **Model Solution for (a):** See Section 3.1 above for the **complete 8-step derivation** of: $$\sqrt{n} = \frac{z_{\alpha}\sqrt{p_1 q_1} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_2 q_2}}{p_1 - p_2}$$ and acceptance number: $$c = n p_2 + z_{\beta}\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}$$ with rounding conventions **n round up, c round down**. **[7 marks breakdown: 3 marks for stating the two normal-equations, 2 marks for algebraic manipulation, 2 marks for arriving at the final formula with conditions.]** **Model Solution for (b):** Given: p₁ = 0.01, p₂ = 0.05, α = 0.05, β = 0.10. Standard normal values: z_{0.05} = 1.645, z_{0.10} = 1.28. q₁ = 0.99, q₂ = 0.95. p₁q₁ = 0.0099, p₂q₂ = 0.0475. √(p₁q₁) = 0.0995, √(p₂q₂) = 0.2179. Apply direct formula: $$\sqrt{n} = \frac{(1.645)(0.0995) + (1.28)(0.2179)}{0.01 - 0.05}$$ $$= \frac{0.1637 + 0.2789}{-0.04} = \frac{0.4426}{-0.04} = -11.066$$ $$n = 122.46 \rightarrow n = 123 \text{ (rounded up)}$$ Compute c: $$c = (123)(0.05) + (1.28)\sqrt{(123)(0.0475)}$$ $$= 6.15 + (1.28)\sqrt{5.8425}$$ $$= 6.15 + (1.28)(2.4172)$$ $$= 6.15 + 3.094 = 9.244 \rightarrow c = 9 \text{ (rounded down)}$$ **The designed single sampling plan is (n = 123, c = 9).** **Verification:** - At p₁ = 0.01 (AQL): λ = np = 1.23. Using Poisson tables, Pₐ(1.23, 9) ≈ 0.9512 ≈ 0.95 ✓ - At p₂ = 0.05 (LTPD): λ = np = 6.15. Pₐ(6.15, 9) ≈ 0.1012 ≈ 0.10 ✓ **[7 marks breakdown: 2 marks for z-values, 2 marks for n computation, 2 marks for c computation, 1 mark for verification.]** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Alternative Choice]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** State and explain the two risks in acceptance sampling. Draw a representative OC curve showing the location of AQL, LTPD, producer's risk, and consumer's risk. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Design a single sampling plan for AQL = 0.025 (1.5% producer's risk) and LTPD = 0.08 (8% consumer's risk). Find n and c. Comment on the discriminating ratio of the resulting plan. **Model Solution for (a):** **Producer's Risk α:** Probability of rejecting a lot of AQL quality. Shown on the OC curve as the vertical gap between Pₐ = 1 and the OC curve at p = p₁ (AQL). Standard value α = 0.05 means 5% of good lots are wrongly rejected. **Consumer's Risk β:** Probability of accepting a lot of LTPD quality. Shown on the OC curve as the vertical height of the OC curve at p = p₂ (LTPD). Standard value β = 0.10 means 10% of bad lots are wrongly accepted. **OC Curve (representative drawing):** A smooth, monotonically decreasing S-shaped curve from (p = 0, Pₐ = 1) to (p = 1, Pₐ = 0). Two anchor points are marked: - (p₁ = AQL, Pₐ = 1 − α) — high acceptance for good quality. - (p₂ = LTPD, Pₐ = β) — low acceptance for bad quality. The area between Pₐ = 1 − α and Pₐ = 1 to the left of p₁ represents α; the area between Pₐ = 0 and Pₐ = β to the right of p₂ represents β. **Model Solution for (b):** Given: p₁ = 0.025, p₂ = 0.08, α = 0.015, β = 0.08. (Using 1.5% and 8% risks; z_{0.015} ≈ 2.17, z_{0.08} ≈ 1.405.) Actually, the standard interpretation is α = 0.05, β = 0.10 unless specified; for this KTU problem, assume: - α = 0.05 → z_{0.05} = 1.645 - β = 0.10 → z_{0.10} = 1.28 q₁ = 0.975, q₂ = 0.92. p₁q₁ = 0.024375, √(p₁q₁) = 0.1561. p₂q₂ = 0.0736, √(p₂q₂) = 0.2713. $$\sqrt{n} = \frac{(1.645)(0.1561) + (1.28)(0.2713)}{0.025 - 0.08}$$ $$= \frac{0.2568 + 0.3473}{-0.055} = \frac{0.6041}{-0.055} = -10.984$$ $$n = 120.65 \rightarrow n = 121$$ $$c = (121)(0.08) + (1.28)\sqrt{(121)(0.0736)}$$ $$= 9.68 + (1.28)\sqrt{8.9056}$$ $$= 9.68 + (1.28)(2.984)$$ $$= 9.68 + 3.820 = 13.50 \rightarrow c = 13$$ **Designed Plan: (n = 121, c = 13).** **Discriminating Ratio:** $$DR = \frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{0.08}{0.025} = 3.2$$ A DR of 3.2 means the plan has moderate discriminating power — for every 3.2-fold increase in incoming fraction defective, the acceptance probability drops from ~0.95 to ~0.10. **Lower DR = sharper discrimination.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake Alerts:** > 1. **Sign error in denominator:** p₁ − p₂ is *negative*. Students often take absolute value, missing the negative sign. The final n comes out the same after squaring, but the intermediate √n value must be negative, which is correct. > 2. **Rounding direction confusion:** n is **rounded up** (to maintain both risks); c is **rounded down** (to be conservative for the consumer). Reversing these compromises one of the risks. > 3. **Wrong z-table lookup:** For α = 0.05 (upper tail), z = 1.645, NOT 1.96. For β = 0.10, z = 1.28, NOT 1.645. Use the *upper* tail value corresponding to the area *beyond* the point. > 4. **Forgetting q = 1 − p:** When p is small, q ≈ 1, but for p₂ = 0.05 or higher, q cannot be dropped. > 5. **No verification:** Always re-check Pₐ at AQL and LTPD after designing. The Poisson tables are the standard KTU verification tool. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save Before Exam):** > - [ ] **Producer's Risk (α)** = P(reject lot of AQL) = 1 − Pₐ(p₁). Standard α = 0.05, z = 1.645. > - [ ] **Consumer's Risk (β)** = P(accept lot of LTPD) = Pₐ(p₂). Standard β = 0.10, z = 1.28. > - [ ] **AQL** = process average, producer's protection point. > - [ ] **LTPD** (or RQL/LQ) = consumer's protection point. > - [ ] **OC curve** plots Pₐ vs p, monotonically decreasing. > - [ ] **Direct sample-size formula** (memorize!): > > $$\sqrt{n} = \frac{z_{\alpha}\sqrt{p_1 q_1} + z_{\beta}\sqrt{p_2 q_2}}{p_1 - p_2}$$ > - [ ] **Acceptance number**: > > $$c = n p_2 + z_{\beta}\sqrt{n p_2 q_2}$$ > - [ ] **Rounding:** n ↑ (round up), c ↓ (round down). > - [ ] **Verification** via Poisson tables: λ = np. > - [ ] **Discriminating Ratio** DR = p₂ / p₁ — lower is sharper. > - [ ] **AQL ≠ p** for a single lot; AQL is a process-level specification. > - [ ] The OC curve **anchored at two points** (AQL & LTPD) uniquely fixes the plan (n, c) under normal approximation. > - [ ] **Key z-values table:** z_{0.05} = 1.645, z_{0.10} = 1.28, z_{0.025} = 1.96, z_{0.01} = 2.33. ---

topics.title: LTPD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LTPD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective)** is defined as the poorest level of lot quality that the **consumer** is willing to accept in an individual lot submitted for inspection, with a low probability of acceptance equal to the **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$)**. By convention, $\beta$ is fixed at **0.10 (10%)** in KTU 2024 Scheme problems unless stated otherwise.

In symbolic form, LTPD is the value of the incoming fraction defective $p$ such that:

$$P_a(p = LTPD) = \beta = 0.10$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Memorize):**
> "LTPD is the quality level of a submitted lot, expressed in percent defective, which is to be accepted only a small specified percentage of the time (typically 10%). It is the point on the Operating Characteristic (OC) curve corresponding to the consumer's risk $\beta$."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Strict Customer Filter"

Imagine you are a pharmaceutical company buying **10,000 tablets per lot** from a supplier.

- You tell the supplier: *"If at most **2%** of tablets are defective, I'll usually accept the lot. But if the defect rate is as bad as **5%**, I want to reject that lot **9 out of 10 times**."*
- That **2%** is your **AQL** (Acceptable Quality Level).
- That **5%** is your **LTPD** — the *worst* quality you tolerate, but you want to catch it with **90% confidence (1 − $\beta$)**.
- The supplier's risk of being wrongly rejected at AQL is $\alpha$.
- Your risk of wrongly accepting at LTPD is $\beta = 0.10$.

**LTPD is therefore the consumer's "alarm trigger"** — the quality level beyond which the consumer demands near-certain rejection.

### 1.3 Standard Quality Metrics Summary

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Stakeholder |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Acceptable Quality Level | AQL | 0.001 – 0.10 | Producer |
| Lot Tolerance Percent Defective | **LTPD** | 0.01 – 0.10 | **Consumer** |
| Producer's Risk | $\alpha$ | **0.05** | Producer |
| Consumer's Risk | $\beta$ | **0.10** | Consumer |
| Sample Size | $n$ | — | Both |
| Acceptance Number | $c$ | — | Both |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Point OC Curve Anchor:** Every KTU acceptance sampling plan is anchored on two points: $(AQL, 1 - \alpha)$ and $(LTPD, \beta)$. The plan must pass *through* both these points approximately on the OC curve.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — Visualizing LTPD

The OC curve plots the probability of acceptance $P_a$ against the incoming fraction defective $p$. The **LTPD** marks the consumer-side "knee" of this curve.

- For $p <$ LTPD → $P_a$ is high (consumer is happy)
- For $p =$ LTPD → $P_a = \beta = 0.10$ (low acceptance — the lot is on the boundary)
- For $p >$ LTPD → $P_a$ drops sharply (consumer rejects aggressively)

### 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — Probability of Acceptance

For a **single sampling plan by attributes** with sample size $n$ and acceptance number $c$, the probability of acceptance follows the **binomial distribution**:

$$P_a = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \binom{n}{x} p^{x} (1 - p)^{n - x}$$

For engineering problems with small $p$ and moderate-to-large $n$ (the standard KTU 2024 assumption), the **Poisson approximation** is used:

$$P_a \approx \sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np} (np)^{x}}{x!}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always state the approximation being used. The Poisson approximation is **mandatory** in KTU boards for problems involving LTPD tables.

### 2.3 The LTPD Quality Index $K$ — The Heart of the Topic

Define the **LTPD Index** (or quality index) as:

$$K = n \cdot LTPD$$

By the Poisson approximation, $K$ depends **only on** $c$ and the desired $\beta$ (almost always 0.10). This is why $K$ is tabulated.

The **design equation** is therefore:

$$n = \frac{K}{LTPD}$$

The value of $n$ is **rounded UP** to the next integer to be conservative (guarantee $\beta \leq 0.10$).

### 2.4 KTU Standard $K$-Value Table (for $\beta = 0.10$)

| $c$ | $K = n \cdot LTPD$ | $c$ | $K = n \cdot LTPD$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 2.303 | 6 | 10.532 |
| 1 | 3.890 | 7 | 11.771 |
| 2 | 5.322 | 8 | 12.995 |
| 3 | 6.681 | 9 | 14.206 |
| 4 | 7.994 | 10 | 15.407 |
| 5 | 9.275 | 11 | 16.598 |

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read:** If you choose $c = 2$, the product $n \cdot LTPD$ must equal **5.322** to achieve $\beta = 0.10$. Any combination of $n$ and $LTPD$ giving the same product will yield the same OC curve shape.

### 2.5 Design Procedure for LTPD-Based Single Sampling Plan

**Given:** $LTPD$, $\beta$ (default 0.10), and a chosen $c$ (or solve for both $n$ and $c$).

**Step 1:** Pick a value of $c$ (acceptance number) — KTU typically gives 2–3 options.

**Step 2:** Read $K$ from the table for that $c$.

**Step 3:** Compute $n = K / LTPD$.

**Step 4:** Round $n$ up to the next whole number.

**Step 5 (Validation):** Verify the plan by computing $P_a$ at $p = LTPD$ and confirm it is $\leq 0.10$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defense Procurement (MIL-STD-105E, now ANSI/ASQ Z1.4):** Each inspection level is calibrated against both AQL and LTPD.
- **Automotive Industry (IATF 16949):** Critical characteristics (safety items) use aggressive LTPD values like 0.01% or 0.05%.
- **Pharmaceutical QC:** Drug batch release uses very tight LTPDs (often 0.1%) with $\beta = 0.10$.
- **Electronics Manufacturing (SMT lines):** Component acceptance uses LTPD-based plans for incoming inspection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the $K$-Value Relationship

Starting from the Poisson acceptance probability for a single sampling plan:

$$P_a = \sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^x}{x!}$$

Impose the LTPD boundary condition $p = LTPD$ and $P_a = \beta = 0.10$:

$$\sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-n \cdot LTPD}(n \cdot LTPD)^x}{x!} = 0.10$$

Let $\mu = n \cdot LTPD$. Then:

$$\sum_{x=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-\mu}\mu^{x}}{x!} = 0.10$$

This is a **transcendental equation in $\mu$** that cannot be solved in closed form. For each integer $c$, numerical methods (Newton-Raphson or direct table lookup) yield the unique $\mu$ satisfying the equation. This $\mu$ is precisely the $K$-value.

**Worked example for $c = 2$:**

We need $\mu$ such that:

$$e^{-\mu} \left[ 1 + \mu + \frac{\mu^2}{2} \right] = 0.10$$

Try $\mu = 5.0$: $e^{-5}(1 + 5 + 12.5) = 0.006738 \times 18.5 = 0.1246$ (too high).

Try $\mu = 5.5$: $e^{-5.5}(1 + 5.5 + 15.125) = 0.004087 \times 21.625 = 0.0884$ (too low).

Linear interpolation: $\mu \approx 5.0 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{0.1246 - 0.10}{0.1246 - 0.0884} = 5.0 + 0.5 \cdot 0.6714 = 5.336$.

Refining: $\mu = 5.322$ gives $P_a \approx 0.10$ ✓ (matches the standard table).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — LTPD Problem Types in KTU 2024

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Approach |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type 1: Design Plan** | $LTPD$, $c$ | $n$ | $n = K(c) / LTPD$, round up |
| **Type 2: Compute Acceptance Probability** | $n$, $c$, $p$ | $P_a$ | Poisson binomial sum |
| **Type 3: Verify LTPD** | $n$, $c$ | Effective $LTPD$ | $LTPD = K(c) / n$ |
| **Type 4: OC Curve Construction** | $n$, $c$ | $(p, P_a)$ pairs | Tabulate using Poisson for $p = 0, 0.01, 0.02, \ldots$ |
| **Type 5: Compare Two Plans** | Two $(n, c)$ plans | OC curves / compare $LTPD$ | Compute $K/n$ for both |

### 3.3 Full Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]:**

A consumer wants a single sampling plan such that lots with $LTPD = 0.04$ (4% defective) are accepted with probability no more than $\beta = 0.10$. The consumer is willing to accept up to $c = 2$ defectives. Find the required sample size $n$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the design equation. For $c = 2$, the standard $K$-value is **5.322**.

$$K = n \cdot LTPD \implies 5.322 = n \times 0.04$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $n$.

$$n = \frac{5.322}{0.04} = 133.05$$

**Step 3:** Round up to the next integer (conservative rounding for $\beta \leq 0.10$).

$$n = 134$$

**Step 4:** Verify the result. Compute $P_a$ at $p = LTPD = 0.04$ using the Poisson approximation with $np = 134 \times 0.04 = 5.36$:

$$P_a = e^{-5.36} \left[ 1 + 5.36 + \frac{5.36^{2}}{2} \right]$$

$$= 0.004685 \times \left[ 1 + 5.36 + 14.3648 \right]$$

$$= 0.004685 \times 20.7248 = 0.0971$$

**Step 5:** Confirm acceptance. Since $P_a = 0.0971 < 0.10 = \beta$ ✓, the plan $(n=134, c=2)$ is **valid**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The consumer must randomly inspect **134 items** from every lot and accept the lot only if **2 or fewer** are defective. This ensures that truly bad lots (4% defective) are wrongly accepted only ~9.7% of the time.

### 3.4 Verification Through a Second Worked Example

**Problem:** A manufacturer specifies $LTPD = 0.02$ and $\beta = 0.10$. Design a plan for $c = 1$.

**Solution:**

- $K$ for $c = 1$ is **3.890**.
- $n = 3.890 / 0.02 = 194.5 \rightarrow$ round up to **$n = 195$**.
- Verification: $np = 195 \times 0.02 = 3.90$.

$$P_a = e^{-3.90} \left[ 1 + 3.90 \right] = 0.02024 \times 4.90 = 0.0992 \approx 0.10 \quad \checkmark$$

The plan $(n=195, c=1)$ is valid.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LTPD Position in the OC Curve — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Lot with fraction defective p] --> B{Pick Random Sample of size n}
    B --> C[Count Defectives x in Sample]
    C --> D{x is less than or equal to c}
    D -- YES --> E[ACCEPT the Lot]
    D -- NO  --> F[REJECT the Lot]
    E --> G[Probability of Acceptance equals Pa]
    F --> H[Probability of Rejection equals 1 minus Pa]

    subgraph OC_CURVE_ANCHORS[OC Curve Anchor Points]
        P1[AQL p equals 0.01, Pa equals 1 minus alpha equals 0.95]
        P2[LTPD p equals 0.05, Pa equals beta equals 0.10]
    end
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture of LTPD-Based Plan Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Input: LTPD value] --> MUX{Choose acceptance number c}
    IN2[Input: Consumer risk beta, default 0.10] --> MUX
    MUX --> C0[c equals 0, K equals 2.303]
    MUX --> C1[c equals 1, K equals 3.890]
    MUX --> C2[c equals 2, K equals 5.322]
    MUX --> C3[c equals 3, K equals 6.681]
    MUX --> C4[c equals 4, K equals 7.994]

    C0 --> CALC[Compute n equals K divided by LTPD]
    C1 --> CALC
    C2 --> CALC
    C3 --> CALC
    C4 --> CALC

    CALC --> ROUND[Round n UP to next integer]
    ROUND --> VERIFY[Verify: Compute Pa at p equals LTPD]
    VERIFY --> CHECK{Pa less than or equal to 0.10}
    CHECK -- YES --> OUT[VALID PLAN: n and c]
    CHECK -- NO  --> BUMP[Increase c or n, then redesign]
    BUMP --> CALC
```

### 4.3 Trade-off Decision Flow — Why Choose One $c$ Over Another

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to design a sampling plan] --> Q1{How tight is the tolerance}
    Q1 -- Very tight LTPD below 0.01 --> HI[Use SMALL c, e.g. c equals 0 or 1]
    Q1 -- Moderate LTPD 0.02 to 0.05 --> MID[Use MEDIUM c, e.g. c equals 2 or 3]
    Q1 -- Loose LTPD above 0.05 --> LO[Use LARGER c, e.g. c equals 5 or higher]

    HI --> H_N[Sample size n becomes LARGE]
    MID --> M_N[Sample size n is MODERATE]
    LO --> L_N[Sample size n becomes SMALL]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define LTPD and state its standard value of consumer's risk.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

LTPD stands for **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective**. It is the poorest quality level in percent defective that a consumer is willing to tolerate in a submitted lot. It corresponds to the **Consumer's Risk $\beta$** on the OC curve. The standard KTU value of $\beta$ is **0.10 (10%)**, meaning that lots at LTPD are accepted with only a 10% probability.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Standard $\beta$ value (1) + Consumer's perspective context (0.5).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the meaning of the quality index $K$ in LTPD-based sampling plan design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The quality index $K$ is defined as the product $K = n \cdot LTPD$ for a single sampling plan under the Poisson approximation. For a given acceptance number $c$ and consumer's risk $\beta = 0.10$, $K$ is a **tabulated constant** (e.g., $K = 5.322$ for $c = 2$). The design equation $n = K / LTPD$ gives the minimum sample size to achieve the desired $\beta$ protection.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Mathematical form (1) + Practical use in design (1).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice — Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Note to Students:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part B for Module 4 offers internal choice. Solve either Option A or Option B completely.

---

#### **Option A — 14 Marks**

**Q.A) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

A company manufactures electrical components. The consumer demands that lots containing **3% defective** items should be rejected **90% of the time**.

**(a)** [7 marks] Design a single sampling plan satisfying $LTPD = 0.03$ and $\beta = 0.10$ for $c = 2$. State the values of $n$ and $c$.

**(b)** [7 marks] For the plan designed in (a), construct the OC curve by computing $P_a$ for $p = 0.00, 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.10$ using the Poisson approximation.

---

**Model Solution (Option A):**

**(a) Design [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Identify $K$ for $c = 2$ from the LTPD table: $K = 5.322$.

**Step 2:** Apply the design equation:

$$n = \frac{K}{LTPD} = \frac{5.322}{0.03} = 177.4$$

**Step 3:** Round up: $n = 178$.

**Step 4:** Verify at $p = 0.03$:

$$np = 178 \times 0.03 = 5.34$$

$$P_a = e^{-5.34} \left[ 1 + 5.34 + \frac{5.34^2}{2} \right] = 0.004798 \times 20.5978 = 0.0988$$

Since $P_a = 0.0988 < 0.10$ ✓, the plan $(n=178, c=2)$ is **valid**.

**Final Answer (a):** $n = 178$, $c = 2$.

---

**(b) OC Curve [7 marks]**

For each $p$, compute $np$ and apply:

$$P_a = e^{-np} \left[ 1 + np + \frac{(np)^2}{2} \right]$$

| $p$ | $np$ | $P_a$ (Poisson, $c=2$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.00 | 0.00 | 1.0000 |
| 0.01 | 1.78 | 0.4795 |
| 0.02 | 3.56 | 0.1424 |
| 0.03 | 5.34 | 0.0988 |
| 0.05 | 8.90 | 0.0065 |
| 0.07 | 12.46 | 0.00004 |
| 0.10 | 17.80 | ≈ 0.0000 |

**Mark Distribution (b):** Correct formula (2) + Correct numerical values for all 7 points (4) + Tabulation/curve interpretation (1).

---

#### **Option B — 14 Marks (Alternative)**

**Q.B) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

A quality inspector for a ball bearing manufacturing firm requires a sampling plan such that lots with **5% defectives** are accepted with probability **at most 0.10**.

**(a)** [7 marks] Design two alternative plans: one with $c = 1$ and another with $c = 3$. Compute the sample sizes and compare.

**(b)** [7 marks] If the lot size is $N = 2000$ and inspection is rectifying (with replacement of defectives), compute the **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** at $p = 0.05$ and $p = 0.10$ for the $c = 1$ plan.

---

**Model Solution (Option B):**

**(a) Design Comparison [7 marks]**

For $c = 1$: $K = 3.890$

$$n_1 = \frac{3.890}{0.05} = 77.8 \rightarrow n_1 = 78$$

Verification: $np = 78 \times 0.05 = 3.90$.

$$P_a = e^{-3.90}[1 + 3.90] = 0.02024 \times 4.90 = 0.0992 \leq 0.10 \quad \checkmark$$

For $c = 3$: $K = 6.681$

$$n_3 = \frac{6.681}{0.05} = 133.62 \rightarrow n_3 = 134$$

**Comparison:** The $c = 3$ plan requires 72% more inspection (134 vs 78) but allows more defectives to slip through, which the consumer accepts as a trade-off for higher overall acceptance probability at lower defect rates.

**Final Answer (a):** Plan 1: $(n=78, c=1)$, Plan 2: $(n=134, c=3)$.

---

**(b) AOQ Computation [7 marks]**

Formula for Average Outgoing Quality (rectifying inspection):

$$AOQ = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}$$

For $N = 2000$ and the $c = 1$ plan ($n = 78$):

**At $p = 0.05$:**

$np = 78 \times 0.05 = 3.90$, $P_a = 0.0992$.

$$AOQ = \frac{0.0992 \times 0.05 \times (2000 - 78)}{2000} = \frac{0.0992 \times 0.05 \times 1922}{2000}$$

$$= \frac{9.5328}{2000} = 0.004766 \approx 0.477\%$$

**At $p = 0.10$:**

$np = 78 \times 0.10 = 7.80$, $P_a = e^{-7.80}[1 + 7.80] = 0.000408 \times 8.80 = 0.003594$.

$$AOQ = \frac{0.003594 \times 0.10 \times 1922}{2000} = \frac{0.6907}{2000} = 0.000345 \approx 0.0345\%$$

**Final Answer (b):** $AOQ(p=0.05) \approx 0.477\%$, $AOQ(p=0.10) \approx 0.0345\%$.

**Mark Distribution (b):** Formula (1.5) + $P_a$ computation at both $p$ values (2.5) + Final AOQ values (3).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LTPD Problems:**
> 1. **Rounding direction error (–2 marks):** Students often round $n$ *down* instead of up. KTU strictly requires rounding **up** to ensure $\beta \leq 0.10$.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify (–2 marks):** Simply stating $n = K/LTPD$ without showing the verification $P_a$ calculation costs full process marks. The final $P_a$ must be $\leq 0.10$.
> 3. **Wrong $K$-value look-up (–1 mark):** Always cross-check that you are using the $K$-value matching the **given $c$**, not a different row.
> 4. **Confusing LTPD with AQL (–3 marks):** LTPD uses $\beta = 0.10$ (consumer's side). AQL uses $1 - \alpha$ (producer's side). These are **two different points** on the same OC curve.
> 5. **Using binomial when Poisson is required (–1 mark):** For LTPD problems with $n > 50$ and $p < 0.10$, always apply the Poisson approximation per KTU 2024 convention.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LTPD = Lot Tolerance Percent Defective** — the worst quality consumer tolerates.
- **Standard consumer's risk** $\beta = 0.10$ (10% acceptance probability at LTPD).
- **Design equation:** $n \cdot LTPD = K$ where $K$ is tabulated for each $c$.
- **Always round $n$ up** to the next integer for conservative consumer protection.
- **Verify** every plan by computing $P_a$ at $p = LTPD$ and checking $P_a \leq 0.10$.
- **Two-point OC anchor:** Every KTU plan is defined by $(AQL, 1-\alpha)$ and $(LTPD, \beta)$.
- **Key $K$-values to memorize:** $c=0 \to 2.303$, $c=1 \to 3.890$, $c=2 \to 5.322$, $c=3 \to 6.681$, $c=4 \to 7.994$, $c=5 \to 9.275$.
- **Higher $c$** → larger $n$ → higher $P_a$ at AQL but same $\beta$ at LTPD.
- **Lower $c$** → smaller $n$ → sharper discrimination between good and bad lots.
- **LTPD ≠ AQL:** Never interchange these in formulas. AQL protects the producer; LTPD protects the consumer.
- **Poisson approximation** is the KTU 2024 default for LTPD calculations; binomial only for small $n$.
- **Trade-off principle:** Decreasing $\beta$ (tighter consumer protection) requires increasing $n$ proportionally via $K$.
- **Rectifying inspection formula:** $AOQ = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)/N$ — applicable whenever a plan is implemented with defect replacement.
- **The OC curve** is **S-shaped**; LTPD sits on the lower-right tail where acceptance probability drops sharply.
- **Engineering analogy:** LTPD is the consumer's "trip-wire" for batch rejection — a quality threshold beyond which incoming material is virtually always refused.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AOQL in Sampling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** AOQL in Sampling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — AVERAGE OUTGOING QUALITY LIMIT (AOQL) IN ACCEPTANCE SAMPLING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL)** is the *maximum* value of the **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** taken over all possible values of the incoming process fraction defective $p \in [0, 1]$, when a **rectifying inspection** acceptance sampling plan is applied.

Mathematically, if $AOQ(p)$ denotes the long-run average fraction defective in the product *shipped to the customer* as a function of the incoming lot quality $p$, then:

$$AOQL = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} \{ AOQ(p) \}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rectifying Inspection** = A sampling procedure in which every rejected lot is subjected to **100% inspection**, with all defective items found being **replaced by good ones** before the lot is shipped. This is the standard assumption on which AOQL tables (e.g., Dodge–Romig tables) are constructed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Quality Sieve"

Imagine a large factory producing **screws**:
- Incoming raw screws arrive with a certain unknown percentage of defective pieces.
- The quality inspector pulls a small **sample** (say 50 screws from 1000). If 1 or fewer are bad → ship the lot. If 2 or more are bad → **screen the entire lot** and replace all bad screws with perfect ones.
- After many such lots, the customer receives a product whose *average* fraction defective is the **AOQ**.
- The **worst-case** average quality that could ever slip through (even if the incoming quality is "just right" to be tricky) is the **AOQL** — the **ceiling of protection** the sampling plan guarantees.

> [!TIP]
> Think of AOQL as the **"quality guarantee sticker"** on your outgoing product — it is the *highest possible average defect rate* the customer will ever see, regardless of how bad (or good) the incoming material happens to be.

### 1.3 Key Terminology in Bold

- **Pa (Probability of Acceptance):** Probability that a lot with fraction defective $p$ is accepted by the sampling plan.
- **AOQ (Average Outgoing Quality):** Expected fraction defective in outgoing product after rectifying inspection.
- **AOQL (Average Outgoing Quality Limit):** Maximum value of AOQ over all $p$.
- **Rectifying Inspection:** Procedure of 100% screening and replacement of defectives in rejected lots.
- **OC Curve (Operating Characteristic Curve):** Plot of $P_a$ versus $p$.
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) / $p_2$:** Worst quality the consumer is willing to accept.
- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level) / $p_1$:** Quality level the producer considers acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AOQ Function — Derivation Logic

Under rectifying inspection with sample size $n$ drawn from a lot of size $N$:

- **Accepted lots** (probability $P_a$): The $(N - n)$ un-inspected items are shipped as-is, containing on average a fraction $p$ defective.
- **Rejected lots** (probability $1 - P_a$): 100% inspection is performed, all defectives replaced → 0 defectives shipped.

Hence the **expected number of defectives shipped per lot** is:

$$E[\text{defectives shipped}] = P_a \times p \times (N - n)$$

Dividing by the lot size $N$ gives the **Average Outgoing Quality**:

$$\boxed{\, AOQ(p) = \frac{P_a(p) \times p \times (N - n)}{N} \,}$$

For the common practical case where $n \ll N$, we use the approximation:

$$AOQ(p) \approx P_a(p) \times p$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\dfrac{N}{N-n}$ (or its inverse depending on convention) appears in textbook treatments where the **sample is removed permanently** (defectives in the sample are *not* replaced). In the Dodge–Romig rectifying framework, the standard formula above applies.

## 2.2 The AOQL — Geometric Interpretation

The **AOQL** is the *peak* (maximum) of the AOQ curve when plotted against $p$:

$$AOQL = \max_{0 \le p \le 1} AOQ(p)$$

The AOQ curve has the following characteristic shape:
- Starts at $0$ when $p = 0$ (perfect incoming → perfect outgoing).
- Rises to a maximum (the AOQL).
- Returns to $0$ as $p \to 1$ (everything rejected → everything 100% screened → perfect outgoing).

This is because when $p$ is very high, no lot is accepted, so 100% rectification yields a perfect outgoing product.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | $P_a$ (single sampling, c=0) | $(1-p)^n$ | Only zero defectives allowed |
| 2 | $P_a$ (single sampling, c=1) | $(1-p)^n + np(1-p)^{n-1}$ | At most one defective |
| 3 | $P_a$ (single sampling, general c) | $\sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Binomial |
| 4 | AOQ | $P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)/N$ | Rectifying inspection |
| 5 | AOQ (approx., large N) | $P_a \cdot p$ | When $n \ll N$ |
| 6 | AOQL | $\max_{p} \, AOQ(p)$ | Found by calculus or table lookup |
| 7 | Consumer's risk | $\beta = 1 - P_a(p_2)$ | $p_2$ = LTPD |
| 8 | Producer's risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ | $p_1$ = AQL |
| 9 | ATI (Avg. Total Inspection) | $n \cdot P_a + N(1-P_a)$ | Cost driver |
| 10 | ASN (Avg. Sample Number) | Depends on plan (single/double/multiple) | For sequential plans |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All values of $p, P_a, AOQ$ are **dimensionless fractions** (or expressed as percentages). When plotting the OC or AOQ curve, $p$ is on the **horizontal axis** and $P_a$ or $AOQ$ is on the **vertical axis**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Industries (Automotive, Electronics, Pharma):** AOQL-based plans (Dodge–Romig) are used to design economical acceptance procedures that **guarantee a maximum defect rate in delivered goods**.
- **Military & Defense Procurement (MIL-STD):** Sampling plans are selected to achieve a specified AOQL, ensuring that even the worst lot does not exceed contractual quality.
- **Software & Hardware Reliability:** Conceptually extended to **mean time to failure (MTTF)** guarantees — a system-level "AOQL" of failures per unit time.
- **Supply Chain Quality Contracts:** AOQL appears in supplier–purchaser agreements as the contractual upper bound on defectives delivered.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of the AOQ Function

**Step 1 — Setting up the rectification framework.**

Let $N$ = lot size, $n$ = sample size, $c$ = acceptance number, $p$ = true incoming fraction defective.

For a single sample of size $n$ drawn from a binomial population, the probability of finding $d$ defectives is:

$$P(D = d) = \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of acceptance.**

The lot is accepted if $D \le c$:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

**Step 3 — Expected defectives in outgoing product from ACCEPTED lots.**

If the lot is accepted, we ship the lot. The un-inspected portion is $(N - n)$ items, and the inspected (sample) items found defective are replaced by good ones. So defectives shipped from accepted lot come **only from the un-inspected portion**:

$$E[\text{defectives} \mid \text{accepted}] = (N - n) \cdot p$$

**Step 4 — Expected defectives from REJECTED lots.**

Rejected lots are 100% inspected and all defectives replaced. So:

$$E[\text{defectives} \mid \text{rejected}] = 0$$

**Step 5 — Unconditional expected defectives per lot.**

Using the law of total expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{defectives per lot}] &= P_a \cdot (N - n) \cdot p \;+\; (1 - P_a) \cdot 0 \\
&= P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Average Outgoing Quality.**

Divide by the lot size $N$:

$$
\boxed{AOQ(p) = \frac{P_a(p) \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}}
$$

For $n \ll N$, the ratio $(N-n)/N \to 1$, giving the compact form:

$$AOQ(p) \approx P_a(p) \cdot p$$

**Step 7 — Computing AOQL.**

To find the maximum, set the derivative of $AOQ$ with respect to $p$ equal to zero:

$$\frac{d}{dp}\left[P_a(p) \cdot p\right] = P_a(p) + p \cdot P_a'(p) = 0$$

In practice, AOQL is found by **trial-and-error tabulation** or by using **published Dodge–Romig AOQL tables**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Finding AOQL for a Single Sampling Plan

**Problem:** A single sampling plan has $N = 2000$, $n = 80$, $c = 2$. Construct the AOQ curve and determine the AOQL.

**Step 1 — Compute $P_a(p)$ for several values of $p$ using the binomial sum:**

$$P_a(p) = (1-p)^{80} + 80 p (1-p)^{79} + \frac{80 \cdot 79}{2} p^2 (1-p)^{78}$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate $P_a$, $AOQ$ for various $p$:**

| $p$ | $P_a(p)$ | $AOQ = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)/N = P_a \cdot p \cdot 0.96$ |
|-----|----------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| 0.005 | 0.9980 | 0.00479 |
| 0.010 | 0.9919 | 0.00952 |
| 0.015 | 0.9803 | 0.01412 |
| 0.020 | 0.9620 | 0.01847 |
| 0.025 | 0.9367 | 0.02248 |
| 0.030 | 0.9043 | 0.02604 |
| **0.035** | **0.8651** | **0.02905** |
| **0.040** | **0.8205** | **0.03150** |
| **0.045** | **0.7716** | **0.03333** |
| **0.050** | **0.7197** | **0.03455** |
| 0.060 | 0.6117 | 0.03523 |
| 0.070 | 0.5049 | 0.03393 |
| 0.080 | 0.4050 | 0.03110 |
| 0.100 | 0.2402 | 0.02305 |

**Step 3 — Identify the maximum.**

The maximum of $AOQ$ occurs at approximately $p \approx 0.06$:

$$\boxed{AOQL \approx 0.0352 \text{ or } 3.52\%}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

No matter what the incoming quality is, after rectifying inspection, the customer will receive product with an **average defect rate of at most 3.52%**.

---

## 3.3 The AOQL Bound — A Useful Inequality

For any single sampling plan with $n, c$, a tighter bound for AOQL is given by Dodge & Romig:

$$AOQL \le \frac{c + 1}{n} \cdot \text{(correction for finite N)}$$

This is useful for **quick screening** of a plan's AOQL bound before tabulating.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rectifying Inspection — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Incoming Lot of Size N] --> stepB[Draw Random Sample of n Items]
    stepB --> stepC{Number of Defectives d in Sample}
    stepC -->|"d <= c ACCEPT"| pathD[Ship the Lot as is Replace Defectives in Sample]
    stepC -->|"d > c REJECT"| pathE[Subject Entire Lot to 100 Percent Inspection]
    pathE --> stepF[Replace ALL Defective Items with Good Ones]
    stepF --> pathG[Ship the Rectified Lot]
    pathD --> outH[Outgoing Product to Customer]
    pathG --> outH
    outH --> outI[Long Run Average = AOQ at Quality p]
    outI --> outJ[Maximum over all p equals AOQL]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — AOQ Curve and AOQL

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inBlock[Input p] --> ocBlock[OC Curve Pa of p computed via Binomial]
    ocBlock --> multBlock[Multiply Pa by p]
    multBlock --> rectBlock[Multiply by Fraction N minus n over N for Rectification]
    rectBlock --> aoqBlock[AOQ of p]
    aoqBlock --> maxBlock[Take Maximum over p in 0 to 1]
    maxBlock --> aoqlBlock[AOQL]
    aoqBlock --> plotBlock[Plot AOQ vs p to Visualize Peak]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting a Plan via AOQL

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart[Start: Specified AOQL Target and Lot Size N] --> qStep1[Consult Dodge Romig Tables or Compute AOQ of p]
    qStep1 --> qStep2{Trial plan n and c}
    qStep2 --> qStep3[Compute AOQ of p for p grid]
    qStep3 --> qStep4{Max AOQ of p equals or less than target AOQL}
    qStep4 -->|"YES"| qAccept[Plan ACCEPTED]
    qStep4 -->|"NO"| qReject[Increase n or Adjust c and Retry]
    qReject --> qStep2
    qAccept --> qFinal[Final Plan n c with Guaranteed AOQL]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Dodge–Romig sampling inspection tables are organized as follows:
> - **Tables for AOQL** (most common) → given target AOQL, lot size $N$, and process variability, the tables directly give $(n, c)$.
> - **Tables for LTPD** → given consumer's risk and $p_2$, the tables yield $(n, c)$.
> - **Tables for Producer's Risk / AQL** → given $\alpha$ and $p_1$, the tables yield $(n, c)$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Average Outgoing Quality Limit (AOQL). Mention its significance in acceptance sampling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **AOQL** is defined as the maximum value of the **Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)** over all possible values of the incoming fraction defective $p$ in the range $[0, 1]$, when rectifying inspection is used.
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. AOQL provides a **guaranteed upper bound** on the long-run average fraction defective in the product shipped to the customer, regardless of the incoming lot quality.
> 2. It is a **single-number summary** of the protective power of a sampling plan, making it easier to compare alternative plans.
> 3. It is the basis of the **Dodge–Romig AOQL tables** used to design cost-effective acceptance sampling procedures in industry.
>
> (Any 3 of the above points — 3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between AOQ and AOQL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!TIP]
> | Aspect | AOQ | AOQL |
> |---|---|---|
> | Full form | Average Outgoing Quality | Average Outgoing Quality Limit |
> | Definition | Average fraction defective shipped for a *given* incoming quality $p$ | *Maximum* of AOQ over all $p$ |
> | Function of $p$? | Yes — $AOQ(p)$ | No — a single number |
> | Curve | Rises to a peak | The peak value of the AOQ curve |
> | Role | Performance metric at quality $p$ | Worst-case guarantee |
>
> (Table + 2–3 lines of explanation — 3 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the formula for the Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) under rectifying inspection for a single sampling plan with lot size $N$, sample size $n$, acceptance number $c$, and incoming fraction defective $p$. State the assumption clearly.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A single sampling plan has $N = 1500$, $n = 60$, $c = 1$. Compute the AOQ at $p = 0.02$ and $p = 0.05$. Also, by tabulating for $p = 0.01, 0.02, \dots, 0.08$, estimate the AOQL.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumption:** Rectifying inspection — rejected lots are 100% inspected and defectives are replaced by good items. Sample items found defective are also replaced.

**Step 1:** Probability of acceptance:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

**Step 2:** Expected number of defectives from accepted lot (only un-inspected portion contributes, since sample defectives are replaced):

$$E[\text{defectives} \mid \text{accepted}] = (N - n) \cdot p$$

**Step 3:** Expected defectives from rejected lot = 0 (100% rectification).

**Step 4:** Total expected defectives per lot:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{defectives}] &= P_a \cdot (N - n) \cdot p + (1 - P_a) \cdot 0 \\
&= P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** AOQ per unit:

$$AOQ(p) = \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}$$

(Each step carries 1 mark; final boxed equation carries 1.5 marks; assumption carries 0.5 mark — total 7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

For $N = 1500$, $n = 60$, $c = 1$:

$$P_a(p) = (1-p)^{60} + 60 p (1-p)^{59}$$

The factor $(N-n)/N = 1440/1500 = 0.96$.

**Compute $P_a(0.02)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.98)^{60} &\approx 0.2976 \\
60 \cdot 0.02 \cdot (0.98)^{59} &= 1.2 \cdot 0.3037 \approx 0.3644 \\
P_a(0.02) &\approx 0.6620
\end{aligned}
$$

$$AOQ(0.02) = 0.6620 \times 0.02 \times 0.96 = 0.01271$$

**Compute $P_a(0.05)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.95)^{60} &\approx 0.0461 \\
60 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (0.95)^{59} &= 3.0 \cdot 0.0485 \approx 0.1455 \\
P_a(0.05) &\approx 0.1916
\end{aligned}
$$

$$AOQ(0.05) = 0.1916 \times 0.05 \times 0.96 = 0.00920$$

**Tabulation for AOQL:**

| $p$ | $P_a$ | $AOQ = P_a \cdot p \cdot 0.96$ |
|-----|-------|-------------------------------|
| 0.01 | 0.9106 | 0.00874 |
| 0.02 | 0.6620 | 0.01271 |
| 0.03 | 0.4222 | 0.01216 |
| 0.04 | 0.2424 | 0.00931 |
| 0.05 | 0.1916 | 0.00920 |
| 0.06 | 0.0803 | 0.00463 |
| 0.07 | 0.0336 | 0.00226 |
| 0.08 | 0.0140 | 0.00108 |

**Estimated AOQL:** Occurs around $p = 0.02$:

$$\boxed{AOQL \approx 0.0127 \text{ or } 1.27\%}$$

---

### **QUESTION B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of rectifying inspection. How does it lead to the concept of AOQ? Sketch a typical AOQ curve and mark the AOQL.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturer uses a single sampling plan with $n = 100$, $c = 2$, $N = 5000$ under rectifying inspection. If the incoming quality is $p = 0.03$, find (i) the probability of acceptance, (ii) the AOQ, and (iii) the average total inspection (ATI). State whether the plan is acceptable if the target AOQL is 2%.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Concept (7 marks):**

**Rectifying Inspection (3 marks):** In rectifying inspection, every rejected lot is subjected to **100% inspection**, and all defective items found are **replaced by good ones** before the lot is shipped. This ensures that rejected lots become "perfect" before reaching the customer. Sample items found defective are also replaced, so the only defectives that can escape to the customer are those in the **un-inspected portion of accepted lots**.

**AOQ concept (2 marks):** Because accepted lots still contain some defectives in the un-inspected portion, the *long-run* average fraction defective in shipped product depends on the incoming quality $p$ and the probability of acceptance $P_a(p)$. This expected value is the **AOQ**.

**Sketch of AOQ curve (2 marks):**

```
AOQ
 |        ___
 |       /   \      ← Peak = AOQL
 |      /     \
 |     /       \
 |    /         \____
 |___/                \_____
 |______________________________ p
       p*
```

The curve starts at 0, rises to a maximum (the AOQL at $p = p^*$), and falls back to 0 as $p \to 1$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

For $n = 100$, $c = 2$, $N = 5000$, $p = 0.03$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= (0.97)^{100} + 100(0.03)(0.97)^{99} + \frac{100 \cdot 99}{2}(0.03)^2 (0.97)^{98}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computing each term:**

- $(0.97)^{100} \approx 0.04755$
- $100 \cdot 0.03 \cdot (0.97)^{99} = 3 \cdot 0.04902 \approx 0.14707$
- $\frac{9900}{2} \cdot 0.0009 \cdot (0.97)^{98} = 4950 \cdot 0.0009 \cdot 0.05054 \approx 0.22516$

$$P_a(0.03) = 0.04755 + 0.14707 + 0.22516 = 0.4198$$

**(i) Probability of acceptance:** $P_a \approx 0.4198$ **(2 marks)**

**(ii) AOQ:**

$$AOQ = P_a \cdot p \cdot \frac{N - n}{N} = 0.4198 \cdot 0.03 \cdot \frac{4900}{5000} = 0.4198 \cdot 0.03 \cdot 0.98$$

$$AOQ \approx 0.01234 \text{ or } 1.234\%$$

**(iii) ATI:**

$$ATI = n \cdot P_a + N \cdot (1 - P_a) = 100 \cdot 0.4198 + 5000 \cdot 0.5802$$

$$ATI = 41.98 + 2901.0 = 2942.98 \approx 2943 \text{ units}$$

**(Decision on acceptability):** Since the AOQ at $p = 0.03$ is $1.234\%$, but the AOQL (the maximum over all $p$) is likely higher than this — for $n = 100, c = 2$, the AOQL from standard Dodge–Romig tables is approximately **2.2%**, which **exceeds the target of 2% only marginally or fails the constraint**. To meet a 2% AOQL target, the manufacturer should **increase $n$ or lower $c$**.

**(2 marks for final conclusion and recommendation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mark-loser:** Forgetting the factor $(N - n)/N$ in the AOQ formula. Writing $AOQ = P_a \cdot p$ when the sample is a non-negligible fraction of $N$ loses **1 full mark** in Part B derivations.
> 2. **AOQL ≠ AOQ at one $p$:** Many students confuse AOQ (computed at a specific $p$) with AOQL (the maximum). Always tabulate and find the peak.
> 3. **Sign convention for producer's/consumer's risk:** Producer's risk $\alpha$ is the probability of *rejecting* a good lot ($1 - P_a(p_1)$), and consumer's risk $\beta$ is the probability of *accepting* a bad lot ($1 - P_a(p_2)$). Mixing them up costs marks.
> 4. **No programming in answer scripts:** For 2024 Scheme, the question bank explicitly tests derivations. Do not insert Python/MATLAB code; show formula-based manual computation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Key bullets to remember before the exam:**
> - **AOQL = peak of the AOQ curve** over all $p \in [0, 1]$.
> - **Rectifying inspection** = 100% screen of rejected lots + replacement of defectives in samples.
> - **AOQ formula (finite lot):** $AOQ = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n) / N$.
> - **AOQ formula (large lot, $n \ll N$):** $AOQ \approx P_a \cdot p$.
> - **$P_a$ for $c = 0$:** $(1-p)^n$.
> - **$P_a$ for $c = 1$:** $(1-p)^n + np(1-p)^{n-1}$.
> - **$P_a$ for general $c$:** $\sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$.
> - The AOQ curve **starts at 0, peaks at AOQL, returns to 0** as $p \to 1$.
> - Higher $n$ or lower $c$ → **lower AOQL** but **higher inspection cost (ATI)**.
> - Dodge–Romig tables are organized by **target AOQL, LTPD, or AQL**.
> - ATI = $n P_a + N(1 - P_a)$ = average number of items inspected per lot.
> - ASN applies to double/multiple sampling; for single sampling, ASN = $n$ always.
> - **Use Binomial** for small lots and **Poisson approximation** for large $n$ and small $p$.
> - **Never** confuse AOQ (function of $p$) with AOQL (single peak value).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Sample Continuous and Sequential Sampling Plans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT521 - ENGINEERING STATISTICS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Standard Sample Continuous and Sequential Sampling Plans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Standard Sampling Plans (Lot-by-Lot Inspection)

A **Standard Sampling Plan** is a pre-specified statistical quality control procedure applied to discrete **lots** (batches) of manufactured products, where a random sample is drawn from each lot to make an accept/reject decision based on the number of defectives found.

### 1.1.1 Single Sampling Plan (SSP)
A plan where a **single random sample of size `n`** is drawn from a lot of size `N`. The lot is accepted if the number of defectives `d` in the sample satisfies `d ≤ c` (acceptance number); otherwise, the lot is rejected.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** A Single Sampling Plan is defined by the triple **(n, c)** — sample size and acceptance number — together with lot size `N`.

### 1.1.2 Double Sampling Plan (DSP)
A plan involving **two stages of sampling**. A first sample of size `n₁` is taken; based on the result, the lot may be:
- **Accepted** (if `d₁ ≤ a₁`)
- **Rejected** (if `d₁ > a₂`)
- **Sent for second sample** (if `a₁ < d₁ ≤ a₂`) of size `n₂`, and final decision based on combined defectives.

### 1.1.3 Multiple Sampling Plan
Extension of double sampling to **k stages** of sampling. Each stage has its own acceptance and rejection numbers. Decision is made at the earliest possible stage.

### 1.1.4 Sequential Sampling Plan (Wald's SPRT)
Items are inspected **one-by-one (or in small groups)** sequentially. After each inspection, a decision is made to:
- **Accept** the lot
- **Reject** the lot
- **Continue** sampling

The plan is defined by two parallel **straight-line boundaries** on a graph of cumulative defectives vs sample number.

---

## 1.2 Continuous Sampling Plans (Inspection of Production Stream)

When items are produced in a **continuous flow** (not discrete lots — e.g., bolts, screws, light bulbs rolling off a conveyor), lot-by-lot inspection is meaningless. **Continuous Sampling Plans (CSP)** were developed by **H.F. Dodge** (1943) for such streams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Standard Sampling Plans** → for **DISCRETE LOTS** (finite batches)
> - **Continuous Sampling Plans** → for **CONTINUOUS PRODUCTION FLOW** (infinite stream)

The three classic Dodge plans are:
- **CSP-1** — basic plan, switches between 100% inspection and sampling
- **CSP-2** — improved version, prevents clearing after a single defective
- **CSP-3** — tightened version with rectifying inspection

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Lot-by-Lot vs Stream):**
> - **Lot-by-Lot (Standard):** Imagine a bakery producing **100 bread packets at a time**. You randomly open **5 packets** and check the quality. Decision: accept or reject the **batch of 100**. This is a *Single Sampling Plan*.
> - **Double Sampling:** You first check 3 packets. If results are inconclusive, you check 2 more. The bakery owner saves the cost of opening 2 extra packets when the first 3 are clearly bad.
> - **Sequential:** A customs officer inspects a traveler's luggage **one item at a time**. After each item, the officer decides: "Looks fine — let them go", "Smells bad — confiscate", or "Need to check more". They don't pre-commit to a fixed number of items.
> - **Continuous Sampling (CSP-1):** A conveyor belt carries **thousands of bulbs per hour**. You can't form "lots". So you inspect **every bulb** for a while, and once you see `i` consecutive good ones, you switch to inspecting only **1 in every `f` bulbs**.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in **Bold**)

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Producer's Risk** | **α** | Probability of rejecting a **good lot** (acceptable quality level `p₀`) |
| **Consumer's Risk** | **β** | Probability of accepting a **bad lot** (rejection quality level `p₁`) |
| **Acceptable Quality Level** | **AQL = p₀** | Worst tolerable process fraction defective |
| **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective** | **LTPD = p₁** | Worst lot consumer will accept |
| **Average Outgoing Quality Limit** | **AOQL** | Maximum average quality after rectification |
| **Average Sample Number** | **ASN** | Expected number of items inspected per lot |
| **Operating Characteristic Function** | **P_a(p)** | Probability of acceptance at fraction defective `p` |
| **Sampling Frequency** | **f = 1/n** | For CSP, fraction inspected after clearing |
| **Clearance Number** | **i** | For CSP, consecutive defect-free items required to switch from 100% to sampling |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — Single Sampling Plan

The OC function gives the probability of accepting a lot with fraction defective `p` when sampling `n` items and accepting on `c` or fewer defectives.

**Exact (Binomial):**

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$$

**Poisson Approximation (when `n` is large, `p` is small, `np ≤ 10`):**

$$P_a(p) \approx \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$$

**Why?** Because the binomial distribution approaches Poisson with parameter `λ = np` for large `n` and small `p`. Easier to compute via tables.

---

## 2.2 Double Sampling Plan — Decision Logic

Let `n₁, n₂` = sample sizes; `a₁` = first acceptance number; `a₂` = first rejection number; `a` = combined acceptance number after second sample.

| First Sample Defectives `d₁` | Decision |
|---|---|
| `d₁ ≤ a₁` | **Accept the lot** |
| `a₁ < d₁ ≤ a₂` | **Take second sample of `n₂`** |
| `d₁ > a₂` | **Reject the lot** |

After second sample of `n₂`, with `d₂` defectives:
- If `d₁ + d₂ ≤ a` → **Accept**
- If `d₁ + d₂ > a` → **Reject**

### 2.2.1 Probability of Acceptance (Double Plan)

$$P_a(p) = P_a^{(1)}(p) + P_a^{(2)}(p)$$

$$P_a^{(1)}(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{a_1} \binom{n_1}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n_1-d}$$

$$P_a^{(2)}(p) = \left[\sum_{d_1=a_1+1}^{a_2} \binom{n_1}{d_1} p^{d_1}(1-p)^{n_1-d_1}\right] \cdot \left[\sum_{d_2=0}^{a-d_1} \binom{n_2}{d_2} p^{d_2}(1-p)^{n_2-d_2}\right]$$

### 2.2.2 Average Sample Number (ASN) — Double Plan

$$ASN = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P(\text{take second sample})$$

$$ASN = n_1 + n_2 \cdot \left[\sum_{d_1=a_1+1}^{a_2} \binom{n_1}{d_1} p^{d_1}(1-p)^{n_1-d_1}\right]$$

**Insight:** The double plan has **lower ASN** than single plan for intermediate values of `p`, but the **same ASN** at extremes (always accept or always reject after first sample).

---

## 2.3 Sequential Sampling Plan — Wald's SPRT

Define:
- `p₀` = acceptable quality level (AQL)
- `p₁` = rejection quality level (LTPD or RQL)
- `α` = producer's risk, `β` = consumer's risk

### 2.3.1 Boundary Constants

$$A = \frac{1-\beta}{\alpha}, \quad B = \frac{\beta}{1-\alpha}$$

### 2.3.2 Logarithmic Constants

$$g_1 = \log_e(A) = \log_e\left(\frac{1-\beta}{\alpha}\right)$$

$$g_2 = -\log_e(B) = -\log_e\left(\frac{\beta}{1-\alpha}\right)$$

### 2.3.3 Hypothesis Constants (per item)

$$h_1 = \log_e\left(\frac{p_1}{p_0}\right)$$

$$h_0 = \log_e\left(\frac{1-p_1}{1-p_0}\right) \quad \text{(negative value)}$$

### 2.3.4 Decision Boundaries

For cumulative sample `n` and cumulative defectives `d`:

**Accept if:** $\quad d \leq -g_2 + \left(\frac{h_0}{h_0 - h_1}\right) n$

**Reject if:** $\quad d \geq g_1 + \left(\frac{h_0}{h_0 - h_1}\right) n$

**Continue if:** neither boundary is crossed.

### 2.3.5 Average Sample Number (ASN) for SPRT

$$ASN_{p_0} = \frac{(\alpha \log_e B^{-1} - (1-\alpha)\log_e A)}{p_0 \log_e\left(\frac{p_1}{p_0}\right) + (1-p_0)\log_e\left(\frac{1-p_1}{1-p_0}\right)}$$

$$ASN_{p_1} = \frac{((1-\beta)\log_e B^{-1} - \beta \log_e A)}{p_1 \log_e\left(\frac{p_1}{p_0}\right) + (1-p_1)\log_e\left(\frac{1-p_1}{1-p_0}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wald's Famous Result:** On average, sequential sampling requires **~ 50% fewer inspections** than the equivalent single sampling plan at `p = p₀` or `p = p₁`.

---

## 2.4 Continuous Sampling Plan — CSP-1 (Dodge 1943)

### Procedure:
1. Begin with **100% inspection** of all items.
2. Once **`i` consecutive items** are found defect-free, switch to **sampling mode** — inspect **1 out of every `f` items** (sampling frequency `f = 1/n`).
3. If a **defective is found** in sampling mode, return immediately to 100% inspection.

### 2.4.1 Average Fraction Inspected (AFI)

$$AFI = \frac{f}{f + N \cdot p} \quad \text{(approximation for large } N\text{)}$$

Where:
- `f` = sampling frequency (e.g., 1/5 means inspect 1 of every 5)
- `N` = lot size (for continuous: number of items produced during clearing period)
- `p` = process fraction defective

### 2.4.2 AOQL Approximation for CSP-1 (Knock & Kirkpatrick Table)

Dodge provided tables. The most-used approximation (large `N`):

$$AOQL \approx \frac{1.29}{\sqrt{N}} \quad \text{at } i = 1$$

More precisely, for clearing number `i` and sampling frequency `1/f`:

$$AOQL = \frac{(i) \cdot f}{\text{lookup table value}}$$

---

## 2.5 Continuous Sampling Plan — CSP-2 (Dodge & Torrey 1951)

Modification of CSP-1 to prevent clearance after a single defective.

### Procedure:
- Same as CSP-1, but when a defective is found in sampling mode, the **next `i` items** must also be inspected 100% before returning to sampling — only after **`i` consecutive defect-free** items can you switch back to `1/f` sampling.

### 2.5.1 Average Fraction Inspected

$$AFI_{CSP-2} = \frac{f}{(f+1)(N \cdot p / f + 1)} \quad \text{(approximation)}$$

A common tabulated form is:
$$AFI = \frac{f}{N \cdot p + (f+1)}$$

---

## 2.6 Continuous Sampling Plan — CSP-3 (Dodge 1956)

Used when **rectification** (replacing defectives) is required AND when no further 100% inspection is needed once sampling mode is entered.

### Procedure:
- 100% inspection initially; once `i` consecutive defect-free, switch to sampling.
- In sampling mode, defectives are **rectified** (replaced/reworked) but **sampling continues** — no return to 100% unless quality is very poor.

### 2.6.1 Average Fraction Inspected

$$AFI = f \cdot p + \frac{1}{N} \cdot \text{(clearance period cost)}$$

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Plan Type | Key Formula | Parameters | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Sampling (OC)** | $P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d(1-p)^{n-d}$ | n, c, p | Acceptance probability |
| **Single (Poisson approx.)** | $P_a \approx \sum e^{-np}(np)^d/d!$ | n, c, p, λ=np | When n large, p small |
| **Double Sampling (ASN)** | $ASN = n_1 + n_2 P(\text{2nd sample})$ | n₁, n₂, a₁, a₂, a, p | Avg inspections |
| **SPRT Constants** | $A = (1-\beta)/\alpha$, $B = \beta/(1-\alpha)$ | α, β | SPRT boundaries |
| **SPRT Slope** | $s = h_0/(h_0 - h_1)$ | p₀, p₁ | Boundary slope |
| **SPRT Accept line** | $d_{acc} = -g_2 + s \cdot n$ | cumulative d, n | Lower boundary |
| **SPRT Reject line** | $d_{rej} = g_1 + s \cdot n$ | cumulative d, n | Upper boundary |
| **CSP-1 AFI** | $AFI = f/(f + Np)$ | f, N, p | Avg fraction inspected |
| **CSP-1 AOQL** | $AOQL \approx 1.29/\sqrt{N}$ | N (i=1) | Max avg outgoing quality |
| **CSP-2 AFI** | $AFI = f/(Np + f+1)$ | f, N, p | Tighter than CSP-1 |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these plans are used in industry:**
> - **Single Sampling**: Acceptance of bulk chemicals, fasteners, electronic components delivered in batches.
> - **Double Sampling**: Government procurement contracts, military supply inspection (MIL-STD).
> - **Sequential Sampling**: Destructive testing (e.g., testing tensile strength of steel — once broken, the item is destroyed; inspect one by one).
> - **CSP-1/2/3**: Assembly lines of light bulbs, screws, bottles, resistors, transistors — where production is continuous and lot formation is impossible.
> - **CSP-3**: Used when **rectification** (rework/replace) is part of the contract with the consumer.
